> **Why "Micro"?** Each individual asperity is on the order of **1 μm – 50 μm** in tip radius. Hence, although a single event removes only nanograms of material, the cumulative effect of **millions of asperities per cm²** under continuous sliding is catastrophic.

### 2.2 The Rabinowicz Micro-Cutting Model

Rabinowicz idealized the abrasive particle as a **rigid conical indenter** of semi-cone angle $\theta$ sliding across a perfectly plastic surface under normal load $W_n$. The horizontal (tangential) force required to slide it is:

$$
W_t \;=\; W_n \cdot \tan\theta
$$

The volume of the groove swept per unit sliding distance is:

$$
V_{\text{groove}} \;=\; \frac{W_n}{H_s} \cdot \cot\theta
$$

But only a fraction $f_c$ of this volume is detached as a chip; the rest is plowed aside. The parameter $f_c$ is called the **fraction of contact making a cutting action**, and lies between **0 (pure ploughing, no wear)** and **1 (pure cutting, maximum wear)**.

The volumetric wear rate per unit sliding distance becomes:

$$
\frac{dV}{dL} \;=\; f_c \cdot \frac{W_n}{H_s} \cdot \cot\theta
$$

For typical abrasives like SiC or Al₂O₃, $\theta \approx 60°$ to $75°$, giving $\cot\theta$ in the range **0.27 to 0.58**. Measured $f_c$ values lie between **0.1 and 0.5** for metals, with ductile metals having low $f_c$ and brittle ones approaching 1.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Validity / Remarks |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dV}{dL} = k_a \cdot \dfrac{W_n}{H_s}$ | $V$ = volume lost; $L$ = sliding distance; $W_n$ = normal load; $H_s$ = surface hardness; $k_a$ = wear coefficient | m³, m, N, Pa, dimensionless | **Archard-Type Abrasive Wear Equation**; valid for two-body abrasion. |
| 2 | $W \;=\; \dfrac{V}{W_n \cdot L}$ | $W$ = specific wear rate | mm³/N·m | Reciprocal of wear resistance; lower is better. |
| 3 | $k \;=\; \dfrac{H_a}{H_s}$ | $H_a$ = abrasive hardness, $H_s$ = surface hardness | dimensionless | Hard abrasion when $k \geq 1.2$. |
| 4 | $W_t = W_n \cdot \tan\theta$ | $W_t$ = tangential force, $\theta$ = semi-cone angle of abrasive | N, N, degrees | Rabinowicz single-asperity model. |
| 5 | $f_c = \dfrac{V_{\text{chip}}}{V_{\text{groove}}}$ | $f_c$ = cutting fraction | dimensionless | $0 \le f_c \le 1$. |
| 6 | $\dfrac{dV}{dL} = f_c \cdot \dfrac{W_n}{H_s} \cdot \cot\theta$ | (see above) | m³/m | Rabinowicz micro-cutting equation. |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{cut}} = \dfrac{f_c \cdot \cot\theta}{2}$ | $\eta_{\text{cut}}$ = cutting efficiency | dimensionless | Used in abrasive-jet machining. |
| 8 | $K_{\text{IC}} \geq \beta \cdot \left(\dfrac{H_s}{E}\right)^{-n}$ | $K_{\text{IC}}$ = fracture toughness; $E$ = Young's modulus | MPa·m^0.5, Pa, Pa | Criterion for transition from plastic to brittle wear. |
| 9 | $\text{WR} \;\propto\; \dfrac{\text{Load} \cdot \text{Sliding distance}}{\text{Hardness}}$ | WR = wear rate | m³ | Empirical design rule. |
| 10 | $\tau_{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{H_s}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Tresca critical shear stress | Pa | Yield criterion for plastic groove formation. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Watch (KTU Examiner's Trap):** Hardness $H_s$ must be in **Pa (or N/m²)** for the equations to yield volume in m³. If hardness is given in **HV (Vickers)** or **HB (Brinell)**, you must convert using $H_s \,[\text{Pa}] \approx 9.81 \times 10^6 \times \text{HV}$. Always state the unit conversions explicitly in your solution.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility and Real-World Significance

- **Mining & Earth-Moving:** Bulldozer blades, crusher liners, and conveyor screws are designed with **abrasion-resistant (AR) steels** (e.g., AR400, AR500) where the numerical suffix denotes the **Brinell hardness**. Selection is governed by the **Richardson abrasion test**.
- **Agricultural Engineering:** Ploughshares and tiller blades suffer three-body abrasion from soil; **hardfacing with WC-Co** or **boronised layers** is standard.
- **Manufacturing:** Grinding wheels, sandblasting nozzles, and slurry pipelines rely on the same physics; **abrasive flow finishing (AFF)** uses the Rabinowicz chip-formation model.
- **Biomedical:** Dental drills and orthopedic bone-cutting tools follow the same mechanics; biocompatible ceramics like **zirconia-toughened alumina (ZTA)** are used for high wear life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Design Rule of Thumb:** Doubling the surface hardness of a steel component **halves the abrasive wear rate** (inverse proportionality in Eq. 1). This is why **surface hardening routes — carburising, nitriding, induction hardening, laser cladding, hard chromium plating, and HVOF WC-CoCr coatings** — are central to the PEPET862 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Archard-Type Abrasive Wear Equation

We start from the assumption that, in steady-state two-body abrasion, the **real area of contact** $A_r$ between the asperities and the softer surface determines the load-bearing capacity.

**Step 1 — Real Area of Contact from Hardness:**

By the Meyer hardness definition, the real area of contact is the load divided by the indentation hardness of the softer material:

$$
A_r \;=\; \frac{W_n}{H_s}
$$

**Step 2 — Geometry of a Single Asperity:**

Assume each abrasive asperity has a conical geometry with average contact area $a$ (cross-section) and that a wear particle of similar dimension $a$ is detached when sliding distance $a$ is covered. The wear volume per asperity per pass is:

$$
\delta V_{\text{asperity}} \;=\; a \cdot a \;=\; a^2
$$

**Step 3 — Number of Asperities in Contact:**

The number of asperities simultaneously in contact is the real area divided by the area of one asperity:

$$
n_{\text{asp}} \;=\; \frac{A_r}{a} \;=\; \frac{W_n}{H_s \cdot a}
$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric Wear per Unit Sliding Distance:**

Sliding distance $dL$ produces $\dfrac{dL}{a}$ asperity events per asperity, hence total wear volume:

$$
dV \;=\; n_{\text{asp}} \cdot \delta V_{\text{asperity}} \cdot \frac{dL}{a} \;=\; \frac{W_n}{H_s \cdot a} \cdot a^2 \cdot \frac{dL}{a} \;=\; \frac{W_n}{H_s} \cdot dL
$$

**Step 5 — Introduction of Wear Coefficient $k_a$:**

Real abrasive contact is rarely 100% efficient at producing detached chips; hence a dimensionless wear coefficient $k_a$ is included:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{dV}{dL} \;=\; k_a \cdot \frac{W_n}{H_s}\;}
$$

This is the **Archard abrasive wear equation**, the single most important equation in PEPET862. $k_a$ typically lies in the range $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$ for metals.

**Step 6 — Specific Wear Rate Form:**

Rearranging gives the specific wear rate $W$ in m²/N:

$$
W \;=\; \frac{1}{k_a \cdot H_s} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Wear rate} \propto \frac{1}{H_s}
$$

This proves the design rule: *increasing surface hardness lowers wear rate linearly.*

### 3.2 Derivation of the Rabinowicz Micro-Cutting Volume

We model the abrasive particle as a rigid cone of semi-angle $\theta$ sliding under a normal load $W_n$.

**Step 1 — Indentation Depth:**

The vertical depth of the groove when the cone is pressed into the surface is found by equating the projected contact area to $W_n / H_s$:

$$
\pi \, r^2 \;=\; \frac{W_n}{H_s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad r \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{W_n}{\pi H_s}}
$$

**Step 2 — Tangential Force Balance:**

For a cone sliding in a plastic medium, the tangential (cutting) force is:

$$
W_t \;=\; \tau_{\text{avg}} \cdot A_{\text{contact}} \;=\; \frac{H_s}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \pi r^2 \cdot \tan\theta
$$

Using $\tau_{\text{avg}} = H_s / \sqrt{3}$ (von Mises) and substituting $A_{\text{contact}} = \pi r^2 = W_n/H_s$:

$$
W_t \;=\; W_n \cdot \frac{\tan\theta}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

A more general form (without the $\sqrt{3}$) used by Rabinowicz directly is:

$$
W_t \;=\; W_n \cdot \tan\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Volume of Groove per Unit Slide:**

The cross-sectional area of the conical groove is:

$$
A_{\text{groove}} \;=\; r^2 \cdot \cot\theta \;=\; \frac{W_n}{H_s} \cdot \cot\theta
$$

**Step 4 — Wear Volume Accounting for Cutting Fraction $f_c$:**

Only a fraction $f_c$ is detached as a chip; the rest is plowed:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{dV}{dL} \;=\; f_c \cdot \frac{W_n}{H_s} \cdot \cot\theta\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with Archard Form:**

Setting the Archard coefficient as:

$$
k_a \;=\; f_c \cdot \cot\theta
$$

For $f_c = 0.3$ and $\theta = 70°$ ($\cot 70° \approx 0.36$):

$$
k_a \;\approx\; 0.3 \times 0.36 \;\approx\; 0.108
$$

This is a *very* high value, showing that pure micro-cutting (without ploughing energy loss) is the worst case.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A mild-steel plough blade (hardness **220 HV**) slides against a sandy soil (quartz particles, hardness **900 HV**) under a normal load of **500 N** at a sliding speed of **1 m/s** for **8 hours** per day. The cutting fraction is **0.25** and the effective semi-cone angle of the abrasive is **75°**. Calculate:
(i) The specific wear rate.
(ii) The total volume of material lost per day.

**Given Data:**
- $H_s = 220 \text{ HV} = 220 \times 9.81 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 2.158 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$
- $H_a = 900 \text{ HV} = 900 \times 9.81 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 8.829 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$
- $W_n = 500 \text{ N}$, $v = 1 \text{ m/s}$, $t = 8 \text{ h} = 28800 \text{ s}$, $L = 28800 \text{ m}$
- $f_c = 0.25$, $\theta = 75° \Rightarrow \cot 75° = 0.2679$

**Solution:**

**Step (i) — Specific wear rate:**

$$
k_a \;=\; f_c \cdot \cot\theta \;=\; 0.25 \times 0.2679 \;=\; 0.06698
$$

$$
W \;=\; \frac{dV}{dL} \cdot \frac{1}{W_n} \;=\; \frac{k_a}{H_s} \;=\; \frac{0.06698}{2.158 \times 10^9} \;=\; 3.10 \times 10^{-11} \;\; \text{m}^2/\text{N}
$$

In more useful engineering units:

$$
W \;=\; 3.10 \times 10^{-11} \times 10^9 \;\; \text{mm}^2/\text{N} \;=\; 3.10 \times 10^{-2} \;\; \text{mm}^2/\text{N} \;\;=\; 3.10 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{N·mm}
$$

**Step (ii) — Volume lost per day:**

$$
V \;=\; k_a \cdot \frac{W_n \cdot L}{H_s} \;=\; 0.06698 \times \frac{500 \times 28800}{2.158 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
V \;=\; 0.06698 \times \frac{1.44 \times 10^7}{2.158 \times 10^9} \;=\; 0.06698 \times 6.673 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 4.47 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{m}^3
$$

Converting to cm³:

$$
V \;\approx\; 447 \;\; \text{cm}^3 \;\; \text{per day}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 447 cm³ loss per day from a plough blade is **unacceptable**. The remedy, derived from the inverse-hardness relationship, is to **increase H_s** to at least **450 HV** (e.g., through boronising or hardfacing with WC-Co) — this alone would halve the wear loss to ~224 cm³/day.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mechanism Selection Chart

| Dominant Mechanism | Material Class | $f_c$ | Wear Coefficient $k_a$ | Counter-Measure |
|--------------------|----------------|-------|-------------------------|-----------------|
| **Micro-Ploughing** | Pure ductile (annealed Cu, Al) | 0.05 – 0.15 | $10^{-5}$ – $10^{-4}$ | Lubrication, surface finish |
| **Micro-Cutting** | Hardened steels, hard alloys | 0.20 – 0.50 | $10^{-3}$ – $10^{-2}$ | Higher hardness, hard coating |
| **Micro-Fatigue** | Surface-treated (carburised) | 0.10 – 0.30 | $10^{-4}$ – $10^{-3}$ | Sub-surface compressive stress (shot peening) |
| **Micro-Cracking** | Ceramics, white cast iron | 0.50 – 0.90 | $10^{-2}$ – $10^{-1}$ | Improve $K_{\text{IC}}$ (toughening) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Two-Body Abrasive Wear System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hard Asperity on Counterface] --> B[Sliding Contact under Normal Load Wn]
    B --> C{Dominance of Mechanism}
    C -->|Ductile Surface| D[Micro Plowing]
    C -->|Sharp Asperity + Hard Surface| E[Micro Cutting]
    C -->|Repeated Passes| F[Micro Fatigue]
    C -->|Brittle Surface| G[Micro Cracking]
    D --> H[Material Piled as Side Ridges]
    E --> I[Continuous Chip Formation]
    F --> J[Subsurface Crack Linkage]
    G --> K[Lateral Median Cracks]
    H --> L[Volume Loss dV and dL]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Archard Equation dV by dL equals ka Wn by Hs]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Three-Body Abrasive Wear System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Loose Hard Particle e.g. Quartz Sand] --> Q1[Trapped Between Two Sliding Surfaces]
    Q1 --> R1{Particle Motion Type}
    R1 -->|Rolling| S1[Low Stress Abrasion]
    R1 -->|Sliding| S2[High Stress Abrasion]
    R1 -->|Embedded| S3[Gouging Abrasion]
    S1 --> T1[Material Loss on Both Surfaces]
    S2 --> T1
    S3 --> T1
    T1 --> U1[Industrial Example Jaw Crusher Liner]
    T1 --> U2[Industrial Example Ball Mill]
    T1 --> U3[Industrial Example Bulldozer Track]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Decision Flow for Wear Mitigation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Component in Abrasive Environment] --> Check{Richardson Ratio k equals Ha by Hs}
    Check -->|k less than 1.2| SoftWear[Soft Abrasion — Low Wear]
    Check -->|k greater than or equal to 1.2| HardWear[Hard Abrasion — Severe]
    HardWear --> Select{Countermeasure}
    Select --> H1[Increase Surface Hardness — Carburise Nitride Harden]
    Select --> H2[Apply Hard Coating — HVOF WC CoCr Chrome Carbide]
    Select --> H3[Use Lubricant or Seal — Reduce Particle Ingress]
    Select --> H4[Material Upgrade — Tool Steel High Chrome Cast Iron]
    H1 --> Eval[Reevaluate k Ratio]
    H2 --> Eval
    H3 --> Eval
    H4 --> Eval
    Eval -->|Still High| NewDesign[Redesign Geometry or Replace Component]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Rabinowicz Single Asperity Force Balance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Asp[Conical Abrasive Asperity Semi Angle Theta] --> Norm[Normal Load Wn Applied Vertically]
    Asp --> Tang[Tangential Load Wt equals Wn tan Theta]
    Norm --> Surf[Soft Surface Yield at Shear Strength Hs by sqrt 3]
    Tang --> Chip[Material Detached as Chip with Fraction fc]
    Surf --> Chip
    Chip --> Vloss[Volumetric Wear dV by dL equals fc Wn cot Theta by Hs]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Reading Guide:** In the **KTU 2024 ESE**, you may be asked to *redraw* such a block diagram in your answer script. Memorize the **flow direction** and the **key variables** ($W_n$, $H_s$, $f_c$, $\theta$, $k_a$) — they are the 5 marks for a "diagram plus explanation" question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define abrasive wear. Distinguish clearly between two-body and three-body abrasive wear with one industrial example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Abrasive wear is the loss of material from a solid surface caused by the sliding action of hard particles or hard protuberances against it, under an applied normal load.

**Two-Body Abrasion (1 mark):** Here, the hard asperities are *fixed* on one of the contacting surfaces (e.g., grinding wheel, file). The hard surface directly scratches the softer one, and the geometry of the contacting asperity controls wear. *Example:* Filing action on mild steel; rock-drilling bit cutting through granite.

**Three-Body Abrasion (1 mark):** Here, loose hard particles (sand, ore, grit) are *trapped* between two sliding surfaces and act as the abrading medium. Both surfaces suffer material loss. *Example:* Jaw crusher mantle crushing quartz ore; bulldozer track pads sliding over sandy terrain.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State the Archard equation for abrasive wear and explain the significance of each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
\frac{dV}{dL} \;=\; k_a \cdot \frac{W_n}{H_s}
$$

- $dV$ = incremental volume of material worn away (m³).
- $dL$ = incremental sliding distance (m).
- $W_n$ = normal load applied at the contact (N).
- $H_s$ = indentation hardness of the softer wearing surface (Pa).
- $k_a$ = dimensionless wear coefficient, ranging from $10^{-4}$ (mild wear) to $10^{-2}$ (severe micro-cutting).

**Significance (½ mark each, 2 marks total):**
1. Wear volume is **directly proportional to load** — doubling load doubles wear.
2. Wear volume is **inversely proportional to hardness** — doubling hardness halves wear.
3. The coefficient $k_a$ bundles the geometry of the abrasive, the cutting fraction, and the wear mode (ploughing vs. cutting) into a single empirical parameter.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, describe the four micro-mechanisms of abrasive wear as classified by Rabinowicz. For each mechanism, state whether the material is detached, and give one example material in which that mechanism dominates.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A steel shaft (hardness **350 HV**) runs against a grinding wheel (abrasive hardness **2000 HV**) under a normal load of **800 N** at a sliding speed of **2 m/s** for **6 hours** per day. Assuming micro-cutting dominates with $f_c = 0.30$ and the effective semi-cone angle of the abrasive is **70°**, compute:
(i) the specific wear rate in mm³/N·m,
(ii) the total volumetric material loss per day, and
(iii) the percentage reduction in wear loss if the steel hardness is raised to **600 HV** by induction hardening.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — The four micro-mechanisms (7 marks = 1.5 marks each + 1 mark for diagram):**

| Mechanism | Material Detached? | Typical Example Material |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| **Micro-Ploughing** | No (sideways plastic displacement, ridges form) | Annealed copper, pure aluminium |
| **Micro-Cutting** | Yes (continuous chip like a turning operation) | Hardened tool steel, Stellite |
| **Micro-Fatigue** | Yes (delamination flakes after repeated passes) | Carburised low-carbon steel |
| **Micro-Cracking** | Yes (lateral/median crack chip-out, brittle) | Alumina, tungsten carbide, white cast iron |

*(In the answer script, draw four small sketches showing a 2D side-view of a conical asperity sliding, with arrows indicating flow of material.)*

**(b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $H_{s1} = 350 \text{ HV} = 350 \times 9.81 \times 10^6 = 3.4335 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$
- $H_{s2} = 600 \text{ HV} = 600 \times 9.81 \times 10^6 = 5.886 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$
- $W_n = 800 \text{ N}$, $v = 2 \text{ m/s}$, $t = 6 \text{ h} = 21600 \text{ s}$, $L = v t = 43200 \text{ m}$
- $f_c = 0.30$, $\theta = 70°$, $\cot 70° = 0.36397$
- $k_a = f_c \cdot \cot\theta = 0.30 \times 0.36397 = 0.10919$

**(i) Specific wear rate:**

$$
W \;=\; \frac{k_a}{H_{s1}} \;=\; \frac{0.10919}{3.4335 \times 10^9} \;=\; 3.181 \times 10^{-11} \;\; \text{m}^2/\text{N}
$$

Converting to mm³/N·m:

$$
W \;=\; 3.181 \times 10^{-11} \times \frac{10^9 \text{ mm}^2}{1 \text{ m}^2} \cdot \frac{1 \text{ m}}{1000 \text{ mm}} \;=\; 3.181 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{N·mm}
$$

In standard units: $\;W = 3.181 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{mm}^3/\text{N·m}$.

**(ii) Volumetric loss per day:**

$$
V_1 \;=\; k_a \cdot \frac{W_n \cdot L}{H_{s1}} \;=\; 0.10919 \times \frac{800 \times 43200}{3.4335 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
V_1 \;=\; 0.10919 \times \frac{3.456 \times 10^7}{3.4335 \times 10^9} \;=\; 0.10919 \times 1.00656 \times 10^{-2} \;=\; 1.099 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m}^3
$$

Converting: $\;V_1 = 1099 \;\; \text{cm}^3 \;=\; 1.099 \text{ litres}$ of steel per day.

**(iii) Reduction after induction hardening:**

Since wear is inversely proportional to hardness:

$$
\frac{V_2}{V_1} \;=\; \frac{H_{s1}}{H_{s2}} \;=\; \frac{350}{600} \;=\; 0.5833
$$

$$
V_2 \;=\; 0.5833 \times 1099 \;=\; 641 \;\; \text{cm}^3
$$

$$
\text{Percentage reduction} \;=\; (1 - 0.5833) \times 100\% \;=\; 41.67\%
$$

**Final Answer:** $W = 3.18 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{mm}^3/\text{N·m}$, $V_1 = 1099 \; \text{cm}^3/\text{day}$, wear reduction = **41.7%**.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Archard equation for abrasive wear starting from the real area of contact and Meyer hardness definition. Clearly state all assumptions used in the derivation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A crusher liner made of high-chromium white cast iron (hardness **650 HV**) operates in a copper ore grinding circuit. The quartz ore particles have a hardness of **1100 HV**, the normal contact load per particle is **2.5 N**, and the **Richardson abrasion index (k)** is reported to be **1.7**.
(i) Justify, using the threshold rule, whether the liner is in the *hard-abrasion* regime.
(ii) If the cutting fraction is **0.40** and the effective semi-cone angle is **65°**, estimate the wear coefficient $k_a$ and the specific wear rate.
(iii) Comment on **two surface-engineering routes** (coatings or heat treatments) that could extend the liner life by at least 50%.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Derivation (7 marks = 5 marks derivation + 2 marks assumptions):**

The full derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The student must reproduce:

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Asperities are conical or pyramidal with a single representative geometry.
2. Material removal occurs by plastic deformation; no elastic recovery effects.
3. The abrasive is much harder than the surface ($H_a \gg H_s$).
4. Wear debris size equals asperity contact size (geometric similitude).
5. Steady-state sliding; no chemical or thermal effects.

**Derivation steps (5 marks, 1 mark each):**
1. $A_r = W_n / H_s$ (Meyer hardness).
2. $n_{\text{asp}} = A_r / a = W_n / (H_s a)$ (asperity count).
3. $\delta V_{\text{asp}} = a^2$ (volume per asperity per pass).
4. $dV = n_{\text{asp}} \cdot \delta V \cdot (dL/a) = (W_n/H_s) \cdot dL$ (Archard result).
5. Insert wear coefficient $k_a$ for non-ideal contact → $\boxed{dV/dL = k_a W_n / H_s}$.

**(b) — Numerical and Discussion (7 marks):**

**(i) Hard-abrasion check (2 marks):**

$$
k \;=\; \frac{H_a}{H_s} \;=\; \frac{1100}{650} \;=\; 1.69 \approx 1.7 \;\;(\text{given})
$$

Since $k = 1.7 \geq 1.2$, the system is firmly in the **hard-abrasion regime** and significant abrasive wear is expected.

**(ii) Wear coefficient and specific wear rate (3 marks):**

$$
\cot 65° \;=\; 0.4663
$$

$$
k_a \;=\; f_c \cdot \cot\theta \;=\; 0.40 \times 0.4663 \;=\; 0.1865
$$

$$
H_s \;=\; 650 \times 9.81 \times 10^6 \;=\; 6.3765 \times 10^9 \;\; \text{Pa}
$$

$$
W \;=\; \frac{k_a}{H_s} \;=\; \frac{0.1865}{6.3765 \times 10^9} \;=\; 2.925 \times 10^{-11} \;\; \text{m}^2/\text{N}
$$

Converting: $\;W = 2.925 \times 10^{-2} \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{N·m}$.

**(iii) Surface engineering routes for ≥ 50% life extension (2 marks):**

Since wear rate $\propto 1/H_s$, to halve the wear rate we must at least **double the effective surface hardness**. Two routes:

1. **Deep Cryogenic Treatment (DCT) + Tempering** of the white cast iron: promotes precipitation of secondary carbides (M₇C₃ → M₂₃C₆), raising hardness from 650 HV to **800–900 HV**. This alone reduces wear by **~25–30%**.

2. **HVOF (High-Velocity Oxy-Fuel) Spraying of WC-CoCr Coating** (1200–1500 HV) on the liner working face. The coating has ~2× the surface hardness of the substrate, hence wear drops by **~50%**. This is the industry standard for crusher liners in mineral processing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert HV → Pa. KTU strictly deducts 1–2 marks for mixing units in the same equation.
> 2. **Forgetting $f_c$ (–1 mark):** Students often write the Archard form but then forget to multiply by $f_c$ when switching to the Rabinowicz micro-cutting version. Be explicit about which regime is assumed.
> 3. **Direction of Proportionality Confusion (–1 mark):** Some students think "higher load means less wear." This is wrong — load is *directly* proportional to wear in the Archard equation.
> 4. **Ignoring the k = 1.2 threshold (–1 mark):** Always check and explicitly state whether the regime is soft or hard abrasion before applying the equation.
> 5. **Inadequate Diagrams (–1 mark):** For 7-mark sub-parts that ask for a *sketch*, a one-line description without a diagram loses at least 1 mark.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Abrasive wear = material loss by hard asperities sliding on a softer surface**, governed by **Archard's equation** $\;dV/dL = k_a \cdot W_n / H_s\;$.
- **Two classes:** **Two-body** (asperity fixed on counterface) and **Three-body** (loose hard particles trapped between surfaces).
- **Four sub-mechanisms** (Rabinowicz): **Micro-ploughing** (no chip), **Micro-cutting** (chip detached), **Micro-fatigue** (delamination), **Micro-cracking** (brittle).
- **Richardson ratio** $k = H_a / H_s \geq 1.2$ defines **hard abrasion**; below it, wear is negligible.
- **Wear rate is inversely proportional to surface hardness** — this is the *single most important design lever* in the PEPET862 syllabus.
- **Wear rate is directly proportional to normal load** and **sliding distance** (or sliding speed × time).
- **Cutting fraction $f_c$** lies between 0 and 1; it represents the *efficiency* of abrasive chip formation. Ductile metals have low $f_c$ (≈ 0.1), brittle ones approach $f_c \to 1$.
- **Effective semi-cone angle $\theta$** of typical abrasives (SiC, Al₂O₃, SiO₂) is 60°–75°; $\cot\theta$ in the range 0.27–0.58.
- **ASTM G65 Dry Sand/Rubber Wheel test** is the standard lab method to report wear rate in **mm³/N·m**.
- **Mitigation hierarchy** (in increasing cost): Lubrication/sealing → Surface finish → Surface hardening (carburising, nitriding) → Hard coatings (HVOF WC-CoCr, hard chrome) → Bulk material upgrade (AR steels, white cast irons, Stellite, cermets).
- **Remember to convert hardness:** $H_s \,[\text{Pa}] = 9.81 \times 10^6 \times \text{HV}$. Always show this conversion step in KTU solutions.
- **Critical hardness rule:** If $H_s \geq 0.8 \, H_a$, abrasive wear transitions from **micro-cutting** to **micro-ploughing** and wear drops sharply — a key concept for material selection.
- **No chemical reaction** is involved in pure abrasive wear — it is a purely *mechanical* phenomenon, distinguishing it from **corrosive wear** (corrosion-assisted) and **oxidative wear** (tribo-oxidation).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wear Phenomena Defined by Dimensionless Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wear Phenomena Defined by Dimensionless Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

In tribology, **Wear Phenomena Defined by Dimensionless Parameters** refers to the classification and quantification of material loss during sliding, rolling, abrasion, erosion, or fatigue contacts using **scale-invariant, normalized indices** that allow comparison between systems of vastly different sizes, loads, and operating speeds.

These dimensionless (or normalized) parameters strip away the dimensional units (mm³, N, m, s) and reveal the **fundamental physical behavior** of the wear mechanism. The most widely accepted dimensionless wear descriptors in KTU-aligned tribology are:

| # | Dimensionless Parameter | Symbol | Significance |
|---|------------------------|--------|--------------|
| 1 | Specific Wear Rate (Dimensionless Wear Coefficient) | $K$ | Archard's number; ratio of actual worn volume to geometric sliding volume |
| 2 | Elastic Strain to Failure | $H/E$ | Resistance of a material to elastic deformation |
| 3 | Plasticity Index (Wear Resistance Index) | $H^3/E^{*2}$ | Resistance to plastic penetration |
| 4 | Normalized Contact Depth | $h/a$ | Geometric similarity in Hertzian contacts |
| 5 | Wear Number | $W_n$ | Material-independent ranking of wear severity |
| 6 | Similarity (Pommer) Parameters | $P_s, S_s$ | Geometric-kinematic scaling of tribosystems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Dimensionless parameters allow the engineer to *compare a brass pin on a steel disc* in a laboratory pin-on-disc test directly with a *bronze journal bearing in a 50 MW turbine* in industry. Without these normalized indices, no comparison is possible.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two children sliding down **two completely different playground slides**:
- Slide A is 2 m long, on a smooth plastic sheet, sliding for 5 s.
- Slide B is 50 m long, on a waxed wooden plank, sliding for 30 s.

If you only report "wear depth" (e.g., 0.1 mm on A, 0.6 mm on B), you **cannot** tell which material is *intrinsically* more wear-resistant. The loads, distances, and times all differ.

Now, if you compute a **dimensionless parameter**, like:

$$
K = \frac{V \cdot H}{W \cdot L}
$$

You are essentially asking: *"If both materials experienced the same load and the same sliding distance, how much volume would each lose?"* The answer is a pure number (no units) — a **fingerprint of the material's wear behavior**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** Dimensionless wear parameters are the "ratings" of the wear world — just like a star rating lets you compare hotels of different sizes, a dimensionless wear coefficient lets you compare materials under different conditions.

## 1.3 The Three Categories of Dimensionless Wear Indices

**1. Material-Governed Indices** (depend only on intrinsic material properties)
- $H/E$, $H^3/E^{*2}$, $H/E^{*2}$ ratios.

**2. System-Governed Indices** (depend on the contact geometry and kinematics)
- $a/R$, $h/R$, $P_s$, $S_s$.

**3. Performance Indices** (combine material and system into a single ranking)
- $K$ (Archard), $W_n$ (Wear Number), $\omega_s$ (Specific Wear Rate normalized).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Dimensionless Wear Theory

### Pillar I — Why "Dimensionless" is the Heart of Tribology

Dimensional analysis, formalized via the **Buckingham $\pi$-Theorem**, reduces the governing variables of a tribosystem to a smaller set of dimensionless groups. For a generic wear process, the dependent variable is the *removed volume* $V$ and the independent variables are *load $W$*, *sliding distance $L$*, *hardness $H$*, *time $t$*, *velocity $v$*, *temperature $T$*, and *material constants*.

By enforcing dimensional homogeneity, we obtain two primary dimensionless groups:

$$
\pi_1 = \frac{V \cdot H}{W \cdot L} \quad \text{(Archard's Dimensionless Wear Coefficient)}
$$

$$
\pi_2 = \frac{v \cdot t}{L} \quad \text{(Kinematic / Similarity Group)}
$$

### Pillar II — The $H/E$ and $H^3/E^{*2}$ Material Philosophy

The Hertzian contact theory tells us that the **onset of plasticity** beneath a sliding contact is governed not by hardness alone, but by the ratio of hardness to the *effective* (reduced) elastic modulus:

$$
E^* = \left[ \frac{1-\nu_1^2}{E_1} + \frac{1-\nu_2^2}{E_2} \right]^{-1}
$$

Two dimensionless indices dominate:

- **Elastic Strain to Failure (H/E):** A high $H/E$ means the material can sustain large elastic strains before yielding → good for resisting *abrasive* and *fatigue* wear.
- **Plasticity Index ($H^3/E^{*2}$):** Proportional to the load required to initiate plastic deformation in a Hertzian contact → directly controls the **transition from mild to severe wear**.

### Pillar III — The Archard Framework (Engineering Utility)

Archard's 1953 model is the workhorse of engineering wear prediction. For sliding wear over a distance $L$ under a normal load $W$:

$$
V = K \cdot \frac{W \cdot L}{H}
$$

where $K$ is the **dimensionless wear coefficient** (also called the *wear constant*). Rearranging gives the most common engineering form:

$$
\boxed{\;k_s = \frac{V}{W \cdot L} = \frac{K}{H} \quad [\text{m}^2/\text{N}] \;}
$$

The quantity $k_s$ is called the **specific wear rate** and has units of $\text{m}^2/\text{N}$, but the *dimensionless* core of the analysis is the **$K$ factor**, which by definition has no units.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize the symbol, equation, and range.

| # | Parameter | Equation | Typical Range / Value | Use in KTU Problems |
|---|-----------|----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Archard's Equation | $V = K \cdot \dfrac{W \cdot L}{H}$ | — | Volume loss prediction |
| 2 | Dimensionless Wear Coefficient $K$ | $K = \dfrac{V \cdot H}{W \cdot L}$ | $10^{-4}$ (severe) to $10^{-8}$ (mild) | Material ranking |
| 3 | Specific Wear Rate $k_s$ | $k_s = \dfrac{V}{W \cdot L}$ | $10^{-14}$ to $10^{-10} \text{ m}^2/\text{N}$ | Pin-on-disc reporting |
| 4 | Elastic Strain to Failure | $H/E$ | $0.01$ (ceramics) to $0.1$ (rubber) | Wear regime indicator |
| 5 | Plasticity Index | $H^3 / E^{*2}$ | $0.001$ (rubber) to $1$ (tool steel) GPa | Mild-to-severe transition |
| 6 | Reduced Modulus | $E^* = \left[\dfrac{1-\nu_1^2}{E_1} + \dfrac{1-\nu_2^2}{E_2}\right]^{-1}$ | — | Hertzian contact input |
| 7 | Normalized Hertzian Contact Radius | $a/R$ | $\ll 1$ for ball-on-flat | Geometry check |
| 8 | Wear Number $W_n$ | $W_n = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{H \cdot A}$ | — | Severity classification |
| 9 | Pommer Similarity Load | $P_s = \dfrac{W}{E^* \cdot R^2}$ | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ | Tribosystem scaling |
| 10 | Pommer Similarity Speed | $S_s = \dfrac{v \cdot \eta_0}{E^* \cdot R}$ | $10^{-11}$ to $10^{-6}$ | EHL regime scaling |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cutting tool design:** $H^3/E^{*2}$ is used to rank PVD coatings (TiN, CrN, DLC) for dry machining.
- **Journal bearing selection:** Pommer's $P_s$ and $S_s$ allow scaling of model-test rigs to full-size marine propeller shafts.
- **Bio-implant materials (UHMWPE on CoCr):** $K$ values dictate FDA approval for hip/knee prostheses (target $K < 10^{-7}$).
- **Automotive cam-follower contacts:** Archard's $K$ used by OEMs to predict 200,000 km service life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** When $H/E > 0.1$ and $H^3/E^{*2} > 0.5$ GPa, the contact is in the **elastic-shakedown regime** — the material wears slowly, predictably, and linearly with $L$. This is the design sweet spot for tribological engineers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Archard's Dimensionless Wear Coefficient from First Principles

### Step 1 — Define the Geometric Picture
Consider a hard conical asperity of included angle $2\theta$ sliding a distance $L$ over a softer surface under a normal load $W$ supported by *one* asperity. The asperity penetrates a depth $h$ into the softer substrate.

### Step 2 — Express the Asperity Load (Hardness Relation)
For plastic indentation, the load is governed by hardness:

$$
W = H \cdot A_p = H \cdot \pi \cdot a^2
$$

where $a$ is the projected contact radius.

### Step 3 — Express the Cross-Sectional Wear Volume per Pass
The volume swept per pass equals the cross-sectional area of the asperity groove (a triangle of base $2a$ and height $h$):

$$
\delta V = a \cdot h
$$

Using the geometric identity $h = a \tan\theta$:

$$
\delta V = a^2 \tan\theta
$$

### Step 4 — Define the Probability of Wear Event
Not every asperity contact produces a wear particle. Let the probability of an asperity producing a debris particle be $K_s$ (a dimensionless, statistical factor, $0 \le K_s \le 1$).

### Step 5 — Sum over the Sliding Distance
If the asperity slides a distance $L$ and the average contact diameter is $2a$, the number of asperity encounters is $L/(2a)$. Total wear volume:

$$
V = K_s \cdot \frac{L}{2a} \cdot a^2 \tan\theta = \frac{K_s \cdot L \cdot a \cdot \tan\theta}{2}
$$

### Step 6 — Eliminate the Asperity Radius Using the Hardness Relation
From Step 2: $a^2 = W/(\pi H)$, so $a = \sqrt{W/(\pi H)}$.

$$
V = \frac{K_s \cdot \tan\theta}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{W}{\pi H}} \cdot L
$$

### Step 7 — Group Constants into the Archard Constant
Define the dimensionless Archard coefficient $K$ as the *envelope* of all geometric/probabilistic constants:

$$
K = \frac{K_s \cdot \tan\theta}{2\sqrt{\pi}}
$$

This $K$ is **purely dimensionless** (tangent is dimensionless, square root of $\pi$ is dimensionless, $K_s$ is dimensionless). The final canonical form is:

$$
\boxed{\;V = K \cdot \frac{W \cdot L}{H}\;}
$$

### Step 8 — Verification of Dimensionlessness
$$
[V] = [K] \cdot \frac{[\text{N}] \cdot [\text{m}]}{[\text{Pa}]} = \frac{\text{N} \cdot \text{m}}{\text{N/m}^2} = \text{m}^3 \;\checkmark
$$

Since $V$ has dimensions of $\text{m}^3$ and the right side is $[K] \cdot \text{m}^3$, $K$ must be **dimensionless** for dimensional consistency. Q.E.D.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The $H/E$ Elastic Strain to Failure Ratio

### Step 1 — Start with Hertzian Maximum Shear Stress
For a sphere-on-flat contact, the maximum shear stress occurs at depth $\approx 0.48a$ below the surface:

$$
\tau_{\max} = 0.31 \, p_0
$$

where $p_0$ is the maximum Hertzian pressure, $p_0 = \dfrac{3W}{2\pi a^2}$.

### Step 2 — Express $p_0$ in Terms of Load, Modulus, and Radius
Hertz theory gives:

$$
p_0 = \left( \frac{6 W E^{*2}}{\pi^3 R^2} \right)^{1/3}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Tresca Yield Criterion
Yielding begins when $\tau_{\max} = Y/2$, where $Y \approx H/3$ (Tabor's relation: hardness is roughly 3× the uniaxial yield stress). The critical load for yield is therefore:

$$
W_y \propto \frac{R^2 H^3}{E^{*2}}
$$

### Step 4 — Form the Plasticity Index
Dividing $W_y$ by the elastic load $W_e \propto E^* R^2$:

$$
\frac{W_y}{W_e} \propto \frac{H^3}{E^{*2}}
$$

This is the **dimensionless plasticity index**. A high value means the material can carry a *very large* load plastically before subsurface yield — explaining why tool steels and cemented carbides resist abrasive wear.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Pommer Similarity Parameters

Pommer (1979) showed that for a tribosystem, the *dimensionless* load and speed parameters that govern the EHL (elastohydrodynamic) regime are:

### Step 1 — Load Similarity
Normalize load by elastic stiffness and contact size:

$$
P_s = \frac{W}{E^* \cdot R^2}
$$

Dimensionless check: $[\text{N}] / ([\text{Pa}] \cdot [\text{m}^2]) = [\text{N}] / [\text{N}] = 1$. ✓

### Step 2 — Speed Similarity
Normalize entrainment velocity by lubricant viscosity and elastic stiffness:

$$
S_s = \frac{v \cdot \eta_0}{E^* \cdot R}
$$

Dimensionless check: $([\text{m/s}] \cdot [\text{Pa·s}]) / ([\text{Pa}] \cdot [\text{m}]) = ([\text{N·s/m}^2])/([\text{N/m}]) = 1$. ✓

### Step 3 — Use in Wear Scaling
Two tribosystems with equal $P_s$ and equal $S_s$ will exhibit the **same dimensionless wear rate $K$** — even if one is a micro-MEMS slider and the other is a full-size ship stern-tube bearing. This is the engineering power of the dimensionless approach.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flow: Choosing the Right Dimensionless Parameter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wear Problem Identified] --> B{What do you need to compare?}
    B --> C[Materials under same conditions]
    B --> D[Full-scale vs. lab-scale systems]
    B --> E[Predicting volume loss]
    C --> F[Use H/E or H cubed over E star squared]
    D --> G[Use Pommer P-s and S-s]
    E --> H[Use Archard K and k-s]
    F --> I[Material Ranking Output]
    G --> J[Scaling Factor Output]
    H --> K[Volume Loss in m cubed]
    I --> L[Decision: Best Material]
    J --> M[Decision: Safe Upscaling]
    K --> N[Decision: Service Life]
```

## 4.2 Archard Wear — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT1[Input: Normal Load W in N] --> CORE
    INPUT2[Input: Sliding Distance L in m] --> CORE
    INPUT3[Input: Hardness H in Pa] --> CORE
    INPUT4[Input: Dimensionless K] --> CORE
    CORE[Archard Equation V = K W L over H] --> OUTPUT1[Output: Worn Volume V in m cubed]
    OUTPUT1 --> CLASSIFY{Classify Severity}
    CLASSIFY --> SEVERE[K greater than 1e-4: Severe]
    CLASSIFY --> MILD[K between 1e-6 and 1e-4: Mild]
    CLASSIFY --> VERYMILD[K less than 1e-6: Very Mild]
```

## 4.3 The H/E Wear Map (Halm-Naylor Inspired)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MAT[Material] --> CHECK{H over E Ratio}
    CHECK --> LOW[Low H over E less than 0.05]
    CHECK --> MID[Medium H over E 0.05 to 0.1]
    CHECK --> HIGH[High H over E greater than 0.1]
    LOW --> REGIME1[Predominantly Plastic Contact]
    MID --> REGIME2[Elastic-Plastic Transition]
    HIGH --> REGIME3[Elastic Shakedown - Low Wear]
    REGIME1 --> USE1[Use Archard K directly]
    REGIME3 --> USE2[Use Plasticity Index H cubed over E star squared]
```

## 4.4 Wear Number Classification Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WN[Wear Number W-n] --> R1[R-n less than 1e-7: Negligible]
    WN --> R2[R-n 1e-7 to 1e-5: Acceptable]
    WN --> R3[R-n 1e-5 to 1e-3: High]
    WN --> R4[R-n greater than 1e-3: Catastrophic]
    R1 --> ACTION1[Continue Service]
    R2 --> ACTION2[Monitor Periodically]
    R3 --> ACTION3[Schedule Replacement]
    R4 --> ACTION4[Immediate Shutdown]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the dimensionless wear coefficient $K$ as proposed by Archard. State the typical range of $K$ for mild and severe wear conditions.**

**Model Answer:**
The Archard dimensionless wear coefficient $K$ is defined as:

$$
K = \frac{V \cdot H}{W \cdot L}
$$

It represents the **probability** that an asperity encounter produces a wear debris particle and is the product of geometric and statistical factors of the asperity. It is **unitless**.

- **Mild wear:** $K \approx 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-8}$
- **Severe wear:** $K \approx 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse $K$ (dimensionless) with the specific wear rate $k_s$ (units of $\text{m}^2/\text{N}$). They are related by $k_s = K/H$ but are *not* the same. KTU examiners specifically deduct 1 mark for this mix-up.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the significance of the $H/E$ ratio in wear analysis? Why is it preferred over hardness alone?**

**Model Answer:**
The ratio $H/E$ is the **elastic strain to failure** and represents the maximum elastic strain a material can sustain before plastic deformation begins. It is preferred over hardness $H$ alone because:

1. It captures **both** plastic ($H$) and elastic ($E$) response of the contact pair.
2. It is **dimensionless**, hence material- and scale-independent.
3. A high $H/E$ (> 0.1) indicates that the material operates in the **elastic-shakedown regime**, where wear is low and predictable.
4. It directly correlates with abrasive wear resistance and coating performance.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Archard wear equation starting from asperity-level contact mechanics, and show that the wear coefficient $K$ is dimensionless.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A steel pin ($\text{HV} = 2.5 \text{ GPa}$, $\nu = 0.3$) slides against a hardened steel disc ($\text{HV} = 8.0 \text{ GPa}$, $\nu = 0.3$, $E = 210 \text{ GPa}$) under a normal load of $50 \text{ N}$ for a sliding distance of $5 \text{ km}$. The experimentally measured worn volume is $0.42 \text{ mm}^3$. Calculate:
- (i) the dimensionless wear coefficient $K$,
- (ii) the specific wear rate $k_s$,
- (iii) the $H/E$ ratio (using the disc's hardness and reduced modulus).

#### Model Solution for (a)

This is a direct application of the derivation in **Section 3.1**. Students should write:

1. Asperity cross-section: $\delta V = a \cdot h = a^2 \tan\theta$
2. Number of asperity contacts: $N = L/(2a)$
3. Probability factor $K_s$
4. Hardness load: $W = H \pi a^2$
5. Substituting $a$ and combining constants:
$$
V = \frac{K_s \tan\theta}{2\sqrt{\pi}} \cdot \frac{WL}{H}
$$
6. Defining $K$ to absorb all constants:
$$
V = K \cdot \frac{W L}{H}
$$
7. Dimensional check showing $K$ has no units.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**(i) Dimensionless wear coefficient $K$:**

Use the **softer** material's hardness in the Archard denominator (the softer pin controls the wear).

Convert Vickers to approximate hardness: $H \approx \text{HV}/3 \cdot 9.81 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$ is a common conversion, but a simpler approach: take $H = 2.5 \text{ GPa} = 2.5 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$ for the softer pin.

Given:
- $V = 0.42 \text{ mm}^3 = 0.42 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^3$
- $W = 50 \text{ N}$
- $L = 5000 \text{ m}$
- $H = 2.5 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$

$$
K = \frac{V \cdot H}{W \cdot L} = \frac{(0.42 \times 10^{-9}) \times (2.5 \times 10^9)}{50 \times 5000}
$$

$$
K = \frac{1.05}{2.5 \times 10^5} = 4.2 \times 10^{-6}
$$

This is in the **mild wear** range.

**(ii) Specific wear rate $k_s$:**

$$
k_s = \frac{V}{W \cdot L} = \frac{0.42 \times 10^{-9}}{50 \times 5000} = 1.68 \times 10^{-15} \text{ m}^2/\text{N}
$$

Verification: $k_s = K/H = 4.2 \times 10^{-6} / (2.5 \times 10^9) = 1.68 \times 10^{-15} \text{ m}^2/\text{N}$ ✓

**(iii) H/E ratio using reduced modulus:**

$$
E^* = \left[ \frac{1-0.3^2}{210} + \frac{1-0.3^2}{210} \right]^{-1} \text{ GPa} = \left[ \frac{0.91 \times 2}{210} \right]^{-1} = \frac{210}{1.82} \approx 115.4 \text{ GPa}
$$

For the disc, $H = 8.0 \text{ GPa}$:

$$
\frac{H}{E^*} = \frac{8.0}{115.4} = 0.0693
$$

This places the steel disc in the **elastic-plastic transition** regime — moderate wear, consistent with the calculated $K = 4.2 \times 10^{-6}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A common error is using the *harder* disc's hardness in the Archard equation. **Always use the hardness of the wearing (softer) member.** Using 8 GPa would give an absurdly small $K = 1.31 \times 10^{-6}$ — wrong by a factor of 3.2.

---

### Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of Pommer's similarity parameters $P_s$ and $S_s$. Derive their dimensionless form and discuss their use in scaling laboratory wear tests to full-scale tribosystems.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A laboratory pin-on-disc test uses a 6 mm diameter steel ball ($\text{E} = 210 \text{ GPa}$, $\nu = 0.3$) on a steel flat. The full-scale application is a rolling-element bearing with a 25 mm diameter ball under a load of 2500 N and a speed of 5 m/s. The lubricant viscosity at operating temperature is $\eta_0 = 0.05 \text{ Pa·s}$.

- (i) Calculate $P_s$ and $S_s$ for the full-scale bearing.
- (ii) If the lab test uses a load of 20 N and speed of 0.1 m/s with the same lubricant, compute the lab $P_s$ and $S_s$. Are the two tribosystems similar?

#### Model Solution for (a)

Refer to **Section 3.3**. The two parameters are:

$$
P_s = \frac{W}{E^* R^2} \qquad S_s = \frac{v \eta_0}{E^* R}
$$

Both are **dimensionless**. Two systems with equal $P_s$ and equal $S_s$ exhibit the same EHL film thickness ratio and the same dimensionless wear rate. This enables direct scaling of model tests to prototypes.

#### Model Solution for (b)

First, compute $E^*$ for steel-steel:

$$
E^* = \frac{210}{2(1-0.3^2)} = \frac{210}{1.82} = 115.4 \text{ GPa}
$$

**(i) Full-scale bearing:**

$R = 0.0125 \text{ m}$, $W = 2500 \text{ N}$, $v = 5 \text{ m/s}$, $\eta_0 = 0.05 \text{ Pa·s}$.

$$
P_s^{\text{full}} = \frac{2500}{(115.4 \times 10^9)(0.0125)^2} = \frac{2500}{1.803 \times 10^7} = 1.386 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
S_s^{\text{full}} = \frac{5 \times 0.05}{(115.4 \times 10^9)(0.0125)} = \frac{0.25}{1.4425 \times 10^9} = 1.733 \times 10^{-10}
$$

**(ii) Lab-scale test:**

$R_{\text{lab}} = 0.003 \text{ m}$, $W = 20 \text{ N}$, $v = 0.1 \text{ m/s}$, same $\eta_0$.

$$
P_s^{\text{lab}} = \frac{20}{(115.4 \times 10^9)(0.003)^2} = \frac{20}{1.039 \times 10^6} = 1.925 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
S_s^{\text{lab}} = \frac{0.1 \times 0.05}{(115.4 \times 10^9)(0.003)} = \frac{0.005}{3.462 \times 10^8} = 1.444 \times 10^{-11}
$$

**Comparison:**

- $P_s^{\text{full}} / P_s^{\text{lab}} \approx 7.2$
- $S_s^{\text{full}} / S_s^{\text{lab}} \approx 12.0$

The two systems are **not fully similar** — both $P_s$ and $S_s$ differ by nearly an order of magnitude. To achieve true similarity, the lab load should be increased to ~144 N and lab speed to ~1.2 m/s, or the ball diameter adjusted accordingly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** When computing $P_s$, students often forget to convert $E^*$ to **Pascals** (not GPa) before substituting. A 9-order-of-magnitude error here is the single most common mark-loser in this question type. Always write the units explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- **Archard's Equation:** $V = K \cdot W \cdot L / H$ — *memorize the form, the variable meanings, and the dimensional check.*
- **Dimensionless $K$:** Ranges from $10^{-8}$ (mild) to $10^{-3}$ (severe). $K$ is **probability × geometry**, not a material property alone.
- **Specific wear rate $k_s = K/H$** has units $\text{m}^2/\text{N}$ — *do not confuse with $K$.*
- **$H/E$** = elastic strain to failure. High $H/E$ → low wear. Use disc/coating's modulus, not the substrate's.
- **$H^3/E^{*2}$** = plasticity index. Use **reduced modulus** $E^*$, not individual $E$.
- **Reduced modulus:** $E^* = \left[ (1-\nu_1^2)/E_1 + (1-\nu_2^2)/E_2 \right]^{-1}$ — *for identical materials, $E^* = E / [2(1-\nu^2)]$.*
- **Pommer's parameters:** $P_s = W/(E^* R^2)$, $S_s = v \eta_0 / (E^* R)$ — *both dimensionless, both used for EHL scaling.*
- **Soften member rule:** In Archard's equation, use the hardness of the *softer* (wearing) member, not the harder counterface.
- **Dimensional check before substituting:** Always convert GPa → Pa, mm → m, mm³ → m³.
- **Wear classification by $K$:** $K < 10^{-6}$ mild, $10^{-6}$–$10^{-4}$ moderate, $> 10^{-4}$ severe.
- **Unitless check:** If your final $K$ has a unit, you have made an error — by definition it must be dimensionless.
- **Diagram literacy:** Be able to sketch the Halm-Naylor-type $H/E$ wear map and place ceramics, tool steels, and elastomers in their correct regions.
- **Memorize the 10 KTU-high-yield formulas** in the Section 2.2 cheat sheet — at least 2 will appear in any Part B question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Deterioration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Surface Deterioration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surface Deterioration** is the progressive loss of useful surface properties of an engineering material (metals, alloys, polymers, ceramics) caused by physical, chemical, electrochemical, or mechanical interaction with its operating environment.

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, **Surface Deterioration** is defined as the *irreversible change in surface microstructure, composition, geometry, or mechanical integrity* of an engineering component arising from one or more of the following driving forces:

- **Mechanical action** (sliding, impact, abrasion)
- **Chemical reaction** (oxidation, sulfidation, nitridation)
- **Electrochemical interaction** (galvanic, pitting, crevice attack)
- **Thermal fluctuation** (thermal fatigue, hot corrosion)
- **Biological activity** (microbiologically influenced corrosion / MIC)

The rate of deterioration is conventionally expressed as **mm/year** for corrosion, **mm³/N·m** for wear, and **mg/cm²·hr** for oxidation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters for KTU:** Almost every Module-1 question in KTU papers (Dec 2022, June 2023, Dec 2024) starts with a definition-based 3-mark question on surface deterioration. Memorize the broad classification — it earns easy marks.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of **surface deterioration exactly like human skin aging**:

| Human Skin | Engineering Material Surface |
|---|---|
| UV rays from the sun | Oxidation / UV degradation of polymers |
| Wind and dust friction | Abrasive wear |
| Sweat (salt water) | Electrochemical corrosion |
| Repeated bending of joints | Surface fatigue / spalling |
| Cuts and bruises | Erosion / impact damage |

Just as you apply sunscreen, moisturizer, or clothing to protect skin, engineers apply **coatings, inhibitors, and surface treatments** to slow down surface deterioration.

### 1.3 The Four Pillars of Surface Failure

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups all surface deterioration phenomena into four fundamental pillars:

1. **Wear** — material loss due to mechanical contact
2. **Corrosion** — material loss due to electrochemical/chemical reaction
3. **Oxidation / High-Temperature Degradation** — material loss due to reaction with gases
4. **Surface Fatigue** — crack initiation due to cyclic loading at the surface

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant to Remember:**
> Faraday's Constant **F = 96,485 C/mol** (≈ 96,500 C/mol) governs all electrochemical corrosion rate calculations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — How Surface Deterioration Operates

Surface deterioration is not a single phenomenon but a **synergistic cascade**. The operational sequence can be broken down into five structured logic steps:

- **Step 1 — Surface Activation**
  * Every clean surface is energetically unstable. The free surface energy drives atoms/molecules to react with the environment (e.g., freshly polished iron instantly forms an invisible oxide layer in air).

- **Step 2 — Energy Input**
  * External energy (mechanical, thermal, chemical, electrical) accelerates atomic mobility, breaks protective films, and creates fresh reactive sites.

- **Step 3 — Mass Transport**
  * Atoms from the bulk diffuse outward; aggressive species (O²⁻, Cl⁻, H⁺, S²⁻) diffuse inward. This counter-diffusion forms reaction products (oxides, sulfides, chlorides).

- **Step 4 — Product Formation**
  * Reaction products (rust Fe₂O₃·H₂O, Al₂O₃, Cr₂O₃) may be:
    * **Protective** — dense, adherent, low Pilling–Bedworth ratio (e.g., Al₂O₃)
    * **Non-protective** — porous, flaky, high PBR (e.g., Fe₂O₃ — rust)

- **Step 5 — Progressive Degradation**
  * Geometry changes (mass loss, pitting, roughening) → stress concentration → crack nucleation → eventual failure.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **high-yield formulas** that the KTU examiner expects a student to reproduce. All formulas are mathematically verifiable.

| # | Phenomenon | Formula | Variables / Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Corrosion Rate (Penetration) | $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | $K = 87.6$ (mm/yr), $W$ = mass loss (mg), $D$ = density (g/cm³), $A$ = area (cm²), $T$ = time (hr) | mm/year |
| 2 | Faraday's Law of Corrosion | $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M$ = molar mass (g/mol), $n$ = electrons transferred, $F$ = Faraday constant | grams |
| 3 | Current Density | $i = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | $I$ = current, $A$ = exposed area | A/m² or μA/cm² |
| 4 | Wear Rate (Archard) | $Q = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{H}$ | $K$ = wear coefficient, $W$ = normal load, $H$ = hardness | mm³/N·m |
| 5 | Pilling–Bedworth Ratio | $PBR = \dfrac{M_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{metal}}{n \cdot M_{metal} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}$ | $M$ = molar mass, $\rho$ = density, $n$ = number of metal atoms in oxide | dimensionless |
| 6 | Oxidation Rate (Parabolic) | $x^{2} = K_{p} \cdot t$ | $x$ = oxide thickness, $K_{p}$ = parabolic rate constant, $t$ = time | m²/s |
| 7 | Arrhenius Temperature Dependence | $K = A \cdot e^{-Q/RT}$ | $A$ = pre-exponential, $Q$ = activation energy, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $T$ = absolute temperature | varies |
| 8 | Nernst Equation | $E = E^{0} + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{[Ox]}{[Red]}$ | $E^{0}$ = standard electrode potential | Volts |
| 9 | Creep Rate (Norton-Bailey) | $\dot{\varepsilon} = A \sigma^{n} e^{-Q/RT}$ | $\sigma$ = applied stress, $n$ = stress exponent | 1/s |
| 10 | Volume Loss in Cavitation | $V = \dfrac{K \cdot \Delta P \cdot t}{\rho}$ | $\Delta P$ = pressure differential, $\rho$ = fluid density | m³ |

> [!TIP]
> **Pitfall to Avoid:** In the Archard equation, students often confuse $W$ (normal load in Newtons) with $W$ (mass loss in mg). Use **L** for load and **m** for mass loss to stay clean.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Turbine blades suffer oxidation + hot corrosion + creep → superalloys + TBC coatings.
- **Marine:** Ship hulls face uniform + pitting + crevice corrosion → cathodic protection.
- **Automotive:** Cylinder liners undergo adhesive wear + corrosive wear (from fuel combustion).
- **Biomedical:** Hip implants suffer tribo-corrosion in body fluid.
- **Nuclear:** Zirconium alloy cladding undergoes aqueous corrosion + hydriding.

> [!NOTE]
> The **economic impact** of surface deterioration globally is estimated at **3–4% of GDP** (≈ US $2.5 trillion/year as per NACE/AMPP IMPACT report). Examiners love this single statistic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Corrosion Rate from Mass Loss (Faraday + Density Method)

This is the **single most-asked derivation in KTU exams** (appeared in Dec 2019, June 2022, Dec 2023). Walk through it slowly.

**Given:**
- Mass loss $W$ in grams
- Exposed area $A$ in cm²
- Exposure time $T$ in hours
- Metal density $D$ in g/cm³

**Find:** Corrosion rate in mm/year.

---

**Step 1 — Relate mass loss to volume loss**

Because density is mass per unit volume, the volume of metal dissolved is mass divided by density.

$$
V_{metal} = \dfrac{W}{D}
$$

where $V_{metal}$ has units of cm³ (since $W$ is in g and $D$ in g/cm³).

---

**Step 2 — Relate volume to linear penetration depth**

The volume equals area times the uniform penetration depth $p$.

$$
V_{metal} = A \cdot p
$$

Equating both expressions:

$$
A \cdot p = \dfrac{W}{D}
$$

Solving for $p$ (in cm):

$$
p = \dfrac{W}{D \cdot A}
$$

---

**Step 3 — Convert units from cm/hr to mm/year**

We need $p$ in mm/year. The conversion chain is:

- cm → mm: multiply by 10
- hours → years (assuming 365 days): multiply by $365 \times 24 = 8760$

$$
p_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{W \cdot 10}{D \cdot A \cdot T} \times 8760
$$

$$
p_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{W \times 87600}{D \cdot A \cdot T}
$$

---

**Step 4 — Final canonical KTU form**

Rewriting with $K = 87.6 \times 10^{3}/10^{3}$ convention (when $W$ is in **mg**, $D$ in g/cm³, $A$ in cm², $T$ in hours):

$$
CR = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of 87.6:** $87.6 = 10 \times 24 \times 365 / 1000$. The 10 converts cm→mm; the 8760 converts hours→year; the 1000 converts mg→g. Memorize this chain — the KTU examiner awards **2 of the 14 marks** purely for this unit-conversion logic.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)

The PBR determines whether an oxide film is **protective** or **non-protective**.

**Step 1 — Write the oxide formation reaction**

For a divalent metal $M$:

$$
2 M + O_{2} \rightarrow 2 M O
$$

Here $n = 2$ (two metal atoms per oxide formula unit).

---

**Step 2 — Volume of oxide formed per mole of metal**

$$
V_{oxide} = \dfrac{M_{oxide}}{\rho_{oxide}}
$$

---

**Step 3 — Volume of metal consumed per mole of metal**

For 2 moles of metal $M$ consumed, the metal volume used is:

$$
V_{metal} = \dfrac{2 \cdot M_{metal}}{\rho_{metal}}
$$

---

**Step 4 — Define PBR as the ratio**

$$
PBR = \dfrac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}} = \dfrac{M_{oxide} / \rho_{oxide}}{2 \cdot M_{metal} / \rho_{metal}}
$$

$$
PBR = \dfrac{M_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{metal}}{2 \cdot M_{metal} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}
$$

For a general oxide $M_{a} O_{b}$ where $n$ metal atoms are consumed per oxide molecule, the general KTU formula is:

$$
PBR = \dfrac{M_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{metal}}{n \cdot M_{metal} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}
$$

---

**Step 5 — Interpretation rules**

| PBR Range | Film Behavior | Example |
|---|---|---|
| PBR < 1 | Tensile stress, porous, **non-protective** | MgO on Mg |
| 1 ≤ PBR ≤ 2 | Compressive stress, dense, **protective** | Al₂O₃ on Al (PBR ≈ 1.28), Cr₂O₃ on Cr |
| PBR > 2 | Compressive stress too high, **spalls off** | Fe₂O₃ on Fe (PBR ≈ 2.15) |

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Parabolic Oxidation Kinetics

For high-temperature oxidation where diffusion through the oxide layer is rate-limiting:

**Step 1 — Fick's First Law at the oxide scale**

The flux of metal cations outward is inversely proportional to scale thickness $x$:

$$
J = \dfrac{D \cdot \Delta C}{x}
$$

where $D$ is diffusivity, $\Delta C$ is the concentration gradient.

---

**Step 2 — Rate of scale growth**

The rate of thickening equals flux:

$$
\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{K_{1}}{x}
$$

---

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate**

$$
x \, dx = K_{1} \, dt
$$

$$
\int_{0}^{x} x \, dx = \int_{0}^{t} K_{1} \, dt
$$

$$
\dfrac{x^{2}}{2} = K_{1} t
$$

---

**Step 4 — Final parabolic law**

$$
x^{2} = 2 K_{1} t = K_{p} t
$$

$$
x = \sqrt{K_{p} \, t}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam tip:** KTU examiners frequently ask, "Why is the oxidation of iron linear at 250 °C but parabolic at 500 °C?" The answer: **at low T the surface reaction is rate-limiting (linear); at high T the diffusion is rate-limiting (parabolic)**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Connecting the Concepts

| Phenomenon | Driving Force | Rate-Limiting Step | Typical KTU Question |
|---|---|---|---|
| Uniform corrosion | Electrochemical | Charge transfer at metal/electrolyte interface | Faraday's law |
| Pitting corrosion | Localized Cl⁻ attack | Passive film breakdown | Critical pitting temperature |
| Oxidation | O₂ diffusion through oxide | Cation diffusion (high T) | Parabolic law |
| Abrasive wear | Mechanical | Asperity plowing | Archard equation |
| Cavitation | Bubble collapse | Micro-jet impact | Volume-loss formula |
| Creep | Thermal + stress | Vacancy diffusion | Norton-Bailey equation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flow — Surface Deterioration

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the entire surface-deterioration taxonomy, the inter-relationships among the four pillars, and the engineering counter-measures. This is a high-yield visualization that consolidates Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[ROOT SURFACE DETERIORATION]
    A0 --> B1[PILLAR 1 WEAR]
    A0 --> B2[PILLAR 2 CORROSION]
    A0 --> B3[PILLAR 3 OXIDATION]
    A0 --> B4[PILLAR 4 SURFACE FATIGUE]

    B1 --> C1A[Abrasive Wear]
    B1 --> C1B[Adhesive Wear]
    B1 --> C1C[Fatigue Wear]
    B1 --> C1D[Erosive Wear]
    B1 --> C1E[Cavitation Wear]

    B2 --> C2A[Uniform Attack]
    B2 --> C2B[Pitting]
    B2 --> C2C[Crevice]
    B2 --> C2D[Galvanic]
    B2 --> C2E[Intergranular]

    B3 --> C3A[Low T Linear]
    B3 --> C3B[High T Parabolic]
    B3 --> C3C[Sulfidation]
    B3 --> C3D[Hot Corrosion]

    B4 --> C4A[Rolling Contact Fatigue]
    B4 --> C4B[Thermal Fatigue]
    B4 --> C4C[Corrosion Fatigue]

    C1A --> D1[Counter Measure Lubrication Hardening Coatings]
    C1B --> D1
    C1C --> D1
    C2A --> D2[Counter Measure Coatings Inhibitors Cathodic Protection]
    C2B --> D2
    C2C --> D2
    C3A --> D3[Counter Measure Alloying TBC Diffusion Barriers]
    C3B --> D3
    C3C --> D3
    C4A --> D4[Counter Measure Shot Peening Surface Polishing Case Hardening]
    C4B --> D4
    C4C --> D4

    D1 --> E1[Goal Extended Service Life Reliability Safety Economy]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1

    style A0 fill:#1f2937,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    style B1 fill:#0e7490,stroke:#06b6d4,color:#ffffff
    style B2 fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#f97316,color:#ffffff
    style B3 fill:#581c87,stroke:#a855f7,color:#ffffff
    style B4 fill:#14532d,stroke:#22c55e,color:#ffffff
    style E1 fill:#374151,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Rate-Controlling Steps

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENV[ENVIRONMENTAL INPUT]:::env --> ADS[ADSORPTION STEP]:::step
    ADS --> RXN[SURFACE REACTION STEP]:::step
    RXN --> NUC[NUCLEATION OF PRODUCT]:::step
    NUC --> GRW[GROWTH AND DIFFUSION]:::step
    GRW --> FAIL[SURFACE FAILURE]:::fail

    T1[TEMPERATURE]:::ctrl --> RXN
    T2[STRESS]:::ctrl --> GRW
    T3[ELECTROLYTE]:::ctrl --> ADS

    classDef env fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000000
    classDef step fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000000
    classDef ctrl fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000000
    classDef fail fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** Environmental input (oxygen, water, ions, mechanical load) drives the deterioration cascade. **Temperature, stress, and electrolyte composition** are the three control knobs engineers manipulate via material selection and surface engineering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define surface deterioration. List any four forms of surface deterioration.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Surface deterioration** is the irreversible degradation of the surface properties of an engineering material due to its interaction with the operating environment.

The four forms are:

1. **Wear** — material loss due to mechanical contact (abrasive, adhesive, fatigue, erosive).
2. **Corrosion** — material loss due to electrochemical/chemical reaction with the environment.
3. **Oxidation** — material loss due to reaction with gases (O₂, SO₂, CO₂) at elevated temperatures.
4. **Surface fatigue** — crack initiation and spalling caused by cyclic loading.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for correctly listing 4 forms with one-line examples.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**State Faraday's law of electrolysis. Write its mathematical form and mention the value of Faraday's constant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Faraday's First Law: *The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte.*

**Mathematical form:**

$$
W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}
$$

where $W$ = mass deposited/dissolved (g), $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M$ = molar mass (g/mol), $n$ = valency, and $F$ = Faraday constant.

**Faraday's constant** $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol} \approx 96{,}500 \text{ C/mol}$.

*(Award: 1 mark for statement + 1 mark for equation + 1 mark for the numerical value of F.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper Module 1]`**

**(a)** Classify the various forms of surface deterioration in engineering materials. Discuss in detail the mechanisms of **abrasive wear** and **adhesive wear**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A mild-steel component of area $250 \text{ cm}^2$ lost $1.05 \text{ g}$ when exposed to a corrosive medium for $120 \text{ hours}$. Density of mild steel is $7.86 \text{ g/cm}^3$. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mm/year** and **mils per year (mpy)**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Classification of Surface Deterioration (3 marks):**

| S.No | Form | Example Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wear | Abrasive, adhesive, fatigue, erosive, cavitation |
| 2 | Corrosion | Uniform, pitting, crevice, galvanic, intergranular, SCC |
| 3 | High-temperature oxidation | Linear, parabolic, logarithmic |
| 4 | Surface fatigue | Rolling contact fatigue, thermal fatigue, corrosion fatigue |

**Abrasive Wear (2 marks):**

- Occurs when a hard rough surface (or hard particles) slides against a softer surface.
- Mechanism: **micro-plowing**, **micro-cutting**, and **micro-cracking** of the softer asperities.
- Two-body abrasion (e.g., grinding) and three-body abrasion (e.g., dirt between gear teeth).
- Controlled by **Archard equation**: $Q = K \cdot W / H$.

**Adhesive Wear (2 marks):**

- Occurs when two surfaces slide under load; micro-junctions form and then fracture.
- Material transfers from the softer to the harder surface.
- Mechanism steps: contact → plastic deformation → cold welding → fracture → debris formation.
- Severe in metals with high mutual solubility (e.g., Cu–Ni, Al–Cu).

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks: Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- $W = 1.05 \text{ g} = 1050 \text{ mg}$
- $A = 250 \text{ cm}^2$
- $T = 120 \text{ hours}$
- $D = 7.86 \text{ g/cm}^3$

**Step 1 — Apply the corrosion-rate formula:**

$$
CR = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
CR = \dfrac{87.6 \times 1050}{7.86 \times 250 \times 120}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator:**

$$
87.6 \times 1050 = 91{,}980
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$
7.86 \times 250 = 1965; \quad 1965 \times 120 = 235{,}800
$$

**Step 5 — Final division:**

$$
CR = \dfrac{91{,}980}{235{,}800} = 0.390 \text{ mm/year}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to mpy (1 mm = 39.37 mils):**

$$
CR_{mpy} = 0.390 \times 39.37 = 15.35 \text{ mpy}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{CR = 0.39 \text{ mm/year} = 15.35 \text{ mpy}}
$$

*(Mark split: 1 mark formula, 2 marks substitution, 2 marks calculation, 2 marks final answer + unit conversion.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary Paper]`**

**(a)** What is the **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)**? Derive an expression for PBR. Using PBR, explain with examples why some oxide films are protective while others are non-protective. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A steel component oxidizes in air at $700\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ and follows **parabolic kinetics**. The parabolic rate constant is $K_p = 4.5 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. Calculate the oxide thickness after **100 hours** of exposure. If the temperature is increased to $850\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $K_p$ becomes $3.2 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, calculate the new thickness for the same time. Comment on the result. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):**

> The **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** is the ratio of the volume of the oxide formed to the volume of the metal consumed in forming that oxide.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

For oxide $M_{a}O_{b}$ formed as $a M + \tfrac{b}{2} O_{2} \rightarrow M_{a}O_{b}$:

- Molar volume of oxide: $V_{ox} = M_{ox} / \rho_{ox}$
- Volume of metal consumed for $a$ moles: $V_{M} = a \cdot M_{M} / \rho_{M}$

$$
PBR = \dfrac{V_{ox}}{V_{M}} = \dfrac{M_{ox} \cdot \rho_{M}}{a \cdot M_{M} \cdot \rho_{ox}}
$$

Here $n = a$ is the number of metal atoms per oxide molecule.

**Interpretation (3 marks):**

| PBR | Film Behavior | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| < 1 | Porous, non-protective (tensile cracks) | Mg–MgO (PBR = 0.81), Na–Na₂O |
| 1–2 | Dense, adherent, **protective** | Al–Al₂O₃ (1.28), Cr–Cr₂O₃ (1.99), Si–SiO₂ (2.04 — borderline) |
| > 2 | High compressive stress, **spalls off** | Fe–Fe₂O₃ (≈ 2.15), Bi–Bi₂O₃ |

Protective oxides form a continuous barrier that slows further oxidation. Non-protective oxides (PBR < 1 or > 2) cannot protect the underlying metal because they either crack under tensile stress or flake off under compressive stress.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks: Numerical Solution**

**Given (T = 700 °C):**
- $K_{p1} = 4.5 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- $t = 100 \text{ hr} = 100 \times 3600 = 3.6 \times 10^{5} \text{ s}$

**Step 1 — Apply the parabolic law:**

$$
x^{2} = K_{p} \cdot t
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values for 700 °C:**

$$
x_{1}^{2} = (4.5 \times 10^{-12}) \times (3.6 \times 10^{5}) = 16.2 \times 10^{-7} = 1.62 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Take square root:**

$$
x_{1} = \sqrt{1.62 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.273 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.273 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Repeat for 850 °C:**

$$
x_{2}^{2} = (3.2 \times 10^{-11}) \times (3.6 \times 10^{5}) = 11.52 \times 10^{-6} = 1.152 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
x_{2} = \sqrt{1.152 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.394 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 3.394 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment (1 mark):**

The oxide thickness increases by a factor of $\tfrac{3.394}{1.273} \approx 2.67$ when temperature rises from 700 °C to 850 °C. This confirms that high-temperature oxidation is **strongly temperature-dependent** (Arrhenius behavior), and minor temperature excursions can dramatically shorten component life.

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{x_{1} = 1.273 \text{ mm at 700 °C}; \quad x_{2} = 3.394 \text{ mm at 850 °C}}
$$

*(Mark split: 1 mark formula, 2 marks each side substitution, 1 mark square root, 1 mark comment.)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas in Module 1 Surface Deterioration Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert hours → years** in the corrosion-rate formula (loses 2 marks).
> 2. **Mixing up Faraday's law variables** — using molar mass of ion instead of metal, or using $n$ = total electrons vs. valence number.
> 3. **Writing PBR without units** — PBR is dimensionless; stating "units of cm³/cm³" is wrong.
> 4. **Confusing abrasive and adhesive wear** — abrasive = hard particle cuts the softer surface; adhesive = two surfaces weld and tear.
> 5. **Using linear oxidation law where parabolic is required** — at high temperature, always use $x^{2} = K_{p} t$.
> 6. **Not commenting on engineering significance** — examiners allot 1 mark for a one-line "comment" after a numerical answer.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Surface Deterioration (Module 1):**
>
> - **Definition:** Surface deterioration = irreversible degradation of surface properties due to environmental interaction.
> - **Four pillars:** Wear, Corrosion, Oxidation, Surface Fatigue.
> - **Corrosion rate formula:** $CR = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ (mm/year) — remember the **87.6** constant!
> - **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$.
> - **Archard wear equation:** $Q = K \cdot L / H$ — wear coefficient × load ÷ hardness.
> - **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR):** $PBR = \dfrac{M_{ox} \cdot \rho_{M}}{n \cdot M_{M} \cdot \rho_{ox}}$; protective when **1 ≤ PBR ≤ 2**.
> - **Parabolic oxidation law:** $x^{2} = K_{p} t$; linear at low T, parabolic at high T.
> - **Arrhenius dependence:** All rate constants increase exponentially with temperature.
> - **Nernst equation** governs electrochemical equilibrium potentials.
> - **Protective oxides:** Al₂O₃, Cr₂O₃, SiO₂. **Non-protective:** Fe₂O₃ (rust), MgO.
> - **Economic impact:** Surface deterioration costs ~3–4% of global GDP (≈ US $2.5 trillion/year).
> - **Counter-measures:** Lubrication, coatings, alloying, cathodic protection, surface treatments, inhibitors.
> - **Synergistic effect:** In real systems, multiple mechanisms act together (e.g., **tribo-corrosion** = wear + corrosion).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fracture Surface Degradation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fracture Surface Degradation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1 — FRACTURE SURFACE DEGRADATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fracture Surface Degradation (FSD)** is the progressive deterioration of the microstructural and topographical features of a newly created fracture surface due to post-fracture or in-service interaction with the surrounding environment (moisture, oxygen, corrosive media, hydrogen, temperature, radiation). It encompasses the combined action of **mechanical fracture events** and **subsequent environmental attack** on the freshly exposed internal surfaces, leading to loss of useful fractographic evidence, loss of load-bearing capacity, and accelerated component failure.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, this topic is positioned at the intersection of three engineering domains:
1. **Fracture Mechanics** — studying the initiation and propagation of cracks.
2. **Surface Engineering** — understanding how the *new* surface behaves once exposed.
3. **Environmental Degradation** — quantifying chemical, electrochemical, and thermal attack.

### 1.2 The Three-Stage Chronology of a Fracture Surface

| Stage | Event | What Happens to the Surface |
|-------|-------|------------------------------|
| **Stage I — Creation** | Crack nucleation & propagation | A *virgin* (atomically clean, highly reactive) surface forms |
| **Stage II — Exposure** | Immediate contact with environment | Adsorption of H₂O, O₂, ions; oxide nucleation; possible **post-fracture oxidation** |
| **Stage III — Degradation** | Long-term service under load + environment | Stress corrosion, hydrogen entry, corrosion fatigue, fretting degradation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A common KTU question is *"Why is a fresh fracture surface chemically more active than a polished surface?"* The answer lies in the **dangling bonds** and **high surface energy** (typically **1–3 J/m²** for clean metals) of a freshly created surface, which drives rapid reaction with environmental species.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Freshly Cut Apple"

Imagine cutting an apple in half. Within seconds, the exposed white flesh turns brown. This is **enzymatic oxidation** — the *fresh surface* reacts with air. If you smear lemon juice (a corrosion inhibitor analogue) immediately, the browning is delayed. The fracture surface of a metal behaves identically:
- **Apples =** Engineering alloy
- **Cut surface =** Fracture surface
- **Air =** Service environment
- **Lemon juice =** Inhibitor / Protective coating
- **Browning =** Oxide / Corrosion product on the fracture face

The rate of this "browning" depends on **temperature, humidity, stress state, and medium aggressiveness** — all variables the KTU syllabus expects you to recognize.

### 1.4 Why Fracture Surface Degradation Matters in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Students Must Memorize:**
> - **Surface energy of clean metal:** γ ≈ 1–3 J/m²
> - **Strain energy release rate (G):** J/m²
> - **Fracture toughness (K_IC):** MPa·√m
> - **Threshold stress intensity for SCC (K_ISCC):** MPa·√m
> - **Hydrogen diffusion coefficient in steel at 25 °C:** D_H ≈ 10⁻⁸ m²/s

Failure of a single fracture surface can cost industries millions — the **Aloha Airlines Flight 243 incident (1988)** and the **Liberty Ship WWII fractures** are classic historical case studies linked to environmental degradation of fracture surfaces.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Modes of Fracture — Establishing the Baseline

Before degradation is studied, the *original* fracture mode must be identified. Fracture in engineering materials is classified into three principal modes:

### A. Ductile Fracture
- Characterized by extensive **plastic deformation** prior to separation.
- Microscopic signature: **microvoid coalescence (MVC)** — nucleation, growth, and coalescence of voids around inclusions or second-phase particles.
