- Macroscopic signature: **cup-and-cone** morphology in tensile specimens.
- Surface energy consumed: dominated by **plastic work (γ_p)**, not true surface energy.

### B. Brittle Fracture
- Occurs with negligible plastic deformation.
- Microscopic signature: **cleavage** (transgranular) or **intergranular** separation.
- Macroscopic signature: flat, shiny fracture surface perpendicular to tensile stress.
- Critical condition: **K_I ≥ K_IC** (linear elastic fracture mechanics).

### C. Fatigue Fracture
- Sub-critical cyclic loading below K_IC.
- Three regions: **(i) crack initiation, (ii) steady-state propagation (striations), (iii) fast fracture**.
- Striation spacing ≈ crack advance per cycle — a direct fingerprint of the cyclic stress intensity range ΔK.

### D. Creep Fracture
- Time-dependent deformation at elevated temperatures (typically T > 0.4 T_m, where T_m is the melting point in Kelvin).
- Signature: **wedge cracks** at grain boundaries (triple points) and **cavitation** leading to rupture.

### E. Environment-Assisted Fracture (EAF)
The family most relevant to **Fracture Surface Degradation**:
- **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)**
- **Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE)**
- **Corrosion Fatigue (CF)**
- **Liquid Metal Embrittlement (LME)**

## 2.2 The Six Mechanisms of Fracture Surface Degradation

The KTU Module 1 syllabus identifies six mechanisms, each with a distinct thermodynamic and kinetic driver:

| # | Mechanism | Driver | Typical Fingerprint on Surface |
|---|-----------|--------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | **Post-Fracture Oxidation** | High ΔG (negative) of oxide formation | Tinted oxides — blue (300 °C), straw (200 °C) for steels |
| 2 | **Stress Corrosion Cracking** | Synergy of tensile stress + corrosive species | Branching cracks, intergranular or transgranular |
| 3 | **Hydrogen Embrittlement** | H⁰ diffusion into lattice | "Fish-eye" features, quasi-cleavage, IG separation |
| 4 | **Corrosion Fatigue** | Cyclic stress + corrosive environment | Striations + corrosion pits superimposed |
| 5 | **Fretting Degradation** | Small-amplitude oscillatory motion | Oxide-debris beds, red/brown Fe₂O₃ powder |
| 6 | **Microbial-Induced Degradation (MIC)** | Bacterial colonies (e.g., *Desulfovibrio*) | Pitting clusters, tubercles, sulfides |

## 2.3 The Griffith–Irwin Foundation (KTU High-Yield)

### Griffith's Energy Criterion (1920)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A crack will propagate when the **decrease in elastic strain energy** equals or exceeds the **increase in surface energy** of the newly created surfaces.

$$G = -\frac{dU}{dA} = 2\gamma_s$$

where:
- $G$ = strain energy release rate (J/m²)
- $U$ = elastic strain energy stored (J)
- $A$ = crack area (m²)
- $\gamma_s$ = true surface energy of the material (J/m²)

### Irwin's Modification for Ductile Materials

For ductile materials, plastic work $γ_p$ dominates over true surface energy:

$$G_c = 2(\gamma_s + \gamma_p) \approx 2\gamma_p$$

### Relation to Stress Intensity Factor (Linear Elastic)

$$G = \frac{K_I^2}{E'} \quad \text{where} \quad E' = \begin{cases} E & \text{(plane stress)} \\ \dfrac{E}{1-\nu^2} & \text{(plane strain)} \end{cases}$$

Combining:

$$K_{IC} = \sqrt{\frac{2E(\gamma_s + \gamma_p)}{1-\nu^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is one of the most frequently tested KTU derivations. Master the transition from energy-based (G) to stress-intensity-based (K) fracture criterion.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table

| Formula | Expression | Application | Units |
|---------|-----------|-------------|-------|
| Griffith Energy Release | $G = 2\gamma_s$ | Crack propagation threshold | J/m² |
| Irwin's Plastic Correction | $G_c = 2(\gamma_s + \gamma_p)$ | Ductile materials | J/m² |
| Stress Intensity (Mode I) | $K_I = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ | Cracked body under tension | MPa·√m |
| G–K Equivalence (plane strain) | $G = \dfrac{K_I^2(1-\nu^2)}{E}$ | Link energy & stress field | J/m² |
| Paris Law (Fatigue) | $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^m$ | Sub-critical fatigue crack growth | m/cycle |
| SCC Threshold | $K_I < K_{ISCC}$ | No SCC sub-critical growth | MPa·√m |
| Fick's First Law (Hydrogen) | $J_H = -D_H \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | H⁰ flux into metal | mol/(m²·s) |
| Arrhenius Diffusion | $D = D_0 \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | Temperature dependence of D | m²/s |
| Pilling–Bedworth Ratio | $PBR = \dfrac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}}$ | Predicts protective vs non-protective oxide | dimensionless |
| Threshold Stress (SCC) | $\sigma_{th} < \dfrac{K_{ISCC}}{Y\sqrt{\pi a}}$ | Safe operating stress | MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes (`|`) in tables** — use \vert or \mid to avoid markdown breakage. Strict KTU convention enforced by examiner scripts.

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

Understanding Fracture Surface Degradation is critical in:
- **Aerospace** — turbine disk post-fracture oxide analysis for forensic investigation.
- **Nuclear** — primary water stress corrosion cracking (PWSCC) of Alloy 600 steam generator tubes.
- **Oil & Gas** — sulfide stress cracking (SSC) of downhole tubulars in H₂S environments (NACE MR0175 standard).
- **Marine** — corrosion fatigue of ship hulls subjected to wave-induced cyclic loading.
- **Bridges & Civil** — Fretting fatigue of riveted/bolted connections.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Griffith's Energy Balance to K_IC

This is the **single most important derivation** in Module 1, tested regularly in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE papers.

### Step 1 — The Cracked Plate Setup
Consider an infinite plate of thickness $B$ (m), containing a central through-thickness crack of length $2a$ (m), subjected to uniform remote tensile stress $\sigma$ (Pa).

### Step 2 — Elastic Strain Energy of a Cracked Plate
The elastic strain energy released per unit crack extension was computed by Inglis (1913) and Westergaard:

$$U_{elastic} = \frac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2 B}{E'}$$

where $E'$ is the effective modulus defined in §2.3.

**Logic:** As the crack grows by an infinitesimal amount $da$, the strain energy released equals the area of the "missing" triangular stress field region.

### Step 3 — Surface Energy Created
When the crack extends by $da$, **two new surfaces** are created (one on each face), each of area $B \cdot da$. The energy required to form them is:

$$U_{surface} = 2\gamma_s B \, da$$

### Step 4 — Total Energy Change
The total potential energy of the system is:

$$\Pi = U_{elastic} + U_{surface} - W_{external}$$

For fixed grips (constant load) and ignoring external work changes:

$$\frac{d\Pi}{da} = \frac{dU_{elastic}}{da} + \frac{dU_{surface}}{da}$$

$$\frac{dU_{elastic}}{da} = \frac{2\pi a \sigma^2 B}{E'}$$

$$\frac{dU_{surface}}{da} = 2\gamma_s B$$

### Step 5 — Griffith's Equilibrium Condition
At the critical crack length, the rate of energy release equals the rate of energy absorption:

$$\frac{d\Pi}{da}\bigg|_{a=a_c} = 0$$

$$\frac{2\pi a_c \sigma^2 B}{E'} = 2\gamma_s B$$

### Step 6 — Critical Stress for Brittle Fracture
Cancelling $2B$:

$$\frac{\pi a_c \sigma_c^2}{E'} = \gamma_s$$

Solving for $\sigma_c$:

$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2E'\gamma_s}{\pi a_c}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Griffith Equation:** $\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E'\gamma_s}{\pi a}}$. This is the critical remote stress required to propagate a crack of half-length $a$ in a perfectly brittle (elastic) material.

### Step 7 — Introducing Irwin's Stress Intensity
Irwin (1957) defined the stress field ahead of a crack tip using the stress intensity factor:

$$K_I = \lim_{r \to 0} \sqrt{2\pi r} \, \sigma_{yy}(r, 0)$$

For the centre-cracked plate geometry, $K_I = \sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$.

The energy release rate is related to $K_I$ by:

$$G = \frac{K_I^2}{E'}$$

At fracture: $G = G_c$ and $K_I = K_{IC}$. Equating the two expressions for critical stress:

$$\sigma_c \sqrt{\pi a_c} = \sqrt{E' G_c} = K_{IC}$$

### Step 8 — Plasticity Correction
For ductile metals, $G_c = 2(\gamma_s + \gamma_p) \gg 2\gamma_s$, so the Griffith equation is modified to:

$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2E'(\gamma_s + \gamma_p)}{\pi a_c}}$$

In terms of fracture toughness:

$$K_{IC} = \sqrt{\frac{2E'(\gamma_s + \gamma_p)}{1-\nu^2}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A high-strength steel plate has $E = 210$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$, $\gamma_s = 1.5$ J/m². A surface crack of half-length $a = 2$ mm is detected. Estimate the critical remote tensile stress for brittle fracture in plane strain.

### Solution

**Given:**
- $E = 210 \times 10^9$ Pa
- $\nu = 0.3$
- $\gamma_s = 1.5$ J/m²
- $a = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m

**Step 1:** Compute effective modulus (plane strain):
$$E' = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2} = \frac{210 \times 10^9}{1 - (0.3)^2} = \frac{210 \times 10^9}{0.91} \approx 230.77 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Griffith's equation:
$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 E' \gamma_s}{\pi a}}$$

$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 230.77 \times 10^9 \times 1.5}{\pi \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

**Step 3:** Numerator evaluation:
$$2 \times 230.77 \times 10^9 \times 1.5 = 6.923 \times 10^{11}$$

**Step 4:** Denominator evaluation:
$$\pi \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 6.2832 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 5:** Ratio:
$$\frac{6.923 \times 10^{11}}{6.2832 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.1018 \times 10^{14}$$

**Step 6:** Square root:
$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{1.1018 \times 10^{14}} \approx 1.0497 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_c \approx 10.5 \text{ MPa}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always show units throughout. The examiner allocates 1 mark for dimensional consistency. $E'$ in Pa, $\gamma_s$ in J/m² = N·m/m² = Pa·m, $a$ in m → $\sigma_c$ emerges in Pa. ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Identifying the Degradation Mechanism

The KTU examiner often gives a fractograph and asks: *"Identify the mechanism."* Use this decision tree:

| Observation on Surface | Most Likely Mechanism | Confirmatory Test |
|------------------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Tinted colours (blue, straw) | Post-fracture oxidation | Color chart comparison |
| Branching secondary cracks | SCC | Match environment with susceptibility chart |
| Fish-eye + white spots | Hydrogen Embrittlement | Hydrogen analysis, charge measurement |
| Striations + pits | Corrosion Fatigue | Compare striation spacing with calculated da/dN |
| Brown powder + polished spots | Fretting | EDX of debris |
| Discrete colonies, tubercles | MIC | Culturing of bacteria |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision logic** — first look at crack path (intergranular vs transgranular), then identify secondary features, and finally correlate with operating environment. This sequence is worth **3 marks** in a 14-mark KTU question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Fracture Surface Degradation Pathway (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Service Stress Applied] --> B{Material Has Pre-existing Flaw?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Crack Initiates at Flaw]
    B -- No --> D[Crack Initiates at Slip Band / Inclusion]
    C --> E[Stage I: Sub-Critical Crack Growth]
    D --> E
    E --> F{K_IC Reached?}
    F -- No --> G[Stage II: Steady-State Propagation]
    G --> H[Environment Interacts with Fresh Surface]
    H --> H1[Post-Fracture Oxidation]
    H --> H2[Stress Corrosion Cracking]
    H --> H3[Hydrogen Entry]
    H --> H4[Corrosion Fatigue Striations]
    H1 --> I[Stage III: Final Fast Fracture]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    F -- Yes --> I
    I --> J[Component Failure]
    J --> K[Forensic Fractography]
    K --> K1[Macroscopic Examination]
    K --> K2[SEM Imaging]
    K --> K3[EDX Chemical Analysis]
    K1 --> L[Root Cause Identified]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
```

## 4.2 Microstructural Degradation Flow (SEM-Level Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VirginFracture["VIRGIN FRACTURE FEATURES"]
        V1[Dimpled MVC Rupture]
        V2[Cleavage Facets]
        V3[Striations]
        V4[Intergranular Facets]
    end
    subgraph DegradationProducts["POST-DEGRADATION OVERLAYS"]
        D1[Oxide Scale]
        D2[Corrosion Pits]
        D3[H Blisters / Fish-Eyes]
        D4[Debris Beds]
    end
    subgraph Identification["FRACTOGRAPHY OUTPUT"]
        I1[EDX Spectra]
        I2[SEM Topography]
        I3[Cross-Section Metallography]
    end
    V1 --> D1
    V2 --> D2
    V3 --> D4
    V4 --> D3
    D1 --> I1
    D2 --> I2
    D3 --> I3
    D4 --> I1
```

## 4.3 Mechanism Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Examine Fracture Surface] --> Q1{Cyclic Loading Evidence?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Striations Present?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Static Tensile + Environment?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> CF[Corrosion Fatigue]
    Q2 -- No --> SF[Simple Fatigue]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Branching Cracks?}
    Q3 -- No --> Q5{Temperature High and Time-Dependent?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> SCC[Stress Corrosion Cracking]
    Q4 -- No --> HE[Hydrogen Embrittlement]
    Q5 -- Yes --> CR[Creep Rupture]
    Q5 -- No --> DF[Ductile or Brittle Fracture]
    SCC --> Out[Apply K ISCC Threshold]
    HE --> Out
    CF --> Out
    CR --> Out
    DF --> Out
    SF --> Out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Note:** Whenever a question shows a fractograph with a decision-tree-like description, the examiner expects you to walk through the **identification pathway** step by step. The block diagrams above are designed to mirror the expected answer structure.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q:** Define "fracture surface degradation" and list any four mechanisms by which a freshly formed fracture surface can degrade in service.

**Model Answer:**
Fracture surface degradation is the progressive deterioration of the microstructural and topographical features of a freshly created fracture surface due to its interaction with the operating environment (chemical, thermal, or mechanical) after the fracture event.

**Four mechanisms:**
1. **Post-fracture oxidation** — oxide scale formation on exposed metal (e.g., blue/straw tints on steel).
2. **Stress corrosion cracking (SCC)** — branching cracks from synergistic tensile stress + corrosive medium.
3. **Hydrogen embrittlement** — atomic H diffusion into lattice causing quasi-cleavage and "fish-eye" features.
4. **Corrosion fatigue** — superimposition of fatigue striations with corrosion pitting.

*(Any four of the six mechanisms from §2.2 accepted.)* — **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q:** Why is a freshly created fracture surface more chemically reactive than a polished or machined surface?

**Model Answer:**
A freshly created fracture surface has:
- A high density of **dangling atomic bonds** (broken metallic bonds).
- High **surface energy** (γ_s ≈ 1–3 J/m²) driving rapid reaction to lower it.
- Absence of any pre-existing protective **oxide or passivation layer**.
- High density of **lattice defects** (dislocations, microvoids) enhancing adsorption.

A polished surface, in contrast, has had time to form a thin stable oxide (~2–5 nm) and has reduced surface energy due to adsorbed species. Therefore, the fresh fracture surface reacts rapidly with O₂, H₂O, and ions in the environment, leading to degradation. — **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B Module 1 carries a compulsory internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**Q: (a)** Starting from Griffith's energy criterion, derive the relationship between critical stress, crack length, and surface energy for a brittle material. Clearly state all assumptions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A through-thickness central crack of half-length $a = 5$ mm exists in a steel plate (E = 200 GPa, ν = 0.3). The material has a true surface energy γ_s = 2 J/m². If the applied remote tensile stress is 100 MPa, determine whether fast fracture will occur. Use plane strain conditions. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Assumptions:** (2 marks)
1. Material is linear elastic and isotropic.
2. Crack is sharp, central, and through-thickness in an infinite plate.
3. Loading is mode I (tensile) and quasi-static.
4. Surface energy is the only energy sink (no plastic work, no environment).
5. Plane strain conditions prevail.

**Derivation Steps:** (5 marks)
- Write elastic strain energy of cracked plate: $U = \dfrac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2 B}{E'}$
- Write surface energy: $U_s = 2\gamma_s B \cdot a$
- Set total potential energy and differentiate: $d\Pi/da = 0$
- Solve for critical stress: $\boxed{\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E'\gamma_s}{\pi a_c}}}$

*(Full derivation as in §3.1, Steps 1–6.)*

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $a = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $E = 200 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\nu = 0.3$, $\gamma_s = 2$ J/m², $\sigma = 100 \times 10^6$ Pa.

**Step 1 — Effective modulus (plane strain):**
$$E' = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2} = \frac{200 \times 10^9}{1 - 0.09} = 219.78 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 2 — Critical stress from Griffith:**
$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 219.78 \times 10^9 \times 2}{\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}}$$
$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{8.7912 \times 10^{11}}{1.5708 \times 10^{-2}}}$$
$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{5.597 \times 10^{13}} \approx 7.481 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \approx 7.48 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison:**
Applied stress $\sigma_{app} = 100$ MPa; $\sigma_c = 7.48$ MPa.

Since $\sigma_{app} \gg \sigma_c$, **fast fracture will occur immediately**.

**Step 4 — Critical crack length (for context):**
$$a_c = \frac{2E'\gamma_s}{\pi \sigma^2} = \frac{2 \times 219.78 \times 10^9 \times 2}{\pi \times (100 \times 10^6)^2}$$
$$a_c = \frac{8.7912 \times 10^{11}}{3.1416 \times 10^{16}} \approx 2.8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 28 \, \mu\text{m}$$

**Conclusion:** The actual crack (5 mm) is **178× larger** than the critical crack. Immediate catastrophic fracture. — **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the mechanism of stress corrosion cracking (SCC) with neat sketches of crack morphology. List four commonly observed SCC systems (metal-environment pairs). (7 marks)

**(b)** A 316L stainless steel pipe (K_IC = 50 MPa·√m) is exposed to a chloride-containing aqueous environment at 80 °C. The measured threshold stress intensity for SCC is K_ISCC = 12 MPa·√m. The pipe has a longitudinal surface crack of depth a = 2 mm with geometry factor Y = 1.12. If the hoop stress in the pipe is 200 MPa, determine if SCC will initiate. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Mechanism of SCC (4 marks):**
SCC occurs due to the **synergistic action of sustained tensile stress and a specific corrosive environment** on a susceptible material. The three classical models are:
1. **Slip-Dissolution (Film Rupture):** Protective passive film is ruptured by localized slip; rapid anodic dissolution at the slip step exposes fresh metal; repassivation competes with dissolution.
2. **Hydrogen-Induced SCC (HE-SCC):** Cathodic reaction generates H⁰ which diffuses into the crack-tip process zone, reducing cohesion.
3. **Film-Induced Cleavage:** Thin brittle dealloyed film at crack tip cleaves under stress, propagating the crack.

**Crack morphology (1 mark):** Branching, often intergranular (sensitized alloys) or transgranular (chlorides in austenitic SS); multiple parallel secondary cracks; little to no bulk plastic deformation.

**Four SCC Systems (2 marks):**
1. Austenitic SS — chlorides (e.g., 304 SS in MgCl₂)
2. Carbon steel — caustic (NaOH)
3. Copper alloys — ammonia (Season Cracking)
4. Titanium alloys — methanol / red fuming nitric acid
5. Low-alloy steels — H₂S (Sulfide Stress Cracking)
*(Any four accepted.)*

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $K_{IC} = 50$ MPa·√m, $K_{ISCC} = 12$ MPa·√m, $a = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $Y = 1.12$, $\sigma = 200$ MPa.

**Step 1 — Compute applied stress intensity factor:**
$$K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$
$$K_I = 1.12 \times 200 \times \sqrt{\pi \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}$$
$$K_I = 224 \times \sqrt{6.2832 \times 10^{-3}}$$
$$K_I = 224 \times 0.07927$$
$$K_I \approx 17.76 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{m}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with thresholds:**
- $K_I = 17.76$ MPa·√m
- $K_{ISCC} = 12$ MPa·√m
- $K_{IC} = 50$ MPa·√m

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
Since $K_{ISCC} < K_I < K_{IC}$:
$$12 < 17.76 < 50$$

The applied K lies **above the SCC threshold but below fast-fracture toughness**. Therefore, **SCC will initiate and propagate sub-critically**. Without intervention, the crack will grow (per Paris-like law) until $K_I$ approaches $K_{IC}$, leading to catastrophic fast fracture.

**Step 4 — Recommendation:**
- Reduce operating stress below $\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{K_{ISCC}}{Y\sqrt{\pi a}} = \dfrac{12}{1.12 \times 0.07927} \approx 135$ MPa, **or**
- Use a more resistant alloy (e.g., 904L, duplex SS), **or**
- Apply cathodic protection / inhibitor injection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Loss of 2–3 Marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting the plane-strain condition** check — always state whether the component satisfies $a, B \geq 2.5(K_{IC}/\sigma_y)^2$. If not, the comparison is invalid.
> 2. **Mixing up K_IC and K_ISCC** — K_ISCC is *always* smaller. SCC initiates whenever $K_I > K_{ISCC}$.
> 3. **Skipping the comparison step** — students often compute K_I correctly but forget to write the inequality explicitly. This costs 1 full mark.
> 4. **Not mentioning unit conversion** — MPa·√m requires $a$ in meters. Writing $a = 2$ mm without converting to SI units is a common error.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Fracture Surface Degradation**

- [x] **Definition:** FSD = post-fracture environmental attack on newly created surfaces.
- [x] **Three stages:** Creation → Exposure → Degradation.
- [x] **Six degradation mechanisms:** Post-fracture oxidation, SCC, HE, corrosion fatigue, fretting, MIC.
- [x] **Griffith criterion:** $\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E'\gamma_s}{\pi a}}$ — memorize cold.
- [x] **Irwin relation:** $G = \dfrac{K_I^2}{E'}$ (plane strain) and $\dfrac{K_I^2}{E}$ (plane stress).
- [x] **Plasticity correction:** $G_c = 2(\gamma_s + \gamma_p)$.
- [x] **SCC threshold:** $K_{ISCC}$ — crack grows sub-critically when $K_I > K_{ISCC}$.
- [x] **Paris Law:** $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^m$ — for corrosion fatigue, exponent $m$ is typically 3–5.
- [x] **Fractography sequence:** Visual → SEM → EDX → Cross-section → Hardness → Chemistry.
- [x] **Fresh surface reactivity:** γ_s ≈ 1–3 J/m², dangling bonds, no passive film.
- [x] **Common SCC systems:** SS-Cl⁻, Cu-NH₃, Steel-NaOH, Ti-methanol, Steel-H₂S.
- [x] **Hydrogen diffusion:** $D_H \approx 10^{-8}$ m²/s in steel at 25 °C, Arrhenius with temperature.
- [x] **Always check plane-strain validity:** $a, B \geq 2.5(K_{IC}/\sigma_y)^2$.
- [x] **Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR):** < 1 = non-protective; 1–2 = protective; > 2 = spalling.
- [x] **Examiners reward:** diagrams, units throughout, explicit comparisons, and use of standard symbols ($K_{IC}$, $K_{ISCC}$, $\gamma_s$, $\gamma_p$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip:** KTU 2024 Scheme ESE papers emphasize *application* over rote memorization. Always frame answers in terms of (i) what mechanism is operating, (ii) what threshold applies, and (iii) what design action prevents it. This three-part structure is what distinguishes a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Heat and Radiation on Surface Degradation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effect of Heat and Radiation on Surface Degradation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surface Degradation** is the irreversible deterioration of a material's outermost atomic layers, altering its mechanical, chemical, optical, or electrical functionality due to environmental interaction.

When the degrading stimulus is **heat**, the phenomenon is broadly classified under **Thermo-Mechanical Surface Degradation**, encompassing:
- **High-Temperature Oxidation** (chemical interaction with O₂, CO₂, H₂O vapor)
- **Thermal Fatigue** (cyclic heating/cooling induced cracking)
- **Creep Damage** (time-dependent deformation at elevated T)
- **Hot Corrosion** (molten salt accelerated attack)
- **Sintering / Grain Coarsening** (microstructural degradation)

When the degrading stimulus is **ionizing radiation**, the phenomenon falls under **Radiation-Induced Surface Degradation**:
- **Sputtering** (ejection of surface atoms by energetic particle bombardment)
- **Radiation-Enhanced Diffusion / Segregation**
- **Surface Embrittlement & Blistering**
- **Displacement Damage** (creation of Frenkel pairs)
- **Transmutation & Swelling**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Imagine a **brick wall** (the surface of a metal) standing in a **forest fire** (heat) and then being **peppered with bullets** (radiation).
> - **Heat** is like a slow, smoldering fire around the wall — it cooks the bricks, makes them expand unevenly, the mortar (oxide layer) cracks, and over hours the wall sags and crumbles.
> - **Radiation** is like a hailstorm of bullets — each bullet (photon/ion/neutron) chips a brick, knocks atoms out of place, and leaves tiny craters and bubbles in the wall.
> Both attacks are "surface-level" events because energy is deposited first where the projectile or heat flux arrives — at the **outermost atomic layer**.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Physical Constants Used in This Module**
> - Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.380 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K} = 8.617 \times 10^{-5} \text{ eV/K}$
> - Gas constant: $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
> - Avogadro's number: $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$
> - Stefan–Boltzmann constant: $\sigma_{SB} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K}^4\text{)}$
> - Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$
> - Standard activation energy ranges for surface diffusion: $Q \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 3.0 \text{ eV/atom}$

### 1.4 KTU Syllabus Highlight

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 1 — KTU 2024 Scheme Learning Anchor:**
> The 2024 scheme explicitly requires students to *"identify, differentiate, and quantify the surface degradation modes arising from thermal and radiative environments, with emphasis on kinetic laws, microstructural response, and engineering mitigation strategies."*
> Exam questions typically appear as: definition (3 marks), comparison (7 marks), or derivation + application (14 marks).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Heat-Induced Surface Degradation — Layered Logic

#### Step 1 — Thermal Energy Influx
The first law governing energy reaching the surface is the **Stefan–Boltzmann radiative heat flux** (dominant in high-T environments, e.g., turbine blades):

$$
q_{rad} = \varepsilon \, \sigma_{SB} \, T^4
$$

- $q_{rad}$ → radiative heat flux (W/m²)
- $\varepsilon$ → emissivity of the surface (0 ≤ ε ≤ 1)
- $\sigma_{SB}$ → Stefan–Boltzmann constant
- $T$ → absolute surface temperature (K)

#### Step 2 — Chemical Attack (Oxidation)
At elevated T, metals react with O₂ to form an oxide scale. The **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** predicts whether the scale is **protective** or **non-protective**:

$$
PBR = \frac{\text{Volume of metal oxide produced}}{\text{Volume of metal consumed}} = \frac{M_{ox} \, \rho_m}{n \, M_m \, \rho_{ox}}
$$

- $M_{ox}, M_m$ → molar masses of oxide and metal
- $\rho_m, \rho_{ox}$ → densities
- $n$ → number of metal atoms per oxide formula unit

> **Rule:** PBR < 1 → non-protective (porous, e.g., Mg, Ca).  
> PBR ≈ 1 → protective (e.g., Al, Cr, Si).  
> PBR >> 2 → spalling (e.g., Fe → Fe₂O₃).

#### Step 3 — Kinetic Growth Laws
| Law | Equation | Controlling Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Linear | $x = k_L t$ | Scale is porous / non-protective |
| Parabolic | $x^2 = 2 k_p t$ | Diffusion through growing scale (Wagner) |
| Logarithmic | $x = k_{log} \ln(t) + C$ | Low T, thin films (Al, Zn) |
| Cubic | $x^3 = 3 k_c t$ | Intermediate T transition regime |

The **Arrhenius temperature dependence** ties rate constants to T:

$$
k = A \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)
$$

- $Q$ → apparent activation energy (J/mol or eV/atom)
- $A$ → pre-exponential factor
- $R$ → universal gas constant

#### Step 4 — Mechanical Degradation under Heat
- **Thermal Fatigue** uses the **Coffin–Manson relation**:

$$
\frac{\Delta \varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f \, (2N_f)^c
$$

- $\Delta \varepsilon_p$ → plastic strain range per cycle
- $N_f$ → cycles to failure
- $\varepsilon'_f$ → fatigue ductility coefficient; $c$ → exponent (–0.5 to –0.7)
- **Norton's Creep Law** (steady-state):

$$
\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A_{cr} \, \sigma^n \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_{cr}}{RT}\right)
$$

### 2.2 Radiation-Induced Surface Degradation — Layered Logic

#### Step 1 — Energy Deposition
Incoming ions/neutrons lose energy via **nuclear stopping** ($S_n$) and **electronic stopping** ($S_e$). The **displacement per atom (dpa)** is the canonical damage metric:

$$
dpa = \phi \, t \, \sigma_d
$$

- $\phi$ → incident particle flux (particles/cm²·s)
- $t$ → exposure time (s)
- $\sigma_d$ → displacement cross-section (cm²)

#### Step 2 — Sputtering Yield
The number of surface atoms ejected per incident ion is given by the **Sigmund–Thompson formula**:

$$
Y(E) = \frac{0.042 \, \alpha \, S_n(E)}{U_s}
$$

- $\alpha$ → dimensionless function of mass ratio $M_2/M_1$ (~0.2–0.4)
- $S_n(E)$ → nuclear stopping power at energy $E$
- $U_s$ → surface binding energy (≈ sublimation enthalpy, eV/atom)

#### Step 3 — Surface Diffusion Under Irradiation
The **radiation-enhanced diffusion coefficient**:

$$
D_{irr} = D_{th} + D_{rad} = D_0 \exp(-Q_{th}/k_B T) + \frac{\phi \, a^2 \, \sigma_d}{6}
$$

where $a$ is the atomic jump distance.

#### Step 4 — Blistering & Embrittlement
Hydrogen/He ions implanted below the surface coalesce into bubbles; the **critical pressure for blister rupture**:

$$
P_{crit} = \frac{2 \gamma_s}{R_b}
$$

- $\gamma_s$ → surface energy (J/m²)
- $R_b$ → bubble radius (m)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q = \varepsilon \sigma_{SB} T^4$ | Radiative heat flux to surface | W/m² |
| 2 | $PBR = \dfrac{M_{ox} \rho_m}{n M_m \rho_{ox}}$ | Volume ratio of oxide/metal | dimensionless |
| 3 | $x^2 = 2 k_p t$ | Parabolic oxide growth | m², s |
| 4 | $k = A \exp(-Q/RT)$ | Arrhenius rate law | various |
| 5 | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A_{cr} \sigma^n \exp(-Q_{cr}/RT)$ | Norton steady-state creep | s⁻¹ |
| 6 | $\Delta \varepsilon_p / 2 = \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^c$ | Coffin– Manson thermal fatigue | dimensionless |
| 7 | $dpa = \phi \, t \, \sigma_d$ | Displacement damage metric | dimensionless |
| 8 | $Y = 0.042 \alpha S_n / U_s$ | Sigmund sputtering yield | atoms/ion |
| 9 | $D_{irr} = D_0 e^{-Q_{th}/k_BT} + \frac{\phi a^2 \sigma_d}{6}$ | Radiation-enhanced diffusion | m²/s |
| 10 | $P_{crit} = 2 \gamma_s / R_b$ | Critical bubble rupture pressure | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Board Exam Tip:** For KTU valuation, always carry forward the **units in every intermediate step** and report the final answer with **SI units**. Examiners often deduct 1 mark for missing unit annotations in derivation questions.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Aerospace turbine blades** (Ni-superalloys) — protect against $T \geq 1100$ °C oxidation using **thermal barrier coatings (TBCs)** engineered with PBR ≈ 1 (e.g., 7YSZ).
> - **Nuclear reactor pressure vessels** — radiation-induced embrittlement is monitored using $dpa$ to predict lifetime.
> - **First-wall materials in fusion reactors (W, Be)** — sputtering from plasma D/T ions is a critical lifetime concern.
> - **Re-entry spacecraft heat shields** — C/C composites rely on controlled oxidation/sublimation for ablative cooling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Parabolic Oxidation Kinetics from First Principles (Wagner Theory)

**Goal:** Derive the parabolic law $x^2 = 2 k_p t$ assuming diffusion-controlled growth.

**Assumptions:**
1. The oxide scale is dense, adherent, and stoichiometric.
2. Growth occurs by **counter-diffusion** of cations outward and anions (oxygen) inward through the scale.
3. The rate-limiting step is solid-state diffusion (Fick's first law).
4. Local thermodynamic equilibrium is established at both scale/gas and scale/metal interfaces.

**Step 1 — Set up Fick's First Law.**
Let $x$ be the instantaneous scale thickness. The diffusing species (e.g., O²⁻) crosses the scale under a constant concentration gradient:

$$
J = -D \, \frac{dC}{dx} \approx -D \, \frac{\Delta C}{x}
$$

where $\Delta C = C_{outer} - C_{inner}$ is the concentration difference of the diffusing species across the scale.

**Step 2 — Convert flux to growth rate.**
The number of oxide formula units formed per unit area per unit time is $J / \nu$, where $\nu$ is the stoichiometric number of diffusing species per oxide unit. Each unit of oxide occupies a molar volume $\Omega$ (m³/mol). The growth rate of the scale is therefore:

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{J \, \Omega}{\nu} = \frac{D \, \Omega \, \Delta C}{\nu \, x}
$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables.**

$$
x \, dx = \frac{D \, \Omega \, \Delta C}{\nu} \, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $x=0$ at $t=0$ to $x$ at time $t$.**

$$
\int_0^x x' \, dx' = \frac{D \, \Omega \, \Delta C}{\nu} \int_0^t dt'
$$

$$
\frac{x^2}{2} = \frac{D \, \Omega \, \Delta C}{\nu} \, t
$$

**Step 5 — Define the parabolic rate constant $k_p$.**

$$
k_p = \frac{D \, \Omega \, \Delta C}{\nu}
$$

**Step 6 — Final form.**

$$
\boxed{\,x^2 = 2 k_p t\,}
$$

with the temperature dependence inherited from $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$, so that:

$$
k_p = k_{p,0} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_{ox}}{RT}\right)
$$

> **Engineering insight:** A **plot of $x^2$ vs $t$** should yield a straight line through the origin. The slope is $2k_p$. Plotting $\ln k_p$ vs $1/T$ gives a straight line whose slope is $-Q_{ox}/R$, from which the activation energy of the rate-limiting diffusion step is extracted.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Sputtering Yield Threshold Energy

**Goal:** Show why a minimum kinetic energy $E_{th}$ is needed to displace a surface atom.

**Step 1 — Conservation of momentum at the surface.**
A projectile of mass $M_1$ and energy $E$ collides with a surface atom of mass $M_2$. The maximum energy transferable in an elastic head-on collision is:

$$
E_{T,max} = \frac{4 M_1 M_2}{(M_1 + M_2)^2} \, E
$$

**Step 2 — Set $E_{T,max}$ equal to the surface binding energy $U_s$.**
For a surface atom to be ejected, it must acquire at least $U_s$ (typically 2–8 eV for metals). Therefore:

$$
E_{th} = \frac{U_s (M_1 + M_2)^2}{4 M_1 M_2}
$$

**Step 3 — Limiting case for heavy projectiles ($M_1 \gg M_2$):**

$$
E_{th} \approx \frac{U_s M_1}{4 M_2} \quad \text{(large)}
$$

**Step 4 — Limiting case for light projectiles ($M_1 \ll M_2$):**

$$
E_{th} \approx \frac{U_s M_1}{4 M_2} \to \text{very large as well — inefficient momentum transfer.}
$$

**Step 5 — Optimal case ($M_1 \approx M_2$):**

$$
E_{th} \approx \frac{U_s}{1} \quad \text{(minimum possible threshold)}
$$

> **Engineering insight:** This is why **inert gas ions close to the mass of the target** (e.g., Ar⁺ on Fe) are highly efficient sputtering agents. Helium sputtering on tungsten has $E_{th}$ ~100 eV, while Ar⁺ on Fe has $E_{th}$ ~20 eV.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Temperature Rise in Radiation-Heated Surface

**Goal:** Compute the steady-state surface temperature $T_s$ of a metal exposed to a particle flux $\phi$ with average deposited energy $E_{dep}$ per particle.

**Step 1 — Power density deposited at the surface:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = \phi \, E_{dep}
$$

(units: W/m²)

**Step 2 — Outgoing heat flux by conduction (semi-infinite solid, T(x) = $T_s$ at x = 0, $T_\infty$ at x → ∞):**

$$
\dot{Q}_{out} = \kappa \, \frac{T_s - T_\infty}{\delta}
$$

where $\kappa$ is thermal conductivity and $\delta$ is a characteristic conduction depth.

**Step 3 — Steady-state balance:** $\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{Q}_{out}$:

$$
\phi \, E_{dep} = \kappa \, \frac{T_s - T_\infty}{\delta}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $T_s$:**

$$
\boxed{\,T_s = T_\infty + \frac{\phi \, E_{dep} \, \delta}{\kappa}\,}
$$

> **Insight:** Low-thermal-conductivity materials (e.g., ZrO₂-coated steels) and high-flux environments (fusion plasmas) drive $T_s$ to thousands of K, accelerating oxidation and spalling.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Heat vs. Radiation Damage

| Parameter | Heat-Induced Degradation | Radiation-Induced Degradation |
|---|---|---|
| **Driving force** | Thermal energy ($k_B T$) | Kinetic energy of projectiles |
| **Time scale** | Continuous, often slow (hours–years) | Can be instantaneous (ns) per pulse |
| **Depth affected** | Bulk (heat conduction) | Shallow (10 nm – 10 μm) |
| **Primary mechanism** | Diffusion, chemical reaction, creep | Atomic displacement, ionization |
| **Kinetics** | $x^2 = 2 k_p t$ (often parabolic) | $dpa = \phi t \sigma_d$ (linear) |
| **Temperature dependence** | Strong (Arrhenius) | Weak (mostly athermal) |
| **Signature damage** | Oxide scale, blisters, creep voids | Frenkel pairs, dislocations, transmutation |
| **Typical mitigation** | TBC, alloying with Cr/Al, cooling | Hard coatings, plasma-facing design |
| **Key formula** | $k = A e^{-Q/RT}$ | $Y = 0.042 \alpha S_n / U_s$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Coupled Heat & Radiation Degradation Pathway

<!-- 
Mermaid Safeguard: All node IDs use alphanumeric prefix (st_, rad_, env_). 
No reserved words. No markdown inside node labels. No HTML in node text.

```mermaid

    st_ox --> st_pbr[Pilling Bedworth Ratio PBR Check]
    st_pbr --> st_prot{Scale Protective}
    st_prot -- Yes PBR 1 to 2 --> st_parab[Parabolic Growth x squared equal 2 kp t]
    st_prot -- No PBR less than 1 --> st_porous[Porous Non Protective Scale]
    st_prot -- No PBR greater than 2 --> st_spall[Spalling and Cracking]
    st_parab --> st_final[Service Lifetime Reduction]
    st_porous --> st_final
    st_spall --> st_final
    
    rad_input[Radiation Flux phi ions or neutrons] --> rad_stop[Energy Deposition Sn plus Se]
    rad_stop --> rad_dpa[Displacement Damage dpa]
    rad_stop --> rad_sput[Sputtering Yield Y]
    rad_dpa --> rad_diff[Radiation Enhanced Diffusion]
    rad_diff --> st_ox
    rad_sput --> st_final
    
    env_input[Environment Oxygen or Coolant] --> st_ox
    env_input --> rad_stop
    
    st_final[mit][Surface Degradation Final]
```
