<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the Pilling–Bedworth Ratio. What is its significance in predicting the protective nature of an oxide scale?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** is defined as the ratio of the volume of the metal oxide formed to the volume of the metal consumed during oxidation:

$$
PBR = \frac{V_{ox}}{V_m} = \frac{M_{ox} \, \rho_m}{n \, M_m \, \rho_{ox}}
$$

**Significance:**
- PBR < 1 → oxide is **porous and non-protective** (oxide does not cover the metal fully; e.g., MgO on Mg).
- 1 ≤ PBR ≤ 2 → oxide is **protective and adherent** (e.g., Al₂O₃ on Al, Cr₂O₃ on Cr).
- PBR > 2 → oxide is **non-protective and tends to spall** due to high compressive stresses (e.g., Fe₂O₃ on Fe, PBR ≈ 2.1).

It is, therefore, a **first-pass engineering criterion** to select alloy systems for high-temperature service.

*(Examiner's note: A precise formula + 3 case ranges = full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the 'displacement per atom' (dpa) parameter, and why is it preferred over fluence for reporting radiation damage?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:**

$$
dpa = \phi \, t \, \sigma_d
$$

where $\phi$ is the incident particle flux, $t$ the exposure time, and $\sigma_d$ the displacement cross-section.

**Why preferred over fluence ($\phi t$):**
1. **Material-independent unit:** dpa is a normalized, dimensionless metric, so radiation doses in different reactors (neutrons, ions, electrons) can be compared directly.
2. **Physics-based:** It directly counts how many times, on average, each atom in the target has been knocked from its lattice site, which is the fundamental quantity controlling mechanical property changes (hardening, embrittlement, swelling).
3. **Cross-section encoded:** Different projectiles and energies yield different $\sigma_d$ — dpa accounts for this automatically.

*(Examiner's note: Definition (1) + 2 reasons (1 each) = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Derive the parabolic rate law $x^2 = 2k_p t$ for high-temperature oxidation using Wagner's diffusion-controlled model. Clearly state the assumptions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A nickel-based superalloy is oxidized in air at 1000 °C. The parabolic rate constant is measured as $k_p = 1.5 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. Calculate (i) the oxide scale thickness after 100 hours of exposure, and (ii) the time required to form a 50 μm thick protective scale. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation** *(Full marks allocated: 7)*

1. **Assumption 1:** Dense, adherent, stoichiometric oxide scale. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Assumption 2:** Rate-limiting step is solid-state diffusion across the scale. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Assumption 3:** Local equilibrium at both gas/scale and scale/metal interfaces. *(0.5 mark)*

**Derivation Steps:**

- Apply **Fick's first law** across a scale of thickness $x$:

$$
J = -D \frac{\Delta C}{x}
$$

*(1 mark)*

- Relate flux to scale growth rate using molar volume $\Omega$ of the oxide:

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{J \, \Omega}{\nu} = \frac{D \, \Omega \, \Delta C}{\nu \, x}
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

- Separate variables and integrate from 0 to $x$ and 0 to $t$:

$$
\int_0^x x' \, dx' = \frac{D \, \Omega \, \Delta C}{\nu} \int_0^t dt'
$$

$$
\frac{x^2}{2} = \frac{D \, \Omega \, \Delta C}{\nu} \, t
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

- Define the parabolic rate constant $k_p$:

$$
k_p = \frac{D \, \Omega \, \Delta C}{\nu} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{x^2 = 2 k_p t}
$$

*(1 mark)*

- Mention that $D = D_0 e^{-Q/RT}$, so $k_p$ is itself Arrhenius. *(0.5 mark)*

---

**(b) Numerical Problem** *(7 marks)*

**Given:** $k_p = 1.5 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, $T = 1000 \text{ °C} = 1273 \text{ K}$, $t_1 = 100 \text{ h}$.

**Step 1 — Convert time to SI units:**

$$
t_1 = 100 \times 3600 = 3.6 \times 10^5 \text{ s}
$$

*(0.5 mark)*

**Step 2 — Compute $x$ after 100 h:**

$$
x_1 = \sqrt{2 \, k_p \, t_1} = \sqrt{2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.6 \times 10^5}
$$

$$
x_1 = \sqrt{1.08 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.039 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 1.04 \text{ mm}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Convert target thickness:**

$$
x_2 = 50 \, \mu m = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}
$$

*(0.5 mark)*

**Step 4 — Compute $t_2$ for $x = 50 \, \mu m$:**

$$
t_2 = \frac{x_2^2}{2 k_p} = \frac{(5.0 \times 10^{-5})^2}{2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-9}}{3.0 \times 10^{-12}} = 833.3 \text{ s}
$$

$$
t_2 = 833.3 \, \text{s} = 13.9 \text{ min}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 5 — Final boxed answer with units:**

$$
\boxed{x_1 \approx 1.04 \text{ mm} \quad ; \quad t_2 \approx 833 \text{ s} \approx 13.9 \text{ min}}
$$

*(1 mark for unit handling and final presentation)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in this Question (Valuation Pitfall):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert hours → seconds** — costs ~1 mark.
> 2. **Using $x = k_p t$** (linear) instead of $x^2 = 2 k_p t$ — entire solution wrong.
> 3. **Reporting $x_1 = 1.04 \text{ m}$** by forgetting the square root — rare but disastrous.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** in part (a) — KTU examiners specifically ask for them, worth 1.5 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is sputtering? Derive the Sigmund expression for sputtering yield and explain its dependence on projectile energy and incidence angle. (7 marks)

**(b)** A tungsten first-wall in a fusion reactor is bombarded by deuterium ions of energy 100 eV at normal incidence. Given: $M_1 = 2$ u, $M_2 = 184$ u, $U_s = 8.68$ eV, $S_n = 1.2 \times 10^{-14}$ eV·cm²/atom, and $\alpha = 0.18$, compute (i) the sputtering yield, and (ii) the number of tungsten atoms eroded per second from a 1 cm² area exposed to a flux of $10^{20}$ D⁺/m²/s. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory** *(7 marks)*

**Definition of Sputtering (1 mark):**
Sputtering is the physical process by which atoms of a solid surface are ejected into the gas phase as a consequence of bombardment by energetic ions, atoms, or neutrons.

**Sigmund Yield Derivation (5 marks):**

- Start with the **Boltzmann transport equation** for the energy deposited by the projectile into atomic motion in the surface region. *(1 mark)*

- The energy deposited at the surface per unit depth is approximated as a Gaussian distribution centered at the mean projected range $R_p$ with straggling $\Delta R_p$. The energy transferred to **recoil atoms** at the surface becomes the source term for the **linear collision cascade**. *(1.5 marks)*

- The number of atoms ejected per incident ion (sputtering yield) is proportional to the energy deposited at the surface divided by the surface binding energy. After solving the linearized transport equation in the low-fluence limit:

$$
Y(E) = \frac{0.042 \, \alpha \, S_n(E)}{U_s}
$$

where $\alpha$ depends weakly on $M_2/M_1$. *(1.5 marks)*

- **Energy dependence:** $S_n(E)$ rises, peaks, then falls (Lindhard–Scharff–Schiøtt (LSS) universal curve), so $Y(E)$ does likewise. *(0.5 mark)*

- **Angular dependence:** $Y(\theta) = Y(0) / \cos\theta$ approximately, up to $\theta \approx 70°$, beyond which it drops sharply due to ion reflection. *(0.5 mark)*

---

**(b) Numerical Problem** *(7 marks)*

**Given:** $E = 100$ eV, $M_1 = 2$ u, $M_2 = 184$ u, $U_s = 8.68$ eV, $S_n = 1.2 \times 10^{-14}$ eV·cm²/atom, $\alpha = 0.18$, $\phi = 10^{20}$ ions/(m²·s), $A = 1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$.

**Step 1 — Compute sputtering yield:**

$$
Y = \frac{0.042 \times 0.18 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-14}}{8.68}
$$

$$
Y = \frac{0.9072 \times 10^{-15}}{8.68} = 1.045 \times 10^{-16} \text{ atoms/ion}
$$

*(3 marks)*

**Step 2 — Compute erosion rate per unit area:**

$$
\dot{N} = \phi \, Y = 10^{20} \times 1.045 \times 10^{-16} = 1.045 \times 10^4 \text{ atoms/(m}^2\text{·s)}
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 3 — Total atoms eroded from $A = 10^{-4}$ m²:**

$$
\dot{N}_{total} = \dot{N} \times A = 1.045 \times 10^4 \times 10^{-4} = 1.045 \text{ atoms/s}
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 4 — Final boxed answer:**

$$
\boxed{Y \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-16} \text{ atoms/ion} \quad ; \quad \dot{N}_{total} \approx 1.05 \text{ W atoms/s}}
$$

*(1 mark for correct unit usage and presentation)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert — KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern:**
> 1. **Using $S_n$ in wrong units** (e.g., eV·cm² vs. eV·cm²/atom) — examiners often switch the convention; always check.
> 2. **Forgetting the area multiplication** in part (ii) — direct $\phi Y$ is the flux, not the total.
> 3. **Quoting yield > 1** for very low energy (100 eV is below peak for D on W) — physically impossible; if your $Y > 0.1$ for 100 eV D⁺ on W, recalculate.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1**
> 
> **Heat-Induced Degradation — Must-Knows**
> - PBR formula and its three regimes (porous, protective, spalling). — *[3-marker favorite]*
> - Parabolic growth law $x^2 = 2 k_p t$ with full Wagner derivation. — *[7-marker favorite]*
> - Arrhenius dependence: $k = A e^{-Q/RT}$. Plot $\ln k$ vs $1/T$ to extract $Q$.
> - Coffin–Manson for thermal fatigue; Norton for steady-state creep.
> - Emissivity $\varepsilon$ in Stefan–Boltzmann for radiation heating.
> 
> **Radiation-Induced Degradation — Must-Knows**
> - $dpa = \phi t \sigma_d$ is the universal damage metric.
> - Sigmund sputtering yield $Y = 0.042 \alpha S_n / U_s$.
> - Radiation-enhanced diffusion has **two additive terms**: thermal + athermal.
> - Critical blister pressure $P_{crit} = 2 \gamma_s / R_b$.
> - Threshold energy for sputtering minimized when $M_1 \approx M_2$.
> 
> **Cross-Cutting & Frequently Confused**
> - **Heat vs. radiation damage depth:** Heat → bulk; radiation → shallow layer (μm scale).
> - **Temperature dependence:** Heat = strong (Arrhenius); radiation = weak (athermal).
> - **Scale integrity:** PBR is a *necessary*, not *sufficient*, condition for protection — adhesion and CTE mismatch matter.
> - **PBR of common systems:** MgO/Mg = 0.81, Al₂O₃/Al = 1.28, Cr₂O₃/Cr = 1.99, Fe₂O₃/Fe = 2.14.
> - **Threshold energy for displacement** $E_d$ ≈ 25 eV (Fe), 68 eV (W).
> 
> **Valuation Golden Rules**
> 1. Always state **assumptions** before any derivation.
> 2. Carry **units** through every line — KTU examiners award method marks for unit consistency.
> 3. Use **boxed final answers** with explicit SI units.
> 4. In numericals, **time conversion** (h → s) is the most common omission.
> 5. For comparison questions, use a **matrix/table** — KTU examiners reward visual structure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Fatigue and Fracture Mechanisms in Wear Debris Formation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Role of Fatigue and Fracture Mechanisms in Wear Debris Formation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wear** is the progressive loss of material from the operating surface of a body as a result of relative motion at the contact interface. Among the various wear mechanisms (abrasive, adhesive, corrosive, erosive, fretting), **fatigue wear** and **fracture-controlled wear** are the two mechanisms in which the **generation of wear debris is governed by sub-surface crack nucleation, propagation, and ultimate fracture** of the surface-adjacent material layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Fatigue wear refers to the removal of material caused by cyclic stresses of magnitude below the static fracture limit, leading to subsurface crack initiation, slow stable crack growth, and final brittle/ductile fracture producing detached wear particles (debris)."*

In the context of **wear debris formation**, the term *fatigue* implies **low-cycle or high-cycle cyclic plastic strain** in a thin sub-surface layer (typically 1–100 µm deep), while *fracture* refers to the **final separation event** that converts a sub-surface crack into a free particle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Paperclip Test**
> Imagine a metal paperclip. It does not break when you bend it once, even though you exceed its yield strength. But if you bend it back and forth (≈ 10–20 cycles), a tiny crack initiates at the bend, grows a little each cycle, and *snap* — a small fragment detaches. **That detached fragment is the "wear debris,"** the cyclic bending is the "fatigue loading," and the final snap is the "fracture event."

- **Asperity contact** ↔ the bent region of the paperclip.
- **Cyclic tangential force** ↔ repeated bending.
- **Sub-surface void/crack** ↔ the propagating crack in the wire.
- **Loose wear chip** ↔ the small detached fragment.

> [!TIP]
> **Slogan to remember:** *"Wear debris is born below the surface — fatigue nurses it, fracture delivers it."*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Hardness | **H** | 1 – 20 GPa |
| Fracture toughness | **K_IC** | 1 – 100 MPa·m^(1/2) |
| Paris exponent | **m** | 2 – 6 (metals) |
| Sub-surface plastic depth | **h_p** | 1 – 100 µm |
| Wear coefficient (fatigue) | **K_w** | 10⁻⁶ – 10⁻⁴ |
| Cyclic plastic strain | **Δε_p** | 10⁻⁴ – 10⁻¹ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Delamination Theory of Wear (Suh, 1973)

Suh's **Delamination Theory** is the canonical KTU-recommended framework that links **fatigue + fracture → wear debris formation**. It is the *most frequently asked* framework in KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic.

### Step-by-Step Logic Chain

1. **Asperity contact & friction traction** – Normal load **W** and tangential load **F_t = µW** act on microscopic asperities.
2. **Subsurface plastic shear** – Maximum shear stress occurs at a depth **z ≈ 0.1 b** (where *b* is the Hertzian contact half-width), producing cyclic plastic strain.
3. **Void/crack nucleation** – Voids form at **inclusions, second-phase particles, or pre-existing defects** (weakest microstructural sites).
4. **Stable crack growth** – Cracks propagate **parallel to the surface** (Mode II / Mode III shear) by **low-cycle fatigue** governed by the Paris–Erdogan Law.
5. **Crack turning upward** – When a crack reaches a critical length, it turns toward the surface under tensile residual stresses (Mode I opening).
6. **Wear-sheet formation & final fracture** – The thin sheet of material between the crack tip and the free surface **fractures**, releasing a **plate-like wear particle** (typical aspect ratio 5:1 to 20:1).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this happens (the *Why*):** Materials cannot sustain infinite cyclic plastic strain. Sub-surface inclusions act as **stress concentrators (K_t ≈ 2–3)**, where the local cyclic stress exceeds the fatigue limit, nucleating cracks.

## 2.2 The Six Sequential Stages of Wear-Debris Formation

| Stage | Physical Event | Governing Discipline |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Asperity contact + frictional heating | Contact mechanics |
| **2** | Subsurface plastic shear accumulation | Plasticity theory |
| **3** | Void nucleation at inclusions | Micromechanics |
| **4** | Sub-surface crack growth (parallel) | Fatigue (Paris law) |
| **5** | Crack turning & linkage | Mixed-mode fracture |
| **6** | Sheet fracture & debris detachment | Linear-elastic fracture mechanics (LEFM) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{da}{dN}=C\,(\Delta K)^{m}$ | **Paris-Erdogan Law** (crack growth per cycle) | m/cycle |
| 2 | $\Delta K=Y\,\Delta\sigma\,\sqrt{\pi a}$ | Stress intensity factor range | MPa·m^(1/2) |
| 3 | $V=\dfrac{K\,W\,L}{H}$ | **Archard Wear Equation** (sliding wear volume) | m³ |
| 4 | $N_{f}=\left(\dfrac{\Delta\varepsilon_{p}}{2\varepsilon_{f}}\right)^{1/c}$ | **Coffin-Manson** (low-cycle fatigue life) | cycles |
| 5 | $\tau_{max}\approx 0.3\,p_{0}$ | Max subsurface shear stress (Hertzian, µ ≈ 0.3) | MPa |
| 6 | $h_{p}\approx 0.1\,b$ | Depth of plastic zone (Hertz contact) | m |
| 7 | $a_{c}=\dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\sigma}\right)^{2}$ | **Critical crack length** (final fracture) | m |
| 8 | $\dot{W}=\dfrac{V}{L\cdot s}=\dfrac{\text{wear volume}}{\text{load}\cdot\text{distance}}$ | Specific wear rate | m³/(N·m) |
| 9 | $K_{t}=1+2\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{\rho}}$ | Stress concentration at a notch/inclusion | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\sigma_{r}=\sigma_{0}\!\left(1-\dfrac{2z}{h_{p}}\right)$ | Residual tensile stress profile (Suh) | MPa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols used:** *a* = crack length, *N* = number of cycles, *C, m* = Paris constants, *Y* = geometry factor, *W* = normal load, *L* = sliding distance, *H* = hardness, *V* = wear volume, *K_IC* = fracture toughness, *p_0* = peak Hertzian pressure, *b* = Hertzian contact half-width, *K_t* = stress concentration factor, *ρ* = notch root radius, *σ_r* = residual stress, *h_p* = plastic depth.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Mechanism Dominant |
|---|---|
| Rolling-element bearings | **Rolling contact fatigue (RCF)** — spalling |
| Gear teeth flanks | Pitting fatigue wear |
| Automotive cam-follower | Delamination, scuffing |
| Aircraft landing gear | Subsurface fatigue delamination |
| Cutting tool flank | Crater wear by fracture |
| Railway rails | Surface and subsurface fatigue |

> [!TIP]
> In every KTU numerical, **identify the dominant mechanism first** (adhesive / abrasive / fatigue / corrosive). Fatigue-fracture dominates when loads are *cyclic* and debris are *plate-like* rather than *grain-like* or *ploughing-ridged*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Wear Rate from Paris Law (KTU Frequently Asked)

**Goal:** Derive a closed-form expression for the **wear volume per unit sliding distance** using the Paris law for sub-surface crack growth.

**Given:**
- Paris law: $\dfrac{da}{dN}=C\,(\Delta K)^{m}$ , with $m=2$ (typical for ductile metals).
- Geometry factor $Y \approx 1$ (long surface crack).
- Cyclic tangential traction: $\Delta\sigma=\mu\,p_{0}$ where $p_{0}$ is the peak Hertzian pressure.
- One load cycle ≈ sliding over one asperity contact length $\ell_{c}$.

---

**Step 1.** Express $\Delta K$ for a sub-surface crack of length $a$ :

$$\Delta K = Y\,\Delta\sigma\,\sqrt{\pi a} \;=\; \mu\,p_{0}\,\sqrt{\pi a}$$

**Step 2.** Substitute into the Paris law (with $m=2$):

$$\dfrac{da}{dN}=C\!\left(\mu\,p_{0}\sqrt{\pi a}\right)^{2} = C\,\mu^{2}\,p_{0}^{2}\,\pi\,a$$

**Step 3.** This is a separable ODE in $a$ and $N$:

$$\dfrac{da}{a}=C\,\mu^{2}\,p_{0}^{2}\,\pi\,dN$$

**Step 4.** Integrate from initial flaw size $a_{i}$ to critical crack length $a_{c}$:

$$\int_{a_{i}}^{a_{c}}\!\dfrac{da}{a} \;=\; C\,\mu^{2}\,p_{0}^{2}\,\pi\!\int_{0}^{N_{f}}\!dN$$

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{a_{c}}{a_{i}}\right)=C\,\mu^{2}\,p_{0}^{2}\,\pi\,N_{f}$$

**Step 5.** Solve for the **number of cycles to form one wear particle**:

$$N_{f}=\dfrac{1}{C\,\mu^{2}\,p_{0}^{2}\,\pi}\;\ln\!\left(\dfrac{a_{c}}{a_{i}}\right)$$

**Step 6.** The **wear volume per particle** equals (thickness $h_{p}$ × area of one sheet $A_{s}$):

$$V_{p}=h_{p}\cdot A_{s}$$

**Step 7.** The **specific wear rate** (volume per unit load per unit sliding distance) is:

$$\dot{W}=\dfrac{V_{p}}{W\cdot \ell_{c}\cdot N_{f}}=\dfrac{h_{p}\,A_{s}\,C\,\mu^{2}\,p_{0}^{2}\,\pi}{W\,\ell_{c}\,\ln(a_{c}/a_{i})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The wear rate is **proportional to the square of the tangential traction** ($\mu^{2} p_{0}^{2}$). This explains why *reducing friction* (e.g., by lubrication) is the single most effective way to combat fatigue-delamination wear.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Critical Crack Length for Final Fracture

**Goal:** Determine the crack length at which the sub-surface crack turns unstable and detaches as wear debris.

**Given:** Fracture criterion $K_{I}=K_{IC}$, residual tensile stress $\sigma_{r}$ acting on a sub-surface edge crack of length $a$ in a semi-infinite body, with geometry factor $Y=\dfrac{1.12}{\sqrt{\pi}}$ (for short edge crack).

---

**Step 1.** Write the mode-I stress intensity factor for the residual-stress-driven crack:

$$K_{I}=Y\,\sigma_{r}\sqrt{\pi a}=\dfrac{1.12\,\sigma_{r}\sqrt{\pi a}}{\sqrt{\pi}}=1.12\,\sigma_{r}\sqrt{a}$$

**Step 2.** Apply the fracture criterion $K_{I}=K_{IC}$:

$$1.12\,\sigma_{r}\sqrt{a_{c}}=K_{IC}$$

**Step 3.** Solve for the **critical (final-fracture) crack length**:

$$a_{c}=\dfrac{1}{1.2544}\!\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{\sigma_{r}}\right)^{2}=0.797\!\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{\sigma_{r}}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 4.** A simpler (and KTU-acceptable) form, used by Suh directly, sets $Y=1$:

$$\boxed{\,a_{c}\;\approx\;\dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{\sigma_{r}}\right)^{2}\,}$$

**Step 5.** Substituting $\sigma_{r}=\sigma_{0}\!\left(1-\dfrac{2z}{h_{p}}\right)$ at the surface ($z=0$):

$$a_{c}=\dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{\sigma_{0}}\right)^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical meaning:** Materials with **higher K_IC** (tougher alloys) can tolerate longer sub-surface cracks before detaching debris — they form *larger, more frequent* wear plates. **Brittle materials** (low K_IC) form *small, angular* debris.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Subsurface Plastic Depth (Hertzian)

**Given:** A sphere-on-flat contact with normal load $W$, sphere radius $R$, effective modulus $E^{*}=\dfrac{E}{2(1-\nu^{2})}$.

**Step 1.** Hertzian contact half-width:

$$b=\sqrt{\dfrac{4WR}{\pi\,E^{*}}}$$

**Step 2.** Peak contact pressure:

$$p_{0}=\dfrac{2W}{\pi b^{2}}=\dfrac{1}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{4W E^{*\,2}}{\pi R^{2}}}$$

**Step 3.** Maximum shear stress (always at $z \approx 0.48\,b$ for µ ≈ 0.3, and **Tabor shows** $\tau_{max}\approx 0.3\,p_{0}$).

**Step 4.** Plastic zone depth (Tresca yield criterion $\tau_{max}=\dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{2}$):

$$0.3\,p_{0}=\dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{2}\;\Rightarrow\;p_{0}=\dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{0.6}$$

**Step 5.** Substituting $p_{0}$ from Step 2 and equating, the plastic zone extends to:

$$h_{p}\;\approx\;0.1\,b\;=\;0.1\sqrt{\dfrac{4WR}{\pi E^{*}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> This is the **length scale** that controls the thickness of the wear sheet $h_{p}$ — and hence the **wear volume per cycle**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Connecting Fatigue Life to Wear Rate

| Parameter | Effect on Wear Rate | Effect on Fatigue Life $N_f$ |
|---|---|---|
| ↑ Load $W$ | ↑ wear | ↓ $N_f$ (faster crack growth) |
| ↑ Friction µ | ↑ wear (∝ µ²) | ↓ $N_f$ |
| ↑ $K_{IC}$ | ↑ $a_{c}$ (larger debris) | ↑ $N_f$ (slower unstable fracture) |
| ↑ Inclusion size $a_{i}$ | ↑ wear (shorter $N_f$) | ↓ $N_f$ |
| ↑ Hardness $H$ | ↓ wear (∝ 1/H) | ↑ $N_f$ (in high-cycle regime) |
| ↑ Sliding speed | ↑ wear (thermal softening) | ↓ $N_f$ (oxidation-assisted) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Wear Debris Formation Pipeline (Suh's Delamination Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1["Asperity contact under normal load W"] --> n2["Tangential friction traction Ft = mu W"]
    n2 --> n3["Subsurface plastic shear zone at z approx 0.1 b"]
    n3 --> n4["Void nucleation at inclusions or second phase particles"]
    n4 --> n5["Stable fatigue crack growth parallel to surface Mode II"]
    n5 --> n6["Crack turning upward under residual tensile stress Mode I"]
    n6 --> n7["Critical crack length a_c reached K_I equals K_IC"]
    n7 --> n8["Final fracture of surface-adjacent sheet"]
    n8 --> n9["Plate-like wear debris detached from surface"]
    n9 --> n10["Debris ejected or trapped in contact zone"]
    n10 -.continues cyclic loading.-> n2
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of Fatigue-Fracture Wear System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOAD["Loading Subsystem"]
        L1["Cyclic normal load W"]
        L2["Tangential friction mu W"]
    end

    subgraph STRESS["Stress Analysis Subsystem"]
        S1["Hertzian contact pressure p0"]
        S2["Subsurface shear tau max approx 0.3 p0"]
        S3["Residual tensile stress sigma r"]
    end

    subgraph DEFECT["Defect Subsystem"]
        D1["Inclusions, voids, second phase"]
        D2["Stress concentration Kt"]
    end

    subgraph FATIGUE["Fatigue Subsystem"]
        F1["Paris law da/dN = C delta K m"]
        F2["Coffin-Manson low cycle fatigue"]
    end

    subgraph FRAC["Fracture Subsystem"]
        FR1["Critical crack length a_c"]
        FR2["Fracture criterion K_I equals K_IC"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Wear Output Subsystem"]
        O1["Plate-like debris"]
        O2["Specific wear rate W dot"]
        O3["Surface damage spalling or pitting"]
    end

    LOAD --> STRESS
    STRESS --> DEFECT
    DEFECT --> FATIGUE
    FATIGUE --> FRAC
    FRAC --> OUTPUT
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    D1 --> D2
    F1 --> F2
    FR1 --> FR2
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Subsurface Crack Stages (Cross-Sectional Schematic in Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Free surface z equals 0"] --> B["Plastic shear zone 0 to h_p"]
    B --> C["Inclusions and voids nucleate cracks"]
    C --> D["Cracks grow parallel to surface Mode II"]
    D --> E["Cracks coalesce into long sub-surface crack"]
    E --> F["Crack turns upward under residual stress"]
    F --> G["Final fracture - wear sheet released"]
    G --> H["Plate-like debris particle detached"]
    A -.free surface indentation.-> C
    H -.newly exposed surface.-> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip for KTU diagrams:** Always label the **free surface (z = 0)** at the **top** and the **depth (z increasing downward)** in your answer diagrams. Examiners mark 1–2 marks for **neat, correctly labelled cross-sections**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define fatigue wear. How does it differ from adhesive and abrasive wear in terms of debris morphology?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Fatigue wear** is the removal of material from a contacting surface caused by **repeated sub-failure cyclic loading**, in which sub-surface cracks nucleate, grow, and finally fracture to release wear debris.

| Wear Type | Debris Shape | Cause |
|---|---|---|
| Adhesive | Smears, transfer flakes | Local microwelding & shear |
| Abrasive | Angular, gouged chips | Ploughing/cutting by hard asperity |
| **Fatigue** | **Plate-like (flakes)** | **Sub-surface crack linkage + final fracture** |

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark comparison table + 1 mark debris-morphology conclusion)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State and explain the Paris-Erdogan equation. What is its significance in fatigue wear?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\dfrac{da}{dN}=C\,(\Delta K)^{m}$$

where $a$ = crack length, $N$ = cycles, $\Delta K$ = stress intensity factor range, and $C, m$ are material constants.

**Significance in fatigue wear:**
- Quantifies **how fast a sub-surface crack grows per cycle**.
- Allows prediction of the **number of cycles to form a wear particle** ($N_f = \int da / [C(\Delta K)^m]$).
- Bridges **fracture mechanics** and **wear-rate prediction**.

*(1 mark equation + 1 mark each for explanation + significance — 3 marks total)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before You Write**
> - Most students **write the equation but skip the integration limits** → loses 2–3 marks.
> - Many forget to state the **assumptions** (e.g., $Y=1$, $m=2$, Hertzian contact) → loses 1–2 marks.
> - Students often **confuse Mode I, II, III** in the diagram → loses 1 mark.
> - Always **draw a labelled cross-section** of the sub-surface crack — minimum 2 marks reserved for neat diagrams.
> - **Units must be consistent**: convert GPa → Pa and µm → m before substituting in Paris law.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **six stages of wear-debris formation** according to **Suh's Delamination Theory**. Discuss the role of **sub-surface crack nucleation** and **final fracture** in producing wear debris. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel shaft (HRC 35, $H = 3.5$ GPa, $K_{IC}=55$ MPa·m^(1/2)) slides against a bronze bushing under a normal load of 2000 N with a coefficient of friction $\mu=0.3$. Sub-surface residual stress is $\sigma_r=200$ MPa. Assuming Paris constant $C=2\times 10^{-11}$ (SI units) and exponent $m=2$, estimate the **number of cycles** required for a sub-surface crack to grow from $a_{i}=5\;\mu$m to the critical length. Use geometry factor $Y=1$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Diagram (2 marks):** Draw cross-section with free surface, plastic zone, inclusions, sub-surface crack parallel to surface, then a turning crack and a detaching plate-like debris.

**Six Stages (1 mark each):**

1. **Asperity contact:** Microscopic contact at asperity tips carries the normal load $W$.
2. **Frictional traction:** Tangential force $F_t = \mu W$ shears the surface.
3. **Subsurface plastic deformation:** Cyclic shear produces a plastic zone of depth $h_p \approx 0.1 b$.
4. **Crack nucleation:** Voids form at inclusions/second-phase particles (stress concentration $K_t$).
5. **Stable crack growth:** Cracks propagate parallel to surface in Mode II by Paris-law fatigue.
6. **Final fracture:** Crack turns upward, reaches $a_c$, and a wear sheet detaches as plate-like debris.

**Role of sub-surface nucleation:** Stress concentration at inclusions (typically $K_t \approx 2$–$3$) initiates cracks **below the surface**, not at the surface — this is the *signature* of fatigue wear and produces the *plate-like* debris.

**Role of final fracture:** The fracture event converts a *sub-surface defect* into a *free particle*. The size is governed by the fracture toughness $K_{IC}$.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $W=2000$ N, $\mu=0.3$, $\sigma_r=200$ MPa, $K_{IC}=55$ MPa·m^(1/2), $C=2\times 10^{-11}$, $m=2$, $Y=1$, $a_i=5\;\mu$m.

**Step 1 — Compute critical crack length (2 marks):**

$$a_{c}=\dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\,\sigma_{r}}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left(\dfrac{55\times 10^{6}}{1\times 200\times 10^{6}}\right)^{2}$$

$$a_{c}=\dfrac{1}{\pi}\,(0.275)^{2}=\dfrac{0.0756}{3.1416}=0.02408\;\text{m}\;\approx\;24.1\;\text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Express $\Delta K$ (1 mark):**

$$\Delta K = Y\,\sigma_r\,\sqrt{\pi a} = 200\times 10^{6}\sqrt{\pi a}\;\;\text{Pa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$$

**Step 3 — Paris law with $m=2$ (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{da}{dN}=C\,(\Delta K)^{2}=C\,\sigma_{r}^{2}\,\pi\,a = 2\times 10^{-11}\times(200\times 10^{6})^{2}\times\pi\,a$$

$$= 2\times 10^{-11}\times 4\times 10^{16}\times\pi\,a = 2.513\times 10^{6}\,a$$

**Step 4 — Integrate (2 marks):**

$$\int_{a_{i}}^{a_{c}}\!\dfrac{da}{a}=2.513\times 10^{6}\!\int_{0}^{N_{f}}\!dN$$

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{a_{c}}{a_{i}}\right)=2.513\times 10^{6}\,N_{f}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{24.1\times 10^{-3}}{5\times 10^{-6}}\right)=\ln(4820)=8.480$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $N_f$ (1 mark):**

$$N_{f}=\dfrac{8.480}{2.513\times 10^{6}}=3.37\times 10^{-6}\;\text{cycles}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A negative exponent is *physically impossible* — this means with these **Paris constants (in SI)** the sub-critical crack growth is *instantaneous*. In practice, $C$ in SI units is usually $10^{-11}$ to $10^{-13}$ **per cycle per (MPa·m^(1/2))^m** (not per (Pa·m^(1/2))^m). If $C=2\times 10^{-11}$ in **MPa·m^(1/2)** units, then we must convert $\Delta K$ in those same units:
> $\Delta\sigma = 200$ MPa, $\sqrt{\pi a} = \sqrt{\pi \times 0.024}\approx 0.275$ m^(1/2)
> $\Delta K = 200\times 0.275 = 55$ MPa·m^(1/2) (which equals $K_{IC}$ — crack is already at critical state).
>
> The correct pedagogical interpretation: **$N_f$ is very small** → a sub-surface crack under this stress reaches fracture within a few hundred to a few thousand cycles, producing *frequent* plate-like debris. This is the typical answer the KTU examiner expects: **a finite, small $N_f$**, indicating **rapid fatigue-delamination wear**.

**Recomputing** with $C=2\times 10^{-11}$ in MPa·m^(1/2) units, $m=2$:

$$\dfrac{da}{dN}=2\times 10^{-11}\,(200\sqrt{\pi a})^{2}=2\times 10^{-11}\times 4\times 10^{4}\times\pi\,a = 2.513\times 10^{-6}\,a$$

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{a_{c}}{a_{i}}\right)=2.513\times 10^{-6}\,N_{f}\;\Rightarrow\;N_{f}=\dfrac{8.480}{2.513\times 10^{-6}}\approx 3.37\times 10^{6}\;\text{cycles}$$

**Answer:** $N_f \approx 3.4 \times 10^{6}$ **cycles** to detach one wear plate (assuming SI-consistent Paris constant $C=2\times 10^{-11}$ (MPa·m^(1/2))^(-2)).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the role of **fracture toughness $K_{IC}$** and **sub-surface residual stresses** in determining the morphology and size of wear debris in fatigue wear. Derive the expression for **critical crack length** $a_c$ at which a sub-surface crack detaches as a wear particle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rolling-sliding contact produces a sub-surface Hertzian contact with $p_0=1.5$ GPa, sphere radius $R=20$ mm, and effective modulus $E^{*}=210$ GPa. For a steel with yield stress $\sigma_y=700$ MPa, calculate: (i) the Hertzian contact half-width $b$, (ii) the depth of the plastic zone $h_p$, and (iii) the wear-sheet thickness expected per delamination event. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Fracture toughness $K_{IC}$ (2 marks):** $K_{IC}$ is the resistance of the material to unstable crack propagation. A higher $K_{IC}$ allows sub-surface cracks to grow to **larger lengths** before detaching as debris, producing **larger plate-like particles**. Brittle materials with low $K_{IC}$ produce **small angular debris**.

**Residual stress $\sigma_r$ (2 marks):** Residual tensile stresses arise from **non-uniform plastic deformation** and **thermal gradients** in the sub-surface. They drive the final Mode I opening of the crack toward the free surface. Higher $\sigma_r$ reduces $a_c$ → smaller, more frequent debris.

**Derivation of $a_c$ (3 marks):**

For an edge crack in a semi-infinite plate with tensile residual stress $\sigma_r$:

$$K_I=Y\,\sigma_r\sqrt{\pi a}$$

At the critical condition $K_I=K_{IC}$ with $Y=1$:

$$K_{IC}=\sigma_r\sqrt{\pi a_c}$$

$$\boxed{\,a_{c}=\dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{\sigma_{r}}\right)^{2}\,}$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $p_0=1.5$ GPa, $R=20$ mm $= 0.02$ m, $E^{*}=210$ GPa, $\sigma_y=700$ MPa.

**Step 1 — Contact half-width (2 marks):**

$$b=\dfrac{\pi\,p_{0}\,R}{2E^{*}}=\dfrac{\pi\times 1.5\times 10^{9}\times 0.02}{2\times 210\times 10^{9}}$$

$$b=\dfrac{9.4248\times 10^{7}}{4.2\times 10^{11}}=2.244\times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}\approx 224\;\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Plastic zone depth $h_p$ (3 marks):**

Using the Tresca criterion $\tau_{max}=\sigma_y/2$ and $\tau_{max}\approx 0.3\,p_0$:

$$0.3\,p_{0}=\dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{2}\;\Rightarrow\;p_{0,yield}=\dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{0.6}=\dfrac{700}{0.6}=1166.7\;\text{MPa}$$

Since $p_0=1500$ MPa $> 1166.7$ MPa, **plastic deformation occurs**.

Plastic depth from Hertzian analysis:

$$h_{p}\approx 0.1\,b = 0.1\times 224\;\mu\text{m}=22.4\;\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Wear-sheet thickness (2 marks):**

The wear sheet produced per delamination event has thickness approximately equal to the plastic zone depth:

$$t_{sheet}\approx h_{p}\approx 22.4\;\mu\text{m}$$

**Answer:** $b \approx 224\;\mu$m, $h_p \approx 22.4\;\mu$m, $t_{sheet} \approx 22.4\;\mu$m.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **Fatigue wear** = removal of material by *cyclic sub-failure stresses*; produces **plate-like (flaky) debris**.
- **Fracture mechanism** = the *final separation* event governed by $K_{IC}$.
- **Delamination theory (Suh, 1973)** is the **standard KTU answer** framework — learn its 6 stages verbatim.
- **Paris-Erdogan Law** $\dfrac{da}{dN}=C(\Delta K)^m$ is the *core equation* — memorize, derive, apply.
- **Critical crack length:** $a_c=\dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{\sigma_r}\right)^{2}$.
- **Plastic zone depth:** $h_p \approx 0.1\,b$ where $b$ is the Hertzian half-width.
- **Wear rate scales with $\mu^2 p_0^2$** — *friction control* is the most effective wear control.
- **Higher $K_{IC}$** → *larger but fewer* debris; **higher $\sigma_r$** → *smaller but more frequent* debris.
- **Always include a labelled cross-sectional diagram** in long-answer questions (minimum 2 marks reserved).
- **Debris morphology is the diagnostic fingerprint:** plate = fatigue, smear = adhesive, angular = abrasive, oxide = corrosive.
- **Coffin-Manson** relation governs low-cycle fatigue life: $N_f=\left(\dfrac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2\varepsilon_f}\right)^{1/c}$.
- **Assumption set to declare in answers:** $Y=1$ (long crack), $m=2$ (ductile metal), isothermal, Hertzian contact.
- **Mode II/III** = sub-surface shear crack growth; **Mode I** = final upward turning.
- **Engineering fixes:** reduce µ (lubricate), increase $H$ (harden surface), reduce inclusions (cleaner steel), induce compressive residual stress (shot peening — lowers $\sigma_r$ and raises $a_c$).
- **Conversion traps:** GPa → Pa (×10⁹), µm → m (×10⁻⁶), MPa → Pa (×10⁶).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line KTU 2024 exam answer opener (recommended):**
> *"According to Suh's Delamination Theory (1973), wear debris in fatigue wear is produced when sub-surface voids nucleate at inclusions, grow parallel to the surface by Paris-law fatigue, and finally fracture when the crack length reaches $a_c=\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{\sigma_r}\right)^{2}$ — releasing a plate-like wear particle."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Friction and Lubrication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Friction and Lubrication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Friction — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Friction** is the *tangential resistance* developed at the common interface when one body slides, rolls, or tends to move relative to another body in contact. Mathematically, it is the force component that opposes the direction of relative motion (or impending motion) between two contacting surfaces.

In the context of **Surface Engineering (PEPET862)**, friction is treated as a **surface-controlled phenomenon** governed by:
- Real area of contact (asperity contact)
- Shear strength of junctions formed at asperities
- Surface roughness, hardness, and the presence of interfacial films (oxides, contaminants, lubricants)

## 1.2 Lubrication — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lubrication** is the process of introducing a *low-shear-strength film* (fluid, semi-fluid, solid, or gas) between two contacting surfaces to reduce friction, wear, and heat generation, and to prevent direct metal-to-metal contact.

The four fundamental functions of a lubricant are summarised as:
- **F**riction reduction
- **W**ear prevention
- **H**eat removal
- **C**orrosion protection (relevant cross-link to Module 2 / 3 of PEPET862)

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Sled and the Snow:**
> Imagine dragging a wooden sledge across dry sand. The sand grains interlock with the wood, producing strong resistance. Now imagine the same sledge sliding on ice — a thin water film separates the surfaces, and the sledge glides easily. The water film is the *lubricant*; the resistance you overcame in the first case is *friction*. Surface engineering aims to deliberately engineer that "ice" between machine parts using controlled films, coatings, and lubricants.

This single analogy captures all of Module 1:
- **Sand on wood** → Boundary lubrication (asperity contact)
- **Wet film** → Hydrodynamic lubrication (full fluid film)
- **Interlocking grains** → Static / sliding friction

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in PEPET862

| Surface Engineering Aspect | Role of Friction & Lubrication |
|---|---|
| Tribology (study of interacting surfaces) | Defines the system boundary condition |
| Wear mechanisms (adhesive, abrasive) | Friction directly drives wear |
| Surface coatings | A coating's *lubricity* is judged by friction coefficient |
| Contact fatigue | Lubricant film pressure supports the load |

## 1.5 Standard Engineering Metrics Used

The following are the **canonical KTU-board quantities** that must be memorised:

- **μ** — Coefficient of friction (dimensionless, typically $\mu = 0.05$ to $0.8$)
- **η (eta)** — Dynamic viscosity of lubricant, units: **Pa·s** or **N·s/m²**
- **ν (nu)** — Kinematic viscosity, $\nu = \eta / \rho$, units: **m²/s** or **cSt** (centistokes)
- **P** — Bearing pressure, $\,P = W/(L\cdot D)$, units: **N/mm²** or **Pa**
- **ZN/P** — Petroff number (dimensionless bearing modulus)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Friction

Friction is categorised by the **state of motion** of the contacting pair:

- **Static Friction ($F_s$):** Force that must be overcome to *initiate* motion. $F_s = \mu_s N$
- **Kinetic / Sliding Friction ($F_k$):** Force resisting *continued* sliding. $F_k = \mu_k N$
- **Rolling Friction ($F_r$):** Resistance from deformation at the contact zone. Typically $F_r \ll F_k$
- **Internal Friction:** Within a fluid (viscosity) or solid (lattice damping)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coulomb's Laws of Dry Friction (for KTU Board):**
> 1. The friction force is **independent of the apparent area of contact**.
> 2. The friction force is **proportional to the normal load** N.
> 3. The kinetic friction force is **independent of the relative sliding velocity**.
> *(In reality, especially for metals, all three are approximations; KTU expects them as the textbook answer.)*

## 2.2 Coefficient of Friction and Friction Angle

The friction angle $\phi$ is geometrically related to the coefficient:

$$\tan \phi = \mu$$

The resultant reaction R from a rough surface is therefore inclined at angle $\phi$ to the normal:

$$R = N\sqrt{1 + \mu^{2}}$$

## 2.3 Classification of Lubrication Regimes

Lubrication is divided into four regimes, best visualised by the **Stribeck Curve** (covered in §4):

1. **Boundary Lubrication** — $\lambda < 1$; asperity contact dominates; $\mu \approx 0.1$ to $0.4$
2. **Mixed Lubrication** — $1 < \lambda < 3$; partial fluid film, partial asperity contact
3. **Hydrodynamic (HD) Lubrication** — $\lambda \geq 3$; full fluid film, $\mu$ falls sharply
4. **Elastohydrodynamic Lubrication (EHL)** — High-pressure regime where surfaces elastically deform and viscosity rises sharply (gears, rolling element bearings)

where **$\lambda$ = specific film thickness = $h_{min}/\sigma$** with $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma_1^{2} + \sigma_2^{2}}$ being the composite RMS roughness.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **minimum formula set** for any KTU 2024 Scheme numerical on Friction and Lubrication. **Memorise this verbatim.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = \mu N$ | Coulomb friction force | N |
| 2 | $\tan \phi = \mu$ | Friction angle relation | dimensionless |
| 3 | $R = N\sqrt{1 + \mu^{2}}$ | Resultant reaction at rough surface | N |
| 4 | $P = \dfrac{W}{LD}$ | Bearing pressure | Pa or N/mm² |
| 5 | $S = \dfrac{\eta N}{P}$ | Sommerfeld number (HD bearing) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta = \rho \nu$ | Dynamic ↔ Kinematic viscosity | Pa·s |
| 7 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h_{min}}{\sigma}$ | Specific film thickness | dimensionless |
| 8 | $T = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}\eta N r^{3} L}{c}$ | Petroff's friction torque | N·m |
| 9 | $f = \dfrac{2\pi^{2} r}{c} \cdot \dfrac{\eta N}{P}$ | Petroff's coefficient of friction | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\dfrac{h^{3}}{\eta}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) = 6U\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ | 1-D Reynolds equation | Pa |

> **Note on notation:** $W$ = load, $L$ = bearing length, $D$ = journal diameter, $r$ = journal radius, $c$ = radial clearance, $N$ = rotational speed (rev/s), $h$ = film thickness, $U$ = surface velocity.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of the Theory

- **HD lubrication theory** underpins the design of **journal bearings** in turbines, IC engines, and pumps.
- **Boundary lubrication** explains the role of **EP (extreme-pressure) additives** like ZDDP and MoS₂ in gear oils — directly connected to PEPET862 surface coatings.
- **Petroff's equation** gives a first-cut estimate of friction power loss in heavily loaded bearings, used in **thermal design of turbomachinery**.
- **Specific film thickness $\lambda$** is the *industrial benchmark* to decide whether a bearing operates in safe (HD) or risky (boundary) regime.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Short-Circuit Mandate:** Every algebraic transition below is fully expanded. No step is implicit. The derivations are the two most frequently asked analytical problems in KTU ESE for this module.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Petroff's Equation for Journal Bearing Friction Torque

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

Consider a **full journal bearing** of:
- Journal radius: $r$
- Bearing length: $L$
- Radial clearance (sump): $c = R - r$
- Rotational speed: $N$ (rev/s)
- Lubricant dynamic viscosity: $\eta$

**Assumptions (Petroff's hypothesis):**
1. The bearing is **infinitely long**, so end-leakage is neglected.
2. The journal is **concentric** with the bearing (no side-leakage, no eccentricity).
3. The lubricant film is **continuous and Newtonian**.
4. Velocity variation across the film is **linear** (Couette flow).
5. Pressure variation across the film is **zero** (no Poiseuille contribution).

### 3.1.2 Derivation

Let $u$ be the velocity of the journal surface at radius $r$:

$$u = 2\pi r N$$

The film thickness at the concentric position is uniform and equal to $c$ (radial clearance).

By Newton's law of viscosity, the shear stress at the journal surface is:

$$\tau = \eta \frac{du}{dy}$$

where $y$ is the coordinate across the film. By the linearity assumption (assumption 4), the velocity gradient is:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{u - 0}{c - 0} = \frac{2\pi r N}{c}$$

Therefore:

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \frac{2\pi r N}{c}$$

The shear force on the journal surface acts over the entire cylindrical wetted area $A = 2\pi r L$:

$$F_{friction} = \tau \cdot A = \eta \cdot \frac{2\pi r N}{c} \cdot 2\pi r L$$

$$\boxed{F_{friction} = \frac{4\pi^{2} \eta N r^{2} L}{c}}$$

The friction torque about the journal axis is force × radius:

$$T = F_{friction} \times r$$

$$T = \frac{4\pi^{2} \eta N r^{2} L}{c} \times r$$

$$\boxed{T = \frac{4\pi^{2} \eta N r^{3} L}{c}}$$

### 3.1.3 Coefficient of Friction Form

The bearing load $W$ is supported by the projected area $L \cdot D = 2rL$, giving the bearing pressure:

$$P = \frac{W}{2rL} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad W = 2rLP$$

The coefficient of friction is defined as:

$$f = \frac{F_{friction}}{W} = \frac{\dfrac{4\pi^{2} \eta N r^{2} L}{c}}{2rLP}$$

$$f = \frac{4\pi^{2} \eta N r^{2} L}{c \cdot 2rLP}$$

$$f = \frac{2\pi^{2} r}{c} \cdot \frac{\eta N}{P}$$

$$\boxed{f = \frac{2\pi^{2} r}{c} \cdot \frac{\eta N}{P}}$$

This dimensionless form is the **Petroff coefficient of friction** and is the one examiners prefer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Reynolds Equation (1-D Form, Sliding Block)

### 3.2.1 Physical Setup

A rectangular slider of width $B$ slides on a stationary surface with velocity $U$. The lubricant film has thickness $h(x)$ and viscosity $\eta$. Pressure $p(x)$ is generated in the film by the **converging wedge** action.

### 3.2.2 Governing Equations

**(a) Continuity (mass conservation) for an incompressible lubricant:**
The net volume flow into an elemental slice $dx$ must equal the rate of film thickness change:

$$\frac{\partial q}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} = 0$$

For **steady state**, $\partial h/\partial t = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial q}{\partial x} = 0$$

**(b) Navier-Stokes for thin film** (neglecting inertia and body forces):

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \eta \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}}$$

**(c) Boundary conditions:**
- $u(0) = U$ at the slider (top surface)
- $u(h) = 0$ at the stationary surface (no-slip)

### 3.2.3 Solution by Double Integration

Integrate (b) twice with respect to $y$:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{1}{\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\, y + C_1$$

$$u = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\, y^{2} + C_1 y + C_2$$

Apply boundary conditions:

At $y = h$, $u = 0$:
$$0 = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\, h^{2} + C_1 h + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = -\frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\, h^{2} - C_1 h$$

At $y = 0$, $u = U$:
$$U = 0 + 0 + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = U$$

Equating both:
$$U = -\frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\, h^{2} - C_1 h$$

$$C_1 = -\frac{U}{h} - \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\, h$$

Therefore the velocity profile is:

$$u = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\left(y^{2} - hy\right) + U\left(1 - \frac{y}{h}\right)$$

### 3.2.4 Volumetric Flow Rate

Integrate across the film:

$$q = \int_{0}^{h} u\, dy$$

$$q = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\int_{0}^{h}(y^{2} - hy)\,dy + U\int_{0}^{h}\!\left(1 - \frac{y}{h}\right)dy$$

$$q = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\left[\frac{h^{3}}{3} - \frac{h^{3}}{2}\right] + U\left[h - \frac{h}{2}\right]$$

$$q = -\frac{h^{3}}{12\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{Uh}{2}$$

### 3.2.5 Apply Continuity (Steady State)

$$\frac{\partial q}{\partial x} = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-\frac{h^{3}}{12\eta}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{Uh}{2}\right) = 0$$

For constant $\eta$ and $U$:

$$-\frac{1}{12\eta}\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(h^{3}\frac{dp}{dx}\right) + \frac{U}{2}\frac{dh}{dx} = 0$$

Multiplying through by $-12\eta$:

$$\boxed{\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(h^{3}\frac{dp}{dx}\right) = 6\eta U\frac{dh}{dx}}$$

This is the **1-D Reynolds Equation** — the foundation of all hydrodynamic lubrication theory. Its 2-D extension is obtained by adding the $y$-derivative term.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Dry Friction (Coulomb) | Boundary Lubrication | Hydrodynamic Lubrication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Load support | Direct asperity contact | Asperity + partial film | Fluid pressure |
| Friction coefficient | $\mu \sim 0.3$ to $0.8$ | $\mu \sim 0.1$ to $0.4$ | $\mu \sim 0.001$ to $0.01$ |
| Wear | Severe | Moderate | Negligible |
| Heat generation | High | Moderate | Low |
| Velocity dependence | None (Coulomb) | Weak | Strong (viscous) |
| Surface engineering role | Hardness, coatings | EP additives, solid films | Viscosity, surface finish |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagrams follow the **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards**: every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed, no markdown is embedded inside labels, and reserved keywords are avoided.

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Lubrication Regime Selection (Stribeck Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Start: System Inputs]) --> nodeB[Load W, Speed N, Viscosity eta]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Compute Bearing Parameter ZN by P}
    nodeC --> nodeD{Compare lambda = h_min by sigma}
    nodeD --> nodeE[lambda less than 1: Boundary Regime]
    nodeD --> nodeF[lambda between 1 and 3: Mixed Regime]
    nodeD --> nodeG[lambda greater than 3: Hydrodynamic Regime]
    nodeE --> nodeH[High mu, asperity load share, EP additives required]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Partial film, transition design, surface finish critical]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Full film, low mu, design via Reynolds and Petroff]
    nodeH --> nodeK([End: Lubricant Selection Output])
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Friction Force Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Applied Tangential Force F] --> node2{Compare F vs Static Friction Limit}
    node2 --> node3[F less than mu_s times N: Body at Rest]
    node2 --> node4[F greater than mu_s times N: Motion Initiates]
    node4 --> node5[Kinetic Regime Activated]
    node5 --> node6[Sliding Friction F_k = mu_k times N]
    node5 --> node7[Rolling Component F_r if Wheel Present]
    node6 --> node8[Heat Generation q = F_k times v]
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9([End: Energy Dissipated at Interface])
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Petroff's Equation Computational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Inputs: eta, N, r, L, c, W] --> nodeB[Step 1: Compute Film Shear Stress tau]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 2: Compute Surface Velocity u = 2 pi r N]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step 3: Velocity Gradient du by dy = u by c]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step 4: tau = eta times u by c]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Step 5: Friction Force F_f = tau times 2 pi r L]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Step 6: Friction Torque T = F_f times r]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Step 7: Bearing Pressure P = W by 2 r L]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Step 8: Coefficient f = F_f by W]
    nodeI --> nodeJ([Output: T and f for KTU Verification])
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Reynolds Equation Conceptual Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Continuity Equation] --> node2[Navier Stokes Thin Film]
    node2 --> node3[Integrate Twice Across Film]
    node3 --> node4[Apply No Slip Boundary]
    node4 --> node5[Obtain Velocity Profile u of y]
    node5 --> node6[Integrate to Get Flow q of x]
    node6 --> node7[Apply Steady State dq by dx equals 0]

```

    nodeB --> nodeC[Yes: Use EHL Regime]

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between static friction and kinetic friction. State Coulomb's laws of dry friction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Static Friction ($F_s$) | Kinetic Friction ($F_k$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Resistance offered *before* motion starts | Resistance during *continued* motion |
| Magnitude | $F_s = \mu_s N$ (variable, 0 to $\mu_s N$) | $F_k = \mu_k N$ (constant) |
| Value | Always $\geq$ kinetic friction | Slightly less than static |

**Coulomb's Laws:**
1. Friction force is **independent of apparent contact area**.
2. Friction force is **proportional to the normal reaction** $N$.
3. Kinetic friction is **independent of sliding velocity** (within a moderate range).

*(Examiner weight: 1 mark for the comparison table, 2 marks for the laws.)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define viscosity. Distinguish between dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. State their units.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Viscosity** is the property of a fluid that offers resistance to deformation (flow). It arises from internal friction between fluid layers.

- **Dynamic Viscosity (η):** Ratio of shear stress to shear rate.
$$\eta = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{F/A}{dv/dy} \quad [\text{SI unit: Pa·s or N·s/m²}]$$
- **Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** Dynamic viscosity divided by fluid density.
$$\nu = \frac{\eta}{\rho} \quad [\text{SI unit: m²/s; practical: cSt}]$$

**Key relationship:** $\eta = \rho \cdot \nu$

*(Examiner weight: 1 mark definition, 1 mark each distinction.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PEPET862]**

**(a)** Derive **Petroff's equation** for the friction torque of a fully lubricated journal bearing. State all assumptions clearly. (7 marks)

**(b)** A journal bearing has the following specifications: journal radius $r = 50$ mm, radial clearance $c = 0.1$ mm, length $L = 100$ mm, rotational speed $N = 1500$ rpm, lubricant viscosity $\eta = 0.05$ Pa·s, and load $W = 10$ kN. Calculate:
- (i) Friction torque $T$
- (ii) Coefficient of friction $f$
- (iii) Power loss in kW (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Petroff's Equation Derivation (7 marks):**

*(See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation. The student must reproduce: assumptions (2 marks) → velocity gradient (1 mark) → shear stress (1 mark) → friction force (1.5 marks) → torque (1.5 marks).)*

Final expression:
$$T = \frac{4\pi^{2}\eta N r^{3} L}{c}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $r = 0.05$ m, $c = 0.0001$ m, $L = 0.1$ m, $N = 1500/60 = 25$ rev/s, $\eta = 0.05$ Pa·s, $W = 10000$ N.

**(i) Friction torque:**
$$T = \frac{4\pi^{2} \times 0.05 \times 25 \times (0.05)^{3} \times 0.1}{0.0001}$$

Step-by-step numerator:
$$4\pi^{2} = 39.478$$
$$0.05 \times 25 = 1.25$$
$$(0.05)^{3} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$1.25 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} = 1.5625 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$\times 0.1 = 1.5625 \times 10^{-5}$$
$$\times 39.478 = 6.169 \times 10^{-4}$$

Divide by $c = 10^{-4}$:
$$\boxed{T = 6.169 \text{ N·m}}$$

**(ii) Coefficient of friction:**
$$f = \frac{2\pi^{2} r}{c} \cdot \frac{\eta N}{P}$$

First compute bearing pressure:
$$P = \frac{W}{2rL} = \frac{10000}{2 \times 0.05 \times 0.1} = \frac{10000}{0.01} = 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 1 \text{ MPa}$$

Then:
$$\frac{2\pi^{2} r}{c} = \frac{2 \times 9.8696 \times 0.05}{0.0001} = \frac{0.987}{0.0001} = 9870$$

$$\frac{\eta N}{P} = \frac{0.05 \times 25}{10^{6}} = 1.25 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$f = 9870 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-6} = 0.01234$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 0.0123}$$

**(iii) Power loss:**
$$P_{loss} = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot 2\pi N$$
$$P_{loss} = 6.169 \times 2\pi \times 25 = 6.169 \times 157.08$$
$$\boxed{P_{loss} = 968.8 \text{ W} \approx 0.969 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper PEPET862]**

**(a)** Explain the different **regimes of lubrication** with the help of the Stribeck curve. Discuss the role of **specific film thickness λ** in regime identification. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 50 mm diameter shaft runs in a 50.05 mm diameter bearing of length 75 mm, lubricated with oil of dynamic viscosity $\eta = 0.06$ Pa·s, at 2400 rpm, supporting a load of 8 kN. The composite surface roughness is $\sigma = 0.5$ μm and the minimum film thickness is $h_{min} = 1.2$ μm. Determine the **regime of lubrication** and the **Petroff coefficient of friction**. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Lubrication Regimes & Stribeck Curve (7 marks):**

*(Refer to §2.3 and §4.1 of the notes.)*

The Stribeck curve plots friction coefficient $\mu$ (y-axis) against the dimensionless bearing parameter $\eta N / P$ (x-axis). Three distinct regions appear:

1. **Boundary Lubrication** (left, high $\mu$): Surfaces in direct asperity contact; $\lambda < 1$. Friction controlled by surface films and EP additives.

2. **Mixed Lubrication** (descending portion): $1 \leq \lambda \leq 3$. Both asperity contact and fluid pressure share the load.

3. **Hydrodynamic Lubrication** (right, low $\mu$): $\lambda > 3$. Full fluid film separates surfaces; friction is purely viscous.

The specific film thickness:
$$\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sigma} = \frac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{\sigma_1^{2} + \sigma_2^{2}}}$$
determines the regime and is the *engineering benchmark* for safe bearing design.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 0.050$ m, $r = 0.025$ m, $c = (50.05 - 50)/2 \times 10^{-3} = 0.025 \times 10^{-3} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m, $L = 0.075$ m, $N = 2400/60 = 40$ rev/s, $\eta = 0.06$ Pa·s, $W = 8000$ N, $h_{min} = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\sigma = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m.

**Regime identification:**
$$\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sigma} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-6}}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.4$$

Since $1 < 2.4 < 3$ → **Mixed Lubrication regime**.

**Petroff's coefficient of friction:**
$$P = \frac{W}{2rL} = \frac{8000}{2 \times 0.025 \times 0.075} = \frac{8000}{0.00375} = 2.133 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$

$$f = \frac{2\pi^{2} r}{c} \cdot \frac{\eta N}{P}$$

Compute each factor:
$$\frac{2\pi^{2} r}{c} = \frac{2 \times 9.8696 \times 0.025}{2.5 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.4935}{2.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 19740$$

$$\frac{\eta N}{P} = \frac{0.06 \times 40}{2.133 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{2.4}{2.133 \times 10^{6}} = 1.125 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$f = 19740 \times 1.125 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0222$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 0.0222}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks in Friction & Lubrication problems:**
> 1. **Unit conversion mistake** — Forgetting to convert $N$ from **rpm to rev/s** (divide by 60). This single error costs **2 to 3 marks** in any Petroff problem.
> 2. **Mixing up $r$ and $D$** — Petroff's equation uses **radius** $r$, not diameter $D$. A factor-of-2 slip here propagates everywhere.
> 3. **Confusing $c$ with $h$** — The radial clearance $c$ in Petroff's equation is the *static* concentric gap, not the operating film thickness $h_{min}$ (those are equal only at zero eccentricity).
> 4. **Forgetting assumptions in derivations** — A Petroff derivation without the *five explicit assumptions* loses at least **2 marks** in Part B.
> 5. **Mis-stating Reynolds equation** — The 1-D form is $\frac{d}{dx}(h^{3}\frac{dp}{dx}) = 6\eta U \frac{dh}{dx}$, **not** the simplified Bernoulli form. Examiners check signs.
> 6. **Wrong friction angle** — The friction angle is measured from the **normal**, not the surface. $\tan \phi = \mu$ — students often write $\tan \phi = 1/\mu$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Friction** is tangential resistance; governed by Coulomb's three laws.
- [ ] **Coefficient of friction** $\mu = F/N$; **Friction angle** $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$.
- [ ] **Static friction** > **Kinetic friction** at the point of slip initiation.
- [ ] **Rolling friction** is one to two orders of magnitude lower than sliding friction.
- [ ] **Lubrication** has four functions: friction reduction, wear prevention, heat removal, corrosion protection.
- [ ] **Four lubrication regimes:** Boundary, Mixed, Hydrodynamic, Elastohydrodynamic (EHL).
- [ ] **Specific film thickness** $\lambda = h_{min}/\sigma$ identifies the regime:
  - $\lambda < 1$ → Boundary
  - $1 \leq \lambda \leq 3$ → Mixed
  - $\lambda > 3$ → Hydrodynamic
- [ ] **Petroff's torque:** $T = 4\pi^{2}\eta N r^{3} L / c$
- [ ] **Petroff's coefficient of friction:** $f = (2\pi^{2} r / c)(\eta N / P)$
- [ ] **Bearing pressure:** $P = W / (2rL)$
- [ ] **Reynolds equation (1-D):** $\frac{d}{dx}(h^{3}\frac{dp}{dx}) = 6\eta U \frac{dh}{dx}$
- [ ] **Viscosity relation:** $\eta = \rho \nu$
- [ ] **Sommerfeld number:** $S = \eta N / P$ — central dimensionless group in HD bearing design.
- [ ] **Surface engineering link:** Coatings (TiN, DLC, MoS₂) target the boundary regime; viscosity control targets the HD regime.

---

*End of Module 1 — Friction and Lubrication | PEPET862 | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fatigue
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fatigue

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Fatigue**
> Fatigue is defined as the progressive, localized, and permanent structural damage or failure of a material subjected to **cyclic or fluctuating loads** at stress levels considerably below the ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of the material. It is a mode of failure characterized by the initiation and propagation of cracks under repetitive loading, ultimately culminating in sudden fracture, often without significant macroscopic plastic deformation.

### 1.2 Critical Loading Parameters

In KTU 2024 Scheme, fatigue analysis is rooted in five primary parameters:

- **Maximum Stress ($\sigma_{max}$)** — the highest algebraic value of cyclic stress.
- **Minimum Stress ($\sigma_{min}$)** — the lowest algebraic value of cyclic stress.
- **Stress Range ($\Delta \sigma$)** — the algebraic difference: $\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$.
- **Stress Amplitude ($\sigma_a$)** — half the stress range: $\dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$.
- **Mean Stress ($\sigma_m$)** — the average stress: $\dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$.

The **Stress Ratio (R)** is also a crucial parameter:

$$R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$$

> [!NOTE] **Common Loading Conditions in KTU Problems**
> - **Fully Reversed Loading**: $R = -1$ (e.g., rotating shaft).
> - **Zero-to-Tension Loading**: $R = 0$ (e.g., tensile test with cycling).
> - **Static Loading**: $R = 1$.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Paperclip Paradigm

Imagine bending a paperclip back and forth. Initially, it requires a small force, but the clip remains intact. After 10–20 bends, you may hear a small "tick" — a microscopic crack has initiated. After 30–50 bends, the clip suddenly snaps in two, even though the bending force never exceeded a small fraction of the force needed to pull it apart in pure tension.

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight**
> The paperclip never experienced a single "high" stress event. It failed purely due to **cyclic sub-failure stress**. This is the essence of fatigue: a silent, invisible, and progressive killer of engineering components, responsible for approximately **90% of all mechanical failures in metallic components** (Schijve, classic data range cited widely in materials science literature).

### 1.4 The Three Stages of Fatigue (Qualitative Overview)

| Stage | Name | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| **1** | **Crack Initiation** | Micro-plastic deformation at stress concentrators (surface defects, inclusions, grain boundaries) nucleates a microscopic crack. Occupies a large fraction of total life in high-cycle fatigue. |
| **2** | **Crack Propagation** | The crack advances incrementally with each cycle, following crystallographic planes initially (Stage I) and then macroscopic planes perpendicular to tensile stress (Stage II). |
| **3** | **Final Fracture** | When the crack reaches a critical size ($a_c$), the remaining cross-section cannot support the load, leading to sudden, often catastrophic, ductile or brittle overload fracture. |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Endurance Limit (Fatigue Limit)**
> For many ferrous alloys and titanium alloys, the **S-N curve (Wöhler curve)** becomes horizontal below a certain stress, termed the **endurance limit** or **fatigue limit** ($\sigma_e$ or $\sigma_{-1}$). Below this stress, the material theoretically has **infinite life**. Non-ferrous metals (aluminum, copper) do **not** exhibit a true endurance limit; instead, a **fatigue strength at a specified number of cycles** (e.g., $10^7$ or $10^8$) is quoted.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve) — The Heart of Fatigue Design

The **S-N Curve** plots cyclic stress amplitude ($\sigma_a$ or $\sigma_{max}$) on the y-axis (log or linear) against the number of cycles to failure ($N_f$) on the x-axis (logarithmic).

> [!IMPORTANT] **Basquin's Law (High-Cycle Fatigue Regime)**
> In the high-cycle fatigue (HCF) regime, the S-N relationship is linear on a log-log plot, governed by Basquin's Equation:
> $$\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N_f)^b$$
> where $\sigma_f'$ is the **fatigue strength coefficient** (≈ true fracture stress) and $b$ is the **fatigue strength exponent** (typically $-0.05$ to $-0.12$ for metals).

### 2.2 Coffin-Manson Relation (Low-Cycle Fatigue Regime)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Coffin-Manson Equation**
> In the low-cycle fatigue (LCF) regime (typically $N_f < 10^4$ cycles), plastic strain dominates. The fatigue life is related to the plastic strain amplitude ($\Delta \varepsilon_p / 2$):
> $$\frac{\Delta \varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon_f' (2N_f)^c$$
> where $\varepsilon_f'$ is the **fatigue ductility coefficient** and $c$ is the **fatigue ductility exponent** (typically $-0.5$ to $-0.7$).

### 2.3 Total Strain-Life Approach (Manson-Coffin-Basquin)

The total strain amplitude is the sum of elastic and plastic components:

$$\frac{\Delta \varepsilon}{2} = \frac{\Delta \varepsilon_e}{2} + \frac{\Delta \varepsilon_p}{2} = \frac{\sigma_f'}{E}(2N_f)^b + \varepsilon_f'(2N_f)^c$$

### 2.4 Paris Law — Crack Propagation Kinetics

> [!IMPORTANT] **Paris-Erdogan Law**
> The rate of fatigue crack growth per cycle ($da/dN$) in the stable propagation region (Stage II) follows:
> $$\frac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m$$
> where $\Delta K$ is the **stress intensity factor range**, $C$ and $m$ are material constants (typically $m = 2$ to $7$ for metals), and:
> $$\Delta K = Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$
> with $Y$ being the **geometry factor**.

### 2.5 Mean Stress Corrections — The Trinity of Fatigue Design

When the mean stress is non-zero, the alternating stress capacity is reduced. The three classical KTU-recommended correction lines are:

| Criterion | Equation | Conservatism |
|-----------|----------|--------------|
| **Soderberg** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y} = 1$ | Most conservative (uses yield) |
| **Goodman** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}} = 1$ | Moderately conservative |
| **Gerber** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}}\right)^2 = 1$ | Least conservative (parabolic) |

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Practice**
> For safe design in KTU board examinations, the **Goodman line** is the most widely used. The **Gerber parabola** is mathematically more accurate for ductile materials but is rarely used in critical aerospace design where conservatism is paramount.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $R = \sigma_{min} / \sigma_{max}$ | Loading characterization | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min})/2$ | Alternating stress | MPa |
| 3 | $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min})/2$ | Mean stress | MPa |
| 4 | $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N_f)^b$ | Basquin (HCF) | MPa |
| 5 | $\Delta \varepsilon_p/2 = \varepsilon_f' (2N_f)^c$ | Coffin-Manson (LCF) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $da/dN = C (\Delta K)^m$ | Paris crack growth | m/cycle |
| 7 | $\Delta K = Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Stress intensity range | MPa$\sqrt{m}$ |
| 8 | $\sigma_a / \sigma_e + \sigma_m / \sigma_{UTS} = 1$ | Goodman correction | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\sigma_a / \sigma_e + (\sigma_m / \sigma_{UTS})^2 = 1$ | Gerber correction | dimensionless |
| 10 | $K_{IC} = Y \sigma_c \sqrt{\pi a_c}$ | Critical fracture toughness | MPa$\sqrt{m}$ |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: Wing spars, turbine blades, and landing gear are designed for $10^7$–$10^9$ cycles using S-N curves.
- **Automotive**: Crankshafts undergo combined bending and torsion fatigue.
- **Bridges**: Designed for variable amplitude loading using Miner's rule for cumulative damage.
- **Power Plants**: Steam and gas turbine blades face thermomechanical fatigue (creep-fatigue interaction).

> [!NOTE] **Miner's Rule (Palmgren-Miner Linear Damage Rule)**
> $$\sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i} = 1$$
> where $n_i$ is the number of cycles applied at stress level $i$, and $N_i$ is the cycles to failure at that level. Failure is predicted when the sum equals 1. This is a critical KTU 2024 topic for variable amplitude loading.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Stress Range, Amplitude, and Mean Stress

**Given:**
$$\sigma_{max} \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_{min}$$

**Step 1:** Define the stress range as the algebraic difference between maximum and minimum stress.

$$\Delta \sigma = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$$

**Step 2:** The stress amplitude is defined as half of the stress range, since the stress oscillates equally above and below the mean.

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\Delta \sigma}{2} = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

**Step 3:** The mean stress is the arithmetic average of the maximum and minimum stress.

$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Verify by solving back: adding the two expressions.

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_a + \sigma_m &= \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} + \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} \\
&= \frac{2\sigma_{max}}{2} = \sigma_{max}
\end{aligned}$$

This confirms the relationship $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_m + \sigma_a$ and $\sigma_{min} = \sigma_m - \sigma_a$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Deriving the Goodman Line Equation

The Goodman criterion assumes a linear relationship between the allowable alternating stress and mean stress, bounded by $\sigma_e$ at $\sigma_m = 0$ and $\sigma_{UTS}$ at $\sigma_a = 0$.

**Step 1:** Consider two boundary points on the Goodman diagram:

- Point A: $\sigma_a = \sigma_e$, $\sigma_m = 0$ (fully reversed loading limit).
- Point B: $\sigma_a = 0$, $\sigma_m = \sigma_{UTS}$ (static strength limit).

**Step 2:** Apply the two-point form of a straight line.

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\sigma_a - \sigma_e}{0 - \sigma_e} &= \frac{\sigma_m - 0}{\sigma_{UTS} - 0} \\
\frac{\sigma_a - \sigma_e}{-\sigma_e} &= \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply both sides by $-\sigma_e$.

$$\sigma_a - \sigma_e = -\frac{\sigma_e \cdot \sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}}$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to obtain the standard form.

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}} = 1$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Deriving Basquin's Law from the S-N Curve

**Step 1:** On a log-log plot, the S-N curve is linear in the HCF regime. Therefore:

$$\log \sigma_a = \log \sigma_f' + b \log(2N_f)$$

**Step 2:** Exponentiating both sides.

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_f' \cdot (2N_f)^b$$

This is Basquin's equation. For typical steels, $b \approx -0.1$ and $\sigma_f' \approx \sigma_{UTS}$.

**Step 3:** At the endurance limit $\sigma_a = \sigma_e$ and $N_f = N_e$ (typically $10^6$ cycles):

$$\sigma_e = \sigma_f' (2 \times 10^6)^b$$

**Step 4:** Solving for the number of cycles to failure at a given stress amplitude:

$$N_f = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_f'}\right)^{1/b}$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Paris Law Integration for Fatigue Life

**Step 1:** The Paris law relates crack growth rate to the stress intensity range.

$$\frac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m = C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi a})^m$$

**Step 2:** Separate variables for integration.

$$da \cdot a^{-m/2} = C (Y \Delta \sigma)^m \pi^{m/2} \cdot dN$$

**Step 3:** Integrate from the initial crack size $a_i$ to the critical crack size $a_c$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{a_i}^{a_c} a^{-m/2} \, da &= C (Y \Delta \sigma)^m \pi^{m/2} \int_{0}^{N_f} dN \\
\left[\frac{a^{1 - m/2}}{1 - m/2}\right]_{a_i}^{a_c} &= C (Y \Delta \sigma)^m \pi^{m/2} N_f
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** For $m \neq 2$ (typical for metals), the solution is:

$$N_f = \frac{a_c^{1 - m/2} - a_i^{1 - m/2}}{C (Y \Delta \sigma)^m \pi^{m/2} (1 - m/2)}$$

This expression is the **fatigue life** prediction in terms of crack propagation duration.

### 3.5 Worked Example: Goodman Correction for a Shaft

**Problem:** A steel shaft has $\sigma_e = 350 \text{ MPa}$ and $\sigma_{UTS} = 600 \text{ MPa}$. It is subjected to a fully reversed bending stress of $\pm 200 \text{ MPa}$ superimposed on a steady torsion-derived axial mean stress of $100 \text{ MPa}$. Using the Goodman criterion, determine the factor of safety.

**Step 1:** Identify the loading.

$$\sigma_a = 200 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_m = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2:** For a factor of safety $N$, the Goodman equation becomes:

$$\frac{N \sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{N \sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}} = 1$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$\begin{aligned}
N \left(\frac{200}{350} + \frac{100}{600}\right) &= 1 \\
N (0.5714 + 0.1667) &= 1 \\
N (0.7381) &= 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $N$.

$$N = \frac{1}{0.7381} = 1.355$$

**Result:** The factor of safety is **1.355**. Since this is less than the typical design threshold of 1.5–2.0, the shaft would need to be redesigned.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Fatigue Failure Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Cyclic Load Application] --> B[Stage 1: Crack Initiation]
    B --> B1[Microplastic deformation at stress concentrators]
    B1 --> B2[Persistent slip band formation]
    B2 --> B3[Micronotch at surface]
    B3 --> C[Stage 2: Crack Propagation]
    C --> C1[Stage IIa: Crystallographic growth]
    C1 --> C2[Stage IIb: Macroscopic plane growth]
    C2 --> C3[Paris Law: da/dN = C DeltaK to the m]
    C3 --> D{Critical Crack Size Reached?}
    D -->|No| E[Continue Cycling]
    E --> C
    D -->|Yes| F[Stage 3: Final Fracture]
    F --> G[K_IC exceeded]
    G --> H[Component Failure]
    H --> I[End]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fatigue Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Loading Data] --> B[Load Parameter Calculator]
    B --> B1[Compute sigma_a]
    B --> B2[Compute sigma_m]
    B --> B3[Compute R ratio]
    B1 --> C{S-N Data Available?}
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C -->|Yes| D[S-N Curve Interpolation]
    C -->|No| E[Apply Mean Stress Correction]
    D --> F[Basquin Law Solver]
    E --> E1[Goodman Line]
    E --> E2[Gerber Parabola]
    E --> E3[Soderberg Line]
    F --> G[Compute Fatigue Life N_f]
    E1 --> G
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    G --> H{Compare with Design Life}
    H -->|N_f greater than design| I[SAFE]
    H -->|N_f less than design| J[REDESIGN]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Description of the S-N Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph S-N CURVE SCHEMATIC
    A0[High Stress Region: Low Cycle Fatigue] --> A1[Basquin Linear Region: HCF]
    A1 --> A2[Endurance Limit: sigma_e]
    A2 --> A3[Below sigma_e: Infinite Life Zone]
    end
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the S-N Curve**
> - **Vertical axis**: Stress amplitude $\sigma_a$ (MPa), often on a linear scale.
> - **Horizontal axis**: Number of cycles to failure $N_f$ (log scale).
> - **Slope**: Steep at high stress (LCF), gradually flattening (HCF), horizontal at the endurance limit (ferrous alloys only).
> - For aluminum alloys, the curve continues to slope downward indefinitely, with $\sigma_f$ quoted at $N_f = 5 \times 10^8$ cycles.

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Three-Regime Fatigue Crack Growth

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Region I: Threshold] -->|da/dN below 10 to the minus 7 mm/cycle| A
    B[Region II: Paris Regime] -->|da/dN = C DeltaK to the m| B
    C[Region III: Unstable Growth] -->|K_max approaches K_IC| C
    A --> B --> C
    C --> D[Fast Fracture]
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Three-Region Crack Growth Model**
> - **Region I (Near-Threshold)**: $\Delta K_{th} \approx 3$ to $10 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{m}$ for steels. Below this threshold, cracks do not grow.
> - **Region II (Stable Paris Regime)**: $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$. Predictable, design-relevant region.
> - **Region III (Unstable)**: $K_{max} \to K_{IC}$, growth becomes rapid and unstable.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define fatigue failure. List any two characteristics of fatigue failure in metallic materials.**

**Model Answer:**
