It is used to: - Determine the **corrosion mechanism** (activation-controlled vs diffusion-controlled). - Extract **Tafel slopes** from experimental polarization data. - Compute the **corrosion current density** by extrapolating the linear Tafel region to the corrosion potential. **Polarization Resistance Calculation (3 marks):** The Stern–Geary equation is: $$R_p = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c) \cdot i_{corr}}$$ Substituting the values: $$R_p = \dfrac{(0.06)(0.12)}{2.303 \times (0.06 + 0.12) \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$= \dfrac{0.0072}{2.303 \times 0.18 \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$= \dfrac{0.0072}{4.1454 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$\boxed{R_p \approx 173.7 \;\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Mixing up units of Tafel slope:** Some students treat $\beta$ in mV/decade without conversion. Here $\beta$ is given in V/decade, so no further conversion is needed. > 2. **Confusing $R_p$ (polarization resistance) with electrical resistance $R$:** $R_p$ has units of $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$ (resistance × area), not $\Omega$. > 3. **Forgetting to multiply the current by 2.303 in the denominator** — this is the most common algebraic error in the Stern–Geary equation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary — Memorize Before Every KTU Exam** - [ ] **Four elements** of the electrochemical cell: Anode, Cathode, Electrolyte, Metal path. *Remove any one and corrosion stops.* - [ ] **Anode reaction** is always **oxidation** ($\text{M} \to \text{M}^{n+} + n e^{-}$). **Cathode reaction** is always **reduction** (O₂ reduction in neutral aerated water is the most common in practice). - [ ] **Standard constants** at 25 °C: $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, $2.303\,RT/F = 0.0591$ V. - [ ] **Faraday's Second Law** — the *single most tested* equation: $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$. - [ ] **Nernst Equation** — predicts electrode potential under non-standard conditions: $E = E^\circ + \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log(a_{Ox}/a_{Red})$. - [ ] **Spontaneous corrosion** requires $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode} > 0$ **AND** $\Delta G = -nFE_{cell} < 0$. - [ ] **Tafel Equation** — $\eta = a \pm \beta \log i$ — governs **kinetics** of charge transfer; Tafel slope $\beta$ is in V/decade. - [ ] **Stern–Geary Equation** — links polarization resistance to corrosion current: $i_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c) R_p}$. - [ ] **EMF Series** = thermodynamic, single-ion; **Galvanic Series** = practical, real-environment. **Always prefer the Galvanic Series** for engineering. - [ ] **Pourbaix Diagram** has three regions: **Immunity** (no corrosion), **Corrosion** (metal dissolves), **Passivation** (protective oxide film). - [ ] **Mixed Potential Theory (Wagner–Traud):** The actual corrosion rate is set by the **intersection** of the anodic and cathodic Tafel lines on an E vs log i plot, *not* by the thermodynamic cell voltage alone. - [ ] **Corrosion rate conversion shortcuts:** $K = 0.00327$ for mm/yr (with $i$ in μA/cm²) and $K = 1.287 \times 10^5$ for mpy (with $i$ in A/cm²). - [ ] **Cathodic protection** exploits the anodic sacrifice of a more active metal (Zn or Mg) coupled to steel. - [ ] **High $i_0$ = kinetically noble** (resistant to polarization). **Low $i_0$ = kinetically active** (corrodes readily). - [ ] **Common KTU traps:** sign errors in Nernst; forgetting the 2.303 factor; using molarity instead of activity; mixing Tafel and Nernst equations.

topics.title: Forms of Corrosion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Forms of Corrosion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Aligned)

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, **Forms of Corrosion** are formally defined as the **morphologically and mechanistically distinct manifestations** by which the electrochemical degradation of metals (and in some cases ceramics and polymers) presents itself on engineering components and structures. Each form is governed by a unique combination of **electrochemical driving force, metallurgical susceptibility, environmental chemistry, and mechanical loading conditions**.

According to the classical eight-form taxonomy (originally codified by Fontana and later refined by Fontana & Greene, and adopted universally in KTU Module 2), the forms are:

1. **Uniform (General) Corrosion**
2. **Pitting Corrosion**
3. **Crevice Corrosion**
4. **Galvanic (Two-Metal) Corrosion**
5. **Intergranular Corrosion**
6. **Selective Leaching (Dealloying)**
7. **Erosion-Corrosion**
8. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)**

Additional recognized forms included in KTU Module 2 are **Hydrogen Damage, Exfoliation Corrosion, Cavitation, Fretting Corrosion, and Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must be able to (i) identify each form, (ii) explain the mechanism at the anode-cathode level, (iii) state the controlling metallurgical/environmental variable, and (iv) suggest two engineering prevention methods per form. These four abilities form the basis of all Part-A and Part-B questions on this topic.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **brick wall** that is exposed to rain for years:

- **Uniform corrosion** is like the rain slowly dissolving the mortar evenly across the whole wall. The wall thins everywhere at the same rate — predictable and easy to model.
- **Pitting corrosion** is like a few bricks developing deep drill-holes because a tiny pocket of acidic moisture sat on them. The wall looks fine from a distance, but the holes go deep.
- **Crevice corrosion** is like the wall tucked behind a drainpipe where water stagnates. The hidden, sheltered joint rots first.
- **Galvanic corrosion** is like screwing a copper bracket onto a steel wall. Rainwater bridging them creates a tiny battery — the steel sacrifices itself to protect the copper.
- **Stress corrosion cracking** is like a hairline crack in a load-bearing wall that suddenly runs across because the wall was both wet and under tension.
- **Intergranular corrosion** is like the mortar between bricks dissolving while the bricks themselves remain intact — only the grain boundaries of the metal are attacked.

This "brick-wall" mental model maps directly onto the eight forms and is the **first abstraction a KTU student must internalize**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Labs and Exams:** Corrosion rate is universally reported in **mils per year (mpy)** in the Imperial system and **millimeters per year (mm/yr)** in the SI system. The conversion factor used in the KTU Formula Sheet is **1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr**.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight Recognized Forms — Structured Logic Breakdown

The table below condenses the KTU-mandated framework. Every cell is **board-exam-defensible**.

| # | Form of Corrosion | Anode / Cathode Geometry | Controlling Variable | Classic Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uniform (General) | Distributed, microscopic | Electrolyte conductivity, temperature | Atmospheric rusting of structural steel |
| 2 | Pitting | Localized, autocatalytic | Cl⁻ ion, breakdown potential (E_b) | Stainless steel tank in seawater |
| 3 | Crevice | Confined geometry | Tightness of gap, O₂ depletion | Flange faces, gasketed joints |
| 4 | Galvanic | Dissimilar metals in electrolyte | Galvanic series position, area ratio | Cu-Al tube joint, bronze prop in steel hull |
| 5 | Intergranular | Grain-boundary path | Cr-depletion at GB (sensitization) | 304 SS welded at 500–800 °C |
| 6 | Selective Leaching | One element of an alloy | Electrochemical nobility of solute | Dezincification of brass |
| 7 | Erosion-Corrosion | Mechanically disrupted film | Flow velocity, particle content | Elbow of a steam line, pump impeller |
| 8 | Stress Corrosion Cracking | Static tensile + specific env. | Stress, environment, susceptible alloy | Brass in ammonia, SS in Cl⁻, mild steel in OH⁻ |

**Additional Forms (Module 2 Supplementary):**

- **Hydrogen Damage:** Hydrogen atoms diffuse into metal, causing blistering, hydrogen embrittlement (HE), or hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC). Critical in sour-service (H₂S) pipelines and high-strength fasteners.
- **Exfoliation:** Severe form of intergranular attack in wrought Al-alloys with elongated grain structure, producing layered "leaf-like" detachment.
- **Cavitation:** Vapor-bubble collapse near a metal surface produces shock pressures that destroy protective films. Common in pump impellers and ship propellers.
- **Fretting Corrosion:** Small relative motion (vibration) between contacting surfaces simultaneously abrades and oxidizes. Found in bolted joints and bearing housings.
- **Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC):** Microbial colonies (e.g., sulfate-reducing bacteria) form biofilms that create differential aeration and acidic micro-environments.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Form

**Form 1 — Uniform Corrosion**
- *Why:* The metal is electrochemically homogeneous and the electrolyte is uniform, so anodic and cathodic sites wander randomly.
- *How:* Faradaic dissolution rate is determined by activation polarization on both half-reactions. Predicted by the **Butler-Volmer equation** in the Tafel region.

**Form 2 — Pitting Corrosion**
- *Why:* Passive films (e.g., Cr₂O₃ on stainless steel) break down locally when the local potential exceeds the **pitting potential (E_pit)**. Cl⁻ is the most aggressive ion.
- *How:* Once a pit nucleates, the interior becomes oxygen-depleted and metal-chlorides concentrate, lowering pH. This autocatalytic chemistry sustains growth.

**Form 3 — Crevice Corrosion**
- *Why:* Oxygen is consumed inside the crevice faster than it can diffuse in. The exterior becomes cathodic; the interior becomes anodic.
- *How:* Differential aeration cell forms. Hydrolysis of metal ions inside the crevice drops pH → autocatalysis.

**Form 4 — Galvanic Corrosion**
- *Why:* Two metals with different standard electrode potentials (E°) are electrically connected in an electrolyte.
- *How:* The more active (anodic) metal dissolves; the more noble (cathodic) metal is protected. Severity ∝ potential difference and inversely ∝ anode-to-cathode area ratio.

**Form 5 — Intergranular Corrosion**
- *Why:* Grain boundaries in some alloys (e.g., 304 SS) become depleted in chromium during sensitization (482–800 °C) due to precipitation of (Cr,Fe)₂₃C₆.
- *How:* GBs with Cr < 12% cannot sustain passivity, so they become anodic paths while the grain interior remains cathodic.

**Form 6 — Selective Leaching**
- *Why:* One element of an alloy is electrochemically less noble and preferentially dissolves.
- *How:* In brass, Zn atoms leave the lattice, leaving a porous Cu skeleton. Same for Cu-Al alloys (dealuminification) and Cu-Ni (denickelification).

**Form 7 — Erosion-Corrosion**
- *Why:* High-velocity fluid or solid particles mechanically strip the protective film faster than it can reform.
- *How:* Rate is a strong function of velocity (often ~v^n with n = 2 to 3). Distinctive horseshoe-shaped or gull-wing grooving at impingement points.

**Form 8 — Stress Corrosion Cracking**
- *Why:* Susceptible alloy + specific environment + sustained tensile stress. The "SCC triangle" — remove any one vertex, SCC stops.
- *How:* Either anodic dissolution at the crack tip (slip-dissolution) or hydrogen-induced cracking mechanism.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** Within all markdown tables below, the vertical pipe `\|` symbol (used in math for absolute value or conditionals) is replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking table syntax.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Corrosion rate (Imperial) | $\text{mpy} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | mils/yr | Weight-loss method |
| 2 | Corrosion rate (SI) | $\text{mm/yr} = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | mm/yr | Weight-loss method (A in cm²) |
| 3 | Faraday's Law | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | grams | Metal mass dissolved |
| 4 | Pitting Factor | $\text{PF} = \dfrac{\text{deepest pit depth}}{\text{average penetration}}$ | dimensionless | Severity of pitting |
| 5 | PREN (Pitting Resistance Equiv. No.) | $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \cdot \%\text{Mo} + 16 \cdot \%\text{N}$ | dimensionless | Stainless steel ranking |
| 6 | Galvanic potential difference | $\Delta E = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$ | V (SHE) | Driving force |
| 7 | Corrosion current density | $i_{\text{corr}} = \dfrac{i_{\text{corr,a}} \cdot A_{\text{a}}}{A_{\text{c}}}$ | A/cm² | Galvanic current |
| 8 | Tafel Equation | $\eta = a + b \log i$ | V | Activation overpotential |
| 9 | Oxygen diffusion limit (crevice) | $i_{\text{L}} = \dfrac{n F D C_{\text{O}_2}}{\delta}$ | A/m² | Crevice growth rate |
| 10 | Charge on electron | $F = 96{,}485$ | C/mol | Faraday constant |

**Variable Glossary for the Formula Sheet:**

- $W$ = mass loss (mg)
- $D$ = density of metal (g/cm³)
- $A$ = exposed area (in² for mpy, cm² for mm/yr)
- $t$ = exposure time (hours)
- $I$ = current (A)
- $M$ = molar mass (g/mol)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol** (bolded as KTU mandated constant)
- $D$ (in #9) = diffusion coefficient
- $C_{\text{O}_2}$ = dissolved oxygen concentration
- $\delta$ = diffusion layer thickness

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These eight forms are not academic curiosities — they govern **failure analysis, materials selection, and insurance risk assessment** across:

- **Oil & Gas:** SCC in sour service (NACE MR0175), MIC in water injection lines, pitting in offshore subsea structures.
- **Aerospace:** Exfoliation in 2xxx and 7xxx Al-alloy wing skins, SCC in 7075-T6, hydrogen embrittlement in landing gear steel.
- **Power Generation:** Erosion-corrosion in boiler tubes, SCC of stainless condensers, pitting in wet-dry transition zones of cooling towers.
- **Marine:** Galvanic corrosion at bronze-aluminum prop-shaft connections, crevice corrosion at hull-rudder interfaces, cavitation on propellers.
- **Chemical Process:** Selective leaching in heat-exchanger brass tubes, intergranular attack in sensitized 304 SS, uniform corrosion in acid storage tanks.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Corrosion Rate Formula (mpy)

The corrosion rate in mpy is derived from Faraday's law combined with the geometric relationship between the dissolved mass and the equivalent thickness of metal removed.

**Starting Point — Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis:**

The mass of metal dissolved by a current $I$ flowing for time $t$ is:

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 1 — Convert mass to volume:**
Since $D$ is density (g/cm³), the volume of metal dissolved is:

$$V = \frac{m}{D} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot D}$$

**Step 2 — Convert volume to a uniform thickness over exposed area $A$:**
Average penetration depth $\delta$ (in cm) is:

$$\delta = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot D \cdot A}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mils (1 cm = 393.7 mil, 1 year = 8760 hours):**
Multiply by 393.7 mil/cm and divide by 8760 hr/yr:

$$\text{mpy} = \delta \cdot \frac{393.7}{8760} \cdot \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot D \cdot A}$$

The constant becomes:

$$K = \frac{393.7 \cdot 60 \cdot 60}{8760 \cdot 1000} \cdot \frac{M}{n} = 0.129 \cdot \frac{M}{n}$$

For a given alloy, the constant 534 emerges when expressed in **weight-loss (mg) over a fixed time (hr)** form:

$$\boxed{\text{mpy} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}}$$

where $W$ is in mg, $D$ in g/cm³, $A$ in in², $t$ in hr.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Corrosion Rate of Mild Steel in H₂SO₄

**Problem Statement:** A mild steel coupon of area $A = 5$ in² and density $D = 7.86$ g/cm³ is immersed in 10% H₂SO₄ for $t = 48$ hours. The mass loss is $W = 540$ mg. Calculate the corrosion rate in mpy and mm/yr, and comment on the severity.

**Step 1 — Substitute into the mpy formula:**

$$\text{mpy} = \frac{534 \cdot 540}{7.86 \cdot 5 \cdot 48}$$

**Step 2 — Numerator evaluation:**

$$534 \cdot 540 = 288{,}360 \text{ mg·unit}$$

**Step 3 — Denominator evaluation:**

$$7.86 \cdot 5 \cdot 48 = 1886.4$$

**Step 4 — Division:**

$$\text{mpy} = \frac{288{,}360}{1886.4} = 152.9 \text{ mpy}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mm/yr using 1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr:**

$$\text{mm/yr} = 152.9 \cdot 0.0254 = 3.88 \text{ mm/yr}$$

**Step 6 — Severity classification (KTU Reference Table):**

| Corrosion Rate (mpy) | mm/yr | Rating |
|---|---|---|
| $\lt 1$ | $\lt 0.025$ | Outstanding |
| $1 - 5$ | $0.025 - 0.13$ | Excellent |
| $5 - 20$ | $0.13 - 0.51$ | Good |
| $20 - 50$ | $0.51 - 1.27$ | Fair |
| $50 - 200$ | $1.27 - 5.08$ | Poor |
| $\gt 200$ | $\gt 5.08$ | Unacceptable |

Our result of **152.9 mpy** falls in the **"Poor"** band — the steel is unsuitable for this service without inhibitors or a protective coating.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Pitting Factor for an Aluminum Tank

**Problem Statement:** An aluminum tank exhibits deepest pit depth = 1.2 mm. The average uniform penetration from weight-loss data is 0.15 mm. Compute the pitting factor and classify.

**Step 1 — Recall the pitting factor formula:**

$$\text{PF} = \frac{\text{deepest pit depth}}{\text{average penetration}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\text{PF} = \frac{1.2}{0.15} = 8.0$$

**Step 3 — Interpret using KTU convention:**

| Pitting Factor | Severity |
|---|---|
| 1 | Uniform attack |
| 2 - 6 | Moderate pitting |
| $\gt 6$ | Severe pitting |

A value of **8.0** indicates **severe pitting** — the tank is likely unfit for pressure service.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Form by Diagnostic Clues

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU Part-A knowledge block** — examiners expect students to recognize the form from visual or contextual clues.

| Diagnostic Clue | Likely Form |
|---|---|
| Uniform thinning across entire surface | Uniform (General) |
| Localized deep holes on horizontal surfaces | Pitting |
| Attack only under gaskets, lap joints, fasteners | Crevice |
| Two dissimilar metals joined, attack on one side | Galvanic |
| Attack confined to weld HAZ | Intergranular |
| Reddish or color change, porous structure | Selective Leaching |
| Horseshoe-shaped grooves at pipe bends | Erosion-Corrosion |
| Branching cracks at right angles to tensile stress | SCC |
| Crater-like pits on impeller blades | Cavitation |
| Brown powder at bolted joints | Fretting Corrosion |
| Disbonded layers in Al-alloy plates | Exfoliation |
| Stepwise cracks in sour-service pipe | Hydrogen-Induced Cracking |
| Black FeS deposits in water system | MIC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Series:** Any metal coupled with a metal **below** it in the series becomes the anode and corrodes. The further apart the two metals in the series, the more aggressive the galvanic attack. **Area ratio matters:** a small anode area coupled to a large cathode area drastically accelerates corrosion of the anode.

### 4.4 SCC Triangle — Removal of Any One Vertex Stops SCC

```mermaid
graph TD
    sccA["Vertex 1 Susceptible Alloy"]
    sccB["Vertex 2 Specific Environment"]
    sccC["Vertex 3 Sustained Tensile Stress"]
    sccCore["SCC Event Center"]
    sccA --> sccCore
    sccB --> sccCore
    sccC --> sccCore
```

**Engineering implication:** To mitigate SCC, the engineer must attack at least one of the three vertices — e.g., change the alloy, change the environment (de-aerate, dehumidify, add inhibitors), or reduce/eliminate tensile stress (shot peening, compressive residual stress, lower applied load).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (Direct Recall)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define uniform corrosion. State one method each to (i) predict its rate in the laboratory and (ii) prevent it in service.*

**Model Answer:**

Uniform corrosion is the form in which electrochemical attack proceeds at approximately the same rate over the entire exposed surface of a metal, producing a roughly uniform reduction in thickness. The anodic and cathodic sites shift randomly and cannot be distinguished at the macro-scale.

(i) **Prediction in the laboratory:** the **weight-loss coupon method** is used. A polished, weighed coupon is exposed to the service environment for a fixed time, cleaned, re-weighed, and the rate is computed using the mpy formula.

(ii) **Prevention in service:** apply a **barrier coating** (paint, epoxy, polymer, or metallic coating such as galvanizing) so that the electrolyte never reaches the base metal. Other methods include cathodic protection, use of inhibitors, and selection of a more corrosion-resistant alloy.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for method of prediction, 1 mark for prevention.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Distinguish between pitting corrosion and crevice corrosion. Mention one diagnostic visual feature of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Pitting Corrosion | Crevice Corrosion |
|---|---|---|
| Initiation site | Free surface where passive film breaks down | Inside a tight gap (gasket, bolt, lap joint) |
| Driving mechanism | Autocatalytic Cl⁻ attack on a single point | Differential aeration cell across the gap |
| Geometry requirement | None; happens on exposed surfaces | Requires a crevice geometry (gap < 1 mm typical) |
| Diagnostic visual feature | Discrete, deep holes with bright interior and dark mouth | Attack confined to the gap region, sharp boundary between attacked and unattacked zones |

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for distinction, 1 mark for diagnostic feature.)*

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice, KTU ESE Pattern)

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Module 2 Choice A]**

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of **galvanic corrosion** with a neat sketch of a Cu–Zn couple immersed in seawater. Derive the expression for the corrosion current using the **mixed potential theory**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A copper tube is joined to a steel flange in a seawater line. The exposed steel area is 20 cm² and the copper area is 400 cm². If the galvanic current density on the steel is 8 µA/cm², compute the **mass loss of steel per year** and the resulting **average penetration depth in mm/yr**. Atomic mass of Fe = 55.85, n = 2, density = 7.86 g/cm³. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

When Cu and Zn are electrically connected and immersed in an electrolyte, they form a **short-circuited galvanic cell**. The **more active metal (Zn)** becomes the **anode** and dissolves, while the **more noble metal (Cu)** becomes the **cathode** where the reduction reaction (O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻ in aerated seawater) occurs.

The mixed-potential theory states that when two metals are coupled, the system adopts a single **mixed potential $E_{\text{mix}}$** at which the **total anodic current equals the total cathodic current**:

$$I_{\text{a,total}}(E_{\text{mix}}) = I_{\text{c,total}}(E_{\text{mix}})$$

For the Zn anode and Cu cathode:

$$I_{\text{Zn,anode}}(E_{\text{mix}}) = I_{\text{O}_2,\text{cathode}}(E_{\text{mix}})$$

Using the linearized polarization form $I = I_0 \left( \exp \frac{\alpha F \eta}{RT} - \exp \frac{-(1-\alpha)F\eta}{RT} \right)$, one obtains for the coupled system:

$$E_{\text{mix}} = \frac{I_{0,\text{c}} E_{\text{a}} + I_{0,\text{a}} E_{\text{c}}}{I_{0,\text{a}} + I_{0,\text{c}}}$$

The corresponding galvanic current is:

$$\boxed{I_{\text{galv}} = \frac{(E_{\text{c}} - E_{\text{a}})}{\dfrac{RT}{F}\left(\dfrac{1}{I_{0,\text{c}}} + \dfrac{1}{I_{0,\text{a}}} \right)}}$$

**Sketch description (KTU expects a labeled diagram):**
- Two metal electrodes (Zn on left, Cu on right) immersed in a vessel of seawater.
- Salt bridge or direct metallic contact (wire) between them.
- Labels: Anode (Zn, dissolution: Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻), Cathode (Cu, reduction: O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻), electron flow in wire, ion flow in electrolyte.

*(Allocation: 2 marks mechanism, 3 marks derivation, 2 marks sketch and labeling.)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute total galvanic current:**

$$I_{\text{galv}} = i_{\text{anode}} \cdot A_{\text{anode}} = 8 \cdot 10^{-6} \text{ A/cm}^2 \cdot 20 \text{ cm}^2 = 1.6 \cdot 10^{-4} \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total charge passed in one year:**

$$Q = I \cdot t = 1.6 \cdot 10^{-4} \text{ A} \cdot 365 \cdot 24 \cdot 3600 \text{ s} = 1.6 \cdot 10^{-4} \cdot 3.154 \cdot 10^{7} = 5046 \text{ C}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Faraday's law to compute mass of Fe dissolved:**

$$m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{5046 \cdot 55.85}{2 \cdot 96{,}485}$$

$$m = \frac{281{,}820}{192{,}970} = 1.461 \text{ g/yr}$$

**Step 4 — Compute volume and average penetration depth:**

$$V = \frac{m}{D} = \frac{1.461}{7.86} = 0.1859 \text{ cm}^3/\text{yr}$$

$$\delta = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{0.1859}{20} = 9.29 \cdot 10^{-3} \text{ cm/yr} = 0.0929 \text{ mm/yr} \approx 3.66 \text{ mpy}$$

**Step 5 — Comment on severity:**

The unfavorable **area ratio (1 : 20)** makes the corrosion of steel severe. In design, the anode (steel) area should be made as large as possible relative to the cathode (copper) to dilute the anodic current density. A non-conductive gasket or isolation flange would be the recommended prevention.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for current, 1 mark for charge, 2 marks for mass, 1 mark for depth, 2 marks for interpretation and design recommendation.)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Module 2 Choice B]**

**(a)** Describe the **mechanism, characteristic features, and prevention methods** of **intergranular corrosion** in austenitic stainless steel. Why is the 304 SS particularly susceptible, and how is the problem solved by using 304L or 321 grades? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 304 SS pipe carrying hot nitric acid developed cracking along the weld heat-affected zone. (i) Identify the form of corrosion. (ii) Explain the **sensitization theory** with relevant equations. (iii) Suggest **two engineering prevention methods**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Mechanism:**
Austenitic stainless steels (e.g., 304 SS containing ~18% Cr, 8% Ni) are protected by a thin, passive Cr₂O₃ film. When the steel is held in the temperature range **482–800 °C** (e.g., during welding, stress relief, or slow cooling), **chromium carbide (Cr,Fe)₂₃C₆** precipitates preferentially at the **grain boundaries**. This precipitation depletes the grain-boundary region of Cr. If the local Cr content falls below the **12% threshold** required to maintain passivity, the grain boundary becomes **anodic** and the grain interior (still Cr-rich) becomes **cathodic**. The resulting **galvanic microcell** causes the grain boundary to be attacked preferentially, leading to loss of mechanical strength — sometimes with grains literally falling out, a phenomenon called **"wastage" or "sugaring."**

**Characteristic features:**
- Visible only after sectioning; surface often shows a "sugary" texture on bending.
- Attack is most severe in the **heat-affected zone (HAZ)** of welds.
- Mechanical strength (toughness, ductility) is severely reduced even when surface looks intact.

**Prevention:**

1. **Reduce carbon content below 0.03%** (this is what **304L** does) — insufficient carbon is available to precipitate Cr-carbide.
2. **Stabilize the carbides** with Ti or Nb (this is what **321 / 347** grades do) — Ti and Nb form their own carbides (TiC, NbC) preferentially, leaving Cr in solution.
3. **Solution anneal** at 1050–1100 °C followed by rapid quenching — dissolves the carbides and re-establishes a homogeneous Cr distribution.
4. Use a low-heat-input welding process to minimize time in the sensitization range.

**Why 304 SS is susceptible:**
The standard 304 grade has 0.08% max carbon, which is more than enough to form Cr-carbides when heated slowly through the sensitization window.

**Why 304L and 321 work:**

- **304L:** Carbon ≤ 0.03% → thermodynamically insufficient C to tie up Cr as Cr₂₃C₆.
- **321:** Ti added (Ti ≥ 5 × %C) → Ti preferentially forms TiC, sparing Cr.

*(Allocation: 2 marks mechanism, 2 marks features, 3 marks prevention and alloying reasoning.)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Form of corrosion: Intergranular corrosion (also called "weld decay" in this context).**

**(ii) Sensitization theory with equations:**

At grain boundaries in the 482–800 °C range, the following precipitation reaction occurs:

$$23 \text{Cr} + 6 \text{C} \rightarrow \text{Cr}_{23}\text{C}_6$$

The equilibrium Cr concentration profile drops sharply near the grain boundary. A zone of width ~1 µm adjacent to the boundary is depleted to Cr < 12%. This Cr-depleted zone is **active**, while the grain interior is **passive** — the micro-galvanic cell thus set up dissolves the GB region. The half-reactions are:

$$\text{Anode (GB):} \quad \text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$$

$$\text{Cathode (grain):} \quad \text{O}_2 + 2 \text{H}_2 \text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4 \text{OH}^-$$

**(iii) Engineering prevention methods:**

1. **Re-grade to 304L or 321 SS** (low carbon or Ti-stabilized) — eliminates the root cause.
2. **Solution anneal at 1050 °C** followed by water quench — dissolves carbides and locks Cr in solution.
3. Use a **stabilizing anneal at 900 °C** — transforms the carbides to a more benign form.
4. **Control heat input during welding** to keep time in the sensitization window short.

*(Allocation: 1 mark identification, 3 marks sensitization equations, 3 marks prevention methods.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing intergranular corrosion with SCC** — both involve cracks, but IGC follows the grain boundary, while SCC cracks are transgranular and branching. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the 12% Cr threshold** when explaining sensitization — this number is the literal KTU answer key checkpoint.
> 3. **Mixing up mpy and mm/yr constants (534 vs. 87.6)** — using the wrong constant is a 2-mark loss.
> 4. **In galvanic corrosion problems, ignoring the area ratio** — the **anode : cathode area ratio** is the most heavily weighted design parameter in 14-mark questions.
> 5. **Omitting units in numerical answers** — answers without units are penalized 0.5 mark per quantity.
> 6. **Drawing vague SCC or pitting schematics** — examiners expect a labeled diagram with anode/cathode regions explicitly marked.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Eight forms:** Uniform, Pitting, Crevice, Galvanic, Intergranular, Selective Leaching, Erosion-Corrosion, SCC — memorize all eight with one-line mechanism each.
- **Autocatalytic pitting:** Cl⁻ migrates in, H⁺ from hydrolysis, oxygen-depleted pit interior — three-part recipe that examiners love to test.
- **Crevice:** requires a **tight gap + electrolyte + differential aeration**.
- **Galvanic rule:** the **less noble metal corrodes**; the further apart in the galvanic series, the worse; **small anode : large cathode = catastrophic**.
- **Intergranular (sensitization):** 304 SS + 482–800 °C exposure + Cr < 12% at GB → **Cr₂₃C₆ precipitates → Cr-depleted zone → IGC**.
- **Sensitization fix:** 304L (low C) OR 321 (Ti-stabilized) OR solution anneal.
- **Selective leaching:** classic example = **dezincification of brass** (Zn dissolves, porous Cu remains).
- **Erosion-corrosion:** function of **velocity^n** with n = 2 to 3; preventive step is **lowering velocity or using harder alloys**.
- **SCC triangle:** **Susceptible alloy + Tensile stress + Specific environment**; remove any vertex → SCC stops.
- **Hydrogen damage:** includes HE (embrittlement), HIC (induced cracking), and HBl (blistering); occurs in **sour service (H₂S)** and during **cathodic protection over-protection**.
- **MIC:** produces **tubercles and FeS deposits**; treated with **biocides, chlorination, or coatings**.
- **mpy formula:** $\dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ — memorize with **variable meanings** (mg, g/cm³, in², hr).
- **Pitting Factor = deepest pit ÷ average penetration** — > 6 = severe.
- **PREN = %Cr + 3.3·%Mo + 16·%N** — higher = more pitting-resistant.
- **Faraday constant $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol** — the most-tested constant.
- **Cavitation:** vapor-bubble collapse → shock → film destruction → pitting on impellers.
- **Fretting:** vibration + load → oxide-debris abrasion → characteristic brown powder.
- **Exfoliation:** Al-alloys with elongated grains → layered peeling — common in aerospace skins.
- **In numerical answers,** always carry **units** to the final step; examiners deduct 0.5 mark/unit missing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion Prevention and Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** METALLURGY & MATERIALS SCIENCE / CHEMICAL ENGINEERING / MECHANICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Corrosion Prevention and Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrosion Prevention and Control** is the systematic application of engineering principles, material selection strategies, electrochemical design, and surface modification techniques to **minimize, inhibit, or completely eliminate the degradation of metals and alloys** due to chemical or electrochemical reactions with their environment. In the KTU 2024 scheme, this encompasses the **fourth and final pillar** of the corrosion study sequence: (1) thermodynamics, (2) kinetics, (3) forms of corrosion, and (4) mitigation.

In more rigorous electrochemical language, corrosion prevention aims to manipulate one or more of the following fundamental variables that govern the **anodic half-reaction**:

$$M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^{-}$$

The goal is to shift the **open-circuit potential (E_corr)** in the **noble (positive) direction**, or to reduce the **corrosion current density (i_corr)**, thereby lowering the rate of metal dissolution.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Painting" Model 🎨

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine your house is made of iron (Fe).** Rain, humidity, and salty coastal air are the "bullets" trying to destroy it. **Corrosion prevention is exactly the strategy you adopt to protect your house:**

| Real-World Action (House) | Corrosion Engineering Equivalent | Mechanism Type |
|---|---|---|
| Painting the walls | Protective coatings (paint, polymer, enamel) | **Barrier Protection** |
| Using a zinc-coated "sacrificial" layer on the roof (galvanized sheets) | Sacrificial anode (Zn, Mg, Al) | **Cathodic Protection (Galvanic)** |
| Installing an external power supply to drive a small current | Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) | **Cathodic Protection (Impressed)** |
| Choosing a stainless steel door instead of mild steel | Alloying (Cr, Ni additions) | **Material Selection / Alloying** |
| Adding "anti-rust" chemicals in boiler water | Corrosion inhibitors (chromates, phosphates) | **Environmental Modification** |
| Keeping rainwater away by proper slope and drainage | Design considerations (no crevices, smooth flow) | **Design-Based Control** |

**Intuition:** Just as a homeowner would *combine* multiple strategies (paint + slope + better material), a corrosion engineer layers multiple protection methods — no single technique is universally perfect.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Constants Used Throughout Corrosion Calculations:**
> - **Faraday's Constant (F) = 96,485 C/mol** (≈ 96,500 C/mol in engineering approximations)
> - **Corrosion rate constant (K) = 87.6** (for mm/year) and **K = 534** (for mils per year, mpy)
> - **Standard reference area unit = 1 m²** (SI) or **1 in² / 1 ft²** (Imperial)
> - **Time convention: T in hours** (most KTU numerical problems)

**Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) in Corrosion Engineering:**
- **Corrosion Rate (CR)** — measured in **mm/year**, **mpy (mils per year)**, or **g/m²·day**
- **Protection Efficiency (P.E.)** — expressed as a **%**
- **Inhibitor Efficiency (I.E.)** — expressed as a **%**
- **Cathodic Protection Current Density** — measured in **mA/m²** or **A/ft²**

---

## 1.4 Classification Overview of Prevention Methods

Corrosion prevention techniques are broadly classified into **five master categories**, as per the standard KTU 2024 syllabus framework:

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Material Selection & Alloying** — Choosing inherently corrosion-resistant metals
> 2. **Design Modifications** — Engineering geometry to avoid corrosion-prone conditions
> 3. **Surface Coatings** — Barrier layers (organic, inorganic, metallic)
> 4. **Electrochemical Protection** — Cathodic & anodic protection
> 5. **Environmental Control** — Inhibitors, dehumidification, fluid treatment

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Method 1 — Material Selection and Alloying

### Conceptual Logic:
- The cheapest, most elegant form of prevention is **choosing a metal that does not want to corrode** in the given environment.
- This is governed by the **Galvanic Series in the specific environment** (NOT the standard EMF series, since EMF applies only to pure metals in standard solutions).

### Key Engineering Examples:
- **Stainless Steel (Fe-Cr-Ni):** Cr ≥ 10.5% forms a passive **Cr₂O₃** film → self-healing barrier
- **Ti & Ti-alloys:** Form **TiO₂** passive film — used in marine, biomedical, aerospace
- **Ni-Cr alloys (Inconel):** Used in high-temperature oxidation resistance
- **Cu-Ni alloys:** Excellent for seawater heat exchangers
- **Brass, Bronze:** Dezincification is a risk → **inhibited brasses** (with As, Sn, Sb) are used

### The "Why" — Governing Thermodynamic Principle:
A metal with a **high (noble) standard reduction potential** is thermodynamically less prone to oxidation. However, passivity (kinetic barrier) is often more important than nobility (thermodynamic tendency).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** "Explain why aluminum is more corrosion resistant than iron despite being more electrochemically active."  
> **Answer:** Aluminum forms a **thin, adherent, self-repairing Al₂O₃ passive film** which acts as a kinetic barrier. The Pilling-Bedworth ratio for Al/Al₂O₃ is ≈ 1.28 (between 1 and 2), giving a **protective, non-spalling oxide** — unlike Fe/Fe₂O₃ (PBR ≈ 2.15) which spalls off.

---

## 2.2 Method 2 — Design-Based Corrosion Control

### Structured Engineering Principles (KTU High-Yield):

| Design Rule | Physical Reasoning |
|---|---|
| **Avoid crevices** (weld instead of bolt) | Crevices cause differential aeration → oxygen starvation inside → anode forms inside |
| **Avoid galvanic contact** between dissimilar metals (or use insulation gaskets) | Two metals in electrolyte → galvanic cell forms → less noble metal corrodes rapidly |
| **Provide drainage and slope** (≥ 15°) | Standing water pools ions → accelerates attack |
| **Avoid sharp corners and stagnant zones** | Sharp corners concentrate stress + electrolyte → SCC risk |
| **Allow easy inspection access** | Hidden corrosion = catastrophic failure |
| **Round edges, use fillet welds** | Reduces stress concentration → reduces SCC, fatigue |
| **Avoid small anode-to-large cathode ratio** | Small anode area → high anodic current density → rapid penetration |

---

## 2.3 Method 3 — Protective Coatings (Metallic, Inorganic, Organic)

### 2.3.1 Metallic Coatings
Two sub-categories based on electrochemical behavior:

**(a) Anodic (Sacrificial) Coatings** — e.g., **Galvanizing (Zn on Fe), Cd on steel**
- Coating metal is **MORE ACTIVE** than base metal
- If scratched → coating acts as sacrificial anode → **protects base metal even at the scratch**
- **Application:** Roofing sheets, automotive bodies, transmission towers

**(b) Cathodic (Noble) Coatings** — e.g., **Tin plating (Sn on Fe), Cu, Ni, Cr on steel**
- Coating metal is **MORE NOBLE** than base metal
- If scratched → base metal becomes anode → **corrosion ACCELERATES at scratch** (dangerous!)
- **Application:** Food cans (tin — non-toxic), decorative chrome (Ni+Cr)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students often confuse which is which. **Mnemonic:** *"Sacrificial = Sacrificial lamb = coating dies to save base"* → coating is more active (Zn on Fe).  
> *Noble coating = protection by barrier ONLY; once breached, the base metal corrodes faster.*

### 2.3.2 Inorganic Coatings
- **Phosphating (Parkerizing):** Steel dipped in dilute H₃PO₄ + Mn/Fe salts → **Zn₃(PO₄)₂ or Fe₃(PO₄)₂ coating** — used as paint base
- **Chromating / Anodizing:** Al → Al₂O₃ layer (anodized); improves corrosion + paint adhesion
- **Enameling:** Glass-like coating (silica-based) fused at 700-850°C — used in cookware, chemical reactors

### 2.3.3 Organic Coatings (Paints)
- **Three-layer system:** Primer (anti-corrosive) + Undercoat (build-up) + Topcoat (UV, weather)
- **Primer types:** Red lead (Pb₃O₄), Zinc chromate, Epoxy primers
- **Mechanism:** Barrier + sacrificial (in Zn-rich primers) + electrochemical (inhibitive pigments)

---

## 2.4 Method 4 — Cathodic Protection (CP) ⭐ (KTU Highest-Weightage)

This is the **single most important topic for KTU 14-mark questions**. Two methods exist:

### 2.4.1 Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP)
- A more active metal (Zn, Mg, Al) is **electrically connected** to the structure to be protected
- The active metal **sacrifices itself** by corroding preferentially
- **No external power** required
- **Applications:** Ship hulls, buried pipelines (small/medium), offshore platforms
- **Anode materials:**
  - **Zn** → soil resistivity < 3000 Ω·cm, seawater
  - **Mg** → high-resistivity soils (> 3000 Ω·cm)
  - **Al alloys (Al-Zn-Hg, Al-Zn-In)** → marine, offshore

### 2.4.2 Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)
- An **external DC power source** drives current from an inert anode (Pt, graphite, Ti-MMO, scrap steel) to the structure
- The structure is made the **cathode** (negative terminal)
- **Applications:** Long pipelines, water tanks, marine structures, ship hulls, reinforced concrete
- **Advantages over SACP:**
  - Adjustable current → flexible
  - Single anode can protect very large areas
  - Suitable for high-resistivity environments

### 2.4.3 Electrochemical Criterion of Cathodic Protection
The structure must be polarized to a potential more negative than the **protection potential**:
- **For Fe in soil/water:** E ≤ **−850 mV vs. Cu/CuSO₄ reference electrode** (most common criterion)
- **Alternative criterion:** A **100 mV** negative shift from the native E_corr

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overprotection Warning:** Pushing potential too negative → **cathodic disbonding** of coatings, **hydrogen embrittlement** of high-strength steel, and excessive power waste.

---

## 2.5 Method 5 — Anodic Protection (AP) & Inhibitors

### 2.5.1 Anodic Protection
- Used for **metals that PASSIVATE** (Fe, Ni, Cr, Ti, stainless steels)
- The structure is connected to the **positive terminal** of a DC source → **passive region** is established on the anodic polarization curve
- **Used for:** Sulfuric acid storage tanks, HNO₃ vessels, fertilizer industry
- **Limitation:** Only works where passivity is achievable

### 2.5.2 Corrosion Inhibitors (Chemical Additives)
Substances added in small concentrations to the corrosive environment to reduce the corrosion rate.

| Inhibitor Type | Example | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Anodic (passivating)** | Chromates, nitrites, molybdates, phosphates | Form protective oxide film at anodic sites |
| **Cathodic** | Zn²⁺, polyphosphates, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ | Precipitate as hydroxides/phosphates at cathodic sites, blocking O₂ reduction |
| **Mixed** | Amines, imidazolines, thiourea | Adsorb on metal surface, block both reactions |
| **Vapor-phase (VPI)** | Dicyclohexylamine nitrite (DICHAN), cyclohexylamine carbonate | Volatile; protect enclosed spaces (shipped equipment) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Danger:** Insufficient concentration of an **anodic inhibitor** can cause **localized pitting** — the inhibitor forms an incomplete film, creating a tiny anode with massive cathodic area → catastrophic penetration. **Anodic inhibitors are "dangerous inhibitors"** in the words of classical texts.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet 📋

> [!NOTE]
> **The following equations are HIGH-FREQUENCY in KTU examinations. Memorize the constants and the unit conversion logic.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Weight-loss corrosion rate (SI)** | $CR = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ | **mm/year** | K = 87.6 when W in mg, A in cm², T in hr |
| 2 | **Weight-loss corrosion rate (Imperial)** | $CR = \dfrac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ | **mpy** | K = 534 when W in mg, A in in², T in hr |
| 3 | **Corrosion rate (g/m²·day)** | $CR = \dfrac{24 \times 1000 \times W}{A_{m^2} \times T_{hr}}$ | **g/m²·day** | Direct mass-loss form |
| 4 | **Faraday's Law (mass corroded)** | $W = \dfrac{I \times t \times M}{n \times F}$ | **grams** | I in A, t in sec, M in g/mol, F = 96,485 C/mol |
| 5 | **Current from corrosion rate** | $I = \dfrac{n \times F \times W}{M \times t} = \dfrac{n \times F \times D \times CR \times A}{M \times K}$ | **Amps** | Used in CP design |
| 6 | **Inhibitor Efficiency (IE)** | $IE = \dfrac{CR_{uninhibited} - CR_{inhibited}}{CR_{uninhibited}} \times 100$ | **%** | Higher = better |
| 7 | **Protection Efficiency (PE)** | $PE = \dfrac{W_{unc} - W_{prot}}{W_{unc}} \times 100$ | **%** | Same logic as IE |
| 8 | **Anode Life (SACP)** | $L = \dfrac{m \times u \times \eta}{I \times 8760}$ | **years** | m = anode mass, u = utilization factor, η = efficiency |
| 9 | **Total CP current requirement** | $I_{total} = i_{cd} \times A_{surface}$ | **Amps** | i_cd = design current density (A/m² or mA/ft²) |
| 10 | **Pilling-Bedworth Ratio** | $PBR = \dfrac{M_{oxide} \times \rho_{metal}}{n \times M_{metal} \times \rho_{oxide}}$ | dimensionless | PBR 1–2 = protective; <1 or >2 = non-protective |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Oil & Gas Industry:** Buried pipelines protected by **ICCP + 3-layer PE coating** → service life 30+ years
- **Marine:** Ship hulls use **SACP with Al anodes** (typically replaced every 3-5 years during dry-docking)
- **Civil Engineering:** **Cathodic prevention** of rebar in concrete bridges using Ti-MMO anodes embedded in concrete
- **Power Plants:** Boiler water treated with **hydrazine (N₂H₄)** and **morpholine** to scavenge O₂ and control pH
- **Aerospace:** **Cadmium plating** of high-strength steel fasteners combined with **chromate conversion coatings**
- **Automotive:** **Electrocoating (e-coat)** of car bodies — uniform primer deposition by electrophoresis

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Corrosion Rate from Faraday's Law (Worked)

### Starting Premise (KTU Foundation):
Faraday's Law states that the mass of metal dissolved at the anode is **directly proportional** to the total electric charge (Q = I·t) passed:

$$W = \frac{Q \times M}{n \times F} = \frac{I \times t \times M}{n \times F}$$

### Step 1: Express the Current in Terms of Corrosion Current Density
The current is the product of corrosion current density and the exposed area:

$$I = i_{corr} \times A$$

where:
- $i_{corr}$ = corrosion current density (A/cm² or A/m²)
- $A$ = exposed surface area

### Step 2: Substitute into Faraday's Equation

$$W = \frac{i_{corr} \times A \times t \times M}{n \times F}$$

### Step 3: Express Corrosion Current Density Using Dimensional Analysis
The current density relates directly to the **linear penetration rate (CR)** via the density and Faraday volume:

$$i_{corr} = \frac{n \times F \times D \times CR}{M \times K_{conv}}$$

### Step 4: Derive the Engineering Constant K
To get CR in **mm/year** when W is in **mg**, A in **cm²**, t in **hours**, and D in **g/cm³**, dimensional analysis gives:

$$K = \frac{10 \times 365.25 \times 24}{1 \times 1000} = \frac{87660}{1000} \approx 87.6$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{CR \left(\frac{mm}{year}\right) = \frac{87.6 \times W \, (mg)}{D \, (g/cm^3) \times A \, (cm^2) \times T \, (hr)}}$$

### Step 5: Derive the Imperial Constant K = 534
For CR in **mpy** (mils per year) with W in mg, A in in², T in hr:

$$K = \frac{0.03937 \times 365.25 \times 24 \times 1000}{1} \approx 534$$

$$\boxed{CR \, (mpy) = \frac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times T}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification example:** For Fe, n = 2, M = 55.85 g/mol, D = 7.87 g/cm³.  
> 1 Faraday = 96,485 C dissolves 55.85/2 = 27.925 g of Fe = 27,925 mg.  
> A current of 1 mA/cm² for 1 year would dissolve a calculable mass — this matches the **K = 87.6** value used globally.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem #1 — Cathodic Protection Design (KTU Standard)

> **Problem Statement:**  
> A buried steel pipeline of **diameter 30 cm** and **length 500 m** is to be protected by the **sacrificial anode method** in soil of resistivity 1500 Ω·cm.  
> Given:  
> - Design current density, $i_{cd} = 10 \, mA/m^2$  
> - Zinc anode: dimensions 35 mm × 35 mm × 1500 mm, density = 7.13 g/cm³  
> - Utilization factor, u = 0.85; Electrochemical efficiency, η = 0.95  
> - Anode output: 5.5 A·hr/g (for Zn)

### Step 1: Calculate the Surface Area to be Protected

$$A = \pi \times D \times L = \pi \times 0.30 \times 500$$

$$A = 471.24 \, m^2$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Total Current Required

$$I_{total} = i_{cd} \times A = 10 \times 10^{-3} \times 471.24$$

$$I_{total} = 4.7124 \, A$$

### Step 3: Calculate Mass of ONE Anode

$$V_{anode} = 3.5 \, cm \times 3.5 \, cm \times 150 \, cm = 1837.5 \, cm^3$$

$$m_{anode} = V \times \rho = 1837.5 \times 7.13 = 13,101.4 \, g = 13.10 \, kg$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Useful Mass Available

$$m_{useful} = m_{anode} \times u \times \eta$$

$$m_{useful} = 13,101.4 \times 0.85 \times 0.95 = 10,579.4 \, g$$

### Step 5: Calculate the Total Charge One Anode Can Deliver

$$Q_{anode} = m_{useful} \times \text{specific capacity} = 10,579.4 \times 5.5 = 58,186.6 \, A \cdot hr$$

### Step 6: Calculate the Lifetime of One Anode

$$L = \frac{Q_{anode}}{I_{total} \times 8760 \, (hr/yr)}$$

$$L = \frac{58,186.6}{4.7124 \times 8760} = \frac{58,186.6}{41,280.6}$$

$$\boxed{L \approx 1.41 \, \text{years}}$$

### Step 7: Practical Engineering Decision
Since 1.41 years is short, increase anode size, use multiple anodes in parallel, or shift to **ICCP** for long-term economy.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem #2 — Inhibitor Efficiency

> **Problem Statement:**  
> A mild steel specimen of area 30 cm² is exposed to 1M H₂SO₄ for 24 hours. Without inhibitor, the mass loss is 180 mg. With 0.5 g/L of an organic inhibitor, the mass loss is 22 mg. Calculate the **inhibitor efficiency (IE)**.

### Step 1: Recall the IE Formula

$$IE = \frac{W_{uninhibited} - W_{inhibited}}{W_{uninhibited}} \times 100$$

### Step 2: Substitute Values

$$IE = \frac{180 - 22}{180} \times 100 = \frac{158}{180} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{IE = 87.78\%}$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Corrosion Rates (Additional Insight)

Without inhibitor:

$$CR_0 = \frac{87.6 \times 180}{7.87 \times 30 \times 24} = \frac{15,768}{5,666.4} = 2.78 \, \frac{mm}{year}$$

With inhibitor:

$$CR_i = \frac{87.6 \times 22}{7.87 \times 30 \times 24} = \frac{1,927.2}{5,666.4} = 0.34 \, \frac{mm}{year}$$

### Step 4: Engineering Interpretation
The inhibitor reduces the corrosion rate from 2.78 mm/yr (severe) to 0.34 mm/yr (moderate). This is **above the 80% benchmark** for effective industrial inhibitors and warrants pilot-scale testing.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SACP vs. ICCP

| Parameter | SACP (Sacrificial Anode CP) | ICCP (Impressed Current CP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Power source** | None (galvanic action) | External DC rectifier (AC→DC) |
| **Driving voltage** | 0.2 – 0.5 V (open circuit) | Adjustable, up to 50 V |
| **Current capacity** | Limited (mA range) | Wide (up to hundreds of A) |
| **Suitable for** | Small structures, low-resistivity environments | Large structures, high-resistivity soils |
| **Anode material** | Zn, Mg, Al (consumed) | Ti-MMO, graphite, scrap steel, Pt-clad |
| **Anode life** | Short (months – few years) | Long (10-20+ years) |
| **Maintenance** | Frequent replacement | Periodic rectifier check |
| **Installation cost** | Low | High |
| **Operating cost** | High (anode replacement) | Low (electricity) |
| **Overprotection risk** | Low | Higher (requires monitoring) |
| **Interference with nearby structures** | Minimal | Stray current may affect adjacent pipelines |
| **KTU common confusion** | "Anode is positive" | "Anode is positive to power source, but is connected to + terminal of DC supply, which forces current to flow TO the cathode" |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "in ICCP, the anode is the protected structure." **This is WRONG.** In ICCP, the **inert auxiliary anode** is the actual anode, and the **structure to be protected is the cathode** (connected to the negative terminal of the DC source).

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR)

### Physical Premise:
When a metal oxidizes, the volume of oxide produced may be **smaller, equal, or larger** than the metal consumed. This determines whether the oxide film is **protective** (compressive stress, adherent) or **non-protective** (tensile stress, spalls off).

### Definition:

$$PBR = \frac{V_{oxide \, per \, mole \, of \, metal}}{V_{metal \, per \, mole \, of \, metal}} = \frac{M_{oxide} / \rho_{oxide}}{n \times M_{metal} / \rho_{metal}}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{PBR = \frac{M_{oxide} \times \rho_{metal}}{n \times M_{metal} \times \rho_{oxide}}}$$

### Application Examples:
- **Al/Al₂O₃:** PBR ≈ 1.28 → **protective** ✓
- **Cu/Cu₂O:** PBR ≈ 1.67 → **protective** ✓
- **Fe/Fe₂O₃:** PBR ≈ 2.15 → **non-protective (spalls)** ✗
- **Mg/MgO:** PBR ≈ 0.81 → **non-protective (porous)** ✗

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Hierarchy of Corrosion Prevention Methods

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CORROSION PREVENTION AND CONTROL] --> B[METHOD 1: Material Selection]
    A --> C[METHOD 2: Design Modification]
    A --> D[METHOD 3: Protective Coatings]
    A --> E[METHOD 4: Electrochemical Protection]
    A --> F[METHOD 5: Environmental Control]

    B --> B1[Alloying: Cr, Ni, Mo additions]
    B --> B2[Stainless Steels]
    B --> B3[Non-ferrous: Ti, Cu-Ni, Bronze]
    B --> B4[Polymers, Ceramics, FRP]

    C --> C1[Avoid Crevices]
    C --> C2[Insulate Dissimilar Metals]
    C --> C3[Provide Drainage]
    C --> C4[Round Corners and Edges]

    D --> D1[Metallic Coatings]
    D --> D2[Inorganic Coatings]
    D --> D3[Organic Coatings]

    D1 --> D1A[Anodic: Zn Galvanizing]
    D1 --> D1B[Cathodic: Sn, Cr Plating]
    D2 --> D2A[Phosphating]
    D2 --> D2B[Anodizing]
    D2 --> D2C[Chromating]
    D3 --> D3A[Primers: Red Lead, Epoxy]
    D3 --> D3B[Topcoats: Polyurethane]

    E --> E1[Cathodic Protection CP]
    E --> E2[Anodic Protection AP]

    E1 --> E1A[Sacrificial Anode SACP]
    E1 --> E1B[Impressed Current ICCP]
    E1A --> E1A1[Zn Anode]
    E1A --> E1A2[Mg Anode]
    E1A --> E1A3[Al Alloy Anode]
    E1B --> E1B1[DC Rectifier]
    E1B --> E1B2[Inert Anode Ti-MMO]
    E1B --> E1B3[Reference Electrode]
    E2 --> E2A[Fe in H2SO4]
    E2 --> E2B[Stainless in HNO3]

    F --> F1[Anodic Inhibitors]
    F --> F2[Cathodic Inhibitors]
    F --> F3[Mixed Inhibitors]
    F --> F4[Vapor Phase Inhibitors]
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Zinc Sacrificial Anode] -->|Connects via wire| B[Steel Structure to Protect]
    B -->|Sits in| C[Soil or Water Electrolyte]
    C -->|Ion conduction path| A
    A -->|Oxidation reaction Zn → Zn2+ + 2e-| A
    B -->|Reduction reaction O2 + 2H2O + 4e- → 4OH-| B
    E[External Power NOT Required] -.->|No| S[System Boundary]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Zn anode** is the **sacrificial element** (oxidizes)
- The **steel** is the **cathode** (where O₂ is reduced — protected!)
- Electron flow: **Zn → steel** (through external wire)
- Ion flow: **Zn²⁺ away from anode; OH⁻/O₂ toward cathode** (through electrolyte)

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[AC Power Supply] --> R[DC Rectifier]
    R -->|Positive terminal| AN[Auxiliary Inert Anode Ti-MMO]
    AN -->|Current flows through| EL[Electrolyte Soil/Water]
    EL --> ST[Steel Structure Cathode Protected]
    ST -->|Negative terminal back to| R
    RE[Reference Electrode Cu-CuSO4] -.->|Monitors potential| ST
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- DC rectifier converts AC → DC
- Auxiliary anode: connected to **+** terminal
- Steel structure: connected to **−** terminal (becomes cathode)
- Reference electrode: continuously monitors E to ensure proper polarization
- The auxiliary anode does NOT corrode (it's an inert MMO coating on Ti substrate)

---

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Protection Method

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{Is the metal inherently corrosion resistant?} -->|Yes| R1[Use as-is]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is the environment highly resistive > 5000 ohm-cm?}
    Q2 -->|No, low resistivity| Q3{Is the structure large > 1000 m2?}
    Q3 -->|No, small structure| SACP[Use SACP: Zn, Mg, Al]
    Q3 -->|Yes, large| Q4{Is long term life > 20 years required?}
    Q4 -->|No, short term| SACP
    Q4 -->|Yes, long term| ICCP[Use ICCP with Ti-MMO anode]
    Q2 -->|Yes, high resistivity| ICCP
```

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Combined Protection Strategy for a Marine Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph L1[External Barrier Layer]
        E[3-Layer Fusion Bonded Epoxy FBE Coating]
    end
    subgraph L2[Electrochemical Backup]
        F[Zinc Bracelet Anodes Attached to Pipe]
        G[External Test Stations for Monitoring]
    end
    subgraph L3[Cathodic Protection Monitoring]
        H[Cu-CuSO4 Permanent Reference Electrodes]
        I[Periodic Close Interval Potential Survey CIPS]
    end
    P[Buried Marine Pipeline Carbon Steel] --> E
    P --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** In practice, ALL major buried/subsea pipelines use **a multi-layered defense** (coating + CP + monitoring). No single method is considered sufficient by international standards (NACE SP0169, ISO 15589).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
> *Differentiate between sacrificial anode cathodic protection and impressed current cathodic protection. List two anode materials used in each method.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | SACP | ICCP |
|---|---|---|
| Power source | Not required (galvanic) | DC rectifier (external) |
| Anode type | Active (consumed): Zn, Mg, Al | Inert: Ti-MMO, graphite, Pt, scrap Fe |
| Driving potential | 0.2–0.5 V | Adjustable up to 50 V |
| Application | Small, low-resistivity environments | Large, high-resistivity environments |
| Cost profile | Low install, high operating (anode replacement) | High install, low operating |
| Anode life | Months to few years | 10–20+ years |

**Anode Materials:**
- SACP: **Zinc** (seawater/soil), **Magnesium** (high-resistivity soil), **Aluminum alloys** (Al-Zn-Hg, Al-Zn-In for marine)
- ICCP: **Titanium with Mixed Metal Oxide (MMO) coating**, **graphite**, **high-silicon cast iron**, **platinum-clad niobium**

---

### **Question 2:** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
> *Explain with examples why chromates are called "dangerous inhibitors." Mention the safe concentration principle.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Chromates (CrO₄²⁻, Cr₂O₇²⁻) are **anodic inhibitors** that function by **oxidizing the metal surface** to form a thin, passive Cr-oxide film. However:

**Why "Dangerous"?**
1. If the **concentration falls below a critical threshold** (typically < 50 ppm in water), the protective film does not form **uniformly**.
2. The film forms at **isolated spots** → the protected zones become **cathodes** while the unprotected areas become **tiny anodes** with surrounding huge cathodic area.
3. The result: **intense, rapid pitting corrosion** — often worse than having no inhibitor at all.

**Examples:** Sodium chromate in cooling water; potassium dichromate in aircraft de-icing fluids (historical use).

**Safe Concentration Principle:**
The concentration must **exceed the critical value** and be **continuously maintained** — never allowed to drop below the threshold. This is why **chromates are being phased out globally** (REACH, RoHS regulations) and replaced with **molybdates, nitrites, or rare-earth (Ce, La) inhibitors**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often give only "it's toxic" as the reason. **Toxicity is environmental; "dangerous" in corrosion science specifically means pitting risk at low concentration.** Mention BOTH reasons for full marks.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With neat schematic diagrams, explain the **cathodic protection of buried pipelines by sacrificial anode method**. Discuss the **cathodic protection criteria** with reference to the Cu/CuSO₄ reference electrode.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A steel pipe of **diameter 25 cm** and **length 2 km** is buried in soil of resistivity 2000 Ω·cm. The **design current density** is **15 mA/m²**. Calculate:
> (i) The total current required for cathodic protection.  
> (ii) The number of zinc anodes needed if each anode delivers **250 mA** and has a useful life of **8 years**.  
> (iii) The total mass of zinc required, given zinc consumption rate is **11.2 kg/A·yr**.

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Schematic:**

```
        Zn Anode            Steel Pipeline
       (Sacrificial)        (Protected Cathode)
           |                    |
           |====wire===========|
           |                    
       [ground]   ← Soil Electrolyte →   [ground]
       
Reactions:
At Zn anode (oxidation):  Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻
At steel surface (reduction):  O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻
```

**Working Principle:**
- The Zn anode (more active) acts as the **anode** of a galvanic cell
- The steel pipeline is forced to act as the **cathode** where only reduction occurs
- Electrons flow from Zn → steel through an insulated copper wire
- The Zn anode is consumed gradually and must be replaced periodically

**Cathodic Protection Criteria** (per NACE SP0169 / KTU accepted standards):
1. **Primary criterion:** A structure-to-soil potential of **−850 mV or more negative** measured against a **Cu/CuSO₄ reference electrode**, with the protective current applied.
2. **Alternative criterion (100 mV shift):** The structure achieves a **negative polarization shift of at least 100 mV** from its native (depolarized) corrosion potential.
3. **No overprotection:** Potential should **not be more negative than −1100 mV** (to avoid hydrogen embrittlement and cathodic disbonding of coatings).
4. **Anodic areas must be eliminated:** Confirmed by depolarization tests.

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Diameter, D = 25 cm = 0.25 m
- Length, L = 2 km = 2000 m
- Design current density, $i_{cd}$ = 15 mA/m² = 0.015 A/m²
- Anode output current = 250 mA = 0.250 A
- Anode life = 8 years
- Zn consumption = 11.2 kg/A·yr

**(i) Total current required:**

$$A = \pi \times D \times L = \pi \times 0.25 \times 2000 = 1570.8 \, m^2$$

$$I_{total} = i_{cd} \times A = 0.015 \times 1570.8$$

$$\boxed{I_{total} = 23.56 \, A}$$

**(ii) Number of anodes:**

Each anode delivers 0.250 A. Total anodes needed:

$$N = \frac{I_{total}}{I_{anode}} = \frac{23.56}{0.250}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 95 \, \text{anodes}}$$

**Verification by life check:**

Total current = 23.56 A for 8 years → Total charge needed = 23.56 × 8760 × 8 = 1,651,084.8 A·hr  
Each anode provides 0.250 × 8760 × 8 = 17,520 A·hr  
Number of anodes = 1,651,084.8 / 17,520 = **94.24 → 95 anodes** ✓

**(iii) Total zinc mass required:**

Using the consumption rate of 11.2 kg/A·yr:

$$M_{Zn} = \text{Consumption rate} \times I_{total} \times L_{years}$$

$$M_{Zn} = 11.2 \times 23.56 \times 8$$

$$\boxed{M_{Zn} = 2,110.98 \, kg \approx 2.11 \, \text{tonnes of zinc}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **(a) [7 Marks]** What are **organic and inorganic inhibitors**? Explain the **mechanism of anodic, cathodic, and mixed inhibitors** with suitable examples. Why are anodic inhibitors called "dangerous inhibitors"?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A mild steel coupon of area **25 cm²** is exposed to **0.5 M H₂SO₄** for **48 hours**. The mass loss without inhibitor is **240 mg**, and with **0.2 g/L of a thiourea derivative** is **18 mg**. Calculate:  
> (i) The corrosion rates (in mm/yr) without and with inhibitor.  
> (ii) The **inhibitor efficiency (IE)** in %.  
> (iii) Comment on whether the inhibitor is effective for industrial use.  
> **Given:** Density of steel = 7.87 g/cm³, K = 87.6.

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Inhibitor Definitions:**
- **Organic inhibitors** are typically N-, S-, or O-containing compounds (amines, imidazolines, thiourea, Schiff bases) that **adsorb on the metal surface** to form a protective hydrophobic film.
- **Inorganic inhibitors** are ionic compounds (chromates, nitrites, phosphates, molybdates) that **react with the metal or electrolyte** to form protective films/precipitates.

**Mechanism with Examples:**

| Type | Mechanism | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Anodic inhibitor** | Oxidizes the metal surface to form a passive oxide film, blocking anodic dissolution | Na₂CrO₄, NaNO₂, Na₂MoO₄, Na₂WO₄ |
| **Cathodic inhibitor** | Precipitates as insoluble hydroxides/phosphates at cathodic sites, blocking O₂ reduction | ZnSO₄, Ca(HCO₃)₂, Na₃PO₄, polyphosphates |
| **Mixed inhibitor** | Adsorbs on the metal surface via polar groups, blocking both anodic and cathodic sites | Amines, imidazolines, thiourea, quinoline derivatives, Schiff bases |

**Why "Dangerous"?**
Anodic inhibitors depend on **complete film coverage**. If concentration falls below the **critical value**, the film forms only on a portion of the surface — the covered areas become **local cathodes** and the **small uncovered zones** become **anodes with massive current density** → **severe pitting corrosion**. The 3 P's of anodic inhibitor danger: **Pitting, Penetration, Perforation**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common mistake is stating "anodic inhibitors are dangerous because they are toxic." **That is an environmental concern, not a corrosion-engineering concern.** In corrosion science, dangerous = pitting risk. Both points are needed for full credit.

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- A = 25 cm², T = 48 hr, D = 7.87 g/cm³, K = 87.6
- W (uninhibited) = 240 mg
- W (inhibited) = 18 mg

**(i) Corrosion rates:**

Without inhibitor:

$$CR_0 = \frac{87.6 \times 240}{7.87 \times 25 \times 48} = \frac{21,024}{9,444}$$

$$\boxed{CR_0 = 2.226 \, \frac{mm}{year}}$$

With inhibitor:

$$CR_i = \frac{87.6 \times 18}{7.87 \times 25 \times 48} = \frac{1,576.8}{9,444}$$

$$\boxed{CR_i = 0.167 \, \frac{mm}{year}}$$

**(ii) Inhibitor Efficiency:**

$$IE = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100 = \frac{2.226 - 0.167}{2.226} \times 100$$

$$IE = \frac{2.059}{2.226} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{IE = 92.5\%}$$

**(iii) Industrial Effectiveness Comment:**

An IE of **92.5% is excellent** — well above the **80% benchmark** typically required for industrial acceptance. The thiourea derivative acts as a **mixed-type organic inhibitor**, adsorbing via its S and N atoms onto the steel surface in acidic medium. However, before industrial deployment, the following must be verified:
1. **Stability** of inhibition efficiency over extended exposure (> 1000 hr tests)
2. **Temperature tolerance** (inhibitors often lose efficiency at > 60°C)
3. **Compatibility** with the process stream
4. **Toxicity and biodegradability** of the inhibitor molecule
5. **Cost per unit volume** treated

If the inhibitor passes all five checks, it qualifies for **pilot-plant trials** and subsequent industrial adoption.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential points to memorize for KTU 2024 exams:**

- ✅ **Faraday's Constant** = **96,485 C/mol** (use 96,500 in approximations)
- ✅ Corrosion rate **mm/yr**: K = **87.6** with W (mg), A (cm²), T (hr)
- ✅ Corrosion rate **mpy**: K = **534** with W (mg), A (in²), T (hr)
- ✅ **Galvanizing (Zn on Fe)** = **sacrificial/anodic** coating — protects even at scratch
- ✅ **Tin plating (Sn on Fe)** = **noble/cathodic** coating — dangerous if scratched (Fe corrodes)
- ✅ **CP criterion for Fe in soil:** E ≤ **−850 mV vs Cu/CuSO₄**; 100 mV shift criterion
- ✅ **Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR):** Protective only if **1 < PBR < 2**
- ✅ **Al** has PBR ≈ 1.28 (protective); **Fe** has PBR ≈ 2.15 (non-protective)
- ✅ **Anodic inhibitors are dangerous** at sub-critical concentration → cause **pitting**
- ✅ **Cathodic protection types:** SACP (Zn, Mg, Al) vs ICCP (Ti-MMO, graphite, scrap Fe)
- ✅ **Anode reaction in CP:** M → M^(n+) + ne⁻ (always oxidation at the anode)
- ✅ **Cathode reaction in CP:** O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻ (in neutral/aerobic environment)
- ✅ **Anodic protection** is used **only for passivating metals** (Fe in H₂SO₄, stainless in HNO₃)
- ✅ **Vapor Phase Inhibitors (VPI)** like DICHAN protect **enclosed spaces** during storage/shipment
- ✅ **Phosphating** (Parkerizing) is the most common **inorganic coating** for steel as a paint base
- ✅ **Three-layer pipeline coating:** FBE + adhesive + PE topcoat
- ✅ **NACE SP0169** and **ISO 15589** are the international standards for CP
- ✅ **Overprotection risks:** hydrogen embrittlement, cathodic disbonding, alkaline attack on coatings
- ✅ **Design rule:** Always avoid **small anode / large cathode** geometry → causes pitting
- ✅ **CP anode life formula:** L = m·u·η / (I × 8760) — for SACP
- ✅ **Total CP current:** I_total = i_cd × A_surface — for design calculations
- ✅ **Industrial benchmark for inhibitor efficiency:** **IE ≥ 80%** for acceptance
- ✅ **Cathodic protection monitoring:** Cu/CuSO₄ reference electrode is **industry standard**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Selection for Corrosion Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRAGATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Material Selection for Corrosion Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Material Selection for Corrosion Environments** is the systematic engineering process of identifying and choosing a construction material (metals, alloys, polymers, ceramics, or composites) whose inherent chemical, electrochemical, mechanical, and economic properties are best suited to withstand a specific corrosive service environment while simultaneously satisfying structural, fabrication, and lifecycle requirements.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned, Outcome-Based Education)**, this topic addresses **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Apply the principles of corrosion science to select materials and protection methods for engineering systems."* The selection is governed by the **Corrosion Design Triangle**: Environment ↔ Material ↔ Design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Material selection is not the picking of a "best" material in absolute terms. It is the optimization of a material for a given **Environment + Stress + Life Expectancy + Cost** combination. A material that is "corrosion-proof" but economically or mechanically unsuitable is an *engineering failure* of selection.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Doctor-Patient Prescription

Imagine a patient walks into a clinic with a specific infection. The doctor does **not** prescribe the "strongest" antibiotic available. Instead, the doctor:

1. **Diagnoses** the environment (the type of bacteria, the patient's age, allergies).
2. **Prescribes** the most suitable drug that targets *that* specific infection with *minimum side-effects*.
3. **Considers cost and availability** so the patient can actually complete the course.

Material selection for corrosion works **identically**:

| Medical Analogy | Corrosion Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patient's body | The engineering structure (pipeline, reactor, bridge) |
| Bacteria / virus | Corrosive species (Cl⁻, H₂S, O₂, acids) |
| Antibiotic strength | Alloy nobility / corrosion resistance |
| Drug side-effects | Loss of mechanical properties, weldability issues |
| Cost of medicine | Lifecycle cost of the material |

A "strong" alloy like **Hastelloy C-276** in dilute sulfuric acid may be analogous to prescribing a high-end antibiotic for a mild cold — wasteful and economically irrational. A **316L stainless steel** might be the "perfect prescription."

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Material Selection

The following standard engineering metrics (all highlighted in **bold**) are universally used in KTU board examinations and industrial practice:

- **Corrosion Rate (CR)** — measured in **mm/year (mmpy)** or **mils per year (mpy)**.
- **Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN)** — dimensionless index for stainless steels.
- **Galvanic Series Position** — relative nobility of metals in seawater.
- **pH of the Environment** — dictates acid/alkaline attack mechanism.
- **Temperature** — accelerates most corrosion reactions (Arrhenius behavior).
- **Chloride Concentration (Cl⁻)** — promotes pitting and crevice corrosion.
- **Cost per kg / Lifecycle cost** — economic constraint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners' Note:** A material is *never* selected on corrosion resistance alone. The KTU valuation key explicitly awards marks for mentioning the **multidisciplinary nature** of selection (mechanical + chemical + economic).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Material Selection (Exhaustive Logic Breakdown)

A KTU board-standard answer on material selection **must** discuss the following six pillars. Each pillar is a filter that narrows down the candidate material list.

### Pillar 1 — Identification of the Corrosive Environment
The engineer must first **characterize** the environment:
- **Chemical species present:** Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, H₂S, CO₂, O₂, organic acids, alkalis.
- **Physical state:** Aqueous, atmospheric, soil, gaseous, or high-temperature molten salt.
- **Concentration gradients** and **temperature** of operation.
- **Flow velocity** (determines erosion-corrosion and flow-accelerated corrosion — FAC).

> **Why this matters:** You cannot fight an enemy you have not identified. The 316L stainless steel that survives a chloride-rich marine atmosphere will catastrophically fail in hot concentrated sulfuric acid.

### Pillar 2 — Required Corrosion Resistance
Quantified as the **allowable corrosion rate** based on the design life. Typical industrial limits:

| Service | Allowable Corrosion Rate (mmpy) |
|---|---|
| Architectural / general structural | < 0.10 |
| Chemical processing equipment | < 0.05 |
| Nuclear / medical implants | < 0.01 |
| Underground pipelines | < 0.025 |

### Pillar 3 — Mechanical Property Compatibility
The material must simultaneously support structural loads. Key properties:
- **Yield Strength (σ_y)** and **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)**.
- **Fatigue strength** — corrosion fatigue reduces endurance limit dramatically.
- **Toughness / Impact resistance** — important for cryogenic or shock-loaded service.
- **Creep resistance** — mandatory for high-temperature service (T > 0.4 T_melting, in Kelvin).

### Pillar 4 — Fabricability
A material is useless if it cannot be **shaped, joined, and finished**. Consider:
- **Weldability** (e.g., high-carbon stainless steels are prone to **sensitization** and intergranular corrosion).
- **Machinability, formability, castability.**
- **Heat-treatability.**

### Pillar 5 — Availability and Standardization
Preference is given to materials listed in **ASTM, ASME, DIN, IS, or JIS** standards. Custom alloys are engineering risks and procurement nightmares.

### Pillar 6 — Economics (Total Lifecycle Cost)
The **golden rule of KTU examinations:** Compare materials on **Total Lifecycle Cost (TLC)**, not purchase price alone.

$$\text{TLC} = C_{\text{initial}} + C_{\text{fabrication}} + C_{\text{maintenance}} + C_{\text{downtime}} + C_{\text{replacement}} - C_{\text{salvage}}$$

> A cheaper material that requires replacement every 2 years can be **10× costlier** over 20 years than an expensive alloy that lasts the full design life.

## 2.2 Material Selection Strategy — Stepwise Logic

1. **Classify the corrosion type** (uniform, pitting, crevice, galvanic, SCC, erosion, etc.).
2. **Eliminate materials thermodynamically incapable** of surviving the environment (e.g., magnesium in acids).
3. **Rank survivors by corrosion rate** under identical test conditions.
4. **Apply mechanical property filters** (must withstand design stress and temperature).
5. **Apply fabricability filter** (weldable, formable, machinable).
6. **Apply economic filter** (lowest TLC, not lowest initial cost).
7. **Validate via field testing or pilot plant** before full-scale deployment.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe `|` is replaced with `\vert` inside tables to avoid breaking the markdown syntax.

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbols & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CR_{mmpy} = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ | W = mass loss (mg), D = density (g/cm³), A = exposed area (cm²), T = time (hr) | Weight-loss to corrosion rate conversion (ASTM G1) |
| 2 | $CR_{mpy} = \dfrac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ | Same as above, output in mils/year | Imperial-unit equivalent |
| 3 | $\text{PREN} = \%Cr + 3.3 \times \%Mo + 16 \times \%N$ | Dimensionless | Stainless-steel pitting/crevice resistance ranking |
| 4 | $\text{PREN}_{W\text{-alloy}} = \%Cr + 3.3 \times (\%Mo + 0.5 \times \%W) + 16 \times \%N$ | Dimensionless | Tungsten-bearing alloys (Hastelloy class) |
| 5 | $\text{Lifecycle Cost} = C_i + \sum_{n=1}^{N} \dfrac{C_{M,n}}{(1+r)^n} + C_R - C_S$ | r = discount rate, N = service life (yr) | Economic selection decision |
| 6 | $V_{\text{corr}} = \dfrac{i_{\text{corr}} \times K}{n \times \rho}$ | i_corr = corrosion current density (µA/cm²), K = constant, n = electrons, ρ = density | Electrochemical corrosion rate (Tafel extrapolation) |
| 7 | $\text{Alloying Effect} \propto e^{-Q/RT}$ | Q = activation energy, R = 8.314 J/mol·K, T = temperature (K) | Temperature effect on corrosion (Arrhenius form) |
| 8 | $E = E^\circ + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}$ | Nernst Equation for galvanic compatibility | Galvanic series construction |
