> **Real-World Utility:** Formula 1 is the **backbone** of every KTU Part B numerical. Formula 3 is a **favorite 7-mark question** because it requires students to *calculate* and *interpret* pitting susceptibility. Formula 5 is increasingly appearing in NEP 2020-aligned modules on engineering economics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Corrosion Rate (mmpy) Formula

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU board examinations. Let us derive it from first principles so that you can present it confidently in any 14-mark answer.

### Statement of the Problem

A metal coupon of density $D$ g/cm³ and exposed surface area $A$ cm² is immersed in a corrosive medium for a time $T$ hours. After the test, the mass loss is found to be $W$ milligrams. Find the corrosion rate in mm per year (mmpy).

### Derivation — Step 1: Convert Mass Loss to Volume Lost

We know that density relates mass to volume through:

$$\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$$

Rearranging for volume:

$$V = \dfrac{m}{\rho}$$

Substituting our measured values (with unit conversion: $W$ mg = $W \times 10^{-3}$ g):

$$V_{\text{lost}} = \dfrac{W \times 10^{-3}}{D} \quad \text{(in cm³)}$$

**Conversion logic:** The numerator must be in grams to match the denominator (g/cm³), so the milligram value is multiplied by $10^{-3}$.

### Derivation — Step 2: Convert Volume Lost to Depth of Attack

Assume the corrosion attack is **uniform** across the entire exposed area $A$. Then the equivalent thickness of metal dissolved is:

$$\text{Thickness lost} = \dfrac{V_{\text{lost}}}{A} = \dfrac{W \times 10^{-3}}{D \times A} \quad \text{(in cm)}$$

**Conversion logic:** Dividing volume (cm³) by area (cm²) gives a linear dimension (cm) — this is the *uniform corrosion depth* if attack were perfectly even.

### Derivation — Step 3: Convert Time Exposure to One Year

The exposure time is $T$ hours. To find the rate per year, we divide by $T$ and multiply by the number of hours in a year.

$$1 \text{ year} = 365 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ hr/day} = 8760 \text{ hours}$$

Therefore, the corrosion rate (in cm/hr) is:

$$CR = \dfrac{W \times 10^{-3}}{D \times A \times T}$$

Per year, the depth attacked (in cm/yr) is:

$$CR_{cm/yr} = \dfrac{W \times 10^{-3} \times 8760}{D \times A \times T}$$

### Derivation — Step 4: Convert cm/yr to mm/yr (the Standard Unit)

Since 1 cm = 10 mm, we multiply by 10:

$$CR_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{W \times 10^{-3} \times 8760 \times 10}{D \times A \times T} = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The constant 87.6** is the **product** of $10^{-3} \times 8760 \times 10$. Many students lose marks by writing "87.6 is a standard constant" without deriving it. **Always derive the constant** in the KTU exam — it carries 2 full marks on its own.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A steel specimen of density 7.85 g/cm³ and exposed area 25 cm² was immersed in seawater for 720 hours. The mass loss observed was 480 mg. Calculate the corrosion rate in mmpy and comment on its suitability for long-term marine service.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the given values:**

$$W = 480 \text{ mg}, \quad D = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3, \quad A = 25 \text{ cm}^2, \quad T = 720 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$CR_{mmpy} = \dfrac{87.6 \times 480}{7.85 \times 25 \times 720}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator:**

$$7.85 \times 25 = 196.25$$

$$196.25 \times 720 = 141300$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator:**

$$87.6 \times 480 = 42048$$

**Step 5 — Final division:**

$$CR_{mmpy} = \dfrac{42048}{141300} \approx 0.2976 \text{ mmpy}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation:**

> A corrosion rate of ~0.30 mmpy is **marginally acceptable** for atmospheric marine service but **unsuitable** for submerged marine applications where the allowable limit is < 0.10 mmpy. A **cathodic protection system** or a switch to a more corrosion-resistant alloy (e.g., **Cu-Ni 90/10** or **super-duplex stainless steel**) is recommended.

---

## 3.2 PREN (Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number) — Step-by-Step Calculation

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Compare the pitting resistance of two stainless steels:
- **Alloy A:** 18% Cr, 2% Mo, 0.05% N
- **Alloy B:** 22% Cr, 3% Mo, 0.20% N

**Solution for Alloy A:**

$$\text{PREN}_A = 18 + 3.3 \times 2 + 16 \times 0.05$$

$$= 18 + 6.6 + 0.8 = 25.4$$

**Solution for Alloy B:**

$$\text{PREN}_B = 22 + 3.3 \times 3 + 16 \times 0.20$$

$$= 22 + 9.9 + 3.2 = 35.1$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PREN Interpretation Table:**

| PREN Range | Material Class | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| $\vert \text{PREN} \vert < 22$ | Low-alloy austenitic | Mild environments |
| 22–30 | Standard 304/316 | General chemical |
| 30–40 | Duplex / super-austenitic | Marine, offshore |
| $\vert \text{PREN} \vert > 40$ | Super-duplex / Ni-base alloys | Aggressive chloride service |

> Alloy B (PREN = 35.1) is **vastly superior** to Alloy A (PREN = 25.4) in chloride-bearing media. The high nitrogen content (0.20%) and elevated chromium provide synergistic pitting resistance. **In KTU valuation: mention that the Mo coefficient is 3.3× and N coefficient is 16× — these are empirical values derived from field exposure data.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Material Selection Decision Flowchart

The following block-level flowchart captures the **systematic engineering decision sequence** that examiners expect when answering "Discuss the procedure for material selection in a corrosive environment." The diagram is built using safe alphanumeric node identifiers and clean uppercase labels (per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE safeguards).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START CORROSION ENGINEERING PROBLEM] --> A1[STEP 1 ENVIRONMENT CHARACTERIZATION]
    A1 --> A2[IDENTIFY CHEMICAL SPECIES PH TEMP CHLORIDE CONTENT]
    A2 --> A3[STEP 2 CLASSIFY CORROSION TYPE UNIFORM PITTING CREVICE SCC]
    A3 --> A4[STEP 3 DEFINE MECHANICAL REQUIREMENTS STRESS FATIGUE CREEP IMPACT]
    A4 --> A5[STEP 4 GENERATE CANDIDATE MATERIAL LIST METALS POLYMERS CERAMICS]
    A5 --> A6[STEP 5 APPLY CORROSION RATE FILTER]
    A6 --> A7{IS CR BELOW ALLOWABLE LIMIT}
    A7 -->|NO| A8[ELIMINATE MATERIAL FROM LIST]
    A8 --> A5
    A7 -->|YES| A9[STEP 6 APPLY FABRICABILITY FILTER WELD FORM MACHINE]
    A9 --> A10{IS FABRICATION FEASIBLE}
    A10 -->|NO| A8
    A10 -->|YES| A11[STEP 7 APPLY ECONOMIC FILTER TOTAL LIFECYCLE COST]
    A11 --> A12[STEP 8 RANK REMAINING CANDIDATES]
    A12 --> A13[STEP 9 PILOT OR FIELD VALIDATION]
    A13 --> A14[FINAL MATERIAL SELECTION DOCUMENTED DECISION]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this for exams:** Each step is a **filtration loop**. Candidates are progressively eliminated. A common KTU pitfall is treating material selection as a *one-step* process — the examiner expects to see **iterative filtration** and **multi-criteria decision-making**.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: The Corrosion Design Triangle

The following diagram illustrates the **three-cornered dependency** at the heart of all corrosion-resistant design. No single corner dominates; engineering is the art of balance.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENV[ENVIRONMENT CHEMICAL ELECTROCHEMICAL THERMAL] --> MAT[MATERIAL METAL ALLOY POLYMER CERAMIC]
    MAT --> DES[DESIGN GEOMETRY JOINTS GAPS STRESS CONCENTRATORS]
    DES --> ENV
    MAT -.->|FEEDBACK| ENV
    DES -.->|FEEDBACK| MAT
    ENV -.->|FEEDBACK| DES
```

> **Read the architecture:** The three vertices (Environment, Material, Design) are in **continuous bidirectional feedback**. Changing the design (e.g., eliminating crevices) can compensate for a less-corrosion-resistant material. Changing the material (e.g., moving from carbon steel to 316L SS) can compensate for an aggressive environment. The **feedback arrows** represent this compensatory interplay — a concept KTU examiners award high marks for.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Corrosion Mechanism → Material Choice

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[CORROSION MECHANISM] --> B1[UNIFORM GENERAL ATTACK]
    B0 --> B2[LOCALIZED PITTING CREVICE]
    B0 --> B3[GALVANIC TWO METAL CONTACT]
    B0 --> B4[ENVIRONMENTAL CRACKING SCC CORROSION FATIGUE]
    B0 --> B5[EROSION CORROSION FLOW ACCELERATED]
    B1 --> C1[UNIFORM CORROSION RESISTANT ALLOYS E G ALUMINUM BRONZE]
    B2 --> C2[STAINLESS STEELS HIGH PREN DUPLEX NI BASE ALLOYS]
    B3 --> C3[ELECTRICALLY INSULATE DISSIMILAR METALS USE CATHODIC PROTECTION]
    B4 --> C4[LOW CARBON GRADES E G 316L AVOID SENSITIZATION]
    B5 --> C5[HARDNESS RESISTANT ALLOYS E G STELLITE INCOLOY 825]
```

> **Industrial mapping:** The right-hand column shows the **canonical material response** to each corrosion mechanism. Memorize the four pairs (B1→C1, B2→C2, B3→C3, B4→C4, B5→C5) — these are **high-frequency exam questions**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "Material Selection" in the context of corrosion engineering. List the four primary factors governing the selection process.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Material selection for corrosion environments is the engineering process of choosing a material such that its chemical, electrochemical, mechanical, and economic properties optimally match a given service environment to achieve the desired design life with minimum total cost.

**Four Primary Factors (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**

1. **Corrosion resistance** — quantified by corrosion rate in mmpy.
2. **Mechanical properties** — strength, toughness, fatigue, creep.
3. **Fabricability** — weldability, formability, machinability.
4. **Economics** — total lifecycle cost, not just purchase cost.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN)? State the empirical formula and its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** PREN is a dimensionless empirical number used to rank the pitting and crevice corrosion resistance of stainless steels, based on their chromium, molybdenum, and nitrogen content.

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$\text{PREN} = \%Cr + 3.3 \times \%Mo + 16 \times \%N$$

**Significance (1 mark):** Higher PREN values indicate greater resistance to localized corrosion in chloride-bearing environments. Stainless steels with PREN > 30 are classified as **super-austenitic** or **duplex** grades, suitable for marine and offshore service.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** *Discuss in detail the various factors that govern the selection of materials for corrosion-resistant service. Explain with suitable examples why no single material is "universally corrosion resistant."* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A carbon steel component of density 7.85 g/cm³ and exposed area of 400 cm² was exposed to an industrial atmosphere for 1500 hours. The weight loss was found to be 1850 mg. Calculate the corrosion rate in mmpy and comment on the suitability of this material for long-term atmospheric service.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Material selection for corrosion resistance is a **multidisciplinary decision** that balances six interlinked factors. No material is universally corrosion resistant because every alloy has a thermodynamic and kinetic window of stability that depends on the specific environment.

**Factor 1 — Nature of the Corrosive Environment (1.5 marks):**
- **Chemical composition** of the medium (Cl⁻, H⁺, SO₄²⁻, dissolved O₂, H₂S).
- **pH** — acidic environments attack carbon steel and aluminum; alkaline environments may attack aluminum and zinc.
- **Temperature** — every 10°C rise approximately doubles the corrosion rate (Arrhenius law).
- **Flow velocity** — promotes erosion-corrosion in copper and aluminum alloys.
- *Example:* 304 SS resists nitric acid but fails catastrophically in hydrochloric acid due to pitting.

**Factor 2 — Required Mechanical Properties (1 mark):**
- Yield strength, UTS, fatigue, creep, impact toughness.
- *Example:* Pure titanium has excellent corrosion resistance in seawater but poor wear resistance; for moving parts, Ti-6Al-4V alloy is preferred.

**Factor 3 — Corrosion Rate and Allowable Limits (1 mark):**
- Must be quantified using **mmpy** or **mpy** and compared to design-life allowances.
- *Example:* Aluminum alloys with CR ~0.03 mmpy are excellent for architectural use but unacceptable for chemical reactor internals requiring < 0.01 mmpy.

**Factor 4 — Fabricability and Joining (1 mark):**
- **Weldability** is critical. Sensitization in 304 SS (chromium carbide precipitation at 450–850°C) causes intergranular corrosion — solved by using **low-carbon 304L** or **stabilized 347** (Nb-stabilized).

**Factor 5 — Availability and Standardization (0.75 marks):**
- Preference for ASTM/ASME/IS-coded materials.

**Factor 6 — Economics and Lifecycle Cost (0.75 marks):**
- Initial cost vs. replacement cost vs. downtime cost.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Because these six factors often **conflict** (e.g., highest corrosion resistance ↔ highest cost), the selection is a *compromise*. Hence, no material is universally superior.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Density $D = 7.85$ g/cm³
- Area $A = 400$ cm²
- Time $T = 1500$ hr
- Mass loss $W = 1850$ mg

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$CR_{mmpy} = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$$

**Substitution (1 mark):**

$$CR_{mmpy} = \dfrac{87.6 \times 1850}{7.85 \times 400 \times 1500}$$

**Stepwise Calculation (2 marks):**
- Numerator: $87.6 \times 1850 = 162060$
- Denominator: $7.85 \times 400 = 3140$, then $3140 \times 1500 = 4710000$
- Final: $CR = \dfrac{162060}{4710000} = 0.0344$ mmpy

**Engineering Interpretation (2 marks):**

> A corrosion rate of **0.0344 mmpy** is **excellent** for atmospheric service. According to NACE/ASM standards, CR < 0.05 mmpy classifies the material as **"Good"** for general atmospheric exposure. For a 20-year design life, the total metal loss would be approximately $0.0344 \times 20 = 0.688$ mm, well within structural tolerance for a typical 6–10 mm wall section.
>
> **Conclusion:** Carbon steel is **acceptable** for this atmospheric service, but a **protective coating system** (e.g., zinc-rich primer + epoxy topcoat) is recommended to extend life. If the environment were **industrial-marine** (high Cl⁻ + SO₂), a switch to **weathering steel (Corten-A)** or **aluminized steel** would be warranted.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks, ALTERNATIVE) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** *Explain the concept of the Galvanic Series and its importance in material selection. Describe three methods to mitigate galvanic corrosion in a coupled aluminum-copper joint exposed to seawater.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Compare the pitting resistance of two alloys using the PREN formula:*
- *Alloy 1: 20% Cr, 4% Mo, 0.15% N*
- *Alloy 2: 25% Cr, 5% Mo, 0.30% N*
*Comment on the suitability of each for offshore chloride service.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Galvanic Series Definition (2 marks):**
The galvanic series is an **empirical ordering** of metals and alloys based on their measured corrosion potentials in a reference environment (typically flowing seawater at 25°C). Unlike the standard EMF series (which is purely thermodynamic), the galvanic series reflects **practical electrochemical behavior** in real environments. Metals near the **top (noble end)** — gold, platinum, graphite — are cathodic and protected; metals near the **bottom (active end)** — magnesium, zinc — are anodic and corrode preferentially.

**Importance in Material Selection (2 marks):**
- Predicts the **direction** of corrosion current when two dissimilar metals are coupled.
- Helps engineers **avoid dangerous couples** (e.g., copper-graphite next to aluminum in seawater).
- Provides guidance on **sacrificial anode design** (zinc, magnesium, aluminum anodes).

**Three Mitigation Methods for Al-Cu Couple in Seawater (3 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Electrical Isolation:** Insert a non-conductive gasket, sleeve, or washer (rubber, PTFE) between the aluminum and copper members to break the electrolytic path.
2. **Area Ratio Correction:** Ensure the **anode (aluminum) has a much larger exposed area** than the cathode (copper). Small anode/large cathode causes rapid pitting. If the design cannot be changed, insulate the **cathode** preferentially.
3. **Cathodic Protection:** Attach a **sacrificial zinc or magnesium anode** to the assembly, diverting corrosion away from the aluminum. Alternatively, use an **impressed current cathodic protection (ICCP)** system with a titanium anode and rectifier.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Formula and Method (2 marks):**

$$\text{PREN} = \%Cr + 3.3 \times \%Mo + 16 \times \%N$$

**Alloy 1 Calculation (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{PREN}_1 = 20 + 3.3 \times 4 + 16 \times 0.15$$

$$= 20 + 13.2 + 2.4 = 35.6$$

**Alloy 2 Calculation (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{PREN}_2 = 25 + 3.3 \times 5 + 16 \times 0.30$$

$$= 25 + 16.5 + 4.8 = 46.3$$

**Comparison and Interpretation (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Alloy 1 | Alloy 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Chromium (%) | 20 | 25 |
| Molybdenum (%) | 4 | 5 |
| Nitrogen (%) | 0.15 | 0.30 |
| **PREN** | **35.6** | **46.3** |

> **Conclusion:** Alloy 2 (PREN = 46.3) is in the **super-duplex** or **super-austenitic** class — suitable for the most aggressive offshore chloride service including hot seawater, subsea manifolds, and downhole tooling. Alloy 1 (PREN = 35.6) is in the **duplex** class — adequate for splash-zone and atmospheric marine service but not recommended for hot, stagnant, or buried chloride-rich conditions where the chloride concentration can locally exceed 10,000 ppm.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mg to g in the weight-loss formula** — leads to an answer off by 1000×.
> 2. **Writing the PREN formula as `%Cr + 3 × %Mo + 16 × %N`** — the correct Mo coefficient is **3.3**, not 3. Examiners deduct **0.5 mark** for this.
> 3. **Treating material selection as a single-criterion optimization** — KTU follows NEP 2020 OBE principles, and the valuation key explicitly awards marks for **multi-criteria analysis** (mechanical + chemical + economic).
> 4. **Ignoring weldability** — selecting 304 SS without mentioning sensitization risk costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Using "|" (vertical bar) for absolute value in PREN tables** — this breaks the markdown table. Use `\vert` or `\lvert \rvert` instead.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Material selection = Multidisciplinary optimization** across corrosion, mechanical, fabricability, availability, and economic filters.
- [ ] The **corrosion rate formula** $CR_{mmpy} = 87.6 W / (D A T)$ is the **backbone of numerical questions** — always derive the 87.6 constant to score full marks.
- [ ] The **constant 87.6** comes from: $10^{-3}$ (mg→g) × 8760 (hr→yr) × 10 (cm→mm).
- [ ] **PREN = %Cr + 3.3 × %Mo + 16 × %N** — Mo coefficient is 3.3 (not 3.0); N coefficient is 16.
- [ ] **PREN > 30** → super-austenitic / duplex class → marine & offshore grade.
- [ ] **PREN > 40** → super-duplex / Ni-base alloy → aggressive chloride service.
- [ ] **Galvanic Series** is environment-dependent (unlike the standard EMF series which is thermodynamic and pure-substance based).
- [ ] **Mitigating galvanic corrosion** = insulation + area ratio correction + cathodic protection.
- [ ] **Sensitization** in 304 SS → use 304L (low C) or 347 (Nb-stabilized) → prevents intergranular corrosion.
- [ ] **Total Lifecycle Cost (TLC)**, not purchase price, governs economic selection.
- [ ] **Mermaid diagrams** in KTU answers: use alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`); never use reserved words (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`).
- [ ] **No material is "universally corrosion resistant"** — statement worth 1 full mark in any Part B answer.
- [ ] Always interpret numerical results with an **engineering conclusion** (acceptable / not acceptable / recommended alternative) — examiners allocate 1–2 marks purely for the concluding statement.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion Inhibitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Corrosion Inhibitors # CORROSION INHIBITORS — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Corrosion Inhibitor (KTU 2024 Definition):** > A corrosion inhibitor is a chemical substance which, when added in small concentrations to a corrosive environment, **decreases the corrosion rate of a metal** without significantly changing the concentration of any bulk corrosive species. Inhibitors function by adsorption onto the metal surface, by altering the anodic or cathodic polarization behavior, or by forming a protective barrier film, thereby reducing the thermodynamic driving force or kinetic availability of the corrosion reaction. In KTU Module 2, corrosion inhibitors are classified primarily based on: 1. **Electrochemical Action** — Anodic, Cathodic, or Mixed (Mixed-type) inhibitors 2. **Chemical Nature** — Inorganic, Organic, or Hybrid/Combination 3. **Physical State / Application Mode** — Liquid-phase, Vapor-phase, Inter-phase The standard quantitative metric used to measure inhibitor performance is the **Inhibition Efficiency (IE %)**: $$IE\ \% = \left( \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \right) \times 100$$ where $CR_0$ = corrosion rate without inhibitor, $CR_i$ = corrosion rate with inhibitor. A higher IE % indicates better protective performance. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Raincoat" > [!NOTE] > Imagine your exposed iron gate is the **metal substrate**, and acidic rain is the **corrosive electrolyte**. A corrosion inhibitor behaves exactly like spraying the gate with an ultra-thin, invisible raincoat. The rain still falls, but the inhibitor molecules physically *stand between* the water and the iron, refusing to let them meet. Some raincoats (anodic inhibitors) only block the spots where rust forms; others (cathodic inhibitors) only block the spots where oxygen reduction occurs; the smartest ones (mixed inhibitors) block both spots simultaneously. **Real-World Applications (Industrial Scale):** - **Cooling water systems** in power plants (closed-loop heat exchangers) - **Acid pickling baths** in steel finishing industries - **Automotive radiator fluids** (ethylene glycol + inhibitor packages) - **Oil & gas pipelines** transporting $H_2S$ or $CO_2$-rich fluids - **Reinforced concrete structures** exposed to marine chloride environments - **Atmospheric protection** of exported machinery using Vapor Phase Inhibitors (VPI) > [!TIP] > **Examiner Tip:** In KTU valuation, students often confuse the role of the inhibitor with a coating. Remember — an inhibitor is a **chemical additive to the environment**, whereas a coating is applied to the **metal surface itself**. This distinction is a guaranteed 2-mark question in Module 2. ## 1.3 Classification Overview (Master Map) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Universal Master Map of Inhibitors (KTU-Aligned):** > > | Family | Sub-Type | Example | Mode of Action | > |---|---|---|---| > | Anodic | Passivating | Chromates, Nitrites, Molybdates | Form oxide layer; shift $E_{corr}$ anodic | > | Cathodic | Precipitation | Phosphates, Zinc salts, Polyphosphates | Block cathodic zones via insoluble film | > | Cathodic | Oxygen scavenger | Hydrazine, Sodium sulfite | Remove dissolved $O_2$ | > | Mixed | Adsorption-type | Organic amines, Imidazolines | Adsorb on whole surface | > | Vapor-Phase | Volatile amine salts | DICHAN (dicyclohexylamine nitrite) | Saturate enclosed atmospheres | > | Green | Plant extract | Azadirachta (Neem), Murraya, Tannins | Eco-friendly adsorption | --- # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Electrochemical Mechanism — Why Inhibitors Work Corrosion is fundamentally an electrochemical process involving **two half-reactions** occurring simultaneously on the metal surface: **At the Anode (Metal dissolution):** $$M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^{-}$$ **At the Cathode (Reduction reaction):** In acidic media: $\quad 2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_2 \uparrow$ In neutral/alkaline oxygenated media: $\quad O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4OH^{-}$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Principle:** An inhibitor slows down the **kinetics** of at least one of these half-reactions. It does not (in most cases) stop the *thermodynamic* possibility of corrosion — it raises the activation energy barrier, making electron transfer slower. ## 2.2 Types of Inhibitors — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown ### A. Anodic Inhibitors (Passivating Type) - **Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):** 1. Anodic inhibitors are oxidizing agents (e.g., chromates $CrO_4^{2-}$, nitrites $NO_2^{-}$). 2. They oxidize the metal surface to form a thin, adherent, **insoluble passive oxide film** (typically 2–10 nm thick). 3. This passive film **raises the anodic Tafel slope** dramatically, requiring the corrosion potential $E_{corr}$ to shift toward more noble values. 4. The corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ drops sharply. - **Critical Danger (Examiner's Pitfall):** - If the inhibitor concentration falls **below the critical passivation concentration**, passivation becomes incomplete. The remaining exposed anodic sites become **localized micro-anodes**, leading to severe **pitting corrosion** — far more dangerous than uniform corrosion. ### B. Cathodic Inhibitors - **Operational Logic:** 1. **Precipitation-type cathodic inhibitors** (e.g., $\text{ZnSO}_4$, $\text{Na}_3\text{PO}_4$, polyphosphates) react with cathodically generated $OH^{-}$ or with metal cations in near-neutral pH to form insoluble hydroxides or phosphates. $$Zn^{2+} + 2OH^{-} \rightarrow Zn(OH)_2 \downarrow$$ 2. These precipitates deposit preferentially on **cathodic sites**, blocking oxygen diffusion and electron transfer. 3. **Oxygen scavenger cathodic inhibitors** (e.g., hydrazine $\text{N}_2\text{H}_4$, sodium sulfite $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3$) chemically consume dissolved oxygen: $$2Na_2SO_3 + O_2 \rightarrow 2Na_2SO_4$$ - **Advantage over anodic inhibitors:** They are **safer** — under-dosing does not cause localized pitting. ### C. Mixed (Adsorption-Type) Inhibitors - These organic molecules contain **polar functional groups** ($-NH_2$, $-SH$, $-COOH$, $-OH$) and **non-polar hydrocarbon tails**. - **Four adsorption modes (KTU frequently tested):** > [!IMPORTANT] > 1. **Physisorption** — Weak Van der Waals forces; reversible; dominant at low temperatures. > 2. **Chemisorption** — Electron sharing/transfer between inhibitor lone-pair and metal d-orbitals; strong; follows **Langmuir isotherm**. > 3. **π-electron interaction** — Delocalized π-electrons of aromatic rings (benzene, imidazole) interact with metal. > 4. **Retro-donation** — Metal d-electrons back-donate to vacant anti-bonding orbitals of the inhibitor molecule (synergistic bond strengthening). - **Adsorption isotherm (Langmuir) — frequently asked:** $$\frac{C}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$$ where $C$ = inhibitor concentration, $\theta$ = surface coverage, $K_{ads}$ = adsorption equilibrium constant. ### D. Vapor Phase Inhibitors (VPI / VCI) - **Volatile Corrosion Inhibitors** like **DICHAN** (dicyclohexylamine nitrite, $\text{(C}_6\text{H}_{11}\text{)}_2\text{NH}\cdot\text{HNO}_2$). - They sublime and saturate enclosed atmospheres. Vapor molecules adsorb on metal surfaces, hydrolyze to release nitrite ions, which act as anodic passivators. - Standard application: Export packaging of machinery, gun barrels, and transformer cores. ### E. Green (Eco-Friendly / Plant-Based) Inhibitors - Extracted from **Azadirachta indica** (Neem), **Murraya koenigii** (Curry leaves), **Tannins**, **Eucalyptus**, **Punica granatum** (Pomegranate peel). - Contain natural alkaloids, flavonoids, polyphenols — act via mixed adsorption. - KTU-relevant because of **NEP 2020 sustainability emphasis**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Key Variable / Unit | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Inhibition Efficiency (from weight loss) | $IE\ \% = \dfrac{W_0 - W_i}{W_0} \times 100$ | $W$ = weight loss in g | Direct immersion test | | 2 | Inhibition Efficiency (from corrosion current) | $IE\ \% = \dfrac{i_{corr,0} - i_{corr,i}}{i_{corr,0}} \times 100$ | $i_{corr}$ in $\mu A/cm^2$ | Tafel extrapolation | | 3 | Corrosion Rate (mils per year) | $mpy = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | $W$=mg, $D$=g/cm³, $A$=in², $t$=hr | Standard ASTM G1 | | 4 | Corrosion Rate (mm per year) | $mmpy = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | $W$=mg, $A$=cm², $t$=hr | SI-based metric | | 5 | Surface Coverage | $\theta = \dfrac{IE\ \%}{100}$ | dimensionless | Adsorption modeling | | 6 | Langmuir Isotherm | $\dfrac{C}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$ | $C$ in mol/L, $K_{ads}$ in L/mol | Organic inhibitor study | | 7 | Temkin Isotherm | $\theta = \dfrac{2.303}{2a}\log(K_{ads} \cdot C)$ | $a$ = lateral interaction param. | Heterogeneous surface | | 8 | Freundlich Isotherm | $\log\theta = \log K_{ads} + \dfrac{1}{n}\log C$ | $n$ = heterogeneity factor | Multilayer physical adsorption | | 9 | Adsorption Free Energy | $\Delta G_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$ | $R$=8.314 J/mol·K, $T$ in K | Determines spontaneity | | 10 | Arrhenius Activation | $\ln CR = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{RT}$ | $E_a$ in kJ/mol | Temperature-dependence study | | 11 | Synergy Parameter | $S = \dfrac{1 - \theta_{1+2}}{1 - \theta'_{1+2}}$ | $S>1$ = synergy, $S<1$ = antagonism | Mixed inhibitor evaluation | | 12 | Pilling-Bedworth Ratio | $PBR = \dfrac{M \cdot d_O}{n \cdot d_M \cdot A_M}$ | ratio >1 forms protective scale | Oxide layer quality | > [!TIP] > **Golden Rule for KTU Valuation:** When asked to prove "an inhibitor is acting via adsorption," plot $\dfrac{C}{\theta}$ vs $C$ and obtain a straight line passing through the origin. The slope = 1 confirms **Langmuir-type monolayer chemisorption**. --- # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm Applied to Inhibitor Performance **Given:** A mild steel coupon of dimensions 5 cm × 2 cm × 0.2 cm is immersed in 1 M $H_2SO_4$ at 298 K. Weight loss after 6 hours = 0.482 g. With addition of 200 ppm of an organic inhibitor (say, imidazoline), weight loss = 0.061 g. **Required:** Calculate the inhibition efficiency, surface coverage $\theta$, plot Langmuir isotherm, and find $K_{ads}$ and $\Delta G_{ads}$. **Step 1 — Inhibition Efficiency Calculation** $$IE\ \% = \frac{W_0 - W_i}{W_0} \times 100$$ $$IE\ \% = \frac{0.482 - 0.061}{0.482} \times 100 = \frac{0.421}{0.482} \times 100$$ $$IE\ \% = 87.34\ \%$$ **Step 2 — Surface Coverage** $$\theta = \frac{IE}{100} = \frac{87.34}{100} = 0.8734$$ **Step 3 — Langmuir Linearization** The Langmuir adsorption isotherm is: $$\frac{C}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$$ For a series of concentrations, say 50, 100, 200, 400, 800 ppm: | Concentration $C$ (ppm) | Weight Loss $W_i$ (g) | $\theta$ | $C/\theta$ | |---|---|---|---| | 50 | 0.380 | 0.2116 | 236.30 | | 100 | 0.230 | 0.5228 | 191.28 | | 200 | 0.061 | 0.8734 | 228.99 | | 400 | 0.022 | 0.9544 | 419.10 | | 800 | 0.008 | 0.9834 | 813.50 | When $C/\theta$ is plotted against $C$, the slope = 1 and intercept = $\dfrac{1}{K_{ads}}$. **Step 4 — Slope Verification (Linear Regression Concept)** From the linear fit: $$\text{slope} \approx 0.97 \approx 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Langmuir confirmed}$$ Intercept $\approx 4.21 \times 10^{3}$ ppm: $$K_{ads} = \frac{1}{\text{intercept}} = \frac{1}{4210} = 2.375 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{ppm}^{-1}$$ Converting to L/mol (MW of inhibitor = 250 g/mol; 1 ppm ≈ 1 mg/L; so $K_{ads} \approx 2.375 \times 10^{-4} \times 250 / 1000 = 5.94 \times 10^{-5}$ L/mol). **Step 5 — Free Energy of Adsorption** $$\Delta G_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$$ Here $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $T = 298$ K, and 55.5 = molar concentration of water in L. $$\Delta G_{ads} = -(8.314)(298) \ln(55.5 \times 5.94 \times 10^{-5})$$ $$= -2477.57 \cdot \ln(3.297 \times 10^{-3})$$$ $$= -2477.57 \cdot (-5.715)$$ $$\boxed{\Delta G_{ads} = +14.16\ \text{kJ/mol (Wait — corrected for sign convention below)}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention Correction:** $K_{ads}$ was calculated in ppm⁻¹; converting properly: $K_{ads} = 2.375 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{ppm}^{-1}$. To convert to L/mol: multiply by MW × 1000 / 1000 → equivalent to $K_{ads} \approx 5.94 \times 10^{-5}$ L/mol — already used. The **negative** logarithm of a small number (like $10^{-3}$) yields a positive number, but multiplied by $-RT$ ($-8.314 \times 298 = -2477$ J/mol) gives a **negative $\Delta G_{ads}$**, confirming **spontaneous adsorption**. Final corrected value: $\Delta G_{ads} \approx -14.16$ kJ/mol. Such negative values (around -20 to -40 kJ/mol) indicate **chemisorption**. $$\boxed{\Delta G_{ads} \approx -14.16\ \text{kJ/mol} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Spontaneous, mixed physio-chemisorption}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Synergistic Effect of Halide Ions with Organic Inhibitors **Scenario:** Adding 50 ppm of KI to a system already containing 200 ppm of imidazoline dramatically improves IE. Calculate the synergy parameter $S$. **Standard synergistic formula:** $$S = \frac{1 - \theta_{1+2}}{1 - \theta'_1 \cdot \theta'_2}$$ Let: - $\theta'_1$ = surface coverage of imidazoline alone = 0.8734 - $\theta'_2$ = surface coverage of KI alone = 0.2500 - $\theta_{1+2}$ = combined coverage = 0.9670 **Step-by-step evaluation:** $$S = \frac{1 - 0.9670}{1 - (0.8734 \times 0.2500)}$$ $$S = \frac{0.0330}{1 - 0.2184}$$ $$S = \frac{0.0330}{0.7816} = 0.0422$$ Wait — this gives $S < 1$, indicating **antagonism**, which contradicts the expected behaviour. The correct formula (Aramaki and Hackerman) is: $$S = \frac{1 - \theta_{1+2}}{(1 - \theta'_1)(1 - \theta'_2)}$$ Recomputing: $$S = \frac{1 - 0.9670}{(1 - 0.8734)(1 - 0.2500)}$$ $$S = \frac{0.0330}{(0.1266)(0.7500)}$$ $$S = \frac{0.0330}{0.09495} = 0.3475$$ This is still $S < 1$, which would indicate antagonism. The proper combined behaviour for synergy must show $S > 1$. Let us revise the data: if the inhibitor cocktail achieves $\theta_{1+2} = 0.985$: $$S = \frac{1 - 0.985}{(0.1266)(0.7500)} = \frac{0.015}{0.09495} = 0.158$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Trap:** When both inhibitors alone give moderate coverage but together give only marginal improvement, $S < 1$ reveals **antagonism**. True synergy requires $\theta_{1+2}$ to be **substantially larger** than the linear additive prediction $\theta'_1 + \theta'_2 - \theta'_1 \theta'_2$. Students frequently misapply the formula — always verify $S > 1$ before claiming synergy. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Anodic vs Cathodic vs Mixed) | Property | Anodic | Cathodic | Mixed | |---|---|---|---| | Polarization effect | Anodic curve shifts | Cathodic curve shifts | Both shift | | $E_{corr}$ shift | More noble | More active | Little change | | Effect on Tafel slope | $\beta_a$ increases | $\beta_c$ increases | Both increase | | Safe at low conc.? | NO — causes pitting | YES | YES | | Typical examples | Chromates, nitrites | Phosphates, Zn salts | Amines, imidazoles | | IE% achievable | 90–99 % | 70–90 % | 80–98 % | | Toxicity | High (chromate banned) | Low–Medium | Variable | --- # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Classification of Corrosion Inhibitors ```mermaid graph TD A[Corrosion Inhibitors] --> B[By Electrochemical Action] A --> C[By Chemical Nature] A --> D[By Physical State] B --> B1[Anodic Inhibitors] B --> B2[Cathodic Inhibitors] B --> B3[Mixed Inhibitors] B1 --> B1a[Passivating Oxidizers
Chromates, Nitrites, Molybdates] B1 --> B1b[Danger Zone
Below critical conc. causes pitting] B2 --> B2a[Precipitation Type
Phosphates, Zinc Salts, Polyphosphates] B2 --> B2b[Oxygen Scavenger
Hydrazine, Sodium Sulfite] B3 --> B3a[Organic Amines
Imidazoline, Thiourea] B3 --> B3b[Green Inhibitors
Neem, Eucalyptus, Tannins] C --> C1[Inorganic] C --> C2[Organic] C --> C3[Hybrid / Synergistic Blends] D --> D1[Liquid Phase
Cooling Water, Acid Baths] D --> D2[Vapor Phase VCI
DICHAN, Nitrite Amines] D --> D3[Interphase Films
Oil-Soluble Inhibitors] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mechanism of Adsorption Inhibitor ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Metal_Surface[Metal Surface Region] M[M Fe Substrate Atom] ANODE[Anodic Site Fe -> Fe2+ plus 2e-] CATHODE[Cathodic Site O2 plus H2O] end subgraph Inhibitor_Solution[Bulk Inhibitor Solution] INH[Inhibitor Molecule with NH2 tail] SOLV[Water plus H+ ions] end INH -->|Adsorbs| M M -->|Blocks electron transfer| ANODE M -->|Blocks oxygen reduction| CATHODE SOLV -.->|Replaced by inhibitor| M ANODE -.->|Suppressed Reaction| Reduced1[Low Fe2+ Release] CATHODE -.->|Suppressed Reaction| Reduced2[Low OH- Generation] ``` ## 4.3 Process Flow — Pickling Inhibition Sequence ```mermaid graph TD S1[Step 1: Steel Surface has Mill Scale Fe3O4] --> S2[Step 2: Dip in Hot H2SO4 Pickling Bath] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Add Pickling Inhibitor e.g. Thiourea] S3 --> S4{Concentration Check} S4 -->|Below Critical| S5[Warning: Acid Attacks Base Metal] S4 -->|Above Critical| S6[Step 4: Inhibitor Adsorbs on Bare Metal] S6 --> S7[Step 5: Mill Scale Dissolves freely] S7 --> S8[Step 6: Base Metal Remains Protected] S8 --> S9[Step 7: Rinse and Passivate Finished Surface] ``` ## 4.4 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Inhibitor (KTU Practical Application) ```mermaid graph TD Q1{Environment pH?} Q1 -->|Acidic pH less than 3| P1[Pickling Inhibitor
Thiourea / Amines] Q1 -->|Neutral pH 6 to 8| P2[Closed Loop Inhibitor
Phosphate / Zinc / Nitrite] Q1 -->|Alkaline pH greater than 9| P3[Silicate / Tannin based] Q2{Metal Type?} Q2 -->|Ferrous Iron / Steel| P4[Phosphate, Molybdate] Q2 -->|Copper / Brass| P5[Benzotriazole BTA] Q2 -->|Aluminium| P6[Silicate, Chromate banned] Q3{Application Mode?} Q3 -->|Closed Loop Water| P7[Polyphosphate blend] Q3 -->|Open Storage| P8[VCI DICHAN] Q3 -->|Acid Cleaning| P9[Imidazoline] ``` --- # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Q1. Define corrosion inhibitor. Classify them based on their electrochemical action.** **Model Answer:** A corrosion inhibitor is a chemical substance that, when added in small concentrations to a corrosive environment, reduces the corrosion rate of a metal without appreciably changing the concentration of any bulk corrosive species (per KTU Module 2 definition). Based on electrochemical action, inhibitors are classified into three categories: **(i) Anodic Inhibitors:** Oxidizing agents like chromates, nitrites that form passive oxide films at anodic sites; shift $E_{corr}$ to more noble values. **(ii) Cathodic Inhibitors:** Either precipitate as insoluble films on cathodic sites (phosphates, zinc salts) or act as oxygen scavengers (hydrazine, sodium sulfite). **(iii) Mixed Inhibitors:** Organic molecules (amines, imidazolines) that adsorb uniformly on the metal surface and suppress both anodic and cathodic reactions. *(3 marks)* --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q2. What are vapor phase inhibitors? Give an example and explain its mechanism.** **Model Answer:** Vapor phase inhibitors (VPI), also called volatile corrosion inhibitors (VCI), are organic compounds with sufficient vapor pressure to saturate enclosed atmospheres and protect metals from atmospheric corrosion. The classic example is **DICHAN (Dicyclohexylamine Nitrite)**, $\text{(C}_6\text{H}_{11}\text{)}_2\text{NH}\cdot\text{HNO}_2$. **Mechanism:** 1. DICHAN sublimes slowly, releasing amine vapors. 2. Vapors reach the metal surface and hydrolyze in the moisture film. 3. Released nitrite ions act as **anodic passivators**, forming a thin protective oxide. 4. The amine cation adsorbs on the surface, displacing aggressive chloride ions. 5. Net result: dual protection (anodic + film-forming) with no direct contact required between inhibitor and metal. *(3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Internal Choice Option A]** **(a)** Discuss in detail the classification of corrosion inhibitors based on electrochemical action. With the help of suitable polarization diagrams, explain how anodic, cathodic, and mixed inhibitors shift the Tafel curves. *(7 marks)* **(b)** An organic inhibitor (MW = 180 g/mol) was tested at four concentrations in 1 M $H_2SO_4$ at 298 K using mild steel coupons. The following data were recorded: | Concentration $C$ (ppm) | Weight loss $W_i$ (mg) | $C/\theta$ | |---|---|---| | 100 | 38.0 | — | | 200 | 14.5 | — | | 400 | 6.2 | — | | 800 | 3.0 | — | Blank weight loss $W_0 = 90$ mg. Verify Langmuir adsorption, calculate $K_{ads}$, and determine $\Delta G_{ads}$. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution — Question A:** **(a) [7 marks]** **Step 1 — Classification:** (2 marks) Already detailed in Section 1.3 — Anodic (passivating), Cathodic (precipitation + oxygen scavenger), Mixed (adsorption-type). **Step 2 — Polarization Behavior:** The Butler-Volmer equation governs the corrosion current: $$i = i_{corr}\left[\exp\left(\frac{\eta_a}{\beta_a}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\eta_c}{\beta_c}\right)\right]$$ **Anodic Inhibitor Effect:** $\beta_a$ increases (anodic Tafel slope becomes steeper); the anodic polarization curve is pushed to the right. The intersection point (which determines $i_{corr}$) moves downward. $E_{corr}$ becomes more noble (shifts right). **Cathodic Inhibitor Effect:** $\beta_c$ increases; cathodic curve flattens; $i_{corr}$ decreases; $E_{corr}$ becomes more active (shifts left). **Mixed Inhibitor Effect:** Both $\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$ increase; $i_{corr}$ drops sharply, but $E_{corr}$ shifts only slightly. **Diagrammatic Description (must be drawn):** - X-axis: Potential $E$ (V vs SCE) - Y-axis: $\log i$ (log current density) - Three curves for anodic, cathodic, mixed compared to blank. The student should draw three superimposed Evans diagrams showing: 1. Blank polarization 2. After inhibitor addition 3. Annotated Tafel slopes and $i_{corr}$ positions **Key Inference:** A small shift in $E_{corr}$ (≤ ±85 mV vs SCE) classifies an inhibitor as **mixed**; larger shifts indicate **anodic** (more noble) or **cathodic** (more active) behaviour. *(7 marks)* --- **(b) [7 marks] — Step-by-Step Solution:** **Step 1: Calculate Inhibition Efficiency and $\theta$ for each concentration** $$IE\ \% = \frac{90 - W_i}{90} \times 100$$ | $C$ (ppm) | $W_i$ (mg) | $IE\ \%$ | $\theta = IE/100$ | |---|---|---|---| | 100 | 38.0 | 57.78 % | 0.5778 | | 200 | 14.5 | 83.89 % | 0.8389 | | 400 | 6.2 | 93.11 % | 0.9311 | | 800 | 3.0 | 96.67 % | 0.9667 | **Step 2: Calculate $C/\theta$** | $C$ (ppm) | $\theta$ | $C/\theta$ | |---|---|---| | 100 | 0.5778 | 173.07 | | 200 | 0.8389 | 238.41 | | 400 | 0.9311 | 429.61 | | 800 | 0.9667 | 827.61 | **Step 3: Linear Regression Check** Fit $C/\theta$ vs $C$. Theoretical Langmuir requires: - Slope = 1 - Intercept = $1/K_{ads}$ Computed slope from endpoints: $(827.61 - 173.07)/(800 - 100) = 654.54/700 = 0.935 \approx 1$ ✓ Intercept $\approx 173.07 - 0.935 \times 100 = 79.57$ ppm $$K_{ads} = \frac{1}{79.57\ \text{ppm}} = 1.257 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{ppm}^{-1}$$ **Step 4: Convert $K_{ads}$ to L/mol** $1\ \text{ppm} = 1\ \text{mg/L}$, and MW = 180 g/mol = 180,000 mg/mol. $$K_{ads} = 1.257 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{ppm}^{-1} \times \frac{10^6}{180,000} = 69.83\ \text{L/mol}$$ **Step 5: Free Energy of Adsorption** $$\Delta G_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$$ $$= -(8.314)(298) \ln(55.5 \times 69.83)$$ $$= -2477.57 \times \ln(3875.57)$$ $$= -2477.57 \times 8.263$$ $$\boxed{\Delta G_{ads} = -20.47\ \text{kJ/mol}}$$ **Step 6: Interpretation** (1 mark) - Negative $\Delta G_{ads}$ confirms **spontaneous adsorption**. - Magnitude (around $-20$ kJ/mol) indicates **mixed physical–chemical adsorption** (physisorption is typically $-20$ kJ/mol or less; chemisorption more negative than $-40$ kJ/mol). - Langmuir isotherm validated by slope ≈ 1. *(7 marks)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Internal Choice Option B]** **(a)** Explain the phenomenon of synergism in corrosion inhibition. Discuss the role of halide ions (e.g., $Cl^{-}$, $Br^{-}$, $I^{-}$) in enhancing the efficiency of organic inhibitors. Derive the synergistic parameter equation. *(7 marks)* **(b)** What are green corrosion inhibitors? List at least four plant extracts studied as inhibitors and explain their mechanism of action. Justify why green inhibitors are gaining industrial importance. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution — Question B:** **(a) [7 marks] — Synergism and Halide Role:** **Definition:** Synergism in corrosion inhibition is the phenomenon where the combined protective effect of two or more inhibitors is **greater than the algebraic sum** of their individual effects. Mathematically quantified by **Aramaki's Synergy Parameter**: $$S = \frac{1 - \theta_{1+2}}{(1 - \theta'_1)(1 - \theta'_2)}$$ where: - $\theta_{1+2}$ = measured surface coverage when both inhibitors are present together - $\theta'_1, \theta'_2$ = surface coverages of inhibitor 1 and 2 individually - $S > 1 \Rightarrow$ **Synergism** - $S = 1 \Rightarrow$ Additive behaviour - $S < 1 \Rightarrow$ Antagonism **Role of Halide Ions (2 marks):** The order of synergistic enhancement by halides is: **$I^{-} > Br^{-} > Cl^{-} > F^{-}$** **Mechanism (3 marks):** 1. Halide ions are specifically adsorbed on the metal surface, replacing water molecules and creating a negatively charged intermediate layer. 2. This layer attracts protonated organic inhibitor cations (e.g., protonated amines) via **electrostatic adsorption**. 3. The organic inhibitor then undergoes **chemisorption** (chemical bond formation with metal d-orbitals). 4. Iodide, being the largest and most polarizable halide, exhibits the strongest synergistic effect — known as **"iodine-effect"** or **"specific adsorption effect"**. 5. The result: dual-layer protection (halide + organic) with much higher IE than either alone. **Industrial Example:** $KI + \text{Imidazoline}$ in $H_2SO_4$ pickling — efficiency jumps from ~70 % to ~98 %. *(7 marks)* --- **(b) [7 marks] — Green Inhibitors:** **Definition:** Green inhibitors (or eco-friendly inhibitors) are corrosion inhibitors derived from natural plant sources (leaves, seeds, peels, roots) or biodegradable organic compounds that offer effective corrosion protection while being non-toxic, biodegradable, and environmentally safe. **Four Common Plant Extracts (4 marks):** 1. **Azadirachta indica (Neem) Extract:** Contains azadirachtin, nimbin, gedunin. Acts as mixed-type inhibitor; adsorbs via $-\text{OH}$, $-\text{NH}$ functional groups. 2. **Murraya koenigii (Curry Leaf) Extract:** Rich in alkaloids and flavonoids; forms protective film on mild steel in acidic media; Langmuir-type adsorption. 3. **Punica granatum (Pomegranate Peel) Extract:** Contains polyphenols, ellagitannins; effective on copper and aluminium; IE up to 90 % in $H_2SO_4$. 4. **Camellia sinensis (Tea) Extract / Tannins:** Tannic acid acts as anodic inhibitor for iron; forms iron-tannate complexes. **Mechanism of Action (2 marks):** Plant extracts contain **heteroatoms (N, O, S)** and **aromatic rings**, which enable both chemisorption and π-electron interaction with the metal surface. The adsorption follows Langmuir/Temkin isotherms, with $\Delta G_{ads}$ values typically between $-25$ to $-40$ kJ/mol. **Industrial Importance — Justification (1 mark):** - Compliance with **REACH, EPA, OSHA** regulations - Replacement of banned **chromates** (Class 1 carcinogen) - Biodegradable, low-cost, abundant raw material - Aligns with **NEP 2020 sustainability mandate** *(7 marks)* --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Zones in KTU Valuation:** > 1. **Confusing adsorption isotherm** — Students often write "Freundlich isotherm" when the data clearly fits Langmuir (linear $C/\theta$ vs $C$ plot). Check the linearity before naming the model. **−2 marks typical.** > 2. **Incorrect $E_{corr}$ shift interpretation** — Students often say "anodic inhibitor makes $E_{corr}$ more negative." Wrong. Anodic passivators shift $E_{corr}$ **more noble (positive)**. Cathodic inhibitors shift it **more active (negative)**. **−1 mark.** > 3. **Forgetting critical passivation concentration** in anodic inhibitor answers — Examiner looks specifically for the warning that below-critical dosing causes **pitting**. **−2 marks if missing.** > 4. **Mis-application of synergy parameter** — Using $S = 1 - \theta_{1+2} / (1 - \theta'_1 \theta'_2)$ instead of the correct Aramaki formula. **−2 marks.** > 5. **Not mentioning MW in $K_{ads}$ conversion** — If asked for $K_{ads}$ in L/mol, you MUST convert from ppm⁻¹ using MW. **−1 mark.** > 6. **Skipping units in final answers** — Always write $\Delta G_{ads}$ in kJ/mol or J/mol. **−0.5 mark.** --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quick-Revision Flashcard:** - [x] **Definition:** Inhibitor = chemical additive that reduces corrosion rate without changing bulk environment composition. - [x] **Three Classes (Electrochemical):** Anodic, Cathodic, Mixed. - [x] **Anodic Inhibitors:** Oxidizing agents (chromates, nitrites, molybdates) → form passive film; **dangerous if under-dosed (pitting)**. - [x] **Cathodic Inhibitors:** Phosphates, Zn salts (precipitation); Hydrazine, Na₂SO₃ (O₂ scavenger); **safe even at low concentration**. - [x] **Mixed (Adsorption) Inhibitors:** Organic amines, imidazolines; adsorb uniformly; shift both Tafel slopes. - [x] **Langmuir Isotherm Equation:** $\dfrac{C}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$; linear plot with slope ≈ 1 confirms monolayer adsorption. - [x] **Inhibition Efficiency Formula:** $IE\ \% = \dfrac{W_0 - W_i}{W_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{i_{corr,0} - i_{corr,i}}{i_{corr,0}} \times 100$. - [x] **Free Energy Test:** $\Delta G_{ads} < 0$ → spontaneous; more negative than -40 kJ/mol → chemisorption. - [x] **Synergy Parameter:** $S > 1$ → synergistic; $S < 1$ → antagonistic; halide order $I^{-} > Br^{-} > Cl^{-}$. - [x] **VPI / VCI:** DICHAN is the standard example; protects metals via volatile amine + nitrite release. - [x] **Green Inhibitors:** Neem, Curry Leaves, Pomegranate Peel, Tea Tannins — eco-friendly, NEP 2020-aligned. - [x] **Examiner's Mantra:** Always include the inhibition efficiency, surface coverage, adsorption isotherm verification, AND free energy sign — for full KTU marks. ---

topics.title: Corrosion Testing and Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Corrosion Testing and Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Corrosion Testing and Measurements** refers to the systematic, standardized, and quantitative set of experimental procedures used to evaluate the **kinetics**, **thermodynamics**, and **mechanisms** of metallic degradation in specific service environments. It bridges the gap between theoretical electrochemistry and real-world engineering performance by providing numerical parameters such as **corrosion current density ($i_{corr}$)**, **corrosion rate (CR)**, **polarization resistance ($R_p$)**, and **Tafel slopes ($\beta_a$, $\beta_c$)** that dictate material selection, life prediction, and protective coating design.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned Outcome-Based Education)**, corrosion testing is classified under Course Outcomes that emphasize:
- **CO2:** Application of electrochemical principles to analyze corrosion behavior.
- **CO3:** Selection and execution of appropriate corrosion testing methods for engineering problem-solving.
- **CO4:** Interpretation of corrosion data for predictive maintenance and design.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The "Patient Diagnostic" Approach**
> Imagine a doctor diagnosing a sick patient. The doctor doesn't just guess — they run **blood tests (lab tests)**, perform **X-rays (non-destructive tests)**, and observe the patient **walking in their natural environment (field tests)**. They measure vitals — temperature, heart rate, blood pressure — to quantify the illness.
>
> **Corrosion engineers do the EXACT same thing with metals:**
> - **Lab tests (Salt Spray, Immersion)** = Blood tests → fast, controlled, standardized
> - **Electrochemical tests (Tafel, LPR, EIS)** = ECG/Heart monitor → real-time, dynamic electrical signature
> - **Field/Plant tests (Coupons, Probes)** = Natural observation → long-term, realistic but slow
>
> Just as a fever reading of "102°F" is meaningless without context (age, symptoms, duration), a **corrosion rate of 1.5 mpy** is only meaningful when paired with **exposure time, surface area, and environment**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following standardized units are mandated by **ASTM G1, ASTM G59, and NACE TM0169** for reporting corrosion data:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Alternate Unit |
|-----------|--------|---------------|----------------|
| Corrosion Rate | CR | **mils per year (mpy)** | **mm/year** |
| Corrosion Current Density | $i_{corr}$ | **$\mu A/cm^2$** | $A/m^2$ |
| Polarization Resistance | $R_p$ | **$\Omega \cdot cm^2$** | $\Omega$ |
| Tafel Slope | $\beta_a$, $\beta_c$ | **V/decade** (or mV/decade) | — |
| Faraday's Constant | $F$ | **96,485 C/mol** | — |
| Equivalent Weight | $EW$ | dimensionless ratio (g/eq) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Every numerical corrosion problem in the university exam will expect answers in **SI units (mm/year)** unless explicitly stated otherwise. Conversion: **1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/year**.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Corrosion Tests (Overview)

Corrosion tests are broadly classified into **three tiers** based on realism, duration, and cost:

```
TIER 1: LABORATORY TESTS (Fast, controlled, repeatable)
   ├── Immersion Tests (ASTM G31)
   ├── Salt Spray / Fog Tests (ASTM B117)
   ├── Electrochemical Tests (Tafel, LPR, EIS)
   └── Humidity Tests (ASTM D2247)

TIER 2: PILOT PLANT / SIMULATED SERVICE TESTS
   ├── Heat Exchanger Loop Tests
   ├── Autoclave Tests (high T, high P)
   └── Flow-Loop / Jet Impingement Tests

TIER 3: FIELD / PLANT TESTS (Real environment, longest)
   ├── Coupon Tests (ASTM G4, G50)
   ├── Electrical Resistance Probes
   ├── Linear Polarization Resistance Probes
   └── Atmospheric Exposure Stations
```

## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Purpose and Objectives of Corrosion Testing

The **six primary objectives** of corrosion testing (as mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus) are:

1. **Material Ranking** — To compare the relative corrosion resistance of candidate alloys.
2. **Mechanism Identification** — To determine whether the corrosion is uniform, pitting, crevice, SCC, or galvanic.
3. **Service Life Prediction** — To extrapolate short-term data into years of field performance.
4. **Inhibitor / Coating Evaluation** — To quantify the effectiveness of chemical inhibitors or protective coatings.
5. **Environmental Severity Assessment** — To characterize the corrosivity of a given service environment.
6. **Quality Control / Specification Compliance** — To verify that materials meet standards like NACE MR0175 or ISO 15156.

### 2.2 The Weight Loss Method (Gravimetric Method)

The **weight loss method** is the **gold standard** of corrosion testing — simple, direct, and the most reliable for uniform corrosion. The procedure involves:

- **Step 1:** Prepare a coupon of known surface area ($A$ in $cm^2$) and initial mass ($W_i$ in grams).
- **Step 2:** Expose the coupon to the corrosive medium for a fixed time ($t$ in hours).
- **Step 3:** Remove corrosion product chemically (per **ASTM G1**) — using inhibited acid pickling.
- **Step 4:** Weigh the cleaned coupon to obtain final mass ($W_f$).
- **Step 5:** Compute mass loss $\Delta W = W_i - W_f$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why' Behind Gravimetric Testing:** Electrochemical tests give instantaneous rates, but **only the weight loss method gives the *true integrated* corrosion damage** over the entire exposure period. It is therefore used to *calibrate* all other techniques.

### 2.3 Electrochemical Measurement Methods

#### (a) Potentiodynamic Polarization (Tafel Method)

A specimen is polarized from a cathodic potential to an anodic potential (typically ±250 mV around OCP) at a slow scan rate (0.1–1 mV/s). The resulting **semi-log plot** of potential (E) vs. log(current density) yields two linear regions — the **anodic Tafel line** and the **cathodic Tafel line**. Their intersection gives:

$$E_{corr} \text{ (corrosion potential)} \quad \text{and} \quad i_{corr} \text{ (corrosion current density)}$$

The **Tafel equation** governs this behavior:

$$\eta = \beta \log\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$$

where $\eta$ is the overpotential, $\beta$ is the Tafel slope, and $i_0$ is the exchange current density.

#### (b) Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR) Method — Stern-Geary Equation

Polarizing the specimen by only **±10 to ±30 mV** around OCP yields a nearly linear region. The slope $\frac{\Delta E}{\Delta i}$ gives the **polarization resistance $R_p$**. The Stern-Geary equation (1957) connects $R_p$ to $i_{corr}$:

$$\boxed{i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{B}{R_p}}$$

where $B$ is the **Stern-Geary constant**, typically **25–52 mV** for most metal-electrolyte systems (commonly taken as **26 mV** for active systems and **52 mV** for passive systems).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why LPR is the industry favorite:** It is **non-destructive**, **rapid** (response in minutes), and can be deployed as a **field probe** for real-time corrosion monitoring in refineries, pipelines, and offshore platforms.

#### (c) Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS)

A small sinusoidal AC perturbation (5–10 mV amplitude) is applied over a wide frequency range (typically **$10^5$ Hz to $10^{-2}$ Hz**). The resulting impedance spectrum is fitted to an **equivalent circuit model** (Randles circuit, etc.) to extract:
- $R_s$ (solution resistance)
- $R_{ct}$ (charge transfer resistance ∝ 1/corrosion rate)
- $C_{dl}$ (double-layer capacitance)

EIS separates the contributions of **corrosion reaction kinetics**, **diffusion**, and **coating capacitance** — making it indispensable for **coated systems and inhibitor studies**.

### 2.4 Salt Spray (Fog) Testing — ASTM B117

A 5% NaCl solution is atomized into a chamber maintained at **35°C**, where salt-laden fog settles on test panels at an angle of **15–30° from vertical**. Exposure durations typically range from **24 to 1000+ hours**. The test is **purely qualitative** — used to compare coating performance, not to predict service life.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Salt spray is *not* a corrosion *rate* test. Students often incorrectly report "corrosion rate in mpy" from salt spray data. The ASTM standard explicitly warns that salt spray results **do not correlate with real atmospheric exposure**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit | Boundary / Validity |
|---|---------|-------------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | $\Delta W = W_i - W_f$ | Mass loss (gravimetric) | g (or mg) | ASTM G1 cleaning required |
| 2 | $CR_{mpy} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Corrosion rate (mils per year) | mpy | $W$ in mg, $D$ in g/cm³, $A$ in in², $t$ in hr |
| 3 | $CR_{mm/y} = \frac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Corrosion rate (mm/year) | mm/yr | $W$ in mg, $D$ in g/cm³, $A$ in cm², $t$ in hr |
| 4 | $EW = \frac{\text{Atomic Weight}}{n}$ | Equivalent weight | g/eq | $n$ = electrons transferred |
| 5 | $i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p}$ | Stern-Geary relation | $\mu A/cm^2$ | $\pm 10$ mV polarization |
| 6 | $B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | Stern-Geary constant | V (or mV) | $B \approx 26$ mV (active), 52 mV (passive) |
| 7 | $CR_{mm/y} = 0.00327 \cdot \frac{i_{corr} \cdot EW}{D}$ | Faraday's conversion | mm/yr | $i_{corr}$ in $\mu A/cm^2$ |
| 8 | $m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot EW}{F}$ | Faraday's mass loss law | g | $I$ in A, $t$ in s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Constants to Memorize for KTU:**
> - **534** → Imperial (mpy, mg, in², hr)
> - **87.6** → SI (mm/y, mg, cm², hr)
> - **0.00327** → Electrochemical → mm/y
> - **1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/year**

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Preferred Test | Why? |
|----------|---------------|------|
| Oil & Gas (Pipelines) | **LPR Probes + ER Probes** | Real-time monitoring in sour (H₂S) service |
| Marine / Shipbuilding | **Salt Spray + Immersion** | Standardized ASTM ranking |
| Aerospace (Coatings) | **EIS** | Separates coating degradation from substrate corrosion |
| Chemical Process Plants | **Autoclave + Weight Loss** | High-T/high-P environments |
| Automotive | **Cyclic Corrosion Tests (CCT)** | Simulates road salt + humidity cycles |
| Cathodic Protection Design | **Tafel Polarization** | Determines protection current requirement |

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Corrosion Rate from Mass Loss (Gravimetric)

**Starting Principle:** Corrosion is an electrochemical process. For a metal M oxidizing as:

$$M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$$

By **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis**, the mass of metal dissolved is directly proportional to the total electric charge passed:

$$m \propto Q \quad \text{where} \quad Q = I \cdot t$$

For a sample of surface area $A$ and exposure time $t$, the corrosion current density is:

$$i_{corr} = \frac{I}{A}$$

The mass loss is:

$$\Delta W = m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot EW}{F} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot A \cdot t \cdot EW}{F}$$

To convert this into a **linear corrosion rate (penetration depth per year)**, we use the definition:

$$CR = \frac{\text{Penetration Depth}}{\text{Time}} = \frac{\Delta W / (D \cdot A)}{t}$$

where $D$ is the metal density.

Now we substitute the expression for $\Delta W$:

$$\Delta W = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot A \cdot t \cdot EW}{F}$$

And the area $A$ cancels out (showing that **corrosion rate is independent of sample area**, a key insight):

$$CR = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot t \cdot EW}{F \cdot D \cdot t} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot EW}{F \cdot D}$$

This gives instantaneous rate in (g-equivalent / Coulomb × cm). To get mm/year, we apply unit conversions:
- Convert $i_{corr}$ from A/cm² to $\mu A/cm²$ (multiply by $10^6$)
- Convert F from C/mol to $\mu A \cdot yr / mol$: $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol} = 96{,}485 \times \frac{1}{31{,}536{,}000} \text{ A·yr/mol}$ → combine with $10^6$ for $\mu A$

After careful unit conversion, the final **SI form** is:

$$\boxed{CR \text{ (mm/yr)} = \frac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{D}}$$

And the **Imperial form** (mils per year, mpy):

$$\boxed{CR \text{ (mpy)} = \frac{0.129 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{D}}$$

where $i_{corr}$ is in $\mu A/cm^2$ and $D$ in $g/cm^3$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Stern-Geary Equation (Linear Polarization Resistance)

**Step 1: Start from the Butler-Volmer Equation**

The net current density for a corrosion reaction near the corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$) is given by the Butler-Volmer equation:

$$i = i_{corr}\left[\exp\left(\frac{2.303(\eta)}{\beta_a}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{2.303(\eta)}{\beta_c}\right)\right]$$

where $\eta = E - E_{corr}$ is the overpotential.

**Step 2: Apply the Linearization Condition**

When $|\eta| \ll \frac{\beta}{2.303}$ (i.e., $|\eta| < 10$ mV typically), we use the Taylor series approximation:

$$\exp(x) \approx 1 + x + \frac{x^2}{2} + \ldots \approx 1 + x \quad \text{(for small x)}$$

Applying this to both exponentials:

$$i \approx i_{corr}\left[\left(1 + \frac{2.303 \eta}{\beta_a}\right) - \left(1 - \frac{2.303 \eta}{\beta_c}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3: Simplify**

The 1's cancel:

$$i \approx i_{corr} \cdot 2.303 \cdot \eta \left(\frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c}\right)$$

Combining the fractions:

$$i \approx i_{corr} \cdot 2.303 \cdot \eta \cdot \frac{\beta_a + \beta_c}{\beta_a \beta_c}$$

**Step 4: Define Polarization Resistance**

By definition, $R_p = \frac{d\eta}{di}\bigg|_{\eta=0}$, and in the linear regime $\eta / i$ is constant:

$$R_p = \frac{\eta}{i} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot i_{corr} (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

**Step 5: Invert to Obtain Stern-Geary Equation**

$$\boxed{i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{B}{R_p}}$$

where the Stern-Geary constant $B$ is defined as:

$$B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

For most active corroding systems, $\beta_a \approx \beta_c \approx 0.1$ V/decade, giving:

$$B = \frac{(0.1)^2}{2.303 \times 0.2} = \frac{0.01}{0.4606} \approx 0.0217 \text{ V} \approx 26 \text{ mV}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Corrosion Rate from Mass Loss (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A cylindrical mild steel coupon of diameter **2.0 cm** and length **5.0 cm** is exposed to seawater for **720 hours**. The initial mass is **125.456 g** and the final mass (after ASTM G1 cleaning) is **125.312 g**. The density of mild steel is **7.86 g/cm³**. Calculate the corrosion rate in **(a) mpy** and **(b) mm/year**.

**Step 1: Compute Mass Loss**

$$\Delta W = W_i - W_f = 125.456 - 125.312 = 0.144 \text{ g} = 144 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 2: Compute Surface Area**

For a cylinder: $A = \pi D L + 2 \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ (sides + two end caps)

$$A_{side} = \pi \times 2.0 \times 5.0 = 31.416 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$A_{ends} = 2 \times \frac{\pi (2.0)^2}{4} = 6.283 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$A_{total} = 31.416 + 6.283 = 37.699 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute CR in mpy (Imperial)**

Using $CR_{mpy} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$:

Convert $A$ to in²: $A = 37.699 / 6.4516 = 5.843 \text{ in}^2$

$$CR = \frac{534 \times 144}{7.86 \times 5.843 \times 720}$$

$$CR = \frac{76{,}896}{33{,}083} = 2.324 \text{ mpy}$$

**Step 4: Compute CR in mm/year (SI)**

Using $CR_{mm/y} = \frac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$:

$$CR = \frac{87.6 \times 144}{7.86 \times 37.699 \times 720}$$

$$CR = \frac{12{,}614.4}{213{,}310} = 0.0591 \text{ mm/yr}$$

**Step 5: Cross-Verification**

$$0.0591 \text{ mm/yr} \div 0.0254 = 2.328 \text{ mpy} \quad \checkmark$$

The values match (small rounding error), confirming both calculations.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — $i_{corr}$ from LPR (Stern-Geary)

**Problem:** A carbon steel electrode in a 3% NaCl solution exhibits $R_p = 440 \text{ }\Omega\cdot cm^2$ when polarized $\pm 10$ mV around OCP. Tafel slopes are $\beta_a = 90$ mV/dec and $\beta_c = 120$ mV/dec. Density = 7.86 g/cm³, equivalent weight $EW = 27.92$ g/eq. Find $i_{corr}$ and CR in mm/yr.

**Step 1: Calculate Stern-Geary Constant $B$**

$$B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{90 \times 120}{2.303 \times (90 + 120)}$$

$$B = \frac{10800}{2.303 \times 210} = \frac{10800}{483.6} = 22.33 \text{ mV} = 0.02233 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Calculate $i_{corr}$**

$$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p} = \frac{0.02233}{440} = 5.075 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A/cm}^2 = 50.75 \text{ }\mu A/cm^2$$

**Step 3: Calculate CR in mm/year**

$$CR = \frac{0.00327 \times i_{corr} \times EW}{D}$$

$$CR = \frac{0.00327 \times 50.75 \times 27.92}{7.86}$$

$$CR = \frac{4.636}{7.86} = 0.590 \text{ mm/yr} \approx 23.2 \text{ mpy}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A corrosion rate of **0.59 mm/year (~23 mpy)** for carbon steel in seawater is considered **"high"** — typical service life for an uncoated pipe would be only a few years. NACE classifies this as **"severe"** corrosivity.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

*What is meant by the Stern-Geary equation? Why is the Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR) technique preferred over the Tafel extrapolation method for routine corrosion rate monitoring in industrial plants?*

**Model Answer:**

The **Stern-Geary equation** (1957) relates the corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ to the **polarization resistance** $R_p$ measured near the corrosion potential:

$$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p}, \quad \text{where} \quad B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

$B$ is the **Stern-Geary constant** (≈ 26 mV for active systems, 52 mV for passive systems).

**Why LPR is preferred over Tafel extrapolation in industry:**

| Criterion | LPR Method | Tafel Method |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| Polarization range | ±10–30 mV | ±250 mV (faradaic) |
| Destructiveness | **Non-destructive** | Destructive (alters surface) |
| Test duration | **Seconds–minutes** | 30–60 min |
| Suitability for online monitoring | **Yes (field probes)** | No (lab only) |
| Accuracy for passive alloys | Lower | Higher |

The small polarization in LPR does not perturb the corrosion mechanism, allowing **continuous, real-time monitoring** of plant equipment without shutting down operations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

*List the steps involved in determining the corrosion rate by the weight loss (gravimetric) method as per ASTM G1. State the formula used for calculating corrosion rate in mpy and explain each term.*

**Model Answer:**

**Procedure (ASTM G1):**
1. Prepare the metal coupon (cut, grind, polish, degrease, dry).
2. Measure initial dimensions (for surface area $A$).
3. Weigh the coupon accurately ($W_i$).
4. Expose to corrosive medium for fixed time ($t$).
5. Remove and clean per ASTM G1 (chemical pickling with inhibited acid).
6. Reweigh to obtain final mass ($W_f$).

**Formula for CR in mpy:**

$$CR \text{ (mpy)} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$$

Where:
- **$W$** = mass loss in mg
- **$D$** = density of the metal in g/cm³
- **$A$** = exposed surface area in in²
- **$t$** = exposure time in hours
- **534** = unit conversion constant (combines time, area, and density conversions)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Module 2 Pitfalls:**
> - **Unit conversion mistakes** between mpy ↔ mm/year cost **2–3 marks** in nearly every numerical.
> - Forgetting to convert **surface area from cm² to in²** (or vice versa) in mixed-unit problems.
> - Confusing **$i_{corr}$ units** ($\mu A/cm^2$ vs. $A/cm^2$) — the constant **0.00327** assumes $\mu A/cm^2$.
> - For LPR problems, **never assume $B = 26$ mV blindly** — always compute $B$ from the given $\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$.
> - In the weight loss method, students often forget that **both end faces** of a cylindrical coupon are exposed to the electrolyte.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Module 2 — Corrosion Testing and Measurements)*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **potentiostatic polarization (Tafel extrapolation)** method for determining the corrosion current density of a metal in an electrolyte. Explain how the Tafel slopes and $i_{corr}$ are obtained from the experimental polarization curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An aluminum alloy (density 2.71 g/cm³, equivalent weight 8.99 g/eq) is tested in 3.5% NaCl solution using the LPR technique. The polarization resistance is **1850 $\Omega \cdot cm^2$** and the Tafel slopes are $\beta_a = 80$ mV/dec and $\beta_c = 110$ mV/dec. Calculate:
- (i) The Stern-Geary constant $B$
- (ii) The corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ in $\mu A/cm^2$
- (iii) The corrosion rate in **mm/year** and **mpy**

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

The **potentiodynamic polarization (Tafel) method** uses a **three-electrode electrochemical cell** comprising:
1. **Working Electrode (WE):** The test metal specimen embedded in epoxy with a defined exposed area (~1 cm²).
2. **Reference Electrode (RE):** Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE) or Ag/AgCl — measures potential without carrying current.
3. **Counter Electrode (CE):** Platinum or graphite — completes the circuit and carries the polarizing current.

A **potentiostat** applies a linearly varying potential from approximately **−250 mV** (cathodic) to **+250 mV** (anodic) relative to the **Open Circuit Potential (OCP / $E_{corr}$)** at a slow scan rate (0.1–1 mV/s). The resulting current is recorded.

**Analysis:**
- A plot of **E (y-axis) vs. log |i| (x-axis)** is drawn.
- The cathodic branch (negative currents) and anodic branch (positive currents) appear as approximately linear regions at higher overpotentials (typically |η| > 50 mV).
- The **slopes** of these linear regions are the **cathodic Tafel slope ($\beta_c$)** and **anodic Tafel slope ($\beta_a$)**.
- **Extrapolating** these two linear Tafel lines back to their **intersection point** gives:
  - The **corrosion potential** $E_{corr}$ on the y-axis.
  - The **corrosion current density** $i_{corr}$ on the x-axis (read at the intersection).

This $i_{corr}$ value is the **kinetic parameter** used to compute the corrosion rate via Faraday's law. The Tafel method requires the metal to be in the **activation-controlled regime**; it is unsuitable for highly passive alloys where Tafel regions are ill-defined.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Density, $D = 2.71$ g/cm³
- Equivalent weight, $EW = 8.99$ g/eq
- Polarization resistance, $R_p = 1850$ $\Omega \cdot cm^2$
- $\beta_a = 80$ mV/dec, $\beta_c = 110$ mV/dec

**(i) Stern-Geary Constant $B$:**

$$B = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{80 \times 110}{2.303 \times (80 + 110)}$$

$$B = \frac{8800}{2.303 \times 190} = \frac{8800}{437.57}$$

$$\boxed{B = 20.11 \text{ mV} = 0.02011 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) Corrosion Current Density $i_{corr}$:**

$$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p} = \frac{0.02011 \text{ V}}{1850 \text{ }\Omega \cdot cm^2}$$

$$i_{corr} = 1.087 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A/cm}^2$$

$$\boxed{i_{corr} = 10.87 \text{ }\mu A/cm^2}$$

**(iii) Corrosion Rate in mm/year:**

$$CR = \frac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{D}$$

$$CR = \frac{0.00327 \times 10.87 \times 8.99}{2.71}$$

$$CR = \frac{0.3194}{2.71}$$

$$\boxed{CR = 0.1179 \text{ mm/year}}$$

**Corrosion Rate in mpy:**

$$CR_{mpy} = \frac{CR_{mm/y}}{0.0254} = \frac{0.1179}{0.0254}$$

$$\boxed{CR = 4.64 \text{ mpy}}$$

**Interpretation:** This corrosion rate (~4.6 mpy) classifies the Al alloy as having **"good"** corrosion resistance in NaCl — typical of aluminum's passive Al₂O₃ layer, which slows down uniform corrosion but may still be susceptible to **pitting**.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice — Module 2)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Retest Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **weight loss method** of corrosion rate determination in detail. Derive the relationship between corrosion current density ($i_{corr}$) and corrosion rate (CR) in **mm/year** using Faraday's law. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mild steel coupon of dimensions **3 cm × 2 cm × 0.3 cm** is immersed in **1M H₂SO₄** for **48 hours**. The initial mass is **13.652 g** and the final mass after cleaning is **13.580 g**. The density of mild steel is **7.86 g/cm³** and its equivalent weight is **27.92 g/eq**. Calculate:
- (i) The corrosion rate in **mpy** and **mm/year**
- (ii) The equivalent corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ in $\mu A/cm^2$

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**The Weight Loss (Gravimetric) Method — ASTM G31 / G1:**

This is the **most fundamental and universally accepted** corrosion testing technique. A metal specimen (coupon) of precisely known dimensions and mass is exposed to a corrosive environment for a specified duration. The **mass loss** due to corrosion is measured after **chemical cleaning** to remove adherent corrosion products.

**Detailed Procedure:**
1. **Specimen Preparation:** Cut the coupon to defined dimensions; grind with successive grit SiC papers (120, 240, 400, 600); degrease with acetone; dry and weigh on an analytical balance (precision ±0.1 mg).
2. **Surface Area Calculation:** Use vernier calipers/micrometer for accurate dimensions.
3. **Exposure:** Suspend the coupon in the test medium using a non-conductive string or holder, ensuring full contact with the electrolyte. Maintain constant temperature.
4. **Retrieval & Cleaning:** After exposure, remove the coupon and **clean per ASTM G1** (e.g., Clarke's solution for steel: 20 g Sb₂O₃ + 50 g SnCl₂ + 1000 mL HCl) to dissolve corrosion products without attacking the base metal.
5. **Final Weighing:** $W_f$ is recorded.
6. **Calculation:** Apply the standard formula.

**Derivation of CR from $i_{corr}$:**

Starting with the electrochemical oxidation reaction:

$$M \rightarrow M^{n+} + n e^-$$

By **Faraday's First Law**, the mass of metal dissolved $\Delta W$ equals the mass equivalent to the total charge $Q = i_{corr} \cdot A \cdot t$ passed:

$$\Delta W = \frac{Q \cdot EW}{F} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot A \cdot t \cdot EW}{F}$$

The **corrosion rate as penetration depth per unit time** is:

$$CR = \frac{\text{Volume dissolved per unit area}}{\text{Time}} = \frac{\Delta W / D}{A \cdot t}$$

Substituting:

$$CR = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot A \cdot t \cdot EW / (F \cdot D)}{A \cdot t} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot EW}{F \cdot D}$$

Applying SI unit conversions ($i_{corr}$ in $\mu A/cm^2$, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, time unit conversion factor for "per year"):

$$\boxed{CR \text{ (mm/year)} = \frac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{D}}$$

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Coupon dimensions: $3 \text{ cm} \times 2 \text{ cm} \times 0.3 \text{ cm}$
- Time: $t = 48$ hours
- Initial mass: $W_i = 13.652$ g
- Final mass: $W_f = 13.580$ g
- Density: $D = 7.86$ g/cm³
- $EW = 27.92$ g/eq

**(i) Corrosion Rate Calculation:**

**Mass Loss:**
$$\Delta W = 13.652 - 13.580 = 0.072 \text{ g} = 72 \text{ mg}$$

**Total Surface Area (all 6 faces of rectangular coupon):**
$$A = 2 \times (3 \times 2) + 2 \times (3 \times 0.3) + 2 \times (2 \times 0.3)$$
$$A = 2(6) + 2(0.9) + 2(0.6) = 12 + 1.8 + 1.2 = 15.0 \text{ cm}^2$$

**CR in mpy (Imperial formula, area in in²):**
$$A_{in^2} = 15.0 / 6.4516 = 2.325 \text{ in}^2$$

$$CR_{mpy} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A_{in^2} \cdot t} = \frac{534 \times 72}{7.86 \times 2.325 \times 48}$$

$$CR = \frac{38{,}448}{877.13} = 43.83 \text{ mpy}$$

**CR in mm/year (SI formula, area in cm²):**
$$CR_{mm/y} = \frac{87.6 \times 72}{7.86 \times 15.0 \times 48} = \frac{6307.2}{5659.2} = 1.115 \text{ mm/year}$$

**Cross-Check:**
$$1.115 / 0.0254 = 43.90 \text{ mpy} \quad \checkmark$$

**(ii) Equivalent Corrosion Current Density:**

Rearranging the Faraday-based equation:

$$i_{corr} = \frac{CR_{mm/y} \cdot D}{0.00327 \cdot EW} = \frac{1.115 \times 7.86}{0.00327 \times 27.92}$$

$$i_{corr} = \frac{8.764}{0.09130} = 96.0 \text{ }\mu A/cm^2$$

$$\boxed{i_{corr} \approx 96.0 \text{ }\mu A/cm^2}$$

**Interpretation:** A corrosion rate of **~44 mpy (~1.1 mm/yr)** in 1M H₂SO₄ is **highly aggressive** — consistent with mild steel being readily attacked by non-inhibited sulfuric acid.
