This is why industrial sulfuric acid storage requires protective coatings or alloy upgrades (e.g., to 316L stainless steel).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 — Corrosion Testing and Measurements: Quick-Fire Revision**

**🔑 Core Concepts:**
- Corrosion testing is performed to **rank materials, identify mechanisms, predict service life, evaluate coatings, and monitor plant integrity**.
- Tests are classified into **Lab (Tier 1), Pilot Plant (Tier 2), and Field (Tier 3)** — each with different cost, duration, and realism.

**🔑 Weight Loss Method:**
- The **gold standard** — directly measures integrated corrosion damage.
- Always clean per **ASTM G1** before reweighing.
- Formula: $CR_{mpy} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ and $CR_{mm/y} = \frac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$.
- Remember to include **all exposed faces** when calculating area.

**🔑 Electrochemical Methods:**
- **Tafel extrapolation:** ±250 mV scan → log plot → linear regions give $\beta_a$, $\beta_c$, and $i_{corr}$ at the intersection.
- **LPR / Stern-Geary:** small ±10–30 mV polarization → $R_p = \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta i}$ → $i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p}$.
- **EIS:** AC perturbation → impedance spectrum → equivalent circuit fitting → separates reaction, diffusion, and coating contributions.

**🔑 Faraday's Law Conversion:**
$$CR_{mm/y} = \frac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{D}$$
- Constants: $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol; **0.00327** assumes $i_{corr}$ in $\mu A/cm^2$.

**🔑 Salt Spray (ASTM B117):**
- 5% NaCl, 35°C, fog atmosphere, panels at 15–30°.
- **Qualitative only** — does NOT measure corrosion rate.
- Cannot be used to predict real-world atmospheric life.

**🔑 Equivalent Weight:**
$$EW = \frac{\text{Atomic Weight}}{n}$$
where $n$ = number of electrons in the oxidation reaction (e.g., Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻, so $n = 2$, $EW = 55.85/2 = 27.92$).

**🔑 NACE Corrosivity Classification (must memorize for KTU):**

| CR (mpy) | CR (mm/yr) | Class |
|----------|-----------|-------|
| < 1 | < 0.025 | Negligible |
| 1–4.9 | 0.025–0.12 | Low |
| 5–10 | 0.12–0.25 | Moderate |
| 10–50 | 0.25–1.27 | High |
| > 50 | > 1.27 | Severe |

**🔑 Key ASTM Standards to Remember:**
- **ASTM G1** — Cleaning of corrosion products
- **ASTM G31** — Laboratory immersion testing
- **ASTM B117** — Salt spray (fog) testing
- **ASTM G5** — Reference test method for potentiodynamic polarization
- **ASTM G59** — LPR measurement
- **ASTM G4 / G50** — Atmospheric / field exposure

**🔑 Stern-Geary Constant Quick Values:**
- Active systems: $B \approx 26$ mV
- Passive systems: $B \approx 52$ mV
- **Never** hardcode these unless the problem explicitly says "assume" — otherwise compute from $\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$.

**🔑 Conversion Factors (always carry these in your exam):**
- 1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/year
- 1 in² = 6.4516 cm²
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol = 26.8 A·hr/mol
- 1 year = 8760 hours ≈ 31.536 × 10⁶ seconds

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy:** When solving numerical problems in the exam, **always show unit conversions as a separate step**. KTU examiners award marks for *methodology* — even if the final number is off due to a single conversion error, you secure partial credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characterisation Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Characterisation Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Characterisation Techniques** in Surface Engineering refer to the systematic set of analytical, microscopic, spectroscopic, diffraction, mechanical, and electrochemical methods employed to probe the **composition, structure, morphology, topography, bonding, phase content, residual stress, and degradation behaviour** of engineered surfaces, coatings, thin films, and corroded interfaces.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, characterisation is treated as the **"verification pillar"** of any surface modification or corrosion mitigation strategy — without quantitative characterisation, a coating is merely an unverified hypothesis. The techniques are categorised into four functional families:

| Family | Function | Typical Techniques |
|---|---|---|
| **Microscopy** | Visualise topography/microstructure | OM, SEM, TEM, AFM, STM |
| **Spectroscopy** | Identify elemental/chemical states | EDS, XPS, AES, FTIR, Raman |
| **Diffraction** | Identify phases, crystal structure, stress | XRD, GIXRD, EBSD |
| **Mechanical/Electrochemical** | Quantify performance | Hardness, Adhesion, Wear, Tafel, EIS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Characterisation techniques are **NOT** studied as isolated instruments. They are studied as **integrated analytical workflows** — i.e., what technique answers *which specific* question about a coating/corrosion system. A 14-mark KTU question will typically ask you to **select and justify a combination** of techniques for a given engineering problem.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Surface Forensics Lab"

Imagine you are a **forensic detective** arriving at a crime scene (your coating/corrosion site). You cannot solve the case with just one tool:

- **Optical Microscope (OM)** → Your naked eye with a flashlight. You see the *overall layout, colour change, large cracks, pits*.
- **SEM (Scanning Electron Microscope)** → A high-powered magnifying glass revealing *fine scratches, pore architecture, fracture morphology* at the micrometre scale.
- **TEM** → An X-ray vision that looks *through* the sample, revealing crystal lattice defects, grain boundaries, and nano-precipitates.
- **XRD (X-Ray Diffraction)** → A "fingerprint database" that identifies *which crystalline phases* are present (like matching a fingerprint to a criminal record).
- **XPS / EDS** → A "chemistry sniffer" that tells you *exactly which elements and chemical bonds* exist on the surface (1–10 nm depth).
- **Hardness/Adhesion Tests** → A "stress test" on the coating — can it withstand real-world loading?
- **Electrochemical Tests (Tafel/EIS)** → A "rate-clock" measuring how fast corrosion is happening right now.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Characterisation:** Always begin with the **cheapest, lowest-resolution tool** (optical microscopy) and progressively move to **higher-resolution, more expensive tools** (SEM → TEM → XPS). This is called the **"Characterisation Hierarchy"** — and KTU examiners *love* testing this workflow logic.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bold)

The following constants appear repeatedly in KTU problems:

- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- **Wavelength of Cu K$\alpha$ radiation:** $\lambda = 1.5406$ Å
- **Wavelength of Co K$\alpha$ radiation:** $\lambda = 1.78897$ Å
- **Resolution limit (Abbe):** $d = \dfrac{0.61 \lambda}{NA}$
- **Typical SEM accelerating voltage:** **1–30 kV**
- **Typical TEM accelerating voltage:** **60–300 kV**
- **XPS analysis depth:** **1–10 nm** (top 3–5 atomic layers)
- **EDS analysis depth:** **0.5–3 µm** (bulk-sensitive)
- **AFM lateral resolution:** **~0.1 nm**; vertical resolution: **~0.01 nm**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Optical Microscopy (OM)

**Operating Principle:** Visible light (400–700 nm) reflected from a polished/etched metallographic surface passes through objective and eyepiece lenses to produce a magnified image.

**Key Equations:**

$$\text{Magnification} = M_{obj} \times M_{eye}$$

$$\text{Resolving Power} = d = \frac{0.61 \lambda}{NA}$$

where $NA$ is the numerical aperture of the objective lens.

**KTU Point:** OM is limited to a resolution of approximately **200 nm** because of the diffraction limit of visible light. It cannot resolve nanoscale features — hence the need for SEM/TEM/AFM.

---

### 2.2 Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)

**Operating Principle:** A focused electron beam (1–30 keV) scans the surface; secondary electrons (SE) and backscattered electrons (BSE) are detected to form an image.

- **SE** → Topographic contrast (resolution ~1–10 nm)
- **BSE** → Atomic number (Z) contrast — heavier elements appear brighter
- **EDS detector** → Captures characteristic X-rays for elemental analysis

**De Broglie Wavelength of Electron (CRITICAL FORMULA):**

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2meV}}$$

For $V$ in volts and substituting constants:

$$\lambda(\text{in Å}) = \sqrt{\frac{150}{V}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** At 30 kV, the electron wavelength is ~0.07 Å — over **100,000× shorter** than visible light. This is why SEM/TEM achieve sub-nanometre resolution.

---

### 2.3 Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM)

**Operating Principle:** High-energy electrons (60–300 keV) are transmitted through an ultra-thin specimen (<100 nm). The interaction produces diffraction patterns and high-resolution images.

**Modes:**
- **Bright-Field (BF):** Mass-thickness contrast
- **Dark-Field (DF):** Diffraction contrast → reveals individual grains
- **HRTEM:** Direct lattice imaging
- **SAED (Selected Area Electron Diffraction):** Local crystal structure

---

### 2.4 X-Ray Diffraction (XRD)

**Operating Principle:** Monochromatic X-rays incident on a crystalline material undergo constructive interference from lattice planes satisfying **Bragg's Law**.

$$n\lambda = 2d_{hkl} \sin\theta$$

**Scherrer Equation (Crystallite Size):**

$$D = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta \cos\theta}$$

where:
- $D$ = average crystallite size (nm)
- $K$ = Scherrer constant (**0.9 for spherical crystallites**)
- $\lambda$ = X-ray wavelength
- $\beta$ = Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) in **radians**
- $\theta$ = Bragg angle

**Williamson-Hall Equation (Size + Microstrain):**

$$\beta \cos\theta = \frac{K\lambda}{D} + 4\varepsilon \sin\theta$$

A plot of $\beta\cos\theta$ (y-axis) vs $4\sin\theta$ (x-axis) gives slope = microstrain $\varepsilon$ and intercept = $\frac{K\lambda}{D}$.

---

### 2.5 X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS / ESCA)

**Operating Principle:** Soft X-rays (typically Al K$\alpha$ = 1486.6 eV or Mg K$\alpha$ = 1253.6 eV) eject core-level photoelectrons; their binding energy is element- and chemical-state-specific.

$$E_{binding} = h\nu - E_{kinetic} - \phi_{workfunction}$$

**Depth Probed:** **1–10 nm** (true surface-sensitive)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why XPS is the "Gold Standard" for Surface Engineering:** Every element except H and He can be detected. Chemical shifts of 0.1–3 eV distinguish oxides from metals (e.g., metallic Fe at 706.7 eV vs Fe₂O₃ at 710.8 eV for Fe 2p₃/₂).

---

### 2.6 Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)

**Operating Principle:** A sharp tip (radius ~10 nm) on a cantilever scans the surface; deflection is monitored by a laser-photodiode system, giving **3D topography** at sub-nanometre resolution.

**Force-Distance Relationship:**

$$F = -k \cdot \Delta z$$

where $k$ is the cantilever spring constant (typically **0.01–50 N/m**).

**Modes:** Contact, Tapping (intermittent), Non-contact

---

### 2.7 Hardness Testing (Micro & Nanoindentation)

**Vickers Hardness:**

$$HV = \frac{1.8544 \cdot F}{d^2}$$

where $F$ is in kgf and $d$ is the diagonal of the indent in mm.

**Nanoindentation — Oliver-Pharr Method:**

$$H = \frac{P_{max}}{A_c}$$

$$E_r = \frac{S \sqrt{\pi}}{2\sqrt{A_c}}$$

where:
- $H$ = hardness
- $P_{max}$ = peak load
- $A_c$ = contact area at peak load
- $S$ = contact stiffness = $\frac{dP}{dh}$ at unloading onset
- $E_r$ = reduced modulus

---

### 2.8 Adhesion Testing

| Test | Principle | Quantitative Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Tape Test (ASTM D3359)** | Cross-hatch + peel with tape | Pass/Fail or 0B–5B rating |
| **Pull-off Test (ASTM D4541)** | Tensile pull with dolly | Pull-off stress (MPa) |
| **Scratch Test (ASTM C1624)** | Progressive normal load until delamination | Critical load $L_c$ (N) |
| **Bend Test** | Mandrel bending to 180° | Pass/Fail |

---

### 2.9 Electrochemical Corrosion Characterisation

**Tafel Extrapolation (Polarisation):**

$$\eta = a \pm b \log \vert i \vert$$

where $\eta$ is overpotential, $b$ is the Tafel slope, and $i$ is current density.

**Stern-Geary Equation (for $i_{corr}$ from $R_p$):**

$$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p}$$

where $B = \dfrac{b_a \cdot b_c}{2.303(b_a + b_c)}$ and $R_p$ is polarisation resistance.

**Corrosion Rate (Pen Test / Mass Loss converted to mpy):**

$$CR (mpy) = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$$

where $W$ = weight loss (mg), $D$ = density (g/cm³), $A$ = area (in²), $t$ = time (hr).

---

### 2.10 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Equation | Application | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ | Bragg's Law (XRD) | $\theta$ in degrees or radians |
| 2 | $D = \dfrac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}$ | Scherrer crystallite size | $\beta$ **must be in radians** |
| 3 | $\beta\cos\theta = \dfrac{K\lambda}{D} + 4\varepsilon\sin\theta$ | Williamson-Hall (size + strain) | Slope = $\varepsilon$, intercept gives $D$ |
| 4 | $\lambda(\text{Å}) = \sqrt{\dfrac{150}{V}}$ | Electron wavelength in TEM/SEM | $V$ in volts |
| 5 | $d = \dfrac{0.61\lambda}{NA}$ | Abbe diffraction limit (OM) | $NA$ dimensionless |
| 6 | $E_b = h\nu - E_k - \phi$ | XPS binding energy | $E_b$ in eV |
| 7 | $HV = \dfrac{1.8544 F}{d^2}$ | Vickers hardness | $F$ in kgf, $d$ in mm |
| 8 | $H = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_c}$ | Nanoindentation hardness | $A_c$ from tip calibration |
| 9 | $\eta = a \pm b \log \vert i \vert$ | Tafel equation | $b$ = 30–120 mV/dec typical |
| 10 | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{B}{R_p}$ | Stern-Geary | $B \approx$ 13–52 mV |
| 11 | $CR(mpy) = \dfrac{534 W}{D A t}$ | Corrosion rate from mass loss | Imperial units |
| 12 | $\sigma_{residual} = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)} \cdot \dfrac{d_{hkl} - d_0}{d_0}$ | Residual stress (XRD sin²ψ) | $E$ = Young's modulus, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio |
| 13 | $L_c \propto \dfrac{H}{\sqrt{R}}$ | Scratch test critical load | $H$ = hardness, $R$ = stylus radius |
| 14 | $Z = R_s + \dfrac{R_{ct}}{1 + (j\omega R_{ct} C_{dl})^2}$ | EIS Nyquist semicircle | $\omega$ = angular frequency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Reality Check:** Memorising equations alone is insufficient. Examiners award marks for **"identifying when each equation is applicable"**. Always state the assumption (e.g., "Scherrer assumes only size broadening, no microstrain").

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Bragg's Law

We derive the condition for constructive interference of X-rays reflected from successive atomic planes.

**Step 1: Geometric Setup**

Consider two parallel lattice planes separated by interplanar spacing $d_{hkl}$. X-rays of wavelength $\lambda$ strike at a glancing angle $\theta$.

**Step 2: Path Difference**

The X-ray reflected from the **second** plane travels an **extra distance** compared to the ray reflected from the first plane. This extra path consists of two segments:
- Down to the second plane: $d \sin\theta$
- Back up from the second plane: $d \sin\theta$

Total path difference:

$$\Delta = 2d_{hkl}\sin\theta$$

**Step 3: Condition for Constructive Interference**

For constructive interference (a diffraction peak), the path difference must equal an integer multiple of the wavelength:

$$\Delta = n\lambda$$

**Step 4: Equate**

$$n\lambda = 2d_{hkl}\sin\theta$$

This is **Bragg's Law** — the foundational equation of XRD. In modern convention, $n$ is folded into $d$ as $d_{hkl}/n$, giving the compact form used in software like POWDERX:

$$\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity:** Bragg's Law assumes *elastic scattering* (no energy loss), *coherent planes*, and *monochromatic radiation*. Real samples deviate due to thermal vibrations (Debye-Waller factor), strain, and instrumental broadening.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Scherrer Equation

We derive the relationship between crystallite size and diffraction peak broadening.

**Step 1: Bragg's Law Differentiated**

Starting from Bragg's Law:

$$n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$$

Taking the differential (holding $\lambda$ constant):

$$0 = 2\Delta d \sin\theta + 2d\cos\theta \cdot \Delta\theta$$

$$\Delta d \sin\theta = -d\cos\theta \cdot \Delta\theta$$

**Step 2: Express $\Delta\theta$ in Terms of Size**

For a crystallite of finite size $D$, the number of lattice planes $N$ contributing to diffraction is:

$$N = \frac{D}{d}$$

The diffraction peak broadens by an amount inversely proportional to $N$:

$$\Delta\theta \approx \frac{\lambda}{D \cos\theta}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the Scherrer Constant**

Replacing $\Delta\theta$ with the measured FWHM $\beta$ and introducing the dimensionless Scherrer constant $K$ (which depends on crystallite shape — typically $K = 0.9$ for spherical, $K = 0.89$ for cubic):

$$D = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}$$

This is the **Scherrer Equation**.

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):**

> A ZnO thin film shows the (002) peak at $2\theta = 34.42°$ with FWHM = 0.30° (using Cu K$\alpha$, $\lambda = 1.5406$ Å). Calculate the crystallite size.

**Solution:**

$$\beta = 0.30° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 5.236 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$

$$\theta = \frac{34.42°}{2} = 17.21°$$

$$\cos(17.21°) = 0.9554$$

$$D = \frac{0.9 \times 1.5406 \text{ Å}}{5.236 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.9554}$$

$$D = \frac{1.3865 \text{ Å}}{5.003 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 277 \text{ Å} \approx 27.7 \text{ nm}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Williamson-Hall Equation

The total peak broadening $\beta_{total}$ is the **sum** of size broadening $\beta_{size}$ and strain broadening $\beta_{strain}$:

$$\beta_{total} = \beta_{size} + \beta_{strain}$$

**Step 1: Substitute Scherrer for Size Broadening**

$$\beta_{size} = \frac{K\lambda}{D\cos\theta}$$

**Step 2: Express Strain Broadening**

Microstrain $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta d}{d}$. Differentiating Bragg's Law:

$$\beta_{strain} = 4\varepsilon\tan\theta$$

**Step 3: Combine**

$$\beta_{total} = \frac{K\lambda}{D\cos\theta} + 4\varepsilon\tan\theta$$

Multiply both sides by $\cos\theta$:

$$\beta_{total}\cos\theta = \frac{K\lambda}{D} + 4\varepsilon\sin\theta$$

This is the **Williamson-Hall Equation** — a straight line $y = mx + c$ when plotted with $4\sin\theta$ on x-axis and $\beta\cos\theta$ on y-axis:

- **Slope** $m = \varepsilon$ (microstrain)
- **Intercept** $c = \dfrac{K\lambda}{D}$ (gives crystallite size)

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Tafel Equation

Starting from the Butler-Volmer equation for a single electrode reaction:

$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a F\eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F\eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

**Step 1: Large Overpotential Approximation**

For $\vert \eta \vert \gg \dfrac{RT}{F}$ (typically $\vert \eta \vert > 50$ mV), one exponential dominates.

For anodic polarisation ($\eta$ large, positive):

$$i \approx i_0 \exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a F\eta}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 2: Take Logarithm**

$$\ln i = \ln i_0 + \frac{\alpha_a F\eta}{RT}$$

**Step 3: Convert to Base-10 Log and Define Tafel Slopes**

$$\log i = \log i_0 + \frac{\alpha_a F\eta}{2.303 RT}$$

Defining $b_a = \dfrac{2.303 RT}{\alpha_a F}$ (anodic Tafel slope, typically 30–120 mV/decade):

$$\eta = b_a \log i - b_a \log i_0$$

$$\eta = a + b_a \log \vert i \vert$$

where $a = -b_a \log i_0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** A *symmetric* Tafel plot (equal $b_a$ and $b_c$) implies a **single-electron transfer rate-determining step**. Asymmetric slopes indicate multi-step mechanisms.

---

### 3.5 Derivation of Stern-Geary Equation

The polarisation resistance $R_p$ is defined as the slope of the polarisation curve at $\eta = 0$:

$$R_p = \left(\frac{d\eta}{di}\right)_{\eta=0}$$

**Step 1: Differentiate Butler-Volmer at $\eta = 0$**

$$\left(\frac{di}{d\eta}\right)_{\eta=0} = i_0 \frac{F}{RT}(\alpha_a + \alpha_c)$$

**Step 2: Invert**

$$R_p = \frac{RT}{i_0 F(\alpha_a + \alpha_c)} = \frac{B}{i_{corr}}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $i_{corr}$**

$$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p}$$

where the proportionality constant is:

$$B = \frac{b_a b_c}{2.303(b_a + b_c)}$$

For typical $b_a = b_c = 0.12$ V/dec, $B \approx 26$ mV.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Technique

| Engineering Question | Best Technique(s) | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| What is the **crystal phase** of a coating? | XRD | Bragg peaks = phase fingerprint |
| What is the **elemental composition** of a corrosion product? | EDS + XRD | EDS = elements, XRD = compounds |
| What is the **chemical state** of Fe in rust (Fe²⁺ vs Fe³⁺)? | XPS | Chemical shift identifies oxidation state |
| What is the **surface roughness** of a coating (Ra, Rz)? | AFM or Profilometer | Direct 3D topography |
| Is the coating **adherent** to substrate? | Scratch test / Pull-off | Quantify $L_c$ or $\sigma_{pull}$ |
| What is the **corrosion rate** in 3.5% NaCl? | Tafel / EIS | $i_{corr}$ or $R_p$ |
| Are there **nano-precipitates** in the coating? | TEM | Sub-nm resolution |
| What is the **residual stress** in the coating? | XRD sin²ψ method | $d$-spacing shift with tilt angle |
| Is the coating **hydrophobic**? | Contact angle goniometer | Sessile drop measurement |
| What is the **top thickness uniformity**? | Calo-test or Ball-cratering | Cross-sectional thickness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU "Justify Your Choice" Rule:** Any time you name a technique, you **must** state *what information it provides* AND *why a different technique was insufficient*. This single habit can earn you 2–3 bonus marks per question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Block Diagram: Integrated Characterisation Workflow for a Surface Coating

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the canonical analytical workflow used in a surface engineering laboratory, showing how techniques link in series to build a complete materials profile.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Sample Reception and Visual Inspection"] --> B["Optical Microscopy OM 50X to 1000X"]
    B --> C{"Is surface uniform?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["Proceed to High Resolution Tools"]
    C -- No --> E["Document Defects and Cross Section"]
    E --> F["Identify Heterogeneity Hotspots"]
    F --> D
    D --> G["SEM Scanning Electron Microscopy"]
    G --> G1["Topography via Secondary Electrons"]
    G --> G2["Atomic Number Contrast via Backscattered Electrons"]
    G --> G3["Elemental Mapping via EDS Detector"]
    G1 --> H["Need Phase Identification"]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> H1["XRD for Crystalline Phases"]
    H --> H2["XPS for Surface Chemistry and Bonding"]
    H1 --> I["Mechanical Performance Testing"]
    H2 --> I
    I --> I1["Microhardness Vickers or Knoop"]
    I --> I2["Nanoindentation Oliver Pharr"]
    I --> I3["Adhesion Scratch or Pull Off"]
    I --> I4["Wear Pin on Disc or Ball on Disc"]
    I1 --> J["Environmental Performance"]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    J --> J1["Salt Spray ASTM B117"]
    J --> J2["Tafel Polarisation"]
    J --> J3["Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy"]
    J --> J4["Mass Loss per ASTM G31"]
    J1 --> K["Final Coating Passport Report"]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    J4 --> K
    D --> L["AFM for Nanoscale Roughness"]
    L --> K
    G --> M["TEM for Internal Structure"]
    M --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each arrow represents an information dependency. OM must precede SEM (cheap pre-screening). SEM + EDS must precede XRD/XPS (to know *where* to probe). Mechanical tests always follow chemical characterisation. The "Coating Passport Report" is the integrated deliverable combining all data streams.

---

### 4.2 Block Architecture: XRD Instrumentation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["X Ray Tube Copper Target 1.5406 Angstrom"] --> FILT["K Beta Filter or Monochromator"]
    FILT --> SLIT1["Divergence Slit"]
    SLIT1 --> SAMP["Sample Stage Theta to 2Theta Goniometer"]
    SAMP --> SLIT2["Receiving Anti Scatter Slits"]
    SLIT2 --> DET["Detector Scintillation or Solid State"]
    DET --> PROC["Data Processing Software"]
    PROC --> OUT["Diffractogram Intensity versus 2Theta"]
    SRC --> HV["High Voltage Generator 30 to 50 kV"]
    HV --> SRC
    PROC --> SCH["Scherrer Analysis for Crystallite Size"]
    PROC --> WH["Williamson Hall for Strain"]
    PROC --> RIET["Rietveld Refinement for Quantitative Phase"]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Architecture: SEM Signal Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EM["Electron Source Tungsten or Field Emission"] --> ACC["Accelerating Anode 1 to 30 kV"]
    ACC --> LENS1["Condenser Lens 1"]
    LENS1 --> LENS2["Condenser Lens 2"]
    LENS2 --> LENS3["Objective Lens"]
    LENS3 --> AP["Final Aperture 10 to 100 micrometer"]
    AP --> SAMP["Sample Surface Interaction Volume"]
    SAMP --> SE["Secondary Electrons lt 50 eV"]
    SAMP --> BSE["Backscattered Electrons gt 50 eV"]
    SAMP --> XRAY["Characteristic X Rays"]
    SAMP --> AUG["Auger Electrons"]
    SAMP --> CL["Cathodoluminescence"]
    SE --> SE_DET["Everhart Thornley Detector"]
    BSE --> BSE_DET["Solid State BSE Detector"]
    XRAY --> EDS["EDS Silicon Drift Detector"]
    AUG --> AUG_DET["Auger Detector if Available"]
    SE_DET --> IMG["Topographic Image"]
    BSE_DET --> IMG2["Atomic Number Contrast Image"]
    EDS --> SPEC["Elemental Spectrum"]
```

---

### 4.4 Block Architecture: Electrochemical Corrosion Cell Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WE["Working Electrode Coating Sample"] --> CELL["Three Electrode Cell"]
    RE["Reference Electrode SCE or Ag AgCl"] --> CELL
    CE["Counter Electrode Platinum Mesh"] --> CELL
    CELL --> POT["Potentiostat Gamry Metrohm Autolab"]
    POT --> SW["Software EC Lab or Gamry Framework"]
    SW --> OUT1["Tafel Plot E versus log i"]
    SW --> OUT2["Nyquist Plot Z Imaginary versus Z Real"]
    SW --> OUT3["Bode Plot Phase and Magnitude versus Frequency"]
    SW --> OUT4["Mott Schottky for Passive Films"]
    OUT1 --> ICORR["Extract i corr by Tafel Extrapolation"]
    OUT2 --> Rct["Fit to R Equivalent Circuit Randles or CPE"]
    OUT3 --> Rct
    OUT4 --> DFL["Donor Density and Flat Band Potential"]
```

---

### 4.5 Block Architecture: XPS Energy Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    XRAY["X Ray Source Al K alpha 1486.6 eV or Mg K alpha 1253.6 eV"] --> SAMP["Sample Surface Top 1 to 10 nm"]
    SAMP --> PE["Core Level Photoelectrons Ejected"]
    PE --> ANA["Hemispherical Energy Analyser"]
    ANA --> MCD["Multichannel Detector"]
    MCD --> SW["CasaXPS or Avantage Software"]
    SW --> PEAK["Survey Scan 0 to 1200 eV"]
    SW --> HIGH["High Resolution Scan per Element"]
    PEAK --> ID["Element Identification from Peak Position"]
    HIGH --> CHEM["Chemical State from Peak Shift"]
    ID --> QUANT["Quantification Using Sensitivity Factors"]
    CHEM --> QUANT
    QUANT --> REPORT["Atomic Percent and Chemical State Report"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** What is the principle of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS)? Why is it considered a "surface-sensitive" technique compared to EDS?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

XPS is based on the **photoelectric effect**. When a sample is irradiated with soft X-rays (typically Al K$\alpha$, $h\nu = 1486.6$ eV), core-level electrons are ejected. Their binding energy is calculated from $E_b = h\nu - E_k - \phi$. Since binding energies are element- and chemical-state-specific, XPS provides both **elemental identification** and **chemical bonding information** (e.g., distinguishing metallic Fe from Fe²⁺ from Fe³⁺).

XPS is more surface-sensitive than EDS because:
- XPS detects **photoelectrons** which have very short inelastic mean free paths in solids (~1–10 nm).
- EDS detects **X-rays** which penetrate 0.5–3 µm into the bulk.
- Therefore, XPS probes only the **top 1–10 nm**, while EDS averages over a much larger depth.

**(Award 1 mark for principle, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for depth comparison)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between SEM and TEM in terms of (i) image formation principle, (ii) sample preparation, and (iii) type of information obtained.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | SEM | TEM |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Image Formation | Scans a focused electron beam across the **surface**; detects emitted electrons (SE/BSE) | Transmits electrons **through** an ultra-thin specimen (<100 nm) |
| (ii) Sample Preparation | Bulk sample, only surface polishing/etching required | Extremely thin (≤100 nm), requires ion-beam milling or ultramicrotomy |
| (iii) Information | Topography, morphology, elemental composition (via EDS) | Internal microstructure, lattice fringes, defects, crystal structure (via SAED) |

**(1 mark per correct differentiation)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of X-Ray Diffraction (XRD). Derive Bragg's Law and the Scherrer equation. State the assumptions and limitations of the Scherrer equation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Ni-P electroless coating on mild steel shows the (111) peak of Ni at $2\theta = 44.5°$ with FWHM = 0.45° using Cu K$\alpha$ radiation ($\lambda = 1.5406$ Å). Calculate:
(i) The interplanar spacing $d_{111}$.
(ii) The crystallite size using the Scherrer equation (assume $K = 0.9$).
**(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Principle:** When monochromatic X-rays of wavelength $\lambda$ strike a crystalline material at glancing angle $\theta$, the lattice planes separated by distance $d_{hkl}$ act as a 3D diffraction grating. Constructive interference occurs at specific angles satisfying Bragg's condition.

**Derivation of Bragg's Law:**

Path difference between rays reflected from two successive planes:

$$\Delta = 2d\sin\theta$$

For constructive interference: $\Delta = n\lambda$, hence:

$$n\lambda = 2d_{hkl}\sin\theta$$

**Derivation of Scherrer Equation:**

Starting from Bragg's Law and differentiating:

$$0 = 2\Delta d \sin\theta + 2d\cos\theta \cdot \Delta\theta$$

For a crystallite of size $D$ containing $N = D/d$ planes, the peak broadening is:

$$\beta \approx \frac{K\lambda}{D\cos\theta} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}$$

**Assumptions of Scherrer:**
1. Peak broadening is due **solely to crystallite size** (no strain, no instrumental broadening).
2. Crystallites are **spherical/equiaxed** (shape factor $K \approx 0.9$).
3. **Monochromatic** radiation.
4. **Uniform size** distribution.

**Limitations:**
- Underestimates size if strain is present (use Williamson-Hall instead).
- Not valid for crystallites > 100–200 nm (peak too sharp to measure $\beta$ accurately).
- Requires instrument-broadening correction: $\beta_{true} = \sqrt{\beta_{obs}^2 - \beta_{inst}^2}$.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**(i) Interplanar spacing:**

From Bragg's Law: $\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$

$$\theta = \frac{44.5°}{2} = 22.25°$$

$$\sin(22.25°) = 0.3787$$

$$d_{111} = \frac{1.5406 \text{ Å}}{2 \times 0.3787} = \frac{1.5406}{0.7574} = 2.034 \text{ Å}$$

**(ii) Crystallite Size:**

Convert FWHM to radians:

$$\beta = 0.45° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$

Compute $\cos\theta$:

$$\cos(22.25°) = 0.9255$$

Apply Scherrer equation:

$$D = \frac{0.9 \times 1.5406}{7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.9255}$$

$$D = \frac{1.3865}{7.269 \times 10^{-3}} = 190.7 \text{ Å} \approx 19.1 \text{ nm}$$

**Answer:** $d_{111} = 2.034$ Å; $D = 19.1$ nm.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM). Compare SEM with Optical Microscopy in terms of resolution, depth of field, and sample preparation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is the principle of Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)? Discuss its three operating modes. Why is AFM preferred over SEM for measuring the surface roughness of a polymer coating? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**SEM Block Diagram (refer Section 4.3 of these notes for the full Mermaid architecture). Key components:**

1. **Electron source** (Tungsten filament or Field Emission Gun) — emits electrons by thermionic emission.
2. **Accelerating anode** — accelerates electrons to 1–30 keV.
3. **Condenser and Objective lenses** — demagnify the source and focus the beam to a ~1 nm spot.
4. **Scan coils** — raster the beam across the sample in X-Y.
5. **Detectors:**
   - **Everhart-Thornley** for secondary electrons (topography).
   - **Solid-state** for backscattered electrons (Z-contrast).
   - **EDS detector** for elemental X-rays.
6. **Display and processing electronics** — synchronise scan signal with detector to build the image.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Optical Microscope | SEM |
|---|---|---|
| Resolution | ~200 nm (Abbe limit) | ~1 nm |
| Depth of Field | ~1 µm | ~30 µm at 1000× |
| Magnification | Up to ~2000× | Up to 1,000,000× |
| Sample Prep | Polishing + etching | Conductive coating (Au/Pt) if non-conducting; otherwise minimal |
| Environment | Ambient air | High vacuum |
| Imaging Source | Reflected photons | Emitted electrons |

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Principle of AFM:** A sharp nanoscale tip (radius ~10 nm) mounted on a flexible cantilever is raster-scanned across the surface. As the tip encounters surface features, the cantilever deflects. A laser beam reflected from the back of the cantilever onto a position-sensitive photodiode measures this deflection, producing a 3D topographic map with sub-nanometre vertical resolution.

**Three Operating Modes:**

1. **Contact Mode:** Tip is in continuous repulsive contact with surface (force ~10⁻⁷ to 10⁻⁶ N). Highest lateral resolution but may damage soft samples. Feedback maintains constant deflection.

2. **Tapping (Intermittent Contact) Mode:** Cantilever oscillates near its resonance frequency; tip "taps" the surface intermittently. Reduces lateral forces and is ideal for soft/sticky samples like polymers, biological specimens.

3. **Non-Contact Mode:** Tip oscillates above the surface (van der Waals attraction regime). Used for very delicate samples but gives lower resolution and requires ultra-clean surfaces.

**Why AFM is preferred for polymer coatings:**

1. **No conductive coating required** — SEM requires sputter-coating of Au/Pt on non-conductors, which would alter the true surface of a polymer.
2. **True 3D quantitative roughness** — AFM directly yields $R_a$, $R_q$, $R_z$ values; SEM gives only qualitative 2D projection.
3. **Higher vertical resolution** — sub-nm vertical resolution vs SEM's ~1 nm.
4. **Operates in ambient air** — no vacuum-induced deformation of the polymer.
5. **No charging artefacts** — SEM charging on polymers distorts topographic images.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner-Verified):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert FWHM to radians.** Scherrer equation *requires* $\beta$ in radians. Using degrees gives an answer off by ~57×. Examiners specifically check this — it is a "trap" worth 2 marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing XPS and EDS depth.** Many students write "XPS probes microns and EDS probes nm." The **opposite** is true. Mix this up and you lose 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Naming a technique without justification.** Writing "Use SEM" alone = 0 marks. Writing "Use SEM because it provides 1 nm topographic resolution on a conducting metallic coating" = full marks. Always justify.
>
> 4. **Mixing up SE and BSE.** Secondary Electrons = topography. Backscattered Electrons = atomic number contrast. Examiners deduct 1 mark per swap.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Scherrer's assumptions.** Citing Scherrer for a 500 nm grain without noting the assumption "no strain broadening" is a valuation trap.
>
> 6. **Tafel slope units.** Always write mV/decade, not "mV". Examiners are strict.
>
> 7. **Wrong index in XRD formula.** Bragg's Law uses $\theta$, not $2\theta$ directly — students frequently plug in $2\theta$ and lose 2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Characterisation is verification, not speculation.** Always link a technique to a specific *question* about the coating.
- [ ] **OM is the first step** — cheap, fast, identifies regions of interest for higher-resolution tools.
- [ ] **SEM uses SE/BSE/EDS** — SE = topography, BSE = Z-contrast, EDS = elemental composition.
- [ ] **TEM requires ultra-thin samples** (<100 nm) — gives internal structure, lattice fringes, SAED patterns.
- [ ] **Bragg's Law:** $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ — foundation of XRD.
- [ ] **Scherrer Equation:** $D = \dfrac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}$ — crystallite size from peak broadening; $\beta$ in **radians**; $K = 0.9$ typical.
- [ ] **Williamson-Hall:** $\beta\cos\theta = \dfrac{K\lambda}{D} + 4\varepsilon\sin\theta$ — separates size and strain.
- [ ] **XPS depth = 1–10 nm; EDS depth = 0.5–3 µm.** XPS = chemistry; EDS = elemental only.
- [ ] **AFM provides 3D topography** with sub-nm vertical resolution — preferred for non-conductive polymer coatings.
- [ ] **Tafel equation:** $\eta = a + b\log \vert i \vert$ — extract $i_{corr}$ from anodic/cathodic branches.
- [ ] **Stern-Geary:** $i_{corr} = \dfrac{B}{R_p}$ — quick corrosion rate from linear polarisation.
- [ ] **Adhesion tests:** Tape (qualitative) < Scratch (semi-quantitative) < Pull-off (quantitative).
- [ ] **Nanoindentation (Oliver-Pharr):** Uses $H = P/A_c$ and reduced modulus from unloading slope.
- [ ] **Characterisation Hierarchy:** OM → SEM → TEM/AFM; XPS is always paired with XRD.
- [ ] **Always justify your technique choice** — naming alone never earns full marks.
- [ ] **Convert FWHM to radians before Scherrer calculation.**
- [ ] **State assumptions** when applying any analytical formula.
- [ ] **Tafel slopes in mV/decade**, not V/decade.
- [ ] **Residual stress** is measured by XRD sin²ψ method; not by SEM/TEM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recycling and Reuse of Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Recycling and Reuse of Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PEPET862 – Corrosion, Environmental Degradation and Surface Engineering (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, *Recycling and Reuse of Materials* refers to the systematic engineering discipline of recovering, reprocessing, and reintroducing end-of-life engineering materials (metals, polymers, ceramics, composites, and electronic scrap) back into the manufacturing supply chain. The discipline is anchored on the principles of the **Circular Economy**, **Industrial Ecology**, and **Sustainable Materials Management (SMM)**, with the explicit goal of minimizing virgin resource extraction, reducing embodied energy, and mitigating the environmental burden of corrosion-related and service-life failures.

Formally, the **International Resource Panel (IRP)** and **ISO 14021** define recycling as *"the reprocessing of waste materials into new products, materials, or substances, whether for the original or other purposes,"* excluding energy recovery and reprocessing into fuels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic is mapped to **Course Outcome CO3** of PEPET862 – *“Apply sustainable engineering practices to mitigate corrosion-driven material loss and promote material conservation through recycling and reuse strategies.”*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Hospital Blood Bank"

Imagine a hospital. When a patient loses blood, the hospital does **not** throw away the empty IV bag. Instead:

1. The blood is **reused** if it can be transfused directly to another patient (analogous to **Reuse** of components).
2. The iron from hemoglobin is chemically extracted and re-synthesized into new blood cells (analogous to **Recycling**).
3. The remaining organic matter is composted for energy (analogous to **Energy Recovery**).

In materials engineering, every corroded steel beam, scrapped automobile, or e-waste PCB is a "spent blood bag." Recycling and reuse are the engineering protocols that keep this "metabolic cycle" of materials alive.

### 1.3 The 5R Hierarchy (KTU High-Yield Point)

The KTU 2024 syllabus prioritizes the **5R Waste Hierarchy** in the following strict descending order of preference:

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5R Pyramid (Top = Most Preferred):**
> 1. **REFUSE** – Avoid using the material altogether.
> 2. **REDUCE** – Minimize material consumption per unit product.
> 3. **REUSE** – Use the same component again for the same or different function.
> 4. **RECYCLE** – Reprocess into a new raw material.
> 5. **RECOVER** – Extract energy via incineration/waste-to-energy (last resort before disposal).

### 1.4 Why Recycling is Critical in Corrosion Engineering

Recycling is intrinsically linked to corrosion science because **corrosion is the primary driver of material obsolescence** in service. Every kilogram of steel lost to rust must be replaced by either:
- Newly mined iron ore (virgin route), or
- Scrap from dismantled structures (recycled route).

Hence, recycling is the **terminal-stage mitigation strategy** in the corrosion engineer's toolkit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used Globally:** The **End-of-Life Recycling Rate (EoL-RR)** is reported as a percentage. The global average for **steel is ~85%**, for **aluminum ~75%**, and for **plastics < 10%** (UNEP 2023 data).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Recycling Routes

KTU examiners frequently ask students to differentiate between the **four distinct recycling routes** defined by the **EU Waste Framework Directive (2008/98/EC)** and adopted in Indian CPCB guidelines:

- **Primary Recycling (Closed-Loop):**
  Conversion of scrap back into a product identical to its original form.
  *Example:* Old aluminum cans → new aluminum cans.
  *Purity:* ≥ 99%.
- **Secondary Recycling (Open-Loop / Downcycling):**
  Scrap is converted into a lower-grade product.
  *Example:* Steel girders → rebar.
  *Purity:* 90–98%.
- **Tertiary Recycling (Chemical/Feedstock Recycling):**
  Depolymerization of plastics into monomers; smelting of e-waste for elemental recovery.
  *Example:* PET bottles → BHET monomer → virgin PET.
- **Quaternary Recycling (Energy Recovery):**
  Combustion of non-recyclable waste (plastics, rubber) to generate electricity/heat.
  *Note:* This is **NOT** classified as recycling by ISO 14021 but is included in the broader 5R framework.

### 2.2 Material-Specific Recycling Pathways

| Material Class | Key Recycling Method | Embodied Energy Saved | Purity Attainable |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ferrous (Steel/Fe) | Electric Arc Furnace (EAF) | ~60–75% | High (with slag control) |
| Non-Ferrous (Al) | Remelting in rotary/induction furnace | ~95% (only 5% of virgin energy needed) | 99.5–99.9% |
| Copper | Pyrometallurgical + electrorefining | ~85% | 99.99% (Cu-CATH-1 grade) |
| Polymers (PET, HDPE) | Mechanical shredding + extrusion | ~70% | Grade-dependent |
| Ceramics/Glass | Crushing, sorting, cullet addition | ~30% | Limited by contamination |
| Composites | Pyrolysis (to recover fibre + matrix) | ~40% | Fibre length degrades |
| E-Waste | Hydrometallurgy + urban mining | ~80% | Au, Pd, Pt, REEs recovered |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **board-exam favorites**. Memorize each variable and its SI unit.

**1. End-of-Life Recycling Rate (EoL-RR):**

$$\text{EoL-RR} = \frac{M_{\text{recycled}}}{M_{\text{total EoL}}} \times 100\%$$

where $M_{\text{recycled}}$ is the mass of material actually reprocessed and $M_{\text{total EoL}}$ is the total mass of the material reaching end-of-life.

**2. Material Recovery Efficiency ($\eta_R$):**

$$\eta_R = \frac{M_{\text{recovered as useful output}}}{M_{\text{input scrap}}} \times 100\%$$

**3. Specific Energy Demand Reduction:**

$$\Delta E = E_{\text{virgin}} - E_{\text{recycled}} \quad [\text{MJ/kg}]$$

The **relative energy saving** is:

$$\%E_{\text{saved}} = \frac{E_{\text{virgin}} - E_{\text{recycled}}}{E_{\text{virgin}}} \times 100\%$$

**4. CO₂ Emission Avoidance (Cradle-to-Gate):**

$$\Delta \text{CO}_2 = M_{\text{recycled}} \times \left( \text{CO}_{2,\text{virgin}} - \text{CO}_{2,\text{recycled}} \right)$$

where the carbon footprints are expressed in $[\text{kg CO}_2\text{e/kg material}]$.

**5. Mass Balance for a Recycling Stream:**

$$M_{\text{input}} = M_{\text{product}} + M_{\text{reject}} + M_{\text{loss}}$$

For a perfectly closed loop: $M_{\text{loss}} = 0$ and $M_{\text{reject}} = 0$.

**6. Recycled Content Ratio (RCR) – ISO 14021:**

$$\text{RCR} = \frac{M_{\text{recycled input}}}{M_{\text{total input}}} \times 100\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These six equations are the **backbone of any Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** report. They are used by Tata Steel, Hindalco, and NALCO in their Annual Sustainability Reports to demonstrate compliance with **SEBI BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting)** mandates.

### 2.4 The Closed-Loop Material Cycle – Why and How

- **Why recycle?** Recycling reduces mining tailings by up to **97%** for aluminum, lowers acidification potential, and prevents the leaching of heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd) from corroded e-waste into groundwater.
- **How is it achieved?** Through a four-stage engineering pipeline:
  1. **Collection** (formal/informal sector in India – kabadiwala system).
  2. **Sorting & Pre-treatment** (eddy current separators, XRF sorters, shredders).
  3. **Reprocessing** (pyrometallurgy, hydrometallurgy, mechanical extrusion).
  4. **Re-manufacture** (cast, forge, roll, or extrude into final product).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **recycling** with **reuse**. *Reuse* keeps the product's **macrostructure** intact (e.g., a refurbished gear), while *recycling* **destroys and reconstitutes** the macrostructure (e.g., gear → scrap → molten metal → new gear blank).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: The "Recycled Aluminum Energy Argument"

This is the **single most important numerical derivation** in PEPET862 Module 3 and has appeared in KTU University Examinations (Dec 2022, June 2023).

> **Problem Setup:** Calculate the percentage energy saved and the CO₂ avoided per tonne of aluminum produced from recycled scrap instead of virgin bauxite ore. Given:
> - $E_{\text{virgin}} = 170 \text{ GJ/tonne Al}$ (Bayer process + Hall–Héroult electrolysis).
> - $E_{\text{recycled}} = 8.5 \text{ GJ/tonne Al}$ (remelting of clean UBC scrap).
> - $\text{CO}_{2,\text{virgin}} = 11.5 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/kg Al}$.
> - $\text{CO}_{2,\text{recycled}} = 0.6 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/kg Al}$.

**Step 1: Calculate absolute energy saved per tonne.**

$$\Delta E = E_{\text{virgin}} - E_{\text{recycled}}$$

$$\Delta E = 170 - 8.5 = 161.5 \text{ GJ/tonne}$$

**Step 2: Calculate percentage energy saved.**

$$\%E_{\text{saved}} = \frac{\Delta E}{E_{\text{virgin}}} \times 100\%$$

$$\%E_{\text{saved}} = \frac{161.5}{170} \times 100\% = 95.0\%$$

**Step 3: Calculate CO₂ avoided per kg.**

$$\Delta \text{CO}_2 = \text{CO}_{2,\text{virgin}} - \text{CO}_{2,\text{recycled}}$$

$$\Delta \text{CO}_2 = 11.5 - 0.6 = 10.9 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/kg Al}$$

**Step 4: Convert to per-tonne basis for final reporting.**

$$\Delta \text{CO}_2 = 10.9 \times 1000 = 10{,}900 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/tonne Al}$$

$$\Delta \text{CO}_2 = 10.9 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e/tonne Al avoided}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Recycling 1 tonne of aluminum saves energy equivalent to running an average Indian household for **over 6 years** (≈25 GJ/year) and avoids emissions equivalent to a passenger car driving **~44,000 km**.

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Mass Balance in a Closed-Loop EAF Steel Plant

Consider a **6-tonne Electric Arc Furnace** charge with the following inputs and outputs:

- Input scrap: 6,000 kg
- Alloying additions (Fe-Mn, Fe-Si): 80 kg
- Electrode carbon: 25 kg
- Oxygen blown (combustion of impurities): oxidizes C, Si, Mn
- Tapping yield: $M_{\text{product}}$ (molten steel)
- Slag (CaO–SiO₂–FeO) formed: $M_{\text{slag}}$
- Dust lost to baghouse: $M_{\text{dust}}$
- Yield loss: $M_{\text{yield loss}}$

**Step 1: Write the mass balance.**

$$M_{\text{input}} = M_{\text{scrap}} + M_{\text{alloy}} + M_{\text{electrode}}$$

$$M_{\text{input}} = 6000 + 80 + 25 = 6105 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2: Apply the output distribution.**

$$M_{\text{input}} = M_{\text{product}} + M_{\text{slag}} + M_{\text{dust}} + M_{\text{yield loss}}$$

**Step 3: Assume standard EAF losses.**

Slag ≈ 120 kg; Dust ≈ 35 kg; Yield loss ≈ 50 kg.

$$6105 = M_{\text{product}} + 120 + 35 + 50$$

**Step 4: Solve for product output.**

$$M_{\text{product}} = 6105 - 205 = 5900 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5: Compute recovery efficiency.**

$$\eta_R = \frac{M_{\text{product}}}{M_{\text{input}}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_R = \frac{5900}{6105} \times 100\% = 96.64\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Benchmark:** A modern EAF achieves 92–97% $\eta_R$. Anything below 90% indicates excessive oxidation losses (over-blowing) or poor slag foaming control — both common corrosion-related degradation concerns at high temperature.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Material Recyclability Index

This matrix is **directly asked** as a 7-mark KTU question. The **Recyclability Index (RI)** is defined as:

$$\text{RI} = \frac{\%E_{\text{saved}} \times \eta_R \times \text{EoL-RR}}{10000}$$

| Material | $\%E_{\text{saved}}$ | $\eta_R$ (%) | EoL-RR (%) | **RI** | KTU Rating |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Aluminum | 95 | 95 | 75 | **6.77** | Excellent |
| Copper | 85 | 90 | 65 | **4.97** | Excellent |
| Steel | 70 | 95 | 85 | **5.65** | Excellent |
| Lead | 65 | 95 | 80 | **4.94** | Good |
| Glass | 30 | 70 | 50 | **1.05** | Moderate |
| HDPE | 70 | 80 | 30 | **1.68** | Moderate |
| PET | 65 | 85 | 40 | **2.21** | Moderate |
| PVC | 50 | 60 | 20 | **0.60** | Poor |
| FRP Composite | 40 | 70 | 15 | **0.42** | Poor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Metals score the highest RI because their atomic bonds are **non-directional (metallic bonding)** and can be remelted without breaking covalent backbones. Polymers degrade due to **chain scission** during reprocessing, reducing molecular weight by 10–30% per cycle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram – The Closed-Loop Material Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Virgin Ore Extraction] --> B[Material Processing and Alloying]
    B --> C[Component Manufacturing]
    C --> D[Service Stage Subject to Corrosion and Wear]
    D --> E{End of Life Decision}
    E -->|Reuse Possible| F[Direct Reuse after Refurbishment]
    E -->|Damaged but Identifiable| G[Sorting and Dismantling]
    E -->|Fully Degraded| H[Shredding and Size Reduction]
    G --> I[Pyrometallurgical Reprocessing]
    H --> I
    H --> J[Hydrometallurgical Chemical Extraction]
    H --> K[Mechanical Polymer Reprocessing]
    F --> D
    I --> L[Secondary Raw Material Ingot]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The arrows from L back to C represent the **closed-loop feed-back** that defines recycling. Note the three parallel reprocessing pathways (I, J, K) for different material classes — this is why material sorting at stage E is the most economically critical step.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram – Reuse versus Recycling Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[End of Life Component] --> T1{Is Macrostructure Intact and Non Corroded}
    T1 -->|Yes| T2{Is Material Grade Still Verified}
    T2 -->|Yes| R1[REUSE Direct]
    T2 -->|No| R2[REUSE Downgraded Application]
    T1 -->|No| T3{Is Material Metallic}
    T3 -->|Yes| T4[Pyrometallurgy and EAF or IF Route]
    T3 -->|No| T4b{Is Material Polymeric}
    T4b -->|Yes| T5[Mechanical Reprocessing or Feedstock Recycling]
    T4b -->|No| T6[Hydrometallurgy or Pyrolysis for Composites]
    T4 --> RC1[RECYCLED INGOT]
    T5 --> RC2[RECYCED PELLET]
    T6 --> RC3[RECOVERED ELEMENTS]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram – Industrial Recycling Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INTAKE[INTAKE BAY]
        B1[Weighbridge and Radiation Monitor]
        B2[Pre Sort Station for Hazardous Components]
    end
    subgraph PREP[PREPARATION PLANT]
        P1[Shredder Hammer Mill]
        P2[Eddy Current Separator]
        P3[Magnetic Drum Separator]
        P4[Air Classifier for Light Fraction]
    end
    subgraph FURNACE[MELTING AND REFINING]
        M1[Electric Arc Furnace or Rotary Furnace]
        M2[Slag Foaming Control System]
        M3[Vacuum Degassing]
    end
    subgraph CAST[CASTING AND PRODUCT]
        C1[Continuous Caster]
        C2[Rolling Mill]
        C3[Quality Assurance Lab]
    end
    INTAKE --> PREP --> FURNACE --> CAST
    CAST --> FEED[Back to Component Manufacturing]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mapping to KTU Outcomes:** This block diagram maps directly to **CO3 (Apply recycling principles)** and **CO5 (Sustainable engineering design)**. The arrows leaving CAST close the loop back to manufacturing, which is the **literal definition of a circular economy**.

### 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart – Hierarchy of Material Recovery Choices

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Material Failure or Service End] --> N{Is Recovery Viable}
    N -->|No| O[Safe Disposal with Leachate Control]
    N -->|Yes| P{Is Refurbishment Cost Less than 40 percent of New}
    P -->|Yes| Q1[REUSE]
    P -->|No| R{Is Same Grade Recovery Possible}
    R -->|Yes| Q2[PRIMARY RECYCLING Closed Loop]
    R -->|No| S{Is Downcycling Acceptable}
    S -->|Yes| Q3[SECONDARY RECYCLING Open Loop]
    S -->|No| T{Is Chemical Decomposition Feasible}
    T -->|Yes| Q4[TERTIARY RECYCLING Feedstock]
    T -->|No| U[QUATERNARY ENERGY RECOVERY]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between Reuse and Recycling with one suitable engineering example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Reuse (1.5 marks):** Reuse is the process of using a product/component again in its **original form** for the same or a different application, without altering its macrostructure. It retains the embodied energy of the manufacturing stage. *Example:* Refurbished railway wagon wheels being reinstalled in a freight train after non-destructive testing (NDT) and re-profiling.

- **Recycling (1.5 marks):** Recycling is the process of **disassembling, shredding, and reprocessing** a product into a new raw material. The original macrostructure is destroyed and the material re-enters the manufacturing cycle as a secondary raw material. *Example:* End-of-life automobiles (ELVs) being shredded and fed into an Electric Arc Furnace to produce new structural steel.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the four classes of recycling as per the EU Waste Framework Directive and give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Primary Recycling (Closed-loop)** – e.g., Used Beverage Can (UBC) scrap remelted into new Al cans.
2. **Secondary Recycling (Downcycling)** – e.g., Steel I-beams from demolished buildings melted into lower-grade rebar.
3. **Tertiary Recycling (Chemical/Feedstock)** – e.g., Depolymerization of PET bottles back into BHET monomer.
4. **Quaternary Recycling (Energy Recovery)** – e.g., Incineration of mixed plastic waste in a Waste-to-Energy plant. *(0.5 marks for the caveat that this is excluded from ISO 14021 definition of recycling.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Module Internal Choice

> **INTERNAL CHOICE STRUCTURE:** KTU 2024 Scheme requires students to answer **ONE of TWO** 14-mark questions from each module. Both questions below are from PEPET862 Module 3 and carry equal marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** – *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, describe the **Electric Arc Furnace (EAF)** route for recycling of ferrous scrap. Explain how slag chemistry is controlled to minimize corrosion-related degradation of the refractory lining.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A steel plant produces **2.4 × 10⁵ tonnes/year** of recycled rebar using 80% scrap input. Calculate:
- (i) The mass of virgin pig iron required per year.
- (ii) The annual CO₂ emission avoided, given $\text{CO}_{2,\text{virgin}} = 2.1 \text{ t CO}_2\text{e/t steel}$ and $\text{CO}_{2,\text{recycled}} = 0.45 \text{ t CO}_2\text{e/t steel}$.

---

**Solution A(a):**

The Electric Arc Furnace (EAF) is the **workhorse of ferrous recycling**. The operational sequence is:

1. **Charging (1 mark):** Scrap is loaded via a charging basket from the roof. Light and heavy scrap are charged in 2–3 batches to maximize density.

2. **Melting (2 marks):** Three-phase graphite electrodes strike an arc (typically 800–1000 V, 50–80 kA). The arc temperature reaches **~3500 °C**, far above the 1538 °C melting point of Fe. Foamy slag is generated by injecting carbon (coal/charcoal) and oxygen to form a **CO gas bubble network** that shields the refractory from the arc — this is critical to **reduce refractory corrosion by 40–60%**.

3. **Refining (1.5 marks):** After meltdown, oxygen is blown to oxidize excess C, Si, Mn, P. Lime (CaO) and dolomite (CaMg(CO₃)₂) are added to flux the slag. Slag basicity ratio:

$$\text{Basicity} = \frac{\% \text{CaO}}{\% \text{SiO}_2}$$

is maintained at **2.5–3.5** to ensure phosphorus removal and to form a stable dicalcium silicate (Ca₂SiO₄) layer that passivates the MgO-C refractory.

4. **Tapping and Continuous Casting (1.5 marks):** The molten steel (1600–1650 °C) is tapped into a ladle, vacuum-degassed, and continuously cast into billets for rolling into rebar.

5. **Off-gas Treatment (1 mark):** Baghouse filters capture ZnO, PbO, and CdO dusts (from galvanized scrap), preventing heavy-metal corrosion of downstream equipment.

---

**Solution A(b):**

**Given:** Annual output = $2.4 \times 10^5$ t/year; scrap fraction = 80%.

**(i) Mass of virgin pig iron required:**

$$M_{\text{virgin}} = M_{\text{output}} \times (1 - 0.80)$$

$$M_{\text{virgin}} = 2.4 \times 10^5 \times 0.20 = 4.8 \times 10^4 \text{ t/year}$$

**(ii) Annual CO₂ emission avoided:**

$$\Delta \text{CO}_2 = M_{\text{output}} \times \left( \text{CO}_{2,\text{virgin}} - \text{CO}_{2,\text{recycled}} \right)$$

$$\Delta \text{CO}_2 = 2.4 \times 10^5 \times (2.1 - 0.45)$$

$$\Delta \text{CO}_2 = 2.4 \times 10^5 \times 1.65 = 3.96 \times 10^5 \text{ t CO}_2\text{e/year}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta \text{CO}_2 \approx 3.96 \times 10^5 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e/year avoided}}$$

This is equivalent to taking **~86,000 passenger cars off Indian roads** for one year.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** – *[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **concept of Urban Mining** for e-waste recovery. Discuss the hydrometallurgical recovery of **gold and copper** from discarded printed circuit boards (PCBs).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A polymer recycling unit processes **500 tonnes/day** of mixed PET waste. The recovery efficiency is 78% and the EoL recycling rate is 35%. Determine:
- (i) The actual mass of recycled PET pellets produced per day.
- (ii) The Recycled Content Ratio (RCR) if the plant consumes 600 tonnes/day of total input (including 100 tonnes of virgin PET).

---

**Solution B(a):**

**Urban Mining (3 marks):** Urban Mining refers to the recovery of valuable metals (Au, Ag, Pd, Pt, Cu, Ni, REEs) from **anthropogenic stocks** — i.e., end-of-life electronics, automobiles, and industrial waste. Unlike ore mining (which extracts primary resources), urban mining exploits the **higher concentration of metals in e-waste** (e.g., 1 tonne of mobile-phone PCBs contains ~300 g of gold, which is **80× richer than the richest gold ore**).

**Hydrometallurgical Recovery of Au and Cu (4 marks):**

1. **Pre-treatment:** PCBs are manually dismantled to remove capacitors and batteries; the remaining boards are shredded to <5 mm.
2. **Pyrometallurgical pre-concentration:** Smelted at 1200 °C in a Cu collector — Au, Ag, Pd dissolve into the Cu button.
3. **Leaching (Aqua Regia for Au, H₂SO₄/H₂O₂ for Cu):**

$$\text{Au} + 4\text{HCl} + 3\text{HNO}_3 \rightarrow \text{HAuCl}_4 + 3\text{NO}_2\uparrow + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{Cu} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CuSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

4. **Selective Precipitation / Electrowinning:** Cu is electrowon at 2.0–2.2 V; Au is precipitated using FeSO₄ or Na₂S₂O₃.

This route **avoids SO₂ emissions** associated with the older pyrometallurgical-only route and achieves **Au recovery > 95%**.

---

**Solution B(b):**

**Given:** $M_{\text{input scrap}} = 500$ t/day; $\eta_R = 78\%$; EoL-RR = 35%; $M_{\text{total input}} = 600$ t/day; $M_{\text{virgin PET}} = 100$ t/day.

**(i) Actual mass of recycled PET pellets:**

$$M_{\text{recycled}} = M_{\text{input scrap}} \times \eta_R \times \text{EoL-RR}$$

$$M_{\text{recycled}} = 500 \times 0.78 \times 0.35$$

$$M_{\text{recycled}} = 136.5 \text{ t/day}$$

**(ii) Recycled Content Ratio (RCR):**

First, find the total recycled input used (this equals the recycled pellets produced in step i, assuming all are consumed).

$$\text{RCR} = \frac{M_{\text{recycled input}}}{M_{\text{total input}}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{RCR} = \frac{136.5}{600} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{RCR} = 22.75\%}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Points (Examiner's Penalties):**
> - **Confusing Reuse and Recycling (–2 marks):** Reuse keeps the macrostructure; recycling breaks it. Examiners deduct marks if these are used interchangeably.
> - **Skipping units in numerical answers (–1 mark):** Every numerical answer in KTU 2024 Scheme must end with **explicit units** (t/year, kg CO₂e, GJ/tonne, etc.). Writing just "3.96 × 10⁵" without units = mark deduction.
> - **Wrong basicity formula (–1.5 marks):** Slag basicity is **CaO/SiO₂**, not CaO/Al₂O₃. Mixing these up is a classic pitfall.
> - **Using ISO 14021 definition of recycling incorrectly (–1 mark):** ISO 14021 **excludes** energy recovery from the definition of recycling. Quaternary recycling is therefore "energy recovery," not "recycling."
> - **Writing the Recycled Content Ratio formula as a fraction (–0.5 marks):** Always multiply by 100 to express as a percentage.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The 5R Hierarchy:** Refuse → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover. Always list in this order in any "explain" question.
- **Reuse ≠ Recycling:** Reuse retains macrostructure; recycling destroys and reconstitutes it.
- **Four Recycling Classes:** Primary (closed-loop), Secondary (downcycling), Tertiary (chemical), Quaternary (energy).
- **Aluminum Recycling:** Saves 95% energy; only 5% of virgin energy needed. This is the **most favourite KTU numerical**.
- **EoL Recycling Rate formula:** $M_{\text{recycled}} / M_{\text{total EoL}} \times 100\%$.
- **RCR formula (ISO 14021):** $M_{\text{recycled input}} / M_{\text{total input}} \times 100\%$.
- **Slag Basicity:** CaO/SiO₂ = 2.5–3.5 for EAF steelmaking.
- **Urban Mining:** Higher ore-grade for Au in e-waste than in primary gold mines.
- **Hydrometallurgy:** Aqua Regia for Au, H₂SO₄/H₂O₂ for Cu.
- **CO₂ Avoidance:** Always quote in **kg CO₂e or t CO₂e**, with explicit time and mass basis.
- **Final numerical check:** Always write the boxed answer with units and verify dimensional consistency.
- **Indian Context:** Mention **CPCB guidelines, SWM Rules 2016, Plastic Waste Management Rules 2022** to score bonus marks on Indian sustainability framework questions.

> **End of Notes – PEPET862 Module 3, Topic: Recycling and Reuse of Materials**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digitization of Established Curves and Data Driven Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Digitization of Established Curves and Data Driven Approach # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Digitization of Established Curves** refers to the systematic process of converting traditionally plotted experimental corrosion data — such as polarization (Tafel) curves, Evans diagrams, Nyquist/Bode impedance plots, weight-loss vs. time curves, and Pourbaix diagrams — from their analog/graphical representation (printed graphs, scanned plots, hand-drawn charts) into machine-readable numerical data pairs (X, Y) using image-processing algorithms, manual coordinate extraction, or automated curve-tracing software. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Data-Driven Approach** in corrosion science and surface engineering is a paradigm that relies on the systematic collection, digitization, statistical analysis, and machine-learning based modeling of large historical/experimental corrosion datasets to predict material degradation behavior, estimate remaining service life, classify corrosion mechanisms, and optimize protective strategies — *without necessarily invoking first-principles mechanistic equations*. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Imagine you are a doctor who has access to **50 years of handwritten patient health records** stored in dusty ledgers. To make a correct diagnosis today, you would need to: 1. **Digitize** the old records (type them into a computer, or scan + OCR them). 2. **Find patterns** — e.g., "patients with X symptom, Y age, and Z blood report always developed disease W." 3. **Apply** these learned patterns to a new patient to predict their future health. This is *exactly* what happens in **Digitization + Data-Driven Corrosion Engineering**: - **Digitization of Curves** = typing/OCR-ing the old "ledger" graphs (Tafel plots, Nyquist curves) into Excel/MATLAB. - **Data-Driven Approach** = using ML/AI/statistics on those digital records to predict *new* corrosion outcomes for *new* alloys/environments. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU context:** Traditional corrosion testing (weight loss, potentiodynamic polarization, EIS) generates one curve per experiment. Building a mechanistic model for *every* alloy-environment combination is impossible (there are > 10,000 combinations). Data-driven methods bypass this by **learning from past curves** and predicting future ones. ## 1.3 Key Physical / Computational Constants & Metrics The following standard metrics govern this domain and must be memorized for KTU examinations: - **Digitization Resolution (Δr):** typically **≥ 300 DPI** for scanned plot images to preserve curve fidelity. - **Root Mean Square Error (RMSE):** standard metric to quantify digitization accuracy — target **< 1 % of plot range**. - **Coefficient of Determination (R²):** goodness-of-fit for data-driven models — target **≥ 0.95** for predictive acceptability. - **Sample-to-Feature Ratio (SFR):** minimum **10 : 1** for any reliable ML model in corrosion prediction. - **Tafel Slopes (βₐ, β_c):** standard physical constants in **mV/decade**; required during curve fitting. - **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) Reference:** **0.000 V** at 25 °C — used as the reference for digitized polarization data. ## 1.4 Source Material of "Established Curves" in Corrosion | S.No. | Established Curve Type | Source Experimental Technique | Standard Reference | |-------|-----------------------|------------------------------|---------------------| | 1 | Tafel / Polarization Curve | Potentiodynamic Polarization | ASTM G5, ASTM G59 | | 2 | Evans Diagram | Mixed Potential Analysis | ASTM G69 | | 3 | Nyquist & Bode Plots | Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy | ASTM G106 | | 4 | Weight-Loss vs. Time Curve | Immersion / Salt-Spray Testing | ASTM G1, ASTM B117 | | 5 | Pourbaix Diagram (E vs. pH) | Thermodynamic Computation | Pourbaix Atlas (Atlas of Electrochemical Equilibria) | | 6 | Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR) | LPR Technique | ASTM G59 | | 7 | Cyclic Polarization Curve | Cyclic Potentiodynamic Polarization | ASTM G61 | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Two-Stage Pipeline The complete "Digitization of Established Curves + Data-Driven Approach" workflow in modern corrosion engineering can be broken into **two logical stages**, each comprising multiple structured steps. ### **STAGE A — Digitization of Established Curves** **Step 1: Acquisition of Analog Source** - The original corrosion curve (Tafel plot, Evans diagram, etc.) exists as a *printed image*, *hand-drawn chart*, or *PDF/figure in a legacy paper*. - Image must be scanned at a **minimum resolution of 300 DPI** in **uncompressed TIFF/PNG** format to prevent quantization loss. **Step 2: Pre-Processing of the Image** - **Grayscale conversion** is applied to remove RGB noise. - **Contrast enhancement** (histogram equalization) sharpens the curve against grid lines. - **Binarization (Otsu's method)** converts the image into pure black-and-white pixels. - **Skeletonization** reduces the curve to a 1-pixel-wide line for accurate tracing. > [!NOTE] > **Why pre-process?** The grid lines, axis labels, and legends in the original plot can mislead the tracing algorithm. Pre-processing mathematically *erases* the grid and *isolates* the curve pixels alone. **Step 3: Axis Calibration (Most Critical Step)** - The user manually clicks **four calibration points**: two on the X-axis (defining `x₁, x₂` in real units, e.g., -1000 mV, +1000 mV vs. SCE) and two on the Y-axis (defining `y₁, y₂` in real units, e.g., 10⁻⁶, 10⁻² A/cm²). - A **linear transformation matrix** is computed to convert every pixel coordinate `(pₓ, p_y)` into a real data coordinate `(X, Y)`: $$X = x_1 + (p_x - p_{x1}) \cdot \frac{(x_2 - x_1)}{(p_{x2} - p_{x1})}$$ $$Y = y_1 + (p_y - p_{y1}) \cdot \frac{(y_2 - y_1)}{(p_{y2} - p_{y1})}$$ **Step 4: Curve Tracing & Coordinate Extraction** - A pixel-following algorithm walks along the skeletonized curve, sampling points at regular intervals (e.g., every 5 pixels) or at curvature inflections. - Output: a tab-separated (X, Y) data table ready for spreadsheet/software ingestion. **Step 5: Quality Assurance** - The reconstructed curve is **re-plotted over the original** and visually compared. - **RMSE** between re-plotted digitized points and the original is computed; an RMSE < 1 % of the Y-axis range is considered acceptable. ### **STAGE B — Data-Driven Approach (Modeling on Digitized Data)** **Step 6: Data Curation & Feature Engineering** - Multiple digitized curves (across different alloys, environments, temperatures) are aggregated into a **master dataset**. - **Features** are extracted: `E_corr`, `i_corr`, `βₐ`, `β_c`, `R_p`, `|Z|`, `χ²`, etc. - **Labels** are defined: corrosion rate, failure mode, predicted lifetime. **Step 7: Choice of Data-Driven Model** | Model Class | Best Use Case | Limitations | |-------------|---------------|-------------| | **Linear / Polynomial Regression** | Simple, low-dimension data | Poor for nonlinear corrosion kinetics | | **Multivariate Adaptive Regression Splines (MARS)** | Capturing piecewise nonlinearities | Sensitive to noise | | **Artificial Neural Networks (ANN)** | Highly nonlinear E vs. log(i) curves | Needs large training data | | **Random Forest (RF)** | Corrosion mechanism classification | Black-box, less interpretable | | **Support Vector Machines (SVM)** | Small-to-medium datasets | Kernel choice critical | | **Gaussian Process Regression (GPR)** | Uncertainty quantification | Computationally expensive | | **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)** | Direct image → prediction (no digitization needed) | Requires very large labelled image database | **Step 8: Model Training, Validation & Deployment** - Dataset split: typically **70 % training / 15 % validation / 15 % test**. - **K-fold cross-validation** (k = 5 or 10) to avoid overfitting. - Performance metrics: **R², RMSE, MAE, Precision, Recall, F1-score**. - Final model deployed for real-time corrosion prediction in industrial pipelines, marine structures, etc. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains **every key equation** required to solve Part A & Part B questions on this topic. Memorize the units and boundary conditions. | # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units | Applicable To | |---|--------------------|-------------|-------|---------------| | 1 | $X = x_1 + (p_x - p_{x1}) \cdot \dfrac{x_2 - x_1}{p_{x2} - p_{x1}}$ | Pixel → real X-coordinate transformation | varies | Image digitization | | 2 | $Y = y_1 + (p_y - p_{y1}) \cdot \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{p_{y2} - p_{y1}}$ | Pixel → real Y-coordinate transformation | varies | Image digitization | | 3 | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(Y_i^{\text{original}} - Y_i^{\text{digitized}})^2}$ | Digitization accuracy metric | same as Y | All digitized curves | | 4 | $\eta = \beta_a \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)$ | Butler–Volmer / Tafel (anodic) | V (η), mV/dec (β) | Polarization curves | | 5 | $\eta = -\beta_c \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)$ | Butler–Volmer / Tafel (cathodic) | V (η), mV/dec (β) | Polarization curves | | 6 | $R_p = \dfrac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303 \, i_{\text{corr}} \, (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | Stern–Geary equation (linear polarization) | Ω·cm² | LPR, data fitting | | 7 | $i_{\text{corr}} = \dfrac{B}{R_p}, \quad B = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | Corrosion current density from Rₚ | A/cm² | LPR analysis | | 8 | $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot i_{\text{corr}} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | Corrosion rate from i_corr | mm/yr | All digital conversion | | 9 | $\text{CR (mpy)} = 0.129 \cdot i_{\text{corr}} \cdot \dfrac{EW}{\rho}$ | Corrosion rate (mils per year, imperial) | mpy | All digital conversion | | 10 | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{\sum_{i}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | Coefficient of determination | dimensionless | Model evaluation | | 11 | $\text{MAE} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Mean Absolute Error | same as y | Model evaluation | | 12 | $Z(\omega) = Z'(\omega) - j Z''(\omega)$ | Complex impedance | Ω | EIS Nyquist plot | | 13 | $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{{Z'}^2 + {Z''}^2}, \quad \theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{Z''}{Z'}\right)$ | Bode magnitude & phase | Ω, degrees | EIS Bode plot | | 14 | $y(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Gaussian distribution (data spread) | dimensionless | Statistical pre-processing | | 15 | $E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$ | Evans diagram driving voltage | V | Galvanic series | > **Standard values to remember (for KTU):** > - K (Faraday's constant conversion) = **3.27 × 10⁻³ mm·g/(µA·cm·yr)** for CR in mm/yr. > - For Fe: EW = **27.92 g/eq**, ρ = **7.87 g/cm³**. > - For Zn: EW = **32.69 g/eq**, ρ = **7.14 g/cm³**. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pipeline Integrity Management:** Digitized historical polarization curves from oil & gas pipelines feed ML models that predict *remaining useful life* of buried pipelines. - **Marine & Offshore Structures:** Salinity-temperature-time digitized data train random-forest classifiers that identify *pitting vs. uniform corrosion* from sensor streams. - **Automotive Industry:** OEMs digitize salt-spray data to design accelerated laboratory tests that *correlate* with real-world 10-year corrosion exposure. - **Aerospace & Defense:** Long-term corrosion-fatigue curves (digitized from 30-year archives) feed physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) for fleet-level damage tolerance. - **Smart Corrosion Sensors:** Modern IoT-enabled sensors (e.g., electrical resistance, LPR, ECN probes) directly output *digital* corrosion rate data — the natural endpoint of the digitization pipeline. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pixel-to-Real-Coordinate Transformation (Stage A Core) ### **Problem Setup** A scanned Tafel plot has the following pixel coordinates for its axes: - X-axis: pixel 100 corresponds to **-1000 mV vs. SCE**, pixel 900 corresponds to **+1000 mV vs. SCE**. - Y-axis (log scale): pixel 800 corresponds to **10⁻⁶ A/cm²**, pixel 100 corresponds to **10⁻² A/cm²**. A point on the digitized curve has pixel coordinates `(pₓ, p_y) = (450, 480)`. Convert this to the real `(E, i)` coordinate. ### **Step-by-Step Solution** **Step 1: Identify the four calibration pairs.** From the axis calibration: $$(p_{x1}, x_1) = (100,\ -1000 \text{ mV}), \quad (p_{x2}, x_2) = (900,\ +1000 \text{ mV})$$ $$(p_{y1}, y_1) = (100,\ 10^{-2} \text{ A/cm}^2), \quad (p_{y2}, y_2) = (800,\ 10^{-6} \text{ A/cm}^2)$$ > *Note that the Y-axis is reversed because pixel coordinates increase downward, but the current-density axis increases upward (in physical space). This sign inversion is handled automatically by the formula.* **Step 2: Apply the X-coordinate linear transformation.** $$E = x_1 + (p_x - p_{x1}) \cdot \frac{x_2 - x_1}{p_{x2} - p_{x1}}$$ Substituting the values: $$E = -1000 + (450 - 100) \cdot \frac{(+1000) - (-1000)}{900 - 100}$$ $$E = -1000 + (350) \cdot \frac{2000}{800}$$ $$E = -1000 + 350 \cdot 2.5$$ $$E = -1000 + 875 = -125 \text{ mV vs. SCE}$$ **Step 3: Apply the Y-coordinate linear transformation.** $$Y = y_1 + (p_y - p_{y1}) \cdot \frac{y_2 - y_1}{p_{y2} - p_{y1}}$$ Substituting: $$Y = 10^{-2} + (480 - 100) \cdot \frac{10^{-6} - 10^{-2}}{800 - 100}$$ $$Y = 10^{-2} + (380) \cdot \frac{(-0.00999)}{700}$$ $$Y = 10^{-2} + 380 \cdot (-1.4271 \times 10^{-5})$$ $$Y = 10^{-2} - 5.423 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$Y = 10^{-2} - 0.005423 = 0.004577 \text{ A/cm}^2 \approx 4.58 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A/cm}^2$$ **Step 4: Final Answer.** $$\boxed{(E, \log_{10} i) = (-125 \text{ mV vs. SCE},\ -2.34)}$$ This single point is then added to the dataset;
