hundreds of such points reconstruct the entire Tafel curve in digital form. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Corrosion Rate Calculation from Digitized Polarization Data (Stern–Geary + Faraday) ### **Problem Setup** A digitized Tafel plot of mild steel in 3.5 % NaCl gives the following fitted parameters from the data-driven regression: - Anodic Tafel slope: $\beta_a = 120 \text{ mV/decade}$ - Cathodic Tafel slope: $\beta_c = 160 \text{ mV/decade}$ - Corrosion current density: $i_{\text{corr}} = 8.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A/cm}^2$ - Equivalent weight of Fe: $EW = 27.92 \text{ g/eq}$ - Density of Fe: $\rho = 7.87 \text{ g/cm}^3$ Compute the **corrosion rate in mm/year**. ### **Step-by-Step Solution** **Step 1: Recall the Faraday-based corrosion rate equation.** $$\text{CR} = \frac{K \cdot i_{\text{corr}} \cdot EW}{\rho}$$ where the constant $K$ has the value $3.27 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm·g/(µA·cm·yr)}$ when $i_{\text{corr}}$ is expressed in **µA/cm²**. **Step 2: Unit-convert the corrosion current density.** $$i_{\text{corr}} = 8.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A/cm}^2 = 8.5 \text{ µA/cm}^2$$ **Step 3: Substitute into the equation.** $$\text{CR} = \frac{(3.27 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (8.5) \cdot (27.92)}{7.87}$$ Compute the numerator: $$3.27 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 8.5 = 2.7795 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$2.7795 \times 10^{-2} \cdot 27.92 = 7.7606 \times 10^{-1} \approx 0.7761$$ Compute the full expression: $$\text{CR} = \frac{0.7761}{7.87} = 0.0986 \text{ mm/yr}$$ **Step 4: Final Answer.** $$\boxed{\text{CR} \approx 0.099 \text{ mm/yr} \approx 99 \text{ µm/yr}}$$ This is consistent with typical mild-steel corrosion rates in seawater (≈ 0.1–0.2 mm/yr). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — R² for a Data-Driven Corrosion Model ### **Problem Setup** A linear regression model was trained to predict the corrosion current density $i_{\text{corr}}$ (in µA/cm²) of zinc coatings as a function of chloride concentration. The 5 test points are: | Actual $i_{\text{corr}}$ | Predicted $\hat{i}_{\text{corr}}$ | |--------------------------|-----------------------------------| | 2.1 | 2.0 | | 4.5 | 4.7 | | 6.3 | 6.0 | | 8.0 | 8.4 | | 10.2 | 9.8 | Compute **R²**. ### **Step-by-Step Solution** **Step 1: Compute the mean of the actual values.** $$\bar{y} = \frac{2.1 + 4.5 + 6.3 + 8.0 + 10.2}{5} = \frac{31.1}{5} = 6.22$$ **Step 2: Compute $\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ (Residual Sum of Squares).** $$(2.1 - 2.0)^2 = 0.01$$ $$(4.5 - 4.7)^2 = 0.04$$ $$(6.3 - 6.0)^2 = 0.09$$ $$(8.0 - 8.4)^2 = 0.16$$ $$(10.2 - 9.8)^2 = 0.16$$ $$\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = 0.01 + 0.04 + 0.09 + 0.16 + 0.16 = 0.46$$ **Step 3: Compute $\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2$ (Total Sum of Squares).** $$(2.1 - 6.22)^2 = 16.97$$ $$(4.5 - 6.22)^2 = 2.96$$ $$(6.3 - 6.22)^2 = 0.0064$$ $$(8.0 - 6.22)^2 = 3.17$$ $$(10.2 - 6.22)^2 = 15.84$$ $$\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 = 16.97 + 2.96 + 0.0064 + 3.17 + 15.84 = 38.95$$ **Step 4: Compute R².** $$R^2 = 1 - \frac{0.46}{38.95} = 1 - 0.0118 = 0.9882$$ **Step 5: Final Answer.** $$\boxed{R^2 = 0.988}$$ Since R² > 0.95, the model is considered **highly acceptable** for corrosion rate prediction. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Curve Digitization | If the source curve is… | Then use… | With expected accuracy… | |--------------------------|-----------|--------------------------| | Hand-drawn chart, high contrast | Otsu thresholding + skeletonization | RMSE < 0.5 % | | Low-resolution scanned PDF | Super-resolution pre-processing (Bicubic) | RMSE < 1.0 % | | Log-scale axis (Tafel, Bode) | Logarithmic axis mapping in calibration | RMSE < 1.5 % | | Multiple overlapping curves (Evans diagram) | Color-channel separation + curve labelling | RMSE < 2.0 % | | Curve with noise / scatter band | Gaussian smoothing + spline fitting | RMSE < 1.0 % | | Noisy EIS Nyquist semicircle | Circle-fit algorithm (least-squares circle) | RMSE < 1.5 % | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Workflow of Digitization + Data-Driven Corrosion Engineering ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Analog Corrosion Curve

Answer in **3–4 lines** with key terms underlined. **No derivations** required. ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, 3 Marks]** **Define the term "digitization of established curves" as applied in corrosion engineering. List any two software tools used for this purpose.** **Model Answer:** > **Digitization of established curves** is the process of converting analog/graphical experimental corrosion data (such as Tafel polarization plots, Evans diagrams, and Nyquist/Bode impedance plots) from printed/scanned images into **machine-readable numerical (X, Y) data pairs** using image-processing algorithms and manual/automated axis calibration. > > Two commonly used software tools are: > 1. **WebPlotDigitizer** (open-source, web-based) > 2. **GetData Graph Digitizer** (Windows-based, supports batch processing) > > Other professional tools include OriginPro, Engauge Digitizer, and MATLAB's `grabit` toolbox. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style, 3 Marks]** **What is a "data-driven approach" in corrosion prediction? State any two advantages and one limitation.** **Model Answer:** > A **data-driven approach** uses historical/experimental corrosion datasets and statistical/machine-learning algorithms to predict material degradation behavior, *without explicitly solving the underlying mechanistic equations* of electrochemistry. > > **Advantages:** > 1. Can capture **nonlinear, multivariate interactions** (e.g., alloy composition × temperature × Cl⁻ concentration) that are difficult to model mechanistically. > 2. Enables **rapid screening** of thousands of alloy-environment combinations using a single trained model. > > **Limitation:** > 1. Requires a **large, high-quality, labelled training dataset**; predictions outside the training distribution (extrapolation) are unreliable ("black-box" behavior). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > Part B questions carry **14 marks** and are split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Always attempt the choice that best matches your studied material. Use diagrams, derivations, and step-by-step calculations to score full marks. --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 3 Internal Choice]** **(a)** Describe in detail the **step-by-step procedure for digitizing a printed Tafel polarization curve** obtained from a legacy research paper. Include a labelled block diagram of the workflow and discuss the role of axis calibration, image pre-processing, and quality assurance (RMSE). **(7 marks)** **(b)** A scanned Tafel plot of aluminium alloy AA6061 in 3.5 % NaCl solution has the following pixel calibration data: - X-axis: pixel 150 = −1200 mV vs. SCE, pixel 850 = +200 mV vs. SCE. - Y-axis (log): pixel 50 = 10⁻⁵ A/cm², pixel 750 = 10⁻² A/cm². A specific point on the anodic branch of the digitized curve has pixel coordinates `(500, 300)`. Calculate the **real (E, i) value** of this point. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for (a):** **Introduction (2 marks):** Digitization bridges legacy analog corrosion data and modern computational analytics. The process converts pixel-based image information into tabulated numerical (X, Y) pairs. **Step 1 – Image Acquisition (1 mark):** The original Tafel plot is scanned at **≥ 300 DPI** in uncompressed TIFF/PNG format. Higher DPI reduces quantization error during coordinate extraction. **Step 2 – Pre-Processing (1.5 marks):** - Grayscale conversion strips RGB color. - Otsu's binarization converts to black-and-white pixels. - Morphological skeletonization reduces the curve to a 1-pixel-thick line. - Grid lines and axis text are mathematically removed using morphological opening. **Step 3 – Axis Calibration (1.5 marks):** - User clicks **4 reference points**: two on X-axis (defining `x₁, x₂` in mV) and two on Y-axis (defining `y₁, y₂` in A/cm²). - Software computes a linear transformation matrix to map every pixel `(pₓ, p_y)` to real coordinates `(E, i)`. **Step 4 – Curve Tracing & Output (0.5 marks):** A walking algorithm samples the skeletonized curve at regular pixel intervals, outputting a (X, Y) table in CSV format. **Step 5 – Quality Assurance (0.5 marks):** RMSE between re-plotted digitized points and original is computed; **RMSE < 1 % of Y-axis range** is acceptable. **Labelled Block Diagram of Workflow:** > *(Examiner expects: Image → Pre-process → Calibrate → Trace → QA → Digital Data → Database/ML Pipeline.)* #### **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** - `(p_{x1}, x_1) = (150, -1200 mV)`, `(p_{x2}, x_2) = (850, +200 mV)` - `(p_{y1}, y_1) = (50, 10⁻⁵ A/cm²)`, `(p_{y2}, y_2) = (750, 10⁻² A/cm²)` - Point pixel `(pₓ, p_y) = (500, 300)` **Step 1: X-coordinate transformation (3.5 marks).** $$E = x_1 + (p_x - p_{x1}) \cdot \frac{x_2 - x_1}{p_{x2} - p_{x1}}$$ $$E = -1200 + (500 - 150) \cdot \frac{(+200) - (-1200)}{850 - 150}$$ $$E = -1200 + 350 \cdot \frac{1400}{700}$$ $$E = -1200 + 350 \cdot 2 = -1200 + 700 = -500 \text{ mV vs. SCE}$$ **Step 2: Y-coordinate transformation (3.5 marks).** $$Y = y_1 + (p_y - p_{y1}) \cdot \frac{y_2 - y_1}{p_{y2} - p_{y1}}$$ $$Y = 10^{-5} + (300 - 50) \cdot \frac{10^{-2} - 10^{-5}}{750 - 50}$$ $$Y = 10^{-5} + 250 \cdot \frac{0.00999}{700}$$ $$Y = 10^{-5} + 250 \cdot 1.4271 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$Y = 10^{-5} + 3.568 \times 10^{-3} = 3.578 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A/cm}^2$$ **Final Answer:** The digitized point is at **(E = -500 mV vs. SCE, i = 3.58 × 10⁻³ A/cm²)**. --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **(a)** Discuss the role of **Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), Random Forest (RF), and Gaussian Process Regression (GPR)** as data-driven models for corrosion prediction. Compare them in terms of dataset size requirement, interpretability, and ability to quantify uncertainty. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Random Forest model was trained on 200 digitized polarization curves to predict $i_{\text{corr}}$ (in µA/cm²) for various alloy-environment combinations. The model achieved the following on the 30-point test set: $$\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = 2.45, \quad \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 = 78.92$$ Compute the **R² and RMSE** of the model. Comment on whether the model is acceptable for industrial deployment. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for (a):** **Introduction (1 mark):** Data-driven models learn from historical digitized corrosion data. Three widely used classes are ANN, RF, and GPR. **Artificial Neural Network (ANN) (2 marks):** - Consists of input layer, hidden layers, and output layer; each neuron applies a nonlinear activation. - **Strength:** Excellent at capturing highly nonlinear corrosion kinetics (Tafel + diffusion + passivation). - **Weakness:** Needs large datasets (> 1000 curves); poor interpretability ("black box"). **Random Forest (RF) (2 marks):** - Ensemble of decision trees trained on bootstrapped subsamples; final prediction is the average (regression) or majority vote (classification). - **Strength:** Robust to noise; provides feature importance ranking (good interpretability). - **Weakness:** Less accurate than deep ANN on very large datasets; uncertainty quantification is indirect (via tree variance). **Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) (1.5 marks):** - A non-parametric Bayesian method that returns a *mean prediction* **and** an *uncertainty (variance)* at every point. - **Strength:** Best for **uncertainty quantification** — critical for safety-critical corrosion assessments. - **Weakness:** Computationally expensive (O(N³)); not suitable for > 10,000-point datasets. **Comparison Table (0.5 marks):** | Criterion | ANN | RF | GPR | |-----------|-----|-----|-----| | Dataset size needed | Very large | Medium | Small-medium | | Interpretability | Low | Medium | Medium | | Uncertainty quantification | No (needs MC dropout) | Indirect | Direct, native | #### **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** - $\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = 2.45$ (Residual SS) - $\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 = 78.92$ (Total SS) - $N = 30$ test points **Step 1: Compute R² (3 marks).** $$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{Residual SS}}{\text{Total SS}} = 1 - \frac{2.45}{78.92} = 1 - 0.0310 = 0.969$$ **Step 2: Compute RMSE (3 marks).** $$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{\text{Residual SS}}{N}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.45}{30}} = \sqrt{0.0817} = 0.286 \text{ µA/cm}^2$$ **Step 3: Comment on Acceptability (1 mark).** Since **R² = 0.969 > 0.95** and **RMSE = 0.286 µA/cm²** is small relative to typical $i_{\text{corr}}$ ranges (0.5–50 µA/cm²), the model is **acceptable for industrial deployment**, provided the test set is representative of the full operating range. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic (As Reported by KTU Valuators):** > > 1. **Forgetting unit conversion in corrosion rate (CR) calculations** — most students plug in $i_{\text{corr}}$ in A/cm² instead of µA/cm², leading to answers off by **a factor of 10⁶** and immediate full-mark loss. Always convert: **1 A/cm² = 10⁶ µA/cm²**. > 2. **Confusing the signs of Tafel slopes** — Anodic βₐ and Cathodic β_c are both *positive* by convention, but appear with opposite signs in the Tafel equation. Marks deducted if signs are inconsistent. > 3. **Not stating the reference electrode** — A digitized E value like "−500 mV" without stating "vs. SCE" or "vs. SHE" is incomplete and loses 0.5–1 mark. > 4. **Skipping axis-calibration units in digitization problems** — Examiner expects explicit mention of `mV vs. SCE`, `A/cm²`, or `Ω·cm²` for every coordinate pair. > 5. **Reporting R² as a percentage** — R² is a *dimensionless number between 0 and 1*; writing "R² = 96.9 %" instead of "R² = 0.969" loses 1 mark. > 6. **Omitting the block diagram in 7-mark descriptive answers** — KTU 2024 scheme mandates at least one diagram/flowchart for full marks on descriptive sub-parts. > 7. **Forgetting RMSE definition in digitization problems** — Even if numerical answer is correct, no credit is awarded if RMSE formula is not explicitly written. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Digitization of Established Curves & Data Driven Approach"** - ✅ **Digitization** converts analog corrosion plots → digital (X, Y) data using pixel-to-coordinate transformations. - ✅ The **5 stages of digitization** are: Acquisition → Pre-processing → Axis Calibration → Tracing → QA (RMSE check). - ✅ Standard digitization resolution = **≥ 300 DPI**; acceptable RMSE = **< 1 %** of Y-axis range. - ✅ Axis calibration uses **4 reference points** (2 on X-axis, 2 on Y-axis) to define a **linear transformation matrix**. - ✅ Common digitization tools: **WebPlotDigitizer, GetData, Engauge Digitizer, OriginPro, MATLAB grabit**. - ✅ **Data-driven approach** uses historical data + ML/statistics to predict corrosion behavior — *no need for first-principles equations*. - ✅ Key data-driven models: **Linear Regression, ANN, Random Forest, SVM, GPR, MARS**. - ✅ The **Stern–Geary equation** $R_p = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \, i_{\text{corr}} (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ is the bridge between LPR and $i_{\text{corr}}$. - ✅ Corrosion rate formula (mm/yr): $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot i_{\text{corr}} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ with $K = 3.27 \times 10^{-3}$ when $i_{\text{corr}}$ is in **µA/cm²**. - ✅ **R² > 0.95** and **low RMSE** are the acceptance criteria for any data-driven corrosion model. - ✅ Dataset split: **70 % train / 15 % validation / 15 % test** with **k-fold cross-validation** (k = 5 or 10). - ✅ **GPR** is the only model that natively provides **uncertainty quantification** — preferred for safety-critical applications. - ✅ **Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs)** combine mechanistic equations with data-driven learning — the emerging hybrid frontier. - ✅ Always report E values with **explicit reference electrode** (vs. SCE, vs. SHE, vs. Ag/AgCl). - ✅ The pixel-coordinate transformation formulas $X = x_1 + (p_x - p_{x1}) \cdot \dfrac{x_2 - x_1}{p_{x2} - p_{x1}}$ (and the Y-counterpart) are **must-memorize** for KTU Part B. - ✅ The **Tafel equation** $\eta = \pm \beta \log_{10}(i/i_0)$ underlies all polarization-curve digitization problems. - ✅ **NYquist plot** uses $Z' = Z'(\omega)$ (real) and $-Z'' = Z''(\omega)$ (imaginary) — Bode plot uses $\vert Z \vert$ and phase $\theta$. - ✅ Common **evaluation metrics** for digitized curves & ML models: **R², RMSE, MAE, Precision, Recall, F1-score**. - ✅ Industrial applications: pipeline integrity, marine structures, automotive salt-spray correlation, aerospace damage tolerance, smart IoT corrosion sensors. - ✅ Final take-home: **Digitization = the bridge**; **Data-driven = the brain**; together they enable **predictive corrosion engineering** in the Industry 4.0 era.

topics.title: Life Cycle Assessment: Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Life Cycle Assessment: Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** is a non-supervised, orthogonal linear transformation technique from multivariate statistics that converts a set of possibly correlated LCA impact category variables (such as GWP, AP, EP, POCP, ODP, HTP) into a smaller set of uncorrelated variables called **Principal Components (PCs)**, ordered so that the first principal component (PC1) accounts for the maximum possible variance in the original data.

In the **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** framework, PCA is employed during the **LCIA (Life Cycle Impact Assessment)** and **Interpretation** phases to:
- Reduce the dimensionality of multi-impact-category datasets.
- Detect hidden patterns, clusters, and dominant environmental stressors.
- Identify correlations between life cycle stages and impact categories.
- Support **normalization and weighting** decisions with empirical evidence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Definition to Memorize (Verbatim for 3-Mark Answers):**
> *“Principal Component Analysis is an orthogonal transformation procedure that reduces the dimensionality of a multivariate LCA dataset by projecting it onto a new coordinate system defined by the eigenvectors of its covariance (or correlation) matrix, with the first principal component aligned along the direction of maximum variance.”*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Thumbprint of an Eco-Footprint"**
> Imagine you are grading 50 different products (cars, refrigerators, detergents) across **10 environmental impact categories** (climate change, acidification, eutrophication, ozone depletion, etc.). That is a 50 × 10 table — too messy to read at a glance. PCA acts like a **smart summarizer** that says: *"Don't look at all 10 axes — look at just 2 or 3 'super-axes' that capture 95% of the story."*
> - **PC1 (Principal Component 1)** = the *biggest environmental story* (e.g., fossil-fuel-driven emissions).
> - **PC2 (Principal Component 2)** = the *next biggest, independent* environmental story (e.g., water-related toxicity).
> - A product's **score** on a PC tells you "how strongly it embodies that story."
> - The **loading** of an impact category on a PC tells you "how much that story is driven by that category."

## 1.3 Key Physical / Mathematical Constants & Metrics

The following standardized quantitative descriptors are mandatory vocabulary for any KTU PCA answer:

- **Variance ($\sigma^2$ or $\lambda$)** — Unit: $(impact\ unit)^2$; e.g., $(kg\ CO_2\text{-}eq)^2$.
- **Covariance ($\sigma_{jk}$)** — Unit: product of two impact units.
- **Eigenvalue ($\lambda_k$)** — Unit: $(impact\ unit)^2$; represents variance captured by PC$_k$.
- **Eigenvector ($v_k$)** — Dimensionless normalized loading vector.
- **Score ($s_{ik}$)** — Unit: impact unit; position of sample $i$ on PC$_k$.
- **Proportion of Variance Explained (PoVE)** — Dimensionless ratio, typically 0 to 1.
- **Kaiser Criterion Threshold** — $\lambda_k > 1$ (when applied to *standardized* data).
- **Cumulative PoVE Threshold** — typically $\ge 0.80$ or $\ge 0.90$ in LCA literature.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why PCA Works in LCA

A typical LCA produces a matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ where $n$ is the number of functional units (products, processes, scenarios) and $p$ is the number of impact categories. The challenge is the **Curse of Dimensionality** — when $p$ is large, visualization, interpretation, and weighting become intractable. PCA resolves this by finding a $p \times k$ projection matrix ($k \ll p$) that preserves as much variance as possible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Meaning:** The PCA transformation *rotates* the original $p$-dimensional impact-coordinate axes so that the new axes (PCs) align with the directions of greatest spread in the data cloud of life-cycle inventories.

## 2.2 The Six Operational Steps of PCA in an LCA Pipeline

1. **Compile the Data Matrix** $\mathbf{X}_{n \times p}$ from LCI/LCIA outputs.
2. **Mean-Center** (and optionally **autoscale / standardize** by dividing by standard deviation $s_j$). Standardization is mandatory when impact categories have different units (e.g., kg CO$_2$-eq vs. kg CFC-11-eq).
3. **Construct the Covariance (or Correlation) Matrix** $\mathbf{C}_{p \times p}$.
4. **Perform Eigendecomposition** $\mathbf{C} \mathbf{V} = \mathbf{V} \boldsymbol{\Lambda}$.
5. **Rank and Select** the top $k$ PCs using a stopping rule (Kaiser, Scree, or fixed PoVE).
6. **Compute Loadings and Scores** to interpret the LCA results.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of PCA in Engineering

| Engineering Sub-domain | PCA Application |
|---|---|
| **Sustainable Manufacturing** | Identifying which life cycle stage dominates multiple impacts simultaneously. |
| **Materials Selection (Eco-design)** | Clustering candidate materials by environmental performance signature. |
| **Bioenergy / Biofuel LCA** | Distinguishing feedstock-driven impacts from conversion-process impacts. |
| **Supply Chain Sustainability** | Detecting correlated environmental hotspots across tier-1 suppliers. |
| **Circular Economy Assessment** | Comparing End-of-Life scenarios (recycle, reuse, landfill) across multiple endpoints. |
| **Building/Construction LCA** | Aggregating 10+ midpoint categories into 2–3 interpretable drivers. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table verbatim — it forms the backbone of every numerical PCA question in KTU ESE.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean of column $j$ | $\bar{x}_j = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij}$ | Centroid of life-cycle impacts in category $j$ |
| 2 | Mean-centered element | $x_{ij}^{c} = x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j$ | Translates origin to data centroid |
| 3 | Standardized element | $z_{ij} = \dfrac{x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j}{s_j}$ | Removes unit-mismatch bias |
| 4 | Covariance | $\sigma_{jk} = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{ij}^{c})(x_{ik}^{c})$ | Joint variability of categories $j$ and $k$ |
| 5 | Variance (diagonal) | $\sigma_{j}^{2} = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{ij}^{c})^{2}$ | Spread of category $j$ across products |
| 6 | Eigendecomposition | $\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{V}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}$ | Spectral factorization of covariance |
| 7 | Characteristic equation | $\det(\mathbf{C} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$ | Solves for variances along PCs |
| 8 | Eigenvector normalization | $\sum_{j=1}^{p} v_{jk}^{2} = 1$ | Ensures unit-length loading vector |
| 9 | Score of sample $i$ on PC $k$ | $s_{ik} = \sum_{j=1}^{p} x_{ij}^{c} \cdot v_{jk}$ | Position of product $i$ in PC-space |
| 10 | Proportion of Variance Explained | $\text{PoVE}_k = \dfrac{\lambda_k}{\sum_{j=1}^{p}\lambda_j}$ | Information retained by PC$_k$ |
| 11 | Cumulative PoVE | $\text{CumPoVE}(K) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \text{PoVE}_k$ | Total information retained by first $K$ PCs |
| 12 | Kaiser criterion | $\lambda_k > 1$ (on standardized data) | Retain rule for PC count |
| 13 | Scree rule | Find "elbow" in $\lambda_k$ vs $k$ plot | Retain rule for PC count |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas above use $n-1$ (Bessel's correction, **unbiased estimator**) which is the KTU-accepted convention for sample-based covariance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

## 3.1 Derivation of the PCA Optimization Objective (Why it Maximizes Variance)

Consider mean-centered data $\mathbf{X}^c$ and a unit direction vector $\mathbf{v}$ (to be determined). The variance of the data projected onto $\mathbf{v}$ is:

$$
\text{Var}(\mathbf{X}^c \mathbf{v}) = \frac{1}{n-1}\,(\mathbf{X}^c \mathbf{v})^{T}(\mathbf{X}^c \mathbf{v}) = \mathbf{v}^{T}\left(\frac{\mathbf{X}^{cT}\mathbf{X}^{c}}{n-1}\right)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}^{T}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{v}
$$

We want to maximize this variance subject to $\mathbf{v}^{T}\mathbf{v} = 1$ (unit-length constraint). Using the **Lagrange multiplier** $\lambda$:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{v},\lambda) = \mathbf{v}^{T}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{v} - \lambda(\mathbf{v}^{T}\mathbf{v} - 1)
$$

Differentiating with respect to $\mathbf{v}$ and setting to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{v}} = 2\mathbf{C}\mathbf{v} - 2\lambda\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}
$$

$$
\therefore\ \mathbf{C}\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v}
$$

**Conclusion:** The directions of maximum variance are the **eigenvectors** of $\mathbf{C}$, and the variances captured are the **eigenvalues**. The first PC corresponds to the largest $\lambda$.

## 3.2 Fully Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

An LCA practitioner has evaluated **4 alternative products** ($n = 4$) on **2 midpoint impact categories** ($p = 2$):
- $X_1$ = Global Warming Potential (kg CO$_2$-eq)
- $X_2$ = Acidification Potential (kg SO$_2$-eq)

The raw LCIA data matrix is:

$$
\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 & 2.4 \\ 0.5 & 0.7 \\ 2.2 & 2.9 \\ 1.9 & 2.2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Tasks:**
1. Compute the mean vector and mean-centered matrix.
2. Construct the covariance matrix.
3. Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors.
4. Determine the proportion of variance explained by PC1.
5. Compute the scores of all 4 products on PC1.

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Mean Vector

$$
\bar{x}_1 = \frac{2.5 + 0.5 + 2.2 + 1.9}{4} = \frac{7.1}{4} = 1.775
$$

$$
\bar{x}_2 = \frac{2.4 + 0.7 + 2.9 + 2.2}{4} = \frac{8.2}{4} = 2.050
$$

$$
\bar{\mathbf{x}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.775 \\ 2.050 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Mean-Centered Matrix

Subtracting $\bar{\mathbf{x}}$ from each row:

$$
\mathbf{X}^{c} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 - 1.775 & 2.4 - 2.050 \\ 0.5 - 1.775 & 0.7 - 2.050 \\ 2.2 - 1.775 & 2.9 - 2.050 \\ 1.9 - 1.775 & 2.2 - 2.050 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} +0.725 & +0.350 \\ -1.275 & -1.350 \\ +0.425 & +0.850 \\ +0.125 & +0.150 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Covariance Matrix

Using the **$n-1 = 3$** denominator:

$$
\sigma_{1}^{2} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(0.725)^{2} + (-1.275)^{2} + (0.425)^{2} + (0.125)^{2}\right]
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{3}\left[0.5256 + 1.6256 + 0.1806 + 0.0156\right] = \frac{2.3475}{3} = 0.7825
$$

$$
\sigma_{2}^{2} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(0.350)^{2} + (-1.350)^{2} + (0.850)^{2} + (0.150)^{2}\right]
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{3}\left[0.1225 + 1.8225 + 0.7225 + 0.0225\right] = \frac{2.6900}{3} = 0.8967
$$

$$
\sigma_{12} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(0.725)(0.350) + (-1.275)(-1.350) + (0.425)(0.850) + (0.125)(0.150)\right]
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{3}\left[0.2538 + 1.7213 + 0.3613 + 0.0188\right] = \frac{2.3550}{3} = 0.7850
$$

$$
\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7825 & 0.7850 \\ 0.7850 & 0.8967 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Trace Check:** $\text{tr}(\mathbf{C}) = 0.7825 + 0.8967 = 1.6792 = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2$ (this equality must hold — we will verify later).

### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Eigenvalues via Characteristic Equation

$$
\det(\mathbf{C} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0 \implies \begin{vmatrix} 0.7825 - \lambda & 0.7850 \\ 0.7850 & 0.8967 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} = 0
$$

Expanding the determinant:

$$
(0.7825 - \lambda)(0.8967 - \lambda) - (0.7850)^{2} = 0
$$

$$
\lambda^{2} - 1.6792\,\lambda + (0.7825)(0.8967) - 0.6162 = 0
$$

$$
\lambda^{2} - 1.6792\,\lambda + 0.7015 - 0.6162 = 0
$$

$$
\lambda^{2} - 1.6792\,\lambda + 0.0853 = 0
$$

Applying the quadratic formula $\lambda = \dfrac{1.6792 \pm \sqrt{1.6792^{2} - 4(0.0853)}}{2}$:

$$
\Delta = 2.8197 - 0.3412 = 2.4785 \implies \sqrt{\Delta} = 1.5743
$$

$$
\lambda_1 = \frac{1.6792 + 1.5743}{2} = \frac{3.2535}{2} = 1.6268
$$

$$
\lambda_2 = \frac{1.6792 - 1.5743}{2} = \frac{0.1049}{2} = 0.0524
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** $\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 1.6268 + 0.0524 = 1.6792 = \text{tr}(\mathbf{C})$ ✓
> $\lambda_1 \cdot \lambda_2 = 1.6268 \times 0.0524 = 0.0852 \approx \det(\mathbf{C}) = 0.0853$ ✓

### 3.2.6 Step 5 — Eigenvector for PC1 ($\lambda_1 = 1.6268$)

Solving $(\mathbf{C} - \lambda_1 \mathbf{I})\mathbf{v}_1 = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 0.7825 - 1.6268 & 0.7850 \\ 0.7850 & 0.8967 - 1.6268 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v_{11} \\ v_{21} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{bmatrix} -0.8443 & 0.7850 \\ 0.7850 & -0.7301 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v_{11} \\ v_{21} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

From the first row:

$$
-0.8443\, v_{11} + 0.7850\, v_{21} = 0 \implies v_{21} = \frac{0.8443}{0.7850}\, v_{11} = 1.0755\, v_{11}
$$

Normalizing so that $v_{11}^{2} + v_{21}^{2} = 1$:

$$
v_{11}^{2} + (1.0755\, v_{11})^{2} = 1 \implies v_{11}^{2}(1 + 1.1567) = 1 \implies v_{11}^{2} = 0.4636
$$

$$
v_{11} = 0.6809,\quad v_{21} = 0.7324
$$

$$
\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6809 \\ 0.7324 \end{bmatrix}\quad (\text{PC1 loadings})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LCA Interpretation:** Both loadings are **positive and similar in magnitude (≈ 0.7 each)**, meaning PC1 represents a *general environmental-burden axis* on which high GWP and high AP co-occur — a fossil-fuel-combustion signature.

### 3.2.7 Step 6 — Eigenvector for PC2 ($\lambda_2 = 0.0524$)

Solving $(\mathbf{C} - \lambda_2 \mathbf{I})\mathbf{v}_2 = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
-0.7301\, v_{12} + 0.7850\, v_{22} = 0 \implies v_{22} = 0.9301\, v_{12}
$$

Normalizing:

$$
v_{12}^{2} + (0.9301\, v_{12})^{2} = 1 \implies v_{12}^{2}(1 + 0.8651) = 1 \implies v_{12}^{2} = 0.5362
$$

$$
v_{12} = 0.7323,\quad v_{22} = -0.6810 \quad (\text{sign chosen to make } v_{12} > 0)
$$

$$
\mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7323 \\ -0.6810 \end{bmatrix}\quad (\text{PC2 loadings})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **LCA Interpretation:** PC2 has **opposite-signed loadings** — high GWP with low AP, or vice versa. This is the *acidification-vs-warming trade-off axis* and is the "minor" story (only 3% of variance).

### 3.2.8 Step 7 — Proportion of Variance Explained

$$
\text{PoVE}_1 = \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2} = \frac{1.6268}{1.6792} = 0.9688 = 96.88\%
$$

$$
\text{PoVE}_2 = \frac{0.0524}{1.6792} = 0.0312 = 3.12\%
$$

**Conclusion:** PC1 alone captures **96.88%** of total variance — a single dimension is sufficient to summarize the environmental story of these 4 products.

### 3.2.9 Step 8 — Scores of All 4 Products on PC1

Using $s_{i1} = x_{i1}^{c}\cdot v_{11} + x_{i2}^{c}\cdot v_{21}$:

| Product $i$ | $x_{i1}^{c}$ | $x_{i2}^{c}$ | Score $s_{i1}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $+0.725$ | $+0.350$ | $0.725(0.6809) + 0.350(0.7324) = 0.4937 + 0.2563 = \mathbf{+0.7500}$ |
| 2 | $-1.275$ | $-1.350$ | $-1.275(0.6809) + (-1.350)(0.7324) = -0.8681 - 0.9887 = \mathbf{-1.8568}$ |
| 3 | $+0.425$ | $+0.850$ | $0.425(0.6809) + 0.850(0.7324) = 0.2894 + 0.6225 = \mathbf{+0.9119}$ |
| 4 | $+0.125$ | $+0.150$ | $0.125(0.6809) + 0.150(0.7324) = 0.0851 + 0.1099 = \mathbf{+0.1950}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** Product 2 (score $-1.86$) is the **most environmentally benign**; Product 3 (score $+0.91$) is the **most environmentally burdened**. The product ranking from "best-to-worst" is: **P2 ≪ P4 < P1 < P3**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PCA Workflow Pipeline in an LCA Study (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start LCA Study] --> nodeB[Compile LCI Inventory Data]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Compute LCIA Midpoint Indicators]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Form n by p Impact Matrix]
    nodeD --> nodeE{Units Homogeneous?}
    nodeE -- NO --> nodeF[Standardize by Z-score]
    nodeE -- YES --> nodeG[Mean Center Columns]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Construct Covariance Matrix C]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Solve C v equals lambda v]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Sort Eigenvalues Descending]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Apply Kaiser or Scree Rule]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Select K Principal Components]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Compute Scores s equals Xc V]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Plot Score and Loading Biplot]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Interpret Dominant Impact Pathways]
    nodeO --> nodeP[End Improved LCA Decision]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of PCA Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputLayer[LCA Data Input]
        nodeA1[LCI Inventory Matrix n by p]
        nodeA2[Impact Category Labels]
    end
    subgraph processLayer[PCA Processing Engine]
        nodeB1[Module 1 Mean Centering]
        nodeB2[Module 2 Covariance Assembly]
        nodeB3[Module 3 Eigendecomposition]
        nodeB4[Module 4 PC Ranking and Selection]
    end
    subgraph outputLayer[Interpretation Output]
        nodeC1[Loadings Matrix p by K]
        nodeC2[Scores Matrix n by K]
        nodeC3[Scree Plot Visualization]
        nodeC4[Variance Explained Table]
        nodeC5[Biplot Diagnostic Chart]
    end
    nodeA1 --> nodeB1
    nodeA2 --> nodeB1
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3
    nodeB3 --> nodeB4
    nodeB4 --> nodeC1
    nodeB4 --> nodeC2
    nodeB4 --> nodeC3
    nodeB4 --> nodeC4
    nodeC1 --> nodeC5
    nodeC2 --> nodeC5
```

## 4.3 Scree-Plot and Biplot Conceptual Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ1[Data Cloud in p-dim Space] --> nodeQ2[Apply PCA Rotation]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeQ3[PC1 captures Max Variance]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeQ4[PC2 Orthogonal to PC1]
    nodeQ3 --> nodeQ5[Biplot x-axis PC1 score]
    nodeQ4 --> nodeQ6[Biplot y-axis PC2 score]
    nodeQ5 --> nodeQ7[Loading arrows for each impact category]
    nodeQ6 --> nodeQ7
    nodeQ7 --> nodeQ8[LCA Cluster Identification]
    nodeQ3 --> nodeQ9[Scree Plot Variance Drop]
    nodeQ4 --> nodeQ9
    nodeQ9 --> nodeQ10[Elbow Locates Optimal K]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The biplot simultaneously shows **products as points** (in score coordinates) and **impact categories as arrows** (in loading coordinates). Closely-aligned arrows = correlated impact categories; arrows pointing in opposite quadrants = trade-off relationships.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Style]**

### Question A.1 (3 Marks)
**"Define Principal Component Analysis. State any two applications of PCA in Life Cycle Assessment."**

**Model Answer:**
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is an orthogonal linear transformation technique that converts a set of possibly correlated LCA impact-category variables into a smaller set of uncorrelated variables called principal components, with PC1 aligned along the direction of maximum variance in the data.
*(2 marks for definition)*

Two applications in LCA:
1. **Dimensionality reduction** of high-dimensional LCIA datasets (e.g., 10+ midpoint categories → 2–3 PCs) for visualization and interpretation. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Identification of dominant environmental drivers** by examining loading vectors that reveal which life-cycle stage or impact category contributes most to overall environmental burden. *(0.5 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** write PCA "removes" or "eliminates" variables — it is a *transformation*, not deletion. State that variance is "re-expressed," not "discarded."

---

### Question A.2 (3 Marks)
**"What is a scree plot? Explain how it is used in deciding the number of principal components to retain in a PCA-based LCA interpretation."**

**Model Answer:**
A scree plot is a line plot of the eigenvalues ($\lambda_k$) of the covariance (or correlation) matrix versus the principal component index $k$, arranged in descending order. *(1 mark)*

**Procedure to use it:** The point on the curve where the slope changes abruptly from "steep" to "flat" forms an "elbow." All components *before* the elbow are retained, and those *after* the elbow are discarded because they contribute negligibly to total variance. *(2 marks)*

In LCA, this rule allows the analyst to retain only those PCs that capture meaningful environmental variation, e.g., retain 2 PCs if the elbow appears at $k = 2$, discarding the noisy remaining components that mostly capture rounding errors or rare-event impacts.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 3 Internal Choice, Dec 2023 Style]**

---

### Question B — Choice A (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain, with a suitable flow diagram, the step-by-step procedure of applying Principal Component Analysis to a Life Cycle Assessment dataset. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Data Assembly (1 mark):** Collect the LCIA results from $n$ products/scenarios across $p$ impact categories into a matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$. Example: $n = 5$ products × $p = 8$ ReCiPe midpoint categories (GWP, AP, EP, HTPc, HTPnc, ODP, POCP, TETP).

**Step 2 — Standardization (1 mark):** Since impact categories have heterogeneous units (e.g., kg CO$_2$-eq vs. kg CFC-11-eq), apply Z-score standardization:
$z_{ij} = \dfrac{x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j}{s_j}$, where $s_j$ is the standard deviation of column $j$.

**Step 3 — Covariance Matrix Construction (1 mark):** Compute $\mathbf{C} = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}^{cT}\mathbf{X}^{c}$, a $p \times p$ symmetric positive semi-definite matrix.

**Step 4 — Eigendecomposition (1 mark):** Solve $\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{V}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}$ to obtain eigenvectors $\mathbf{v}_k$ and eigenvalues $\lambda_k$.

**Step 5 — PC Selection (1 mark):** Rank eigenvalues descending. Use the **Kaiser criterion** ($\lambda_k > 1$ for standardized data) and/or **Cumulative PoVE ≥ 0.85** to select $K$ PCs.

**Step 6 — Score Calculation (1 mark):** Project mean-centered data: $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{X}^{c}\mathbf{V}_K$, where $\mathbf{V}_K$ contains the first $K$ eigenvectors.

**Step 7 — Interpretation & Reporting (1 mark):** Construct the **biplot**, identify product clusters, and report loadings to map PCs back to the original impact categories, then translate findings into LCA-improvement recommendations.

**Flow Diagram (Embedded Reference):** See *Section 4.1 Mermaid Pipeline* of these notes — a fully labeled 15-node PCA-in-LCA workflow.

---

**(b) An LCA study yielded the following 4-scenario × 2-indicator dataset on cumulative energy demand and CO$_2$ emissions. Compute the principal components, the proportion of variance explained, and rank the scenarios. (7 marks)**

$$
\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 5 \\ 4 & 2 \\ 8 & 6 \\ 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Mean vector:**

$$
\bar{x}_1 = \frac{10+4+8+2}{4} = 6.0,\quad \bar{x}_2 = \frac{5+2+6+1}{4} = 3.5
$$

**Step 2 — Mean-centered matrix $\mathbf{X}^{c}$:**

$$
\mathbf{X}^{c} = \begin{bmatrix} +4 & +1.5 \\ -2 & -1.5 \\ +2 & +2.5 \\ -4 & -2.5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Covariance matrix (n-1 = 3):**

$$
\sigma_1^2 = \frac{1}{3}(16 + 4 + 4 + 16) = \frac{40}{3} = 13.333
$$

$$
\sigma_2^2 = \frac{1}{3}(2.25 + 2.25 + 6.25 + 6.25) = \frac{17.0}{3} = 5.667
$$

$$
\sigma_{12} = \frac{1}{3}(4\cdot1.5 + (-2)(-1.5) + 2\cdot2.5 + (-4)(-2.5)) = \frac{1}{3}(6.0 + 3.0 + 5.0 + 10.0) = \frac{24.0}{3} = 8.000
$$

$$
\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 13.333 & 8.000 \\ 8.000 & 5.667 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Eigenvalues:**

$$
\det(\mathbf{C} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = (13.333 - \lambda)(5.667 - \lambda) - 64 = 0
$$

$$
\lambda^2 - 19.000\,\lambda + 75.555 - 64 = 0 \implies \lambda^2 - 19\,\lambda + 11.555 = 0
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{19 \pm \sqrt{361 - 46.22}}{2} = \frac{19 \pm \sqrt{314.78}}{2} = \frac{19 \pm 17.742}{2}
$$

$$
\lambda_1 = 18.371,\quad \lambda_2 = 0.629
$$

**Step 5 — Proportion of Variance Explained:**

$$
\text{PoVE}_1 = \frac{18.371}{19.000} = 0.9669 = 96.69\%,\quad \text{PoVE}_2 = 3.31\%
$$

**Step 6 — PC1 Loading vector (eigenvector of $\lambda_1$):**

$$
(13.333 - 18.371)v_{11} + 8.000\, v_{21} = 0 \implies -5.038\,v_{11} + 8.000\,v_{21} = 0
$$

$$
v_{21} = 0.6298\, v_{11} \implies \text{normalizing: } v_{11} = 0.8464,\ v_{21} = 0.5326
$$

$$
\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8464 \\ 0.5326 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7 — Scores on PC1:**

| Scenario | $x_1^c$ | $x_2^c$ | Score $s_{i1} = 0.8464 x_1^c + 0.5326 x_2^c$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | +4 | +1.5 | $+3.386 + 0.799 = +4.185$ |
| 2 | −2 | −1.5 | $-1.693 - 0.799 = -2.492$ |
| 3 | +2 | +2.5 | $+1.693 + 1.332 = +3.025$ |
| 4 | −4 | −2.5 | $-3.386 - 1.332 = -4.718$ |

**Final Ranking (Best to Worst Environmental Performance):**

$$
\boxed{\text{Scenario 4} \ (\text{score }-4.72) \prec \text{Scenario 2}\ (-2.49) \prec \text{Scenario 3}\ (+3.03) \prec \text{Scenario 1}\ (+4.19)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting Bessel's correction** ($n-1$ vs $n$) — costs **1 mark** if divisor is wrong.
> 2. **Not normalizing the eigenvector** — must satisfy $v_{11}^2 + v_{21}^2 = 1$; partial credit only.
> 3. **Omitting PoVE computation** — without it, the answer is incomplete; lose **1 mark**.
> 4. **Misinterpreting signs of loadings** — opposite sign on PC1 between $X_1$ and $X_2$ would mean *trade-off*; here both are *positive* meaning *co-occurrence*. Spelling this out earns the interpretation mark.

---

### Question B — Choice B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE]

**(a) Discuss the role of PCA in LCA interpretation. Differentiate between loadings and scores, illustrating with a biplot description. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of PCA in LCA Interpretation (4 marks):**

PCA is a critical post-LCIA tool that converts the multi-impact-category outputs of an LCA into a small number of orthogonal axes that summarize the *dominant environmental performance dimensions* of a product portfolio. Specifically, it serves to:

1. **Synthesize complexity:** A typical ReCiPe LCIA yields 16–18 midpoint categories. PCA reduces this to 2–3 principal components that retain ~85–95% of the variance, making communication with non-technical stakeholders (e.g., managers, regulators) tractable.
2. **Detect correlation structures:** Impact categories that load strongly on the same PC are *correlated* and likely driven by a common life-cycle stage (e.g., GWP and AP both load on PC1 → fossil-fuel combustion is the common cause).
3. **Reveal trade-offs:** PCs with *opposite-signed* loadings (e.g., +GWP, −land-use) reveal genuine trade-offs — improving one impact harms another.
4. **Cluster products:** The score matrix $\mathbf{S}$ enables k-means or hierarchical clustering to group products by environmental behavior.
5. **Reduce weighting subjectivity:** The empirically-derived PCs offer an *objective* data-driven basis for normalization/weighting decisions in LCIA.

**Loadings vs. Scores (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Loadings ($v_{jk}$) | Scores ($s_{ik}$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Mathematical role** | Eigenvector elements — give the *cosine* of the angle between the original variable axis and the PC axis. | Projection of each sample on the PC axis. |
| **Dimension** | $p \times K$ (impact categories × PCs) | $n \times K$ (products × PCs) |
| **Interpretation in LCA** | Quantifies *how strongly* an impact category contributes to a PC. | Quantifies *how strongly* a product embodies a particular environmental pattern. |
| **Magnitude range** | $\sum_j v_{jk}^2 = 1$ (unit length) | Unbounded; same units as original variables. |

**Biplot Description (1 mark):** A biplot overlays a **scatter plot of scores** (products as points) and **arrows for loadings** (impact categories as arrows from origin). Arrows pointing in similar directions indicate correlated categories; arrows pointing in opposite directions indicate trade-offs; product positions on the arrow side indicate high contribution of that category to that product's burden.

---

**(b) Explain the Kaiser criterion and the Scree-test rule for selecting the number of principal components. Under what circumstances is standardization mandatory before PCA in LCA? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Kaiser Criterion (3 marks):**
The Kaiser criterion states: *Retain every principal component whose eigenvalue $\lambda_k > 1$* — but **only after standardization** of the original data. Eigenvalue 1 is the threshold because it corresponds to the variance of a *single* standardized variable (which by definition has variance 1). In LCA applications with heterogeneous impact categories (e.g., kg CO$_2$-eq versus kg CFC-11-eq), this rule is widely used because it provides a simple, parameter-free stopping rule. *Limitation:* It tends to retain fewer PCs in large datasets ($p > 20$), often under-representing cumulative variance.

**Scree-Test Rule (2 marks):**
The scree test (Cattell, 1966) plots eigenvalues $\lambda_k$ versus index $k$. The *elbow* — the point at which the curve transitions from a steep decline to a flat plateau — separates *signal* (left of elbow) from *noise* (right of elbow). All PCs *before* the elbow are retained. The scree plot is visual and subjective, but more flexible than Kaiser when eigenvalues drop gradually (which often occurs in LCA with many correlated impact categories).

**When Standardization is Mandatory (2 marks):**
Standardization is required whenever the impact categories have:
- **Different physical units** (e.g., GWP in kg CO$_2$-eq vs. AP in kg SO$_2$-eq vs. land use in m²·yr).
- **Different magnitudes of typical values** (e.g., GWP values range in tens, but HTPc values range in 10⁻⁶).
- **Different measurement scales** (cumulative vs. per-functional-unit indicators).

Without standardization, PCA on the *raw* covariance matrix will be **dominated by the highest-variance impact category**, which is arbitrary and not based on environmental importance. The fix: standardize first → use the **correlation matrix** (which equals the covariance of standardized data) → apply Kaiser criterion with $\lambda > 1$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. Students frequently state "standardize the data" without explaining *why* (the unit-mismatch argument). Always tie standardization to the *correlation* matrix, not just the *covariance* matrix.
> 2. Kaiser criterion **only applies to eigenvalues of the correlation matrix** — students lose 1 mark if they apply it to the raw covariance matrix.
> 3. The scree test is *visual* and *subjective* — be sure to mention that this is both its strength (intuitive) and weakness (low reproducibility across analysts).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Master Before the KTU Exam**

### 5.3.1 Conceptual Pillars (Definition-Level)
- [ ] PCA is an **orthogonal linear transformation** that **maximizes variance** along successive axes.
- [ ] PC1 has the **largest eigenvalue**; subsequent PCs are **mutually orthogonal** and have **descending eigenvalues**.
- [ ] PCA is **unsupervised** — no labels, no training set, no predictor variable.
- [ ] PCA is used in the **Interpretation phase** of LCA (ISO 14040 framework).

### 5.3.2 Numerical Pillars (Computation-Level)
- [ ] Always **mean-center** before constructing $\mathbf{C}$ (formulas assume centered data).
- [ ] Use **$n - 1$** (Bessel's correction) in the denominator of variance/covariance.
- [ ] $\mathbf{C}$ is **symmetric** and **positive semi-definite** — all eigenvalues are $\ge 0$.
- [ ] Eigenvectors must be **normalized** to unit length: $\sum_j v_{jk}^2 = 1$.
- [ ] Verify with: $\sum_k \lambda_k = \text{tr}(\mathbf{C})$ and $\prod_k \lambda_k = \det(\mathbf{C})$.
- [ ] **PoVE$_k$** = $\dfrac{\lambda_k}{\sum_j \lambda_j}$; **CumPoVE** = sum of PoVE for retained PCs.

### 5.3.3 Interpretive Pillars (Application-Level)
- [ ] **Loadings** = how each impact category contributes to a PC.
- [ ] **Scores** = how each product performs on a PC.
- [ ] **Biplot** = overlay of scores (points) and loadings (arrows).
- [ ] Same-sign loadings → *correlated* categories; opposite-sign loadings → *trade-off* categories.
- [ ] Standardize when units differ; use **Kaiser** ($\lambda > 1$) for correlation matrix; use **Scree plot** for visual elbow.

### 5.3.4 Common LCA-Specific Use Cases to Recall
- [ ] **Carbon-footprint aggregation** across GWP, AP, EP.
- [ ] **Biofuel LCA** to distinguish feedstock vs. conversion impacts.
- [ ] **Building materials comparison** (concrete vs. steel vs. timber).
- [ ] **End-of-Life scenario assessment** (recycle vs. landfill).
- [ ] **Eco-design hotspot identification** at process level.

### 5.3.5 Top 5 Examiner's Pitfalls (Lose These Marks at Your Own Risk)
1. Forgetting $n - 1$ → wrong covariance.
2. Not normalizing eigenvectors → wrong scores.
3. Applying Kaiser to raw covariance (only valid for *correlation* matrix).
4. Confusing "loadings" with "scores" in interpretation answers.
5. Stating PCA "removes" variables — it *transforms* them, retaining information in rotated axes.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure/Fault Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Failure/Fault Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Failure Analysis (Fault Analysis)** is the systematic, multidisciplinary engineering investigation conducted to determine the *root cause*, *mechanism*, and *contributing factors* responsible for the in-service degradation, fracture, or functional loss of an engineering component, structure, or system. It is an *evidence-driven*, *post-mortem* diagnostic science that integrates principles of materials science, fracture mechanics, surface science, tribology, corrosion science, and non-destructive evaluation.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with NEP 2020 outcome-based education, failure analysis is mapped to higher-order Bloom's levels — **Analyze (L4)**, **Evaluate (L5)**, and **Create (L6)** — because students must synthesize fragmented physical evidence (fracture surfaces, chemical residues, microstructural anomalies) into a coherent causal narrative.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Failure analysis is treated as the *culminating skill* of the surface engineering course because it validates whether prior modules (corrosion mechanisms, wear types, coating performance) actually protected the component under real operating conditions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Detective Work" Intuition

Imagine a **hospital emergency room (ER)** receiving a critically injured patient. The trauma team does not simply treat the visible wound — they investigate:

- **What** caused the injury? (Gunshot, fall, collision — analogous to fracture mode)
- **Why** did it happen? (Speeding, fatigue, negligence — analogous to root cause)
- **When** did the damage initiate? (Hours, weeks, years — analogous to incubation time)
- **How** fast did it propagate? (Sudden vs. progressive — analogous to crack growth rate)

Failure analysis is *exactly* this process, but applied to *inanimate objects*. The **fracture surface is the patient's body**, the **metallurgical microstructure is the medical history**, and the **operating conditions are the lifestyle**. A failure analyst plays the role of a forensic doctor, except the patient cannot speak — only the *fracture surface* and *debris* tell the story.

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Truth:** *A component never fails "without reason." Every failure has a deterministic, identifiable cause. The analyst's job is to convert silent physical evidence into an unambiguous engineering verdict.*

## 1.3 Fundamental Objectives of Failure Analysis

| S.No | Objective | Engineering Significance |
|:----:|:----------|:-------------------------|
| 1 | Identify the **failure mode** (ductile, brittle, fatigue, creep, SCC, wear, overload) | Establishes *what physically happened* |
| 2 | Determine the **root cause** (design flaw, material defect, misuse, environment) | Establishes *why it happened* |
| 3 | Quantify the **mechanism** (stress level, temperature, environment) | Establishes *how fast it happened* |
| 4 | Recommend **corrective and preventive actions** (CAPA) | Establishes *how to stop recurrence* |
| 5 | Establish **legal and contractual liability** (forensic documentation) | Establishes *who is responsible* |

## 1.4 Why Failure Analysis Matters — Engineering Drivers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Economic Impact:** Industrialized economies lose an estimated **3 % to 6 % of GDP annually** to materials failure-related costs (corrosion, wear, fracture, fatigue). A single catastrophic failure (aircraft, pressure vessel, bridge) can cost **billions of dollars** in liability and human life.

The seven canonical drivers for failure analysis studies are:

1. **Safety** — Prevention of loss of life and injury.
2. **Economic** — Reduction of direct repair costs and indirect downtime losses.
3. **Reliability** — Improvement of Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF).
4. **Legal** — Litigation support, warranty disputes, insurance claims.
5. **Regulatory** — Compliance with ASME, ASTM, ISO, API, BIS standards.
6. **Knowledge** — Generation of design feedback for next-generation components.
7. **Forensic** — Establishing *cause-and-effect* chains for accidental events.

## 1.5 Failure Analysis vs. Fault Analysis — Terminology Distinction

| Aspect | Failure Analysis | Fault Analysis |
|:-------|:-----------------|:----------------|
| **Domain** | Materials, mechanical, structural | Systems, electrical, software, process |
| **Trigger Event** | Component fracture, rupture, deformation | Functional deviation, abnormal output |
| **Scope** | Physical, microstructural, fractographic | Logical, statistical, probabilistic |
| **Primary Tools** | SEM, EDS, XRD, optical microscopy | FTA, FMEA, RCA, Ishikawa diagrams |
| **KTU 2024 Focus** | **Both are integrated** under this module | **Both are integrated** under this module |

The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly merges these disciplines because a *physical fracture* may originate from a *system-level fault* (e.g., incorrect sensor calibration → overpressure → rupture).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Engineering Failures — The Master Taxonomy

Engineering failures can be classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

### Axis 1: By Mechanism (Most Common in KTU Examinations)

1. **Mechanical Overload Failure**
   - Static (Monotonic) Overload
   - Ductile Fracture
   - Brittle Fracture
   - Cleavage Fracture

2. **Fatigue Failure**
   - High-Cycle Fatigue (HCF), $N > 10^5$ cycles
   - Low-Cycle Fatigue (LCF), $N < 10^5$ cycles
   - Thermal Fatigue
   - Corrosion Fatigue
   - Fretting Fatigue

3. **Creep Failure**
   - Primary, Secondary, Tertiary Stages
   - Rupture at Elevated Temperature
   - Stress Rupture
   - Creep-Fatigue Interaction

4. **Environment-Assisted Failure**
   - Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)
   - Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE)
   - Liquid Metal Embrittlement (LME)
   - Hydrogen-Induced Cracking (HIC)

5. **Wear Failure**
   - Adhesive Wear
   - Abrasive Wear
   - Erosive Wear
   - Cavitation Wear
   - Fretting Wear

6. **Combined / Synergistic Failure**
   - Tribo-Corrosion
   - Corrosion-Fatigue
   - Creep-Fatigue-Oxidation
   - Hydrogen-Assisted Fatigue

### Axis 2: By Scale of Observation

| Scale | Observable Features | Typical Tool |
|:------|:-------------------|:-------------|
| **Macroscale** | Gross deformation, necking, crack path | Visual, 10× magnifier |
| **Mesoscale** | Beach marks, cleavage facets | Stereo-optical microscope |
| **Microscale** | Striations, dimples, microvoids | SEM, TEM |
| **Nanoscale** | Dislocation cell structure, GP zones | HR-TEM, AFM |
| **Atomic Scale** | Bond rupture, segregation | Atom Probe Tomography |

### Axis 3: By Temperature Regime

- **Cold Failure:** $T < 0.3 T_m$ (fracture, fatigue, SCC, wear)
- **Warm Failure:** $0.3 T_m < T < 0.5 T_m$ (warm creep, blue brittleness)
- **Hot Failure:** $T > 0.5 T_m$ (creep, oxidation, hot corrosion)

> Where $T_m$ is the **absolute melting temperature** in Kelvin.

### Axis 4: By Location

- **Surface-Initiated Failure** (fatigue, wear, SCC, fretting)
- **Subsurface-Initiated Failure** (internal fatigue, HIC, rolling contact fatigue)
- **Bulk-Initiated Failure** (overload, ductile/brittle fracture)

## 2.2 Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM) — Foundational Theory

LEFM assumes small-scale yielding and provides the **stress intensity factor (SIF)** $K$ as the governing fracture parameter.

### Mode of Loading

- **Mode I** (Opening mode): $K_I$ — most common, most critical
- **Mode II** (Sliding / in-plane shear): $K_{II}$
- **Mode III** (Tearing / anti-plane shear): $K_{III}$

### General SIF Expression

For a central crack of length $2a$ in an infinite plate under remote tensile stress $\sigma$:

$$
K_I = \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}
$$

For an edge crack of length $a$ in a semi-infinite plate:

$$
K_I = 1.12 \, \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}
$$

For a finite-width plate (width $W$) containing a central crack:

$$
K_I = \sigma \sqrt{\pi a} \cdot f\left(\frac{a}{W}\right)
$$

Where $f(a/W)$ is a geometry-dependent correction factor.

### Critical Stress for Fracture (Fracture Toughness Criterion)

$$
K_I \geq K_{IC}
$$

Where:
- $K_{IC}$ = **plane strain fracture toughness** (material property, MPa·m$^{1/2}$)
- $a_c$ = **critical crack length** (m)

### Critical Crack Length (Rearranged Griffith Equation)

$$
a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \left(\frac{K_{IC}}{\sigma Y}\right)^2
$$

Where $Y$ is a dimensionless geometry factor typically in the range $1 \leq Y \leq 2$.

## 2.3 Griffith–Orowan Energy Criterion

The original energy balance approach (1920, refined by Orowan in 1948 for metals):

$$
\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2E(\gamma_s + \gamma_p)}{\pi a}}
$$

Where:
- $E$ = **Young's modulus** (Pa or MPa)
- $\gamma_s$ = **true surface energy per unit area** (J/m²)
- $\gamma_p$ = **plastic work per unit area** (J/m²)
- $a$ = **half-crack length** (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For ductile metals, $\gamma_p \gg \gamma_s$ (typically by **3 orders of magnitude**). This is why Griffith's original equation underestimated the strength of metals but worked beautifully for brittle ceramics and glasses.

## 2.4 Fatigue Crack Growth — Paris Law

The most widely applied empirical law for stable fatigue crack propagation:

$$
\frac{da}{dN} = C \left(\Delta K\right)^m
$$

Where:
- $\frac{da}{dN}$ = **crack growth rate** (m/cycle)
- $\Delta K = K_{max} - K_{min}$ = **stress intensity factor range** (MPa·m$^{1/2}$)
- $C$ = **Paris constant** (material-environment dependent)
- $m$ = **Paris exponent** (typically $2 \leq m \leq 7$ for metals; $m > 10$ for ceramics)

### The Three Regimes of Fatigue Crack Growth (Sigmoidal da/dN vs. ΔK Curve)

| Regime | Behavior | Mechanism |
|:------:|:---------|:----------|
| **I (Near-threshold)** | $\Delta K_{th} < \Delta K < \Delta K^*$ | Microstructural sensitivity, oxide-induced closure |
| **II (Paris)** | Linear on log-log scale | Stable striation growth |
| **III (Unstable)** | $\Delta K > \Delta K_c$ | K-controlled unstable fracture |

The threshold stress intensity range for crack non-propagation:

$$
\Delta K_{th} \approx (5 \text{ to } 10) \times 10^{-3} \, K_{IC}
$$

## 2.5 Creep Failure — Norton–Bailey Power Law

For steady-state (secondary) creep at elevated temperature:

$$
\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = A \, \sigma^n \, \exp\left(-\frac{Q_c}{RT}\right)
$$

Where:
- $\dot{\epsilon}_{ss}$ = **steady-state creep strain rate** (s$^{-1}$)
- $A$ = **material constant** (Dorn constant)
- $\sigma$ = **applied stress** (MPa)
- $n$ = **creep stress exponent** (typically $3 \leq n \leq 8$)
- $Q_c$ = **apparent activation energy for creep** (kJ/mol)
- $R$ = **universal gas constant** = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = **absolute temperature** (K)

### Larson–Miller Parameter (for Creep Rupture Life Prediction)

$$
P_{LM} = T \left[ \log(t_r) + C \right]
$$

Where:
- $t_r$ = **rupture time** (hours)
- $C$ = **Larson–Miller constant** (typically $C \approx 20$ for steels)

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Key Variables | Units | Typical Validity |
|:-:|:-------------|:---------|:--------------|:------|:-----------------|
| 1 | Mode I SIF (infinite plate) | $K_I = \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | $\sigma$, $a$ | MPa·m$^{1/2}$ | $a \ll W$ |
| 2 | Edge crack SIF | $K_I = 1.12 \, \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | $\sigma$, $a$ | MPa·m$^{1/2}$ | Single edge |
| 3 | Critical crack length | $a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \left(\frac{K_{IC}}{\sigma Y}\right)^2$ | $K_{IC}$, $\sigma$, $Y$ | m | LEFM |
| 4 | Griffith–Orowan | $\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2E(\gamma_s + \gamma_p)}{\pi a}}$ | $E$, $\gamma$, $a$ | Pa | Brittle/ductile |
| 5 | Paris Law | $\frac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^m$ | $\Delta K$, $C$, $m$ | m/cycle | Stage II fatigue |
| 6 | Fatigue threshold | $\Delta K_{th} \approx 0.005 \, K_{IC}$ | $K_{IC}$ | MPa·m$^{1/2}$ | Long-life regime |
| 7 | Norton–Bailey creep | $\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = A\sigma^n \exp(-Q_c/RT)$ | $\sigma$, $T$ | s$^{-1}$ | Steady-state creep |
| 8 | Larson–Miller | $P_{LM} = T[\log t_r + C]$ | $T$, $t_r$ | K · log(h) | Rupture |
| 9 | Goodman line | $\sigma_a = \sigma_{-1}\left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u}\right)$ | $\sigma_a$, $\sigma_m$ | MPa | Mean-stress fatigue |
| 10 | Monkman–Grant | $\log t_r + m \log \dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = C_{MG}$ | $t_r$, $\dot{\epsilon}_{ss}$ | h, s$^{-1}$ | Creep–rupture |
| 11 | Stress-corrosion threshold | $K_{ISCC} < K_{IC}$ | $K_{ISCC}$ | MPa·m$^{1/2}$ | SCC |
| 12 | Wear rate (Archard) | $Q = \frac{k W L}{H}$ | $k$, $W$, $L$, $H$ | m³/s | Adhesive wear |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Questions frequently combine two formulas. For example, "calculate $a_c$ from Paris Law integration and discuss the implications." Always carry **units** throughout your derivation — examiners *do* deduct marks for unit inconsistency.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Failure Analysis Application |
|:----------------|:----------------------------|
| **Aerospace** | Turbine disk burst, fan blade fracture, fuselage fatigue (e.g., Aloha Airlines 243) |
| **Nuclear** | Pipe cracking, steam generator tube rupture, Zircaloy corrosion |
| **Oil & Gas** | Downhole drill string fatigue, sulfide stress cracking, $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ embrittlement |
| **Automotive** | Axle fatigue, valve spring failure, brake disc cracking |
| **Marine** | Propeller cavitation, hull corrosion, shaft bearing wear |
| **Civil** | Bridge cable fatigue (e.g., Morandi Bridge collapse), rebar SCC |
| **Biomedical** | Hip implant fatigue, stent fracture, dental crown cracking |

> The **"Why"** behind mastering failure analysis: it transforms every catastrophic event into a *learning opportunity*, preventing recurrence and saving lives.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Crack Length from Fracture Toughness

**Problem Statement:** A high-strength steel plate ($K_{IC} = 55 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$, $Y = 1.0$) is subjected to a uniform tensile stress $\sigma = 250 \text{ MPa}$. Determine the *critical crack length* $a_c$ at which catastrophic brittle fracture will occur.

### Step 1: Identify the Governing Fracture Criterion

According to LEFM, fracture initiates when the applied Mode I stress intensity factor equals the material's plane-strain fracture toughness:

$$
K_I = K_{IC}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the SIF Expression for a Central Crack

For a central through-thickness crack of half-length $a$ in an infinite plate:

$$
\sigma \sqrt{\pi a_c} = K_{IC}
$$

### Step 3: Isolate the Critical Crack Length Algebraically

Square both sides:

$$
\sigma^2 \, \pi a_c = K_{IC}^{\,2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
a_c = \frac{K_{IC}^{\,2}}{\pi \sigma^2}
$$

### Step 4: Include the Geometry Factor $Y$ (General Case)

In general, with $Y \neq 1$:

$$
a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \left(\frac{K_{IC}}{\sigma Y}\right)^2
$$

### Step 5: Numerical Evaluation

Substitute the given numerical values (note: $K_{IC}$ must be in $\text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$ and $\sigma$ in MPa, with $a_c$ in metres):

$$
a_c = \frac{1}{\pi} \left(\frac{55 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}}{250 \text{ MPa} \times 1.0}\right)^2
$$

Simplify the fraction inside the parenthesis:

$$
\frac{55}{250} = 0.220
$$

Therefore:

$$
\left(\frac{K_{IC}}{\sigma Y}\right)^2 = (0.220 \, \sqrt{\text{m}})^2 = 0.0484 \, \text{m}
$$

Now divide by $\pi$:

$$
a_c = \frac{0.0484}{3.14159} = 0.01541 \, \text{m}
$$

### Step 6: Final Answer and Engineering Interpretation

$$
a_c \approx 15.4 \, \text{mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A flaw as small as **15.4 mm** (less than a 1-rupee coin's diameter) is enough to trigger catastrophic brittle fracture in this steel plate. This is why fracture-critical components are inspected for cracks well below $a_c$ (typically with a safety factor of 3 to 10), giving allowable crack sizes of **1.5 mm to 5 mm**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fatigue Life from Integrated Paris Law

**Problem Statement:** An aircraft wing component has an initial surface crack of length $a_i = 1.0 \text{ mm}$. The stress intensity range is $\Delta K$ applied at constant amplitude, with $K_{max} = 35 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$ and $R = K_{min}/K_{max} = 0.1$. Given Paris constants $C = 1.5 \times 10^{-11}$ (in SI units when $\Delta K$ is in MPa$\sqrt{\text{m}}$) and $m = 3.0$, calculate the **fatigue life** $N_f$ until the crack reaches a critical length $a_c = 10 \text{ mm}$. Assume geometry factor $Y = 1.0$.

### Step 1: Express $\Delta K$ in Terms of Crack Length

$$
\Delta K = K_{max} - K_{min} = K_{max}(1 - R)
$$

$$
\Delta K = 35 \, \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}} \times (1 - 0.1) = 31.5 \, \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}
$$

But since the geometry is fixed (centre crack), $\Delta K$ will *grow* as the crack length grows:

$$
\Delta K = \Delta\sigma \, Y \sqrt{\pi a}
$$

Where:

$$
\Delta\sigma = \frac{\Delta K_0}{\sqrt{\pi a_i}} = \frac{31.5}{\sqrt{\pi \times 0.001}} \text{ MPa}
$$

### Step 2: Write the Paris Law Equation

$$
\frac{da}{dN} = C \left( \Delta\sigma \, Y \sqrt{\pi a} \right)^m
$$

### Step 3: Separate Variables

$$
dN = \frac{da}{C \left( \Delta\sigma \, Y \sqrt{\pi} \right)^m \, a^{m/2}}
$$

$$
dN = \frac{da}{C \left( \Delta\sigma \, Y \sqrt{\pi} \right)^m \, a^{m/2}}
$$

### Step 4: Integrate Between Initial and Critical Crack Lengths

$$
N_f = \int_{a_i}^{a_c} \frac{da}{C \left( \Delta\sigma \, Y \sqrt{\pi} \right)^m \, a^{m/2}}
$$

Assuming $m = 3$:

$$
N_f = \frac{1}{C \left( \Delta\sigma \, Y \sqrt{\pi} \right)^3} \int_{a_i}^{a_c} a^{-3/2} \, da
$$

### Step 5: Evaluate the Integral

$$
\int_{a_i}^{a_c} a^{-3/2} \, da = \left[ -2 a^{-1/2} \right]_{a_i}^{a_c}
$$

$$
= -2 a_c^{-1/2} + 2 a_i^{-1/2}
$$

$$
= 2 \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{a_i}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{a_c}} \right)
$$

### Step 6: Compute the Numerical Values

$$
\sqrt{a_i} = \sqrt{0.001} = 0.03162 \, \text{m}^{1/2}
$$

$$
\sqrt{a_c} = \sqrt{0.010} = 0.10000 \, \text{m}^{1/2}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{a_i}} = 31.623 \, \text{m}^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{a_c}} = 10.000 \, \text{m}^{-1/2}
$$

Difference:

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{a_i}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{a_c}} = 21.623 \, \text{m}^{-1/2}
$$

### Step 7: Compute $\Delta\sigma$

$$
\Delta\sigma = \frac{31.5}{\sqrt{\pi \times 0.001}} = \frac{31.5}{0.05605} = 561.96 \text{ MPa}
$$

### Step 8: Compute the Denominator

$$
C \left( \Delta\sigma \, Y \sqrt{\pi} \right)^3 = 1.5 \times 10^{-11} \times (561.96 \times 1.0 \times 1.7725)^3
$$

Inner product:

$$
561.96 \times 1.7725 = 996.05 \, \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}
$$

Cubed:

$$
(996.05)^3 \approx 9.883 \times 10^8
$$

Multiply by $C$:

$$
1.5 \times 10^{-11} \times 9.883 \times 10^8 = 1.482 \times 10^{-2}
$$

### Step 9: Final Fatigue Life

$$
N_f = \frac{2 \times 21.623}{1.482 \times 10^{-2}}
$$

$$
N_f = \frac{43.246}{1.482 \times 10^{-2}}
$$

$$
N_f \approx 2919 \text{ cycles}
$$

### Step 10: Engineering Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The component has only **~2920 cycles** of safe life before catastrophic fracture. For an aircraft wing experiencing ~1 cycle per flight minute, this corresponds to roughly **48 hours** of flight time. This is the rationale behind the **Safe-Life** and **Damage-Tolerance** philosophies in aviation (FAR Part 25, CS-25).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Creep Rupture Life via Larson–Miller Parameter

**Problem Statement:** A chrome–molybdenum steel boiler tube is designed for service at $T = 600^\circ\text{C}$ ($T = 873 \, \text{K}$). Short-term tests at $T = 650^\circ\text{C}$ ($T = 923 \, \text{K}$) show rupture at $t_{r,test} = 5000 \, \text{h}$ under the same stress. Assuming $C = 20$ (Larson–Miller constant), estimate the **long-term rupture life** at the design temperature.

### Step 1: Calculate the Larson–Miller Parameter at the Test Condition

$$
P_{LM,test} = T_{test} \left[ \log(t_{r,test}) + C \right]
$$

$$
P_{LM,test} = 923 \times \left[ \log(5000) + 20 \right]
$$

$$
\log(5000) = \log(5) + 3 = 0.6990 + 3 = 3.6990
$$

$$
P_{LM,test} = 923 \times (3.6990 + 20) = 923 \times 23.6990
$$

$$
P_{LM,test} = 21874.18 \, \text{K} \cdot \log(\text{h})
$$

### Step 2: Equate the LMP at Design Conditions

The Larson–Miller principle states that for a given stress, $P_{LM}$ is constant (independent of temperature). Therefore:

$$
P_{LM,design} = P_{LM,test}
$$

$$
T_{design} \left[ \log(t_{r,design}) + C \right] = P_{LM,test}
$$

### Step 3: Solve for $t_{r,design}$

$$
\log(t_{r,design}) + 20 = \frac{21874.18}{873} = 25.057
$$

$$
\log(t_{r,design}) = 25.057 - 20 = 5.057
$$

$$
t_{r,design} = 10^{5.057}
$$

$$
t_{r,design} \approx 1.14 \times 10^5 \, \text{h}
$$

### Step 4: Convert to Years

$$
t_{r,design} = \frac{1.14 \times 10^5}{8760 \, \text{h/year}} \approx 13.0 \, \text{years}
$$

### Step 5: Engineering Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Verdict:** The tube is projected to last approximately **13 years** under creep conditions. The 50 K drop in service temperature (from 650 °C test to 600 °C design) *more than doubled* the projected life — a non-linear Arrhenius effect characteristic of thermally activated processes.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stress-Corrosion Cracking Threshold via Slow-Strain-Rate Test (SSRT) Interpretation

**Problem Statement:** A stainless-steel specimen ($K_{IC} = 120 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$) is tested in boiling $\text{MgCl}_2$ solution (a standard SCC environment per ASTM G36). The threshold stress intensity for SCC is measured as $K_{ISCC} = 24 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$. Compute the *fraction* of fracture toughness lost to environment.

### Step 1: Define the SCC Susceptibility Ratio

$$
\phi_{SCC} = \frac{K_{ISCC}}{K_{IC}}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute Numerical Values

$$
\phi_{SCC} = \frac{24}{120} = 0.20
$$

### Step 3: Interpret the Result

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The environment has degraded the material's effective fracture toughness by **80 %**. The alloy that nominally resists $120 \, \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$ of crack-driving force in inert air fails at only $24 \, \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$ in chloride solution. This is why austenitic stainless steels (304, 316) are **banned from use in warm chloride environments** without protective coatings or alternative alloys.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Systematic Failure Analysis Methodology Flow

This flowchart depicts the canonical **eight-step methodology** prescribed by ASM Handbook Volume 11 and the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Initial Assessment and Safety Check] --> B[Step 2: Background Data Collection]
    B --> C[Step 3: Preliminary Visual Examination]
    C --> D[Step 4: Non Destructive Testing NDT]
    D --> E[Step 5: Selection and Preservation of Fracture Surfaces]
    E --> F[Step 6: Macroscopic Fractographic Analysis]
    F --> G[Step 7: Microscopic Analysis SEM TEM EDS XRD]
    G --> H[Step 8: Mechanical and Chemical Testing]
    H --> I[Step 9: Hypothesis Formation and Verification]
    I --> J{Step 10: Conclusive Root Cause Identified?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Step 11: Final Report with CAPA]
    J -->|No| L[Step 9b: Return to Hypothesize Additional Scenarios]
    L --> I
    K --> M[End: Closure of Failure Investigation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The loop between Steps 9 and 10 represents the **iterative hypothesis-testing nature** of failure analysis. Often, the initial hypothesis (e.g., "fatigue") is invalidated and replaced by a competing hypothesis (e.g., "stress-corrosion cracking"). This is normal and expected.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Master Classification Tree of Mechanical Failures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Mechanical Failure of Component] --> A1[Overload Fracture]
    ROOT --> A2[Fatigue Failure]
    ROOT --> A3[Creep Failure]
    ROOT --> A4[Environment Assisted Failure]
    ROOT --> A5[Wear Failure]

    A1 --> B1[Ductile Overload]
    A1 --> B2[Brittle Overload]
    A1 --> B3[Cleavage Fracture]
    A1 --> B4[Quasi Cleavage]

    A2 --> C1[High Cycle Fatigue HCF]
    A2 --> C2[Low Cycle Fatigue LCF]
    A2 --> C3[Thermal Fatigue]
    A2 --> C4[Corrosion Fatigue]
    A2 --> C5[Fretting Fatigue]

    A3 --> D1[Primary Creep]
    A3 --> D2[Secondary Steady State Creep]
    A3 --> D3[Tertiary Creep and Rupture]
    A3 --> D4[Stress Rupture]

    A4 --> E1[Stress Corrosion Cracking SCC]
    A4 --> E2[Hydrogen Embrittlement HE]
    A4 --> E3[Liquid Metal Embrittlement LME]
    A4 --> E4[Hydrogen Induced Cracking HIC]

    A5 --> F1[Adhesive Wear]
    A5 --> F2[Abrasive Wear]
    A5 --> F3[Erosive Wear]
    A5 --> F4[Cavitation Wear]
    A5 --> F5[Fretting Wear]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Note:** This tree is the most commonly tested concept in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions. Students are expected to *list and briefly describe* at least four of these sub-types. Memorize the bold-face labels.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Failure Analysis Laboratory

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Failed Component from Field] --> STAGE1[Stage 1 Intake and Documentation]
    STAGE1 --> STAGE2[Stage 2 Visual and Macroscopic]
    STAGE2 --> STAGE3[Stage 3 Sample Preparation]

    STAGE3 --> SEM[Scanning Electron Microscope SEM]
    STAGE3 --> TEM[Transmission Electron Microscope TEM]
    STAGE3 --> EDS[Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy EDS]
    STAGE3 --> XRD[X Ray Diffraction XRD]
    STAGE3 --> OPT[Optical Microscopy]
    STAGE3 --> UT[Ultrasonic Testing UT]
    STAGE3 --> MT[Magnetic Particle Testing MT]
    STAGE3 --> PT[Liquid Penetrant Testing PT]

    SEM --> ANALYZE[Stage 4 Data Fusion and Hypothesis]
    TEM --> ANALYZE
    EDS --> ANALYZE
    XRD --> ANALYZE
    OPT --> ANALYZE
    UT --> ANALYZE
    MT --> ANALYZE
    PT --> ANALYZE

    ANALYZE --> MECHDB[Stage 5 Mechanical Testing Database]
    MECHDB --> REPORT[Stage 6 Final Failure Report]
    REPORT --> CAPA[Corrective and Preventive Actions]
    CAPA --> OUTPUT[Design Feedback to Manufacturing]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern failure analysis is **multi-instrumental** — no single tool tells the full story. SEM reveals *fracture mode*, EDS reveals *chemical contamination*, XRD reveals *phase composition*, and NDT reveals *subsurface crack geometry*. The art is in **correlating** outputs across instruments to converge on a single root cause.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Fracture Surface Feature Recognition Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SURFACE[Fracture Surface Under Microscope] --> Q1{Is there macroscopic plastic deformation?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, large necking| R1[Ductile Overload: Cup and Cone Microvoid Coalescence]
    Q1 -->|Minimal deformation| Q2{Are there cleavage facets?}
    Q2 -->|Yes, river patterns| R2[Brittle Cleavage Fracture]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Are there concentric beach marks?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| R3[Fatigue Failure: Striations on SEM]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is the surface intergranular?}
    Q4 -->|Yes, grain boundary facets| R4[Intergranular SCC or HE]
    Q4 -->|No, transgranular with branching| R5[Transgranular SCC]
    Q4 -->|No| Q6{Is there oxidation and multiple cracks?}
    Q6 -->|Yes| R6[Creep or Stress Rupture]
    Q6 -->|No| R7[Unclassified: Return to Step 7 for SEM]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fractographic Decision Tree:** This decision-tree logic is the standard for ASM Level 2 Fractography certification. KTU questions on "fracture surface interpretation" follow exactly this flow.
