**Distinguish between Preventive Maintenance (PM) and Predictive Maintenance (PdM) with one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Preventive Maintenance (PM) | Predictive Maintenance (PdM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Calendar / hours-run based (time-triggered) | Real-time condition data (condition-triggered) |
| **Decision Basis** | Statistical schedule, irrespective of current condition | Vibration spectrum, oil analysis, UT thickness, IR thermography |
| **Risk of Over/Under-maintenance** | Higher (may service healthy equipment) | Lower (intervenes only when needed) |
| **Example** | Replacing pump bearings every 12 months regardless of vibration level | Replacing pump bearings only when vibration velocity exceeds 7.1 mm/s (ISO 10816 threshold) |
| **Cost Profile** | Lower monitoring cost, higher spare-part consumption | Higher sensor cost, lower spare-part consumption |

*(Full 3 marks require the distinction in trigger, example, and at least one economic implication.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 1 × 14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Module Pattern:** Internal choice between two 14-mark questions. Each question typically splits as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Solve EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B**.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Define **Availability, Reliability, and Maintainability**. For a CNC machining centre, the following data were recorded over 2 years (17,520 hours): Total running time = 16,720 hours; number of failures = 8; total repair time = 800 hours. Calculate:
   (i) MTBF
   (ii) MTTR
   (iii) Availability
   (iv) Reliability for a 100-hour mission if failure rate is assumed constant.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **five phases of the equipment life cycle** in Terotechnology with a focus on the role of **corrosion engineering decisions** at each phase. Illustrate with a **rebar-reinforced concrete bridge** example.

**(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Question A(a):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**

- **Availability (A):** Probability that the equipment is in a functioning state at any randomly chosen instant. Expressed as $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$.
- **Reliability (R):** Probability that the equipment performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified duration of time.
- **Maintainability (M):** Probability that a failed equipment is restored to a functional state within a specified downtime, given that maintenance action is initiated per prescribed procedures.

**Numerical Computation (5 marks):**

Given: Total observed time $T = 17{,}520$ h; Operating time = $16{,}720$ h; Failures $N_f = 8$; Total repair time = $800$ h.

**Step 1 — MTBF:**

$$MTBF = \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}} = \dfrac{16{,}720}{8} = 2090\ \text{hours}$$

**Step 2 — MTTR:**

$$MTTR = \dfrac{\text{Total Repair Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}} = \dfrac{800}{8} = 100\ \text{hours}$$

**Step 3 — Availability:**

$$A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{2090}{2090 + 100} = \dfrac{2090}{2190} = 0.9543 = 95.43\%$$

**Step 4 — Failure Rate:**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{2090} = 4.784 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{failures/hour}$$

**Step 5 — Reliability for 100-hour mission:**

$$R(100) = e^{-\lambda \cdot 100} = e^{-0.04784} = 0.9533 = 95.33\%$$

**Final Tabulated Answer (1 mark):**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| MTBF | 2090 h |
| MTTR | 100 h |
| Availability | 95.43% |
| Reliability at 100 h | 95.33% |

---

##### **Model Solution — Question A(b):**

**The five phases of terotechnology applied to a reinforced-concrete bridge (7 marks):**

1. **Design Phase:** Specify concrete cover ≥ 50 mm (as per IS 456), use of **corrosion-resistant rebar** (epoxy-coated, galvanized, or stainless steel), design crack widths to ≤ 0.2 mm to limit chloride ingress. Cathodic-protection anodes may be embedded at design stage.
2. **Acquisition / Construction Phase:** Procurement of rebar with mill certificates, third-party inspection of coatings, controlled storage on site to avoid coating damage.
3. **Operation Phase:** Routine visual inspection of deck soffit, drainage maintenance to prevent ponding, half-cell potential surveys every 5 years to detect active corrosion.
4. **Maintenance Phase:** Application of **silane/siloxane penetrating sealers**, crack injection with epoxy, **impressed-current cathodic protection retrofit** when half-cell potentials exceed −350 mV (Cu/CuSO₄ electrode).
5. **Replacement / Disposal Phase:** Strategic replacement of severely spalled beams; recycling of demolished concrete as aggregate; safe disposal of contaminated debris.

**Synthesis (for full marks):** Each phase's **corrosion decision** should be informed by the previous phase's **data** — closed-loop terotechnology.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Life-Cycle Costing (LCC)**. Two pumps are being evaluated. Pump X costs ₹ 8,00,000 with annual maintenance ₹ 1,00,000 and life 8 years. Pump Y costs ₹ 15,00,000 with annual maintenance ₹ 30,000 and life 15 years. Salvage is negligible. Take discount rate $i = 12\%$. Determine the EUAC for each and recommend.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)**. List its **eight pillars** and explain any **two** pillars in detail with reference to a manufacturing plant.

**(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Question B(a):**

**Conceptual Explanation (2 marks):**

Life-Cycle Costing (LCC) is a terotechnology tool that accounts for **all costs incurred over an asset's life** — acquisition, installation, operation, maintenance, and disposal — discounted to a common time basis. It prevents the **"lowest-bid trap"** where a cheap pump that consumes ₹ 1 lakh/year in repairs is selected over a costlier pump consuming only ₹ 30,000/year.

**Capital Recovery Factor Computation (2 marks):**

$$CRF(12\%,8) = \dfrac{0.12(1.12)^8}{(1.12)^8 - 1} = \dfrac{0.12 \times 2.4760}{2.4760 - 1} = \dfrac{0.29712}{1.4760} = 0.20130$$

$$CRF(12\%,15) = \dfrac{0.12(1.12)^{15}}{(1.12)^{15} - 1} = \dfrac{0.12 \times 5.4736}{5.4736 - 1} = \dfrac{0.65683}{4.4736} = 0.14683$$

**EUAC Computation (2 marks):**

$$EUAC_X = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.20130 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}61{,}040 + 1{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 2{,}61{,}040\ \text{/year}$$

$$EUAC_Y = 15{,}00{,}000 \times 0.14683 + 30{,}000 = 2{,}20{,}245 + 30{,}000 = \text{₹ } 2{,}50{,}245\ \text{/year}$$

**Recommendation (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\text{Pump Y is recommended — annual savings of ₹ 10,795 over a 15-year horizon.}}$$

---

##### **Model Solution — Question B(b):**

**TPM Definition (1 mark):**

Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) is a **company-wide, proactive and preventive maintenance philosophy** developed by Seiichi Nakajima (Japan, 1971) that aims to maximize **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** by involving **every employee — from operators to top management** — in maintenance activities.

**Eight Pillars of TPM (1 mark listing + 5 marks for two explained):**

1. **Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen)**
2. **Planned Maintenance (Keikaku Hozen)**
3. **Quality Maintenance (Hinshitsu Hozen)**
4. **Focused Improvement (Kobetsu Kaizen)**
5. **Early Management / Early Equipment Management**
6. **Training and Education**
7. **Safety, Health, and Environment (SHE)**
8. **TPM in Administration (Office TPM)**

**Detailed Explanation — Pillar 1: Autonomous Maintenance (2.5 marks)**

In a manufacturing plant producing pump impellers, **machine operators** are trained to perform daily tasks traditionally done by maintenance crew: lubrication, cleaning, tightening, inspection for oil leaks, and basic condition checks (temperature, vibration feel, abnormal sound). This **empowers operators** to detect early signs of wear/corrosion on the cast-iron pump bodies and report them before failure. **Result:** 70% of daily anomalies are caught at operator level, freeing maintenance engineers to focus on technical root-cause analysis.

**Detailed Explanation — Pillar 2: Planned Maintenance (2.5 marks)**

A **time-based and condition-based** combined schedule is derived from MTBF data and CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management System) records. For example, CNC turning centres have:
- **Quarterly:** Coolant pH check, chip-pan cleaning.
- **Half-yearly:** Slide-way lubrication, spindle vibration spectrum analysis.
- **Annual:** Way-cover replacement, ballscrew backlash check.
The planner combines **PM (calendar)** with **PdM (vibration, oil analysis)** to minimize both over-maintenance and breakdown risk.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting CRF**: Students often compare two alternatives of unequal lives by simple LCC division, which is **wrong**. Always convert to **EUAC** using CRF. Examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for skipping this step.
> 2. **MTBF vs MTTR mix-up**: Many students compute MTTR = Total Repair Time × Number of Failures (multiplication instead of division). This single error can cost **4 marks** in a 7-mark sub-part.
> 3. **Reliability formula misuse**: Using $R(t) = 1 - \lambda t$ instead of $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. The first is a linear approximation valid only when $\lambda t \ll 1$. KTU expects the **exponential** form.
> 4. **OEE = 100% confusion**: Writing $OEE = A + P + Q$ instead of $OEE = A \times P \times Q$. The metric is **multiplicative**, not additive. Lost 1 mark minimum.
> 5. **Neglecting salvage value**: In EUAC calculations, **always subtract salvage** from acquisition before applying CRF: $EUAC = (C_{acq} - S) \cdot CRF + C_m$.
> 6. **Ignoring TPM pillars**: Naming the pillars without **applying them to a real plant scenario** loses 2 marks. Always ground your answer in a manufacturing context.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- [x] **Terotechnology = "Technology of Care"** — integrated life-cycle asset management per BSI BS 3811.
- [x] **Five phases:** Design → Acquisition → Operation → Maintenance → Replacement/Disposal.
- [x] **Master Equation 1:** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ — know the cyclic-uptime derivation.
- [x] **Master Equation 2:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ with $MTBF = 1/\lambda$.
- [x] **Master Equation 3:** $EUAC = (C_{acq} - S) \cdot CRF + C_m$ with $CRF = i(1+i)^N / [(1+i)^N - 1]$.
- [x] **OEE = A × P × Q** (multiplicative, not additive; target ≥ 85% in world-class plants).
- [x] **Maintenance hierarchy (4 levels):** CM → PM → PdM → Proactive.
- [x] **Bathtub curve** has 3 stages — infant mortality, useful life, wear-out — each with its own terotechnology countermeasure.
- [x] **TPM** = 8 pillars, founded by Seiichi Nakajima (1971, Japan).
- [x] **Corrosion decisions** must be evaluated on **LCC, not initial cost** — case of mild steel vs stainless steel pipe giving ₹ 99,060/year savings.
- [x] **Optimal economic life $N^*$** is the integer $N$ that minimizes $EUAC(N)$.
- [x] **Always use CRF when comparing alternatives of unequal life.**
- [x] **Discount rate $i$** appears in every LCC calculation — read the question carefully for its value.
- [x] **Reliability is dimensionless**, but **MTBF and MTTR are in hours** — always state units in your final answer.
- [x] **Half-cell potential ≤ −350 mV (CSE)** is a standard corrosion activity threshold in reinforced concrete terotechnology.
- [x] **In a 14-mark answer**, expect 7 marks for numerical + 7 marks for descriptive; never skip either part.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Treatments: Surface Coating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Surface Treatments: Surface Coating # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Surface Coating** is a surface engineering process in which a distinct layer of material — metallic, non-metallic inorganic, or organic (polymeric) — is deliberately deposited, formed, or applied onto the surface of a base substrate (parent material) to engineer one or more of the following surface-dependent properties: 1. **Corrosion resistance** (barrier protection, sacrificial/galvanic protection, or inhibition). 2. **Wear resistance** (hardness, abrasion, erosion). 3. **Aesthetic appearance** (colour, gloss, texture). 4. **Thermal insulation / thermal stability.** 5. **Electrical conductivity or insulation.** 6. **Wettability, solderability, or bio-compatibility.** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Emphasis:** A *coating* is fundamentally different from a *surface modification* technique. In coating, a **foreign, chemically distinct layer** is added on top of the substrate surface. In surface modification (carburizing, nitriding, induction hardening, shot peening), the **substrate itself** is altered in chemistry, phase, or stress state — no new material is added. This distinction is a guaranteed short-answer question in KTU exams. > [!NOTE] > **Standardized Industry Metric:** Coating quality is universally measured by **coating thickness** (in micrometres, $\mu m$, or mils) and **adhesion strength** (in MPa, tested by pull-off or tape tests as per **ASTM B571** and **ISO 2819**). A typical decorative Ni-Cr automotive coating is **20–40 $\mu m$** thick, while heavy-duty offshore zinc coatings can be **85–200 $\mu m$**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building's Raincoat" Imagine your skin on a rainy day. Your body is the **substrate (base metal)**, and the rain is the **corrosive environment (oxygen, water, chlorides)**. - You have two options to stay dry: - **Option A:** Alter your skin itself (e.g., develop thicker, waterproof skin). This is *surface modification* (carburizing, nitriding). - **Option B:** Wear a separate, removable raincoat over your skin. This is *surface coating* (painting, plating, galvanizing). A good raincoat: 1. **Stops water from reaching the skin** (barrier protection). 2. If it gets a tear, it must "sacrifice" itself slowly so the skin underneath survives (this is how a **zinc coating** protects scratched steel — the zinc corrodes *instead* of the iron). 3. **Sticks well to the skin** (adhesion); a raincoat flapping in the wind is useless. This is the entire philosophy of surface coating in one analogy. ## 1.3 KTU Classification of Surface Coatings (Board-Exam Pillar) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Three Master Families of Coatings** (memorize this hierarchy — it is a guaranteed 3-mark question): | Family | Examples | Protection Mechanism | |---|---|---| | **A. Organic Coatings** | Paints, lacquers, varnishes, epoxies, polyurethanes, powder coatings | Pure **barrier** (and sometimes inhibitive pigment action) | | **B. Metallic (Inorganic) Coatings** | Zinc (galvanizing), tin, chromium, nickel, copper, aluminium, gold | Barrier + (sometimes) **sacrificial/galvanic** action | | **C. Inorganic Non-Metallic Coatings** | Enamels, glass-ceramics, anodized alumina, chromate conversion, phosphate conversion, TiN, TiC (CVD/PVD) | Barrier + **chemical conversion of surface** | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Organic Coatings (Paints) — Composition & Cure Mechanism A modern paint is **not just coloured liquid**. It is a precisely engineered 4-component system: 1. **Binder (Resin):** The film-forming polymer. E.g., alkyd, epoxy, polyurethane, acrylic, phenolic. Gives mechanical strength, adhesion, chemical resistance. 2. **Pigment:** Provides colour, UV protection, and **active corrosion inhibition** (e.g., red lead, zinc chromate, zinc phosphate, micaceous iron oxide). 3. **Solvent / Carrier:** Thins the resin for application; evaporates during cure. 4. **Additives:** < 5 % — wetting agents, anti-settling, anti-foam, driers, biocides. **Mechanism of Protection:** - **Barrier effect:** Pigment flakes (especially micaceous iron oxide, MIO) are arranged in overlapping, parallel "shingles" — the corrosive electrolyte must follow a long, tortuous path to reach the substrate. This is called the **tortuosity effect**. - **Inhibitive effect:** Pigments like zinc chromate release inhibitive anions (e.g., $\text{CrO}_4^{2-}$) that passivate the underlying steel. - **Sacrificial effect:** Pigments like zinc dust (> 95 % in primer) make the coating electrically behave like a galvanic anode. **Failure Modes (must know for KTU):** Blistering, under-film corrosion, cathodic delamination, chalking, cracking, and pinholing. ## 2.2 Metallic Coatings — Detailed Taxonomy ### 2.2.1 Hot-Dip Coating (Galvanizing, Tinning, Aluminizing) - The substrate is **dipped into a molten bath** of the coating metal. - **Galvanizing:** Steel dipped in molten zinc at **~450 °C**. - A series of **intermetallic compounds** form at the interface (e.g., in galvanizing: $\Gamma$, $\delta$, $\zeta$ Fe-Zn phases, then pure $\eta$-Zn outer layer). This is what gives galvanizing its legendary **adhesion by metallurgical bonding**, not just mechanical keying. - Coating thickness: **45–85 $\mu m$** (batch) up to **100–200 $\mu m$** (continuous sheet). ### 2.2.2 Electroplating (Electrodeposition) - An **aqueous electrolyte** contains ions of the coating metal. - The substrate is made the **cathode**, an inert anode (or soluble anode of the same metal) is used. - A DC current drives the reduction reaction: $\text{M}^{n+} + ne^- \rightarrow \text{M}$. - Examples: decorative **Cu-Ni-Cr** on automobiles, hard **Cr** on hydraulic shafts, **Ag/Au** on electronics. - Controlled by **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis** (see Section 3). ### 2.2.3 Electroless Plating (Autocatalytic Deposition) - **No external power source.** The reducing agent is in the solution. - Classic example: **Electroless Nickel (EN)** using sodium hypophosphite ($\text{NaH}_2\text{PO}_2$). - The P from the reducer becomes incorporated into the deposit, giving **Ni-P alloy** (typically 3–11 % P). - High-P EN (> 10 % P) is **amorphous, non-magnetic, and highly corrosion resistant.** - Advantages over electroplating: **uniform thickness on complex shapes, no edge build-up, no "dog-boning".** ### 2.2.4 Conversion Coatings (Anodizing, Chromating, Phosphating) - The **substrate itself is chemically converted** into a protective compound layer. - **Anodizing (Aluminium):** Al is the anode in $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ electrolyte. Reaction: $2\text{Al} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 6\text{H}^+ + 6e^-$. The porous $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ is then "sealed" in hot water. - **Phosphating (Steel):** Reaction with $\text{Zn(H}_2\text{PO}_4)_2$ produces crystalline **hopeite** ($\text{Zn}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 \cdot 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$). The crystalline phosphate is an excellent paint base. ### 2.2.5 Vapour-Phase Coatings (CVD & PVD) - **PVD (Physical Vapour Deposition):** Sputtering, evaporation, ion plating. Lower temperature (~200–500 °C). Used for **TiN, CrN, DLC** decorative/coated cutting tools. - **CVD (Chemical Vapour Deposition):** Gaseous precursors react on a hot substrate (~800–1000 °C). Used for **TiC, TiN, polycrystalline diamond.** ### 2.2.6 Thermal Spraying - Molten or semi-molten metal/ceramic particles are sprayed at high velocity onto the surface. - **Flame spray, Arc spray, Plasma spray, HVOF (High Velocity Oxy-Fuel).** - Coating is **lamellar, porous (1–10 %), with mechanical interlocking (not metallurgical) bonding.** - Used for **large structures** — boiler tubes, bridge girders, marine propellers. ## 2.3 Cathodic vs Anodic Coatings — The Critical Distinction > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **single most important conceptual question** in Module 4. A KTU question literally asks: "With a neat sketch explain the protection offered by a tin coating on steel if (i) coating is intact, (ii) coating is scratched." | Parameter | **Anodic (Sacrificial) Coating** | **Cathodic (Noble) Coating** | |---|---|---| | Coating metal | More active than substrate | More noble than substrate | | Example | **Zn on Fe** | **Sn, Cu, Ni, Cr, Ag, Au on Fe** | | If scratched... | Coating corrodes, **substrate PROTECTED** | Substrate corrodes **rapidly (galvanic couple)** | | Why used? | Corrosion protection in harsh atmospheres | Decorative, wear, food (Sn for tin cans!) | | Failure mode | Slow, even loss of coating | **Severe localized pitting** of base metal | > [!WARNING] > **The Tin-Can Paradox:** Tin is *cathodic* to steel. So why doesn't a scratched tin can rust catastrophically? Because inside a sealed tin can there is **no electrolyte (no oxygen, no water)** — the galvanic couple cannot operate. This is a **classic KTU "trick" question**. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **Critical Markdown Rule:** Pipes $\vert$ inside formulas are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking table syntax. All current-efficiency, thickness, and deposition-rate equations are examinable. | # | Formula / Law | Equation | Symbols & Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Faraday's 1st Law** | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | $m$ = mass deposited (g), $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M$ = molar mass (g/mol), $n$ = electrons per ion, $F = \mathbf{96500\ C\ mol^{-1}}$ | Mass of metal deposited in electroplating | | 2 | **Faraday's 2nd Law** | $\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2}$ | $E$ = equivalent weight = $M/n$ | Mass ratios of two metals from same charge | | 3 | **Current Efficiency (Coulombic)** | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\,\%$ | $\eta$ in % | Real plating efficiency is **60–95 %** in industry | | 4 | **Coating Thickness (theoretical)** | $t = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A}$ | $t$ = thickness (cm), $m$ = mass (g), $\rho$ = density (g/cm³), $A$ = area (cm²) | Convert deposited mass to thickness | | 5 | **Combined Plating Equation** | $t = \dfrac{I \cdot T \cdot \eta \cdot M}{\rho \cdot A \cdot n \cdot F}$ | $T$ = plating time, all SI | **Most used KTU board formula** | | 6 | **Tafel Corrosion Rate** | $CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{\text{corr}} \cdot E}{\rho}$ | $i_{\text{corr}}$ in $\mu A/cm^2$, $E$ in g/eq, $\rho$ in g/cm³, $CR$ in **mm/year** | Predicting coating life | | 7 | **Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** | $\text{PBR} = \dfrac{V_{\text{oxide}}}{V_{\text{metal}}}$ | $V$ = molar volume | Predicts if oxide scale is **protective** | | 8 | **Anodizing Mass Gain** | $m = k \cdot I \cdot t$ | $k$ = electrochemical equivalent | Quality control in anodizing | | 9 | **Spraying Efficiency** | $\eta_s = \dfrac{W_{\text{deposit}}}{W_{\text{fed}}} \times 100\,\%$ | Weight basis | Thermal spray QC | | 10 | **Adhesion Pull-Off Stress** | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | $F$ in N, $A$ in m², $\sigma_a$ in MPa | Coating-substrate bond strength | --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Mandate:** The following derivations are **guaranteed to appear** in Part B of the ESE (14-mark question). Each step is shown explicitly. No algebraic jumps are permitted in valuation. ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Combined Plating Thickness Equation (from Faraday's Laws) This is the most derived equation in Module 4. We start from **Faraday's 1st Law**: $$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ **Step 1 — State the definitions clearly.** - $m$ is the theoretical mass (g) of metal deposited. - $I \cdot t = Q$ is the total electric charge passed (Coulombs). - $M / n$ is the equivalent weight. - $F = 96500\ C/eq$ is Faraday's constant. **Step 2 — Account for real-world losses via current efficiency $\eta$.** In practice, side reactions (hydrogen evolution, oxygen reduction) consume charge. The *actual* mass deposited is: $$m_{\text{actual}} = \eta \cdot m = \frac{\eta \cdot I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ **Step 3 — Convert mass to volume using density.** Since $\rho = m / V$, we have $V = m / \rho$. In coating, $V = A \cdot t_{\text{thickness}}$, so: $$A \cdot t = \frac{m_{\text{actual}}}{\rho}$$ **Step 4 — Solve explicitly for thickness $t$.** $$t = \frac{m_{\text{actual}}}{\rho \cdot A} = \frac{\eta \cdot I \cdot T \cdot M}{\rho \cdot A \cdot n \cdot F}$$ **Step 5 — State the units in SI for clarity.** $$t \ [\mu m] = \frac{\eta \cdot I \ [A] \cdot T \ [s] \cdot M \ [g/mol]}{\rho \ [g/cm^3] \cdot A \ [cm^2] \cdot n \cdot 96500} \times 10^4$$ The factor $10^4$ converts cm to $\mu m$. **This is the KTU "ready-to-use" equation.** > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** This equation is the basis of every **automatic plating rectifier** in a hard-chrome shop. The operator dials in the desired thickness (say 50 $\mu m$), the controller computes the required ampere-hours using this exact relation, and switches off current automatically. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worked Numerical Problem (14-Mark Pattern) > **PROBLEM [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]:** > A steel sheet of total surface area **$1200\ cm^2$** is to be electroplated with nickel. The plating is carried out for **45 minutes** at a current of **8 A**. The cathode current efficiency is **90 %**. Calculate: > (a) The mass of nickel deposited. > (b) The average thickness of the nickel coating. > (c) Verify the result using equivalent-weight form. > Given: $M_{\text{Ni}} = 58.7\ g/mol$, $n = 2$, $\rho_{\text{Ni}} = 8.9\ g/cm^3$, $F = 96500\ C/eq$. **Part (a) — Mass deposited:** Step 1. Theoretical mass from Faraday's 1st Law: $$m_{\text{th}} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{8 \times (45 \times 60) \times 58.7}{2 \times 96500}$$ Step 2. Calculate the numerator: $$8 \times 2700 \times 58.7 = 1267920$$ Step 3. Calculate the denominator: $$2 \times 96500 = 193000$$ Step 4. Divide: $$m_{\text{th}} = \frac{1267920}{193000} = 6.569\ g$$ Step 5. Apply 90 % efficiency: $$m_{\text{actual}} = 0.90 \times 6.569 = 5.912\ g$$ $$\boxed{m_{\text{actual}} \approx 5.91\ g}$$ **Part (b) — Coating thickness:** Step 1. Volume of deposit: $$V = \frac{m_{\text{actual}}}{\rho} = \frac{5.912}{8.9} = 0.6643\ cm^3$$ Step 2. Thickness: $$t = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{0.6643}{1200} = 5.536 \times 10^{-4}\ cm$$ Step 3. Convert to micrometres: $$\boxed{t \approx 5.54\ \mu m}$$ **Part (c) — Verification via equivalent weight:** Step 1. Equivalent weight of Ni: $$E_{\text{Ni}} = \frac{M}{n} = \frac{58.7}{2} = 29.35\ g/eq$$ Step 2. Charge passed: $$Q = I \cdot t = 8 \times 2700 = 21600\ C$$ Step 3. Theoretical mass via Eq. form: $$m_{\text{th}} = \frac{E \cdot Q}{F} = \frac{29.35 \times 21600}{96500} = 6.569\ g \ \checkmark$$ The result matches Part (a) — derivation is consistent. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR) and its Engineering Meaning The PBR predicts whether an oxide scale formed on a metal is **protective or non-protective**: $$\text{PBR} = \frac{V_{\text{oxide}}}{V_{\text{metal}}} = \frac{M_{\text{oxide}} \cdot \rho_{\text{metal}}}{n \cdot M_{\text{metal}} \cdot \rho_{\text{oxide}}}$$ **Step 1 — Logic.** If PBR < 1, the oxide occupies less volume than the metal consumed → tensile stress, scale cracks → **non-protective** (e.g., Mg, alkali metals). **Step 2.** If 1 < PBR < 2, the oxide is in compression → **dense, adherent, protective** (e.g., Al, Cr, Ti, stainless steels). **Step 3.** If PBR >> 2, the oxide is too bulky → spalling, buckling → **non-protective** (e.g., Fe on steel at high T, where PBR ≈ 2.15, just above the safe range). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Example:** Calculate the PBR for $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ on Al. > $M_{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3} = 102\ g/mol$, $\rho_{\text{oxide}} = 3.99\ g/cm^3$, $M_{\text{Al}} = 27\ g/mol$, $\rho_{\text{metal}} = 2.70\ g/cm^3$, $n = 2$ (since 2 Al atoms yield 1 oxide). $$\text{PBR} = \frac{102 \times 2.70}{2 \times 27 \times 3.99} = \frac{275.4}{215.46} \approx 1.28$$ $$\boxed{\text{PBR}_{\text{Al}} \approx 1.28 \in (1,2) \ \Rightarrow \ \text{Protective}}$$ This is why aluminium is naturally corrosion resistant — its own oxide is the **in-situ self-healing coating**. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Coating Process | Selection Criterion | Hot-Dip | Electroplating | Electroless | PVD | Thermal Spray | Paint | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Cost** | Low | Med | Med-High | Very High | Medium | Lowest | | **Coating thickness control** | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent (thin) | Good | Fair | | **Uniformity on complex shapes** | Good | Poor (edge build-up) | **Excellent** | Fair | Fair | Good | | **Substrate heating** | Yes (450 °C) | None | ~90 °C | 200–500 °C | None to mild | None | | **Bond type** | Metallurgical | Metallurgical | Metallurgical | Mechanical/atomic | Mechanical | Adhesion/chemical | | **Typical thickness ($\mu m$)** | 45–200 | 5–50 | 5–50 | 1–10 | 50–500+ | 25–250 | | **Best application** | Structural steel | Decorative/electronics | Precision engineering | Cutting tools | Reclamation | Atmospheric | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > **Note on Diagram Engine:** Mermaid block diagrams are used here to map the functional architecture and decision logic of surface-coating processes, since physical process drawings of molten baths and spray plumes cannot be rendered in text. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > *Distinguish between anodic and cathodic coatings with a suitable example each. What happens when a tin coating on steel is scratched?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Anodic Coating | Cathodic Coating | |---|---|---| | Definition | Coating metal is **more electrochemically active** (more negative $E^\circ$) than the base metal | Coating metal is **more noble** (more positive $E^\circ$) than the base metal | | Example | **Zinc on steel** (galvanizing) | **Tin on steel** (tin-plated cans) | | Behaviour at scratch | **Coating corrodes preferentially** (sacrificial) | **Base metal corrodes** as small anode coupled to large cathode | | Effect on base metal | **Protected** | **Severe pitting** | **For the scratched tin-on-steel case:** Since tin is cathodic to iron, at the scratch the exposed steel becomes a tiny anode coupled to the large tin cathode. This produces **severe localized galvanic corrosion** of the steel. However, in a sealed tin can this is prevented by the absence of an electrolyte. **(1 mark for the specific tin-steel explanation = full 3 marks).** --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** > *What is the role of (i) binder, (ii) pigment, and (iii) solvent in an organic coating?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(i) Binder (Resin):** The film-forming polymer that provides **mechanical strength, adhesion to the substrate, and chemical/weather resistance** (1 mark). - **(ii) Pigment:** Insoluble particles that give **colour, opacity, UV protection, and active corrosion inhibition** (e.g., $\text{ZnCrO}_4$ acts as an inhibitive pigment) (1 mark). - **(iii) Solvent:** Volatile carrier that **reduces viscosity for application** (by brush, spray, or dip) and **evaporates during curing/drying** (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing "anodic" and "cathodic" coatings** is the #1 reason students lose 3–4 marks. Always state the relative position in the **galvanic series**, not just the word. > 2. **Forgetting the current efficiency $\eta$** in Faraday's-law problems — it almost always appears in the question stem; missing it is an automatic 1-mark cut. > 3. **Wrong unit conversions in plating thickness** (cm vs. $\mu m$ vs. mm) — the $10^4$ factor trips up 60 % of students. > 4. **Painting-style "list" answers without sketches** — the KTU 2024 scheme explicitly requires diagrams for full marks on these topics. --- ### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** > **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** > (a) Explain the mechanism of corrosion protection by **organic coatings (paints)**. Discuss the role of each constituent with special reference to the **tortuosity effect** of micaceous iron oxide. (7 marks) > (b) A steel component of surface area **$800\ cm^2$** is to be **electroplated with copper** from a $\text{CuSO}_4$ bath. Calculate the **time required** to deposit a **$30\ \mu m$** thick uniform coating, using a current of **6 A** and assuming **95 % cathode current efficiency**. Given: $M_{\text{Cu}} = 63.5\ g/mol$, $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 8.96\ g/cm^3$, $n = 2$, $F = 96500\ C/eq$. (7 marks) #### **Model Solution for Q.A:** **(a) Organic coating mechanism (7 marks):** A paint is a **four-component system**: binder, pigment, solvent, additives. **1. Barrier Protection (3 marks):** The cured paint film acts as a physical barrier that separates the metal substrate from the corrosive electrolyte ($\text{O}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{Cl}^-$). For the electrolyte to reach the substrate it must permeate the film — this is a **diffusion-controlled process**. Pure binder films (e.g., epoxy) have low permeability, but the addition of plate-like pigment particles — especially **micaceous iron oxide (MIO, $\alpha$-Fe₂O₃ in flake form)** — increases the diffusion path length dramatically. The flakes orient parallel to the substrate in overlapping "shingles" (like roof tiles), forcing the corrosive species to take a long, winding, **tortuous path** around each flake. This **tortuosity effect** reduces the effective diffusion coefficient $D_{\text{eff}}$ by the **Tortuosity Factor $\tau$**: $$D_{\text{eff}} = \frac{D_0}{\tau^2}$$ where $\tau$ can be 5–15 for well-formulated MIO primers. The **time-to-failure** (e.g., to first blister) increases as $\tau^2$. **2. Inhibitive Pigment Action (2 marks):** Certain pigments — notably **zinc chromate, zinc phosphate, and red lead** — are sparingly soluble. They release inhibitive anions (e.g., $\text{CrO}_4^{2-}$, $\text{PO}_4^{3-}$) that **passivate** the underlying steel by forming a thin, stable oxide/hydroxide film, effectively converting the steel to a nobler, passive state. **3. Sacrificial Pigment Action (2 marks):** When the pigment content is very high (e.g., **zinc-rich primers** with > 90 % Zn dust by weight in the dry film), the pigment particles are in **electrical contact** with each other and with the steel substrate. Even if the film is mechanically damaged, the zinc pigments act as a **distributed sacrificial anode**, providing cathodic protection — the same principle as hot-dip galvanizing, but in paint form. --- **(b) Time calculation (7 marks):** We are given: - $A = 800\ cm^2$ - Required thickness $t = 30\ \mu m = 30 \times 10^{-4}\ cm = 0.003\ cm$ - $I = 6\ A$, $\eta = 0.95$ - $M_{\text{Cu}} = 63.5\ g/mol$, $\rho = 8.96\ g/cm^3$, $n = 2$, $F = 96500\ C/eq$ **Step 1 — Calculate the volume of copper required:** $$V = A \cdot t = 800 \times 0.003 = 2.4\ cm^3$$ **Step 2 — Calculate the mass required:** $$m = V \cdot \rho = 2.4 \times 8.96 = 21.504\ g$$ **Step 3 — Use Faraday's Law with efficiency to find time:** Rearranging $m = \dfrac{\eta \cdot I \cdot T \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ for $T$: $$T = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{\eta \cdot I \cdot M}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute values:** $$T = \frac{21.504 \times 2 \times 96500}{0.95 \times 6 \times 63.5}$$ **Step 5 — Compute numerator:** $$21.504 \times 2 \times 96500 = 4150272$$ **Step 6 — Compute denominator:** $$0.95 \times 6 \times 63.5 = 361.95$$ **Step 7 — Divide:** $$T = \frac{4150272}{361.95} \approx 11466\ s$$ **Step 8 — Convert to hours:** $$\boxed{T \approx 11466\ s \approx 3.18\ hours \approx 3\ hours\ 11\ minutes}$$ --- ### **Part B — Question B (Alternative, 14 Marks)** > **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** > (a) With a neat sketch, describe the **hot-dip galvanizing process** for protecting steel. Explain the **Fe-Zn intermetallic phases** formed and state the **advantages and limitations** of galvanizing. (7 marks) > (b) Explain the **anodizing of aluminium**. Discuss the role of the **barrier layer and porous layer**, and describe the **sealing process**. Compare anodizing with chromate conversion coating. (7 marks) #### **Model Solution for Q.B:** **(a) Hot-dip galvanizing (7 marks):** **Process Description (3 marks):** Hot-dip galvanizing is the process of immersing cleaned steel (or iron) articles into a bath of **molten zinc** held at **~440–460 °C** (just above zinc's melting point of 419.5 °C). The standard sequence is: 1. **Surface Preparation:** Degreasing (alkaline cleaner) → Pickling (dilute $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ or HCl) → Rinsing → Fluxing (typically $\text{ZnCl}_2 + \text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$ to prevent oxidation). 2. **Immersion:** Dipped in molten Zn for 3–10 minutes depending on thickness. 3. **Withdrawal & Cooling:** Slowly withdrawn, excess Zn drips off, then air- or water-cooled. **Intermetallic Phases (2 marks):** During immersion, **Zn atoms diffuse into Fe** and Fe diffuses into Zn, forming four distinct Fe-Zn intermetallic layers (in the order: substrate → surface): | Phase | Formula | % Fe (approx.) | Property | |---|---|---|---| | $\Gamma$ (Gamma) | $\text{Fe}_3\text{Zn}_{10}$ | 21–28 | Brittle, thin | | $\delta$ (Delta) | $\text{FeZn}_{10}$ | 7–11 | Ductile, dense | | $\zeta$ (Zeta) | $\text{FeZn}_{13}$ | 5–6 | Hard, brittle | | $\eta$ (Eta) | Pure Zn | < 0.03 | Soft, ductile, outer layer | This **metallurgical bonding** is what makes galvanizing far superior to paint in adhesion. **Advantages and Limitations (2 marks):** - **Advantages:** Long service life (50+ years in rural atmosphere), self-healing at scratches, low cost per $\mu m$-year, no maintenance, metallurgical bond. - **Limitations:** Not suitable for parts above **~500 °C service** (Zn melts at 419 °C), poor appearance (spangled grey), cannot be used for parts requiring tight dimensional tolerances, white rust if stored in wet conditions. --- **(b) Anodizing of aluminium (7 marks):** **Process and Cell Setup (3 marks):** Anodizing is an **electrolytic conversion process** that grows a controlled $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ layer from the aluminium substrate itself — the part is the **anode**, and a lead or stainless steel cathode is used in a **dilute $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ (15 %)** electrolyte (Type II) or chromic acid (Type I) at 18–22 °C with DC voltage of 15–20 V. **Electrochemistry:** $$2\text{Al} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 6\text{H}^+ + 6e^- \quad \text{(at anode)}$$ **Two-Layer Structure (2 marks):** 1. **Barrier Layer (Inner):** A thin (~10–20 nm), non-porous, amorphous $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ layer directly on the substrate. Dielectric, formed at the start. 2. **Porous Layer (Outer):** The barrier layer breaks down under the electric field, and oxide grows as a regular array of hexagonal cells (~25–500 nm diameter) with central pores. The pores can be **dyed** for colour (architectural black, gold, blue). **Sealing (1 mark):** After dyeing, the part is immersed in **boiling deionized water** (or nickel acetate solution) for 15–30 min. This **hydrates the porous oxide** to form **boehmite** ($\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}$), which **swells and closes the pores**, sealing the coating. Sealing is **critical** — unsealed anodized parts will "bleed" dye and have poor corrosion resistance. **Comparison with Chromate Conversion (1 mark):** | Feature | Anodizing | Chromate Conversion | |---|---|---| | Coating type | Thick oxide (5–25 $\mu m$) | Thin (0.5–2 $\mu m$) | | Process | Electrolytic | Chemical dip | | Colour | Clear / dyed | Yellow / clear | | Self-healing | No | **Yes (Cr⁶⁺ re-passivates scratches)** | | Toxicity | Low | High (Cr⁶⁺ carcinogenic — being phased out by **REACH**) | | Application | Architectural, aerospace | Aerospace primer base | --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final 60-Second Revision Block — Read this on the morning of the exam:** - **Definition lock-in:** Coating = adding a *new, foreign material* on top. Surface modification = altering the substrate *in situ*. - **Three families:** Organic (paint), Metallic (Zn, Cr, Ni), Inorganic non-metallic (anodized $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$, enamel, TiN). - **Anodic coatings** (Zn, Al on Fe) are **sacrificial** — protect even when scratched. **Cathodic coatings** (Sn, Cu, Ni, Cr, Ag, Au on Fe) are noble — *accelerate* corrosion of the base metal if scratched. **Tin cans survive only because of zero electrolyte inside.** - **Faraday's 1st Law:** $m = I t M / n F$. Always multiply by $\eta$ (current efficiency) in practice. - **The 5-step thickness derivation** (mass → volume → thickness with the $10^4$ factor for $\mu m$) must be memorized. - **Galvanizing** = metallurgical bond via **Fe-Zn intermetallics** ($\Gamma, \delta, \zeta, \eta$ phases). - **Anodizing of Al** = two layers (barrier + porous); sealing in boiling water forms boehmite. - **Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR):** 1 < PBR < 2 ⇒ protective oxide. PBR < 1 or > 2 ⇒ non-protective. - **Tortuosity effect of MIO pigments** multiplies diffusion path; $\tau$ factor = 5–15 typical. - **CVD needs high T (~1000 °C); PVD is low T (~500 °C) but gives thin, very hard coatings** (TiN for drills). - **Pipes in tables:** when writing the PBR or absolute value in an exam answer, write **$V_{\text{oxide}}$** and **$V_{\text{metal}}$** explicitly, *not* $|x|$ symbols that confuse the table renderer. - **Key constants to memorize:** $F = 96500\ C/eq$, $\rho_{\text{Zn}} = 7.14\ g/cm^3$, $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 8.96\ g/cm^3$, $\rho_{\text{Ni}} = 8.9\ g/cm^3$, $\rho_{\text{Al}} = 2.70\ g/cm^3$, $\rho_{\text{Cr}} = 7.19\ g/cm^3$. - **ASTM/ISO standards to mention** (impresses the examiner!): ASTM B571 (adhesion), ASTM B499 (thickness), ISO 2819 (QC), ASTM B117 (salt spray), REACH (Cr⁶⁺ ban). - **Always draw a diagram** in 14-mark answers — the KTU 2024 scheme has explicit rubric rows for "neat sketch with labels." > **End of Notes — Module 4 / Topic: Surface Treatments: Surface Coating**

topics.title: Plating and Anodizing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plating and Anodizing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Plating and Anodizing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Electroplating — Formal KTU Definition

**Electroplating** is an electrochemical surface engineering process in which a thin, adherent, and uniform metallic coating is deposited onto a conductive substrate (cathode) from an electrolytic solution containing ions of the coating metal, driven by an externally applied direct current (DC) source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Electroplating is governed by **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis** and is treated as a *cathodic process* — the substrate being plated is always connected to the **negative terminal** of the power supply and acts as the **cathode**, where **reduction** of metal ions occurs.

### 1.2 Anodizing — Formal KTU Definition

**Anodizing** is an electrochemical conversion-coating process in which the metallic surface (typically aluminum, titanium, magnesium, or tantalum) is converted into a hard, protective, and often porous oxide layer by making it the **anode** of an electrolytic cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Distinction:** In *electroplating* we **add** a foreign metal onto a surface; in *anodizing* we **convert** the surface metal itself into its own oxide. Hence anodizing is a *conversion coating*, whereas plating is an *additive coating*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — Electroplating:**
> Imagine a swimming pool (the electrolyte bath) filled with metallic "skeleton keys" floating around (the dissolved metal ions). You immerse an iron key (the cathode/substrate) and connect a battery. The electricity acts like a magnet that pulls these metallic keys out of the water and firmly locks them onto the surface of your iron key, layer upon layer, until you have a shiny, protective coating.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — Anodizing:**
> Now imagine instead that you take an aluminum plate and connect it as the **positive** electrode. The electric field forces oxygen to migrate to the plate and chemically react with the aluminum, growing a tough "skin" of aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃) directly out of the parent metal — like rust, but engineered and tightly controlled.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Faraday's Constant (F)** = **96,485 C mol⁻¹ ≈ 96,500 C mol⁻¹** — the charge carried by one mole of electrons.
- **Standard units:** Current (A), Time (s), Mass (kg or g), Current Density (A/m² or A/dm²).
- **Anodizing types (MIL-A-8625):** Type I — Chromic Acid; Type II — Sulfuric Acid; Type III — Hard Anodize.
- **Typical coating thickness range:** Electroplating — **5 µm to 50 µm**; Anodizing — **5 µm to 150 µm** (hard anodizing up to 250 µm).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electroplating — Step-by-Step Operating Logic

The electroplating operation can be broken down into **six sequential sub-processes**:

1. **Surface Preparation (Pre-treatment)**
   - Degreasing (alkaline or solvent cleaning) to remove oils/greases.
   - Pickling (acid dipping) to remove oxides and mill scale.
   - Rinsing with deionized water.

2. **Cell Assembly**
   - **Anode** = pure coating metal (soluble anode) or inert electrode (insoluble).
   - **Cathode** = substrate to be plated.
   - **Electrolyte** = aqueous solution of a salt of the coating metal (e.g., CuSO₄ for copper, NiSO₄ for nickel).

3. **Application of DC Power**
   - Current density typically **10–50 A/dm²** depending on bath chemistry.
   - Voltage range: **2 V – 12 V**.

4. **Faradaic Reactions**
   - At cathode (reduction): **Mⁿ⁺ + n e⁻ → M (deposited metal)**
   - At anode (oxidation, soluble): **M → Mⁿ⁺ + n e⁻** (replenishes ions)

5. **Mass Transport & Crystal Nucleation**
   - Metal ions migrate to the cathode via diffusion, convection, and migration.
   - Nucleation-and-growth produces a coherent crystalline layer.

6. **Post-treatment**
   - Rinsing, drying, and sometimes passivation or chromate conversion coating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Faraday's Law so central?** Because electroplating is the most direct industrial application of electrolysis — mass deposited is **proportional to charge passed**, allowing precise thickness control.

## 2.2 Anodizing — Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Pre-cleaning** — identical to plating pre-treatment.
2. **Etching / Desmutting** — removes the natural oxide and surface impurities.
3. **Electrolytic Oxidation**
   - Substrate connected as **anode (+)**.
   - Cathode usually lead or stainless steel.
   - Electrolyte choice defines the anodizing type.
4. **Pore Formation (for Al)** — hexagonal cells form, each containing a central pore of ~10–25 nm diameter.
5. **Coloring (optional)** — dyes or electrolytic coloring fills the pores.
6. **Sealing** — hydrate the oxide to close pores and lock in corrosion resistance.

### 2.3 The Mechanism of Anodizing of Aluminum (H₂SO₄ Process)

Two simultaneous reactions occur at the anode:

- **Oxide formation (forward):** 2 Al + 3 H₂O → Al₂O₃ + 6 H⁺ + 6 e⁻
- **Oxide dissolution (reverse):** Al₂O₃ + 3 H₂SO₄ → Al₂(SO₄)₃ + 3 H₂O

> [!NOTE]
> The **dynamic equilibrium** between these two reactions is what creates the porous, honeycomb-like structure — the oxide grows inward into the metal while simultaneously dissolving at the surface.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Law | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Faraday's First Law** | $m = Z \cdot I \cdot t$ | $m$ = mass (g); $I$ = current (A); $t$ = time (s); $Z$ = electrochemical equivalent (g/C) | Predict deposited mass in electroplating |
| 2 | Expanded Faraday's Law | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | $M$ = molar mass (g/mol); $n$ = valency; $F$ = 96,485 C/mol | Compute exact mass deposited |
| 3 | **Faraday's Second Law** | $\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2}$ | $E$ = equivalent weight | Multi-metal deposition analysis |
| 4 | **Coulombic (Current) Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\,\%$ | dimensionless % | Bath efficiency, wastage |
| 5 | **Coating Thickness** | $t_{\text{coat}} = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A}$ | $t_{\text{coat}}$ (cm); $\rho$ = density (g/cm³); $A$ = area (cm²) | Design plating time for desired thickness |
| 6 | **Plating Time Required** | $t = \dfrac{t_{\text{coat}} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M}$ | $t$ in seconds | Production planning |
| 7 | **Anodizing Voltage–Thickness Relation** | $h \approx 1.4 \cdot V \;\; (\mu\text{m})$ (for Al in H₂SO₄, approx.) | $V$ = applied volts; $h$ = oxide thickness | Quick thickness estimate |
| 8 | **Hydrogen Side Reaction (pH rise)** | $2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\uparrow$ | — | Explains H₂ evolution at cathode |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Pitfall:** Always convert **minutes → seconds** and **dm² → cm²** correctly. Most KTU numerical errors occur due to mismatched units in Faraday's Law.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Decorative + hard chromium plating on bumpers, piston rings.
- **Aerospace:** Hard anodizing of aluminum airframe parts; cadmium plating of fasteners.
- **Electronics:** Gold/silver plating of connectors and PCBs for low contact resistance.
- **Marine:** Zinc and zinc-nickel plating for cathodic (sacrificial) protection of steel.
- **Architecture:** Anodized aluminum facades (color-fast, weather-resistant).
- **Medical:** Titanium anodized implants for biocompatibility.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Faraday's First Law from Fundamental Principles

We begin with the stoichiometry of a single reduction event at the cathode:

$$\text{M}^{n+} + n \, e^- \longrightarrow \text{M}$$

By definition, the charge carried by **one mole of electrons** is the Faraday constant:

$$F = N_A \cdot e = 96{,}485 \;\; \text{C/mol}$$

where $N_A$ = **Avogadro's number = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹** and $e$ = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** is the elementary charge.

For **1 mole of metal atoms** of valency $n$ to deposit, exactly $n$ moles of electrons are required:

$$Q_{\text{required}} = n \cdot F \;\; \text{coulombs per mole}$$

Therefore, the **mass deposited per mole** is the molar mass $M$, giving the electrochemical equivalent:

$$Z = \dfrac{M}{n \cdot F} \;\; \text{(g/C)}$$

If a steady current $I$ flows for time $t$, total charge $Q = I \cdot t$. Hence the deposited mass is:

$$m = Z \cdot I \cdot t = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

This is **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis** — the linear proportionality between mass deposited and total charge passed.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Board Exam Style

> **[Problem]** A steel component of surface area **200 cm²** is to be electroplated with nickel to a thickness of **30 µm**. The plating bath uses Ni²⁺ ions. Given: density of nickel $\rho = 8.9$ g/cm³, atomic mass $M = 58.7$ g/mol, valency $n = 2$, current $I = 5$ A, current efficiency $\eta = 90\%$. Find the **plating time** in minutes.

### Step 1 — Compute the volume of nickel to be deposited

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= A \cdot t_{\text{coat}} \\
  &= 200 \;\text{cm}^2 \times \left(30 \times 10^{-4}\right) \;\text{cm} \\
  &= 200 \times 3 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{cm}^3 \\
  &= 0.6 \;\text{cm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the mass required

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{required}} &= \rho \cdot V \\
                    &= 8.9 \;\text{g/cm}^3 \times 0.6 \;\text{cm}^3 \\
                    &= 5.34 \;\text{g}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the theoretical charge required (Faraday's Law)

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{theoretical}} &= \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} \\
\Rightarrow t_{\text{theoretical}} &= \dfrac{m_{\text{required}} \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M} \\
                                  &= \dfrac{5.34 \times 2 \times 96{,}485}{5 \times 58.7} \;\text{seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

Numerator: $5.34 \times 2 = 10.68$; $10.68 \times 96{,}485 = 1{,}030{,}560$
Denominator: $5 \times 58.7 = 293.5$

$$t_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{1{,}030{,}560}{293.5} = 3{,}512 \;\text{s}$$

### Step 4 — Apply current efficiency

Because only 90 % of the current is used productively (rest goes into H₂ evolution), the **actual plating time** must be longer:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{actual}} &= \dfrac{t_{\text{theoretical}}}{\eta} \\
                 &= \dfrac{3{,}512}{0.90} \\
                 &= 3{,}902 \;\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to minutes

$$t_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{3{,}902}{60} \approx 65.03 \;\text{minutes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer: Approximately 65 minutes** are required to plate 200 cm² of steel with 30 µm of nickel under the given conditions.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Plating vs. Anodizing

| Property | Electroplating | Anodizing |
|---|---|---|
| Type of coating | **Additive** (foreign metal) | **Conversion** (oxide of base metal) |
| Substrate polarity | **Cathode (−)** | **Anode (+)** |
| Coating material | Different metal (Ni, Cr, Cu, Au, Zn) | Same base metal oxide (Al₂O₃, TiO₂) |
| Typical thickness | 5 – 50 µm | 5 – 150 µm (Type III up to 250 µm) |
| Governing law | Faraday's Law | Faraday's Law + oxide dissolution kinetics |
| Porosity | Generally dense | Inherently porous (must seal) |
| Hardness | 200 – 1000 HV (Cr) | 400 – 1500 HV (Type III hard anodize) |
| Color/dye ability | Limited | Excellent (clear, black, colored) |
| Dimensional change | Adds dimension outward | Grows **into** and out of base metal |
| Major industrial use | Decorative, sacrificial, wear | Corrosion, wear, electrical insulation |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Electroplating Cell — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PSU[DC Power Supply] -- Positive lead --> ANO[Anode Pure Ni Metal]
    PSU -- Negative lead --> CAT[Cathode Steel Substrate]
    ANO -- Ionizes into bath --> BATH[Electrolyte NiSO4 + H3BO3 + Cl ions]
    CAT -- Ni2+ ions deposit --> BATH
    BATH -- Provides Ni2+ --> BATH
    H2O[Water + H+ ions] -- Reduced at cathode --> H2E[H2 Gas Evolution Side Reaction]
    CAT -- Coated part emerges --> OUT[Ni Coated Steel Component]
    ANO -- Dissolves gradually --> WASTE[Anode Sludge + Replenishment]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The DC Power Supply pumps electrons from the anode (where nickel dissolves as Ni²⁺) through the external circuit into the cathode, where Ni²⁺ ions from the bath are reduced back to metallic nickel and deposited on the steel. Hydrogen evolution is a competing side-reaction that lowers current efficiency.

## 4.2 Anodizing Cell — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PSU2[DC Power Supply 15 to 20 V] -- Positive lead --> ANO2[Anode Aluminum Workpiece]
    PSU2 -- Negative lead --> CAT2[Cathode Lead or Stainless Steel]
    ANO2 -- Oxidized Al to Al2O3 --> SURF[Aluminum Surface Oxide Layer Growth]
    SURF -- Simultaneously dissolves --> PORE[Porous Honeycomb Structure]
    BATH2[Electrolyte H2SO4 15 percent by weight] -- Provides O2 and H2O --> SURF
    PORE -- Optional dye impregnation --> DYE[Colored Oxide]
    DYE -- Hydrothermal sealing --> SEAL[Sealed Hard Anodized Part]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The aluminum part is positively biased, drawing O²⁻/OH⁻ toward it. The aluminum oxidizes to Al₂O₃, but the sulfuric acid partially dissolves the oxide at the surface, forming pores. Sealing (boiling water or nickel acetate) hydrates the oxide to Al₂O₃·H₂O, closing the pores and locking in corrosion resistance.

## 4.3 Process Flow — From Raw Substrate to Finished Plated Component

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RAW[Raw Steel Substrate] --> DEG[Degreasing Alkaline Cleaner]
    DEG --> PICK[Pickling H2SO4 Dip]
    PICK --> RINSE1[Rinse Deionized Water]
    RINSE1 --> ACT[Activation Dilute Acid]
    ACT --> PLATE[Electroplating Tank]
    PLATE --> RINSE2[Post Plating Rinse]
    RINSE2 --> DRY[Drying Hot Air]
    DRY --> QC[Quality Check Thickness Adhesion]
    QC --> OUT2[Finished Coated Component]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — From Raw Aluminum to Anodized Component

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AL[Raw Aluminum Part] --> CLEAN[Alkaline Cleaning]
    CLEAN --> ETCH[Etching NaOH]
    ETCH --> DESMUT[Desmutting HNO3]
    DESMUT --> RINSEA[Water Rinse]
    RINSEA --> ANOTANK[Anodizing Tank H2SO4 18 to 20 degrees C]
    ANOTANK --> RINSEB[Rinse]
    RINSEB --> DYEOpt[Dyeing Optional]
    DYEOpt --> SEAL2[Sealing Boiling Water or Ni Acetate]
    SEAL2 --> FIN[Anodized Finished Part]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]
State **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis**. Define electrochemical equivalent and write its SI unit.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Faraday's First Law:** *The mass of substance liberated at an electrode during electrolysis is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.*

> **Mathematical form:** $m = Z \cdot Q = Z \cdot I \cdot t$

> **Electrochemical Equivalent (Z):** The mass of a substance (in grams) liberated by **one coulomb** of electric charge.

> **SI Unit:** $\text{kg/C}$ (commonly expressed in g/C for engineering calculations).

> **Relation to molar mass:** $Z = \dfrac{M}{n \cdot F}$, where $M$ is the molar mass (g/mol), $n$ is the valency, and $F$ = 96,485 C/mol.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]
Distinguish between **electroplating** and **electroless plating**. Mention any **two advantages** of electroless plating.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Electroplating | Electroless Plating |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | External DC power supply | Chemical reducing agent (e.g., NaH₂PO₂) |
| Conduction requirement | Substrate must be electrically conductive and connected to cathode | Conductivity **not required**; works on insulators after sensitization |
| Coating uniformity | Thicker at edges/shaar points ("dog-boning") | **Uniform** thickness even in recesses and blind holes |
| Equipment | Power supply, rectifier, anode bars | Simpler — heated tank only |

> **Two advantages of electroless plating:**
> 1. **Uniform thickness** on complex geometries (no edge build-up).
> 2. **Deposits on non-conductors** (plastics, ceramics) after Pd/Sn activation — used in PCB manufacturing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the **electroplating process**. Discuss the role of **anode, cathode, electrolyte**, and **current density** in determining the quality of the deposit.

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Schematic** (as per Section 4.1 of these notes).

**Anode:** Provides metal ions to replenish the bath. In soluble anode plating (e.g., Ni in NiSO₄), the anode dissolves at the same rate the cathode plates, keeping ion concentration constant.

**Cathode:** The substrate where **reduction** of metal ions occurs. Surface finish, cleanliness, and current distribution dictate deposit quality.

**Electrolyte:** Aqueous salt solution (e.g., NiSO₄·6H₂O + NiCl₂ + H₃BO₃). Boric acid is a **buffer** to maintain pH ≈ 3.5–4.5 and prevent burning of deposit.

**Current Density:** Critical parameter. Too low → slow, possibly dendritic growth; too high → burnt, powdery deposits due to concentration polarization.

$$\text{Recommended: } 2 - 5 \;\text{A/dm}^2 \text{ for decorative Ni; up to } 50 \;\text{A/dm}^2 \text{ for hard Cr}$$

**Quality Influencing Factors:**
- Bath temperature (40–60 °C for Ni)
- pH (3.5–4.5)
- Agitation (air or mechanical)
- Addition agents (brighteners, levellers)

> **(b) [7 Marks]** A brass component of area **400 cm²** is to be plated with **gold** (Au³⁺) to a thickness of **2 µm**. Density of gold = **19.3 g/cm³**, atomic mass = **197 g/mol**, current = **3 A**, current efficiency = **85 %**. Calculate the **time of plating** in minutes.

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Volume of gold**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= A \cdot t = 400 \times (2 \times 10^{-4}) = 0.08 \;\text{cm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Mass required**

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \rho \cdot V = 19.3 \times 0.08 = 1.544 \;\text{g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Theoretical time using Faraday's Law**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{theo}} &= \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M} \\
                &= \dfrac{1.544 \times 3 \times 96{,}485}{3 \times 197} \\
                &= \dfrac{446{,}917.7}{591} \\
                &= 756 \;\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Correct for current efficiency (85 %)**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{actual}} &= \dfrac{756}{0.85} = 889 \;\text{s} \approx 14.82 \;\text{minutes}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Final Answer: ≈ 14.82 minutes**

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **anodizing of aluminum** with suitable diagram. Describe the **role of electrolyte, voltage, and temperature** on the formation of anodic oxide film. Mention the **types of anodizing** as per MIL-A-8625.

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Mechanism:**
- Aluminum part made **anode** in dilute **H₂SO₄ (15 % by weight)** bath.
- Reactions at anode: 2 Al + 3 H₂O → Al₂O₃ + 6 H⁺ + 6 e⁻
- Simultaneous dissolution: Al₂O₃ + 3 H₂SO₄ → Al₂(SO₄)₃ + 3 H₂O
- The balance creates a **porous, hexagonal-cell** structure (Keller-Hunter-Robinson model).

**Diagram:** See Section 4.2.

**Role of parameters:**

| Parameter | Effect |
|---|---|
| **Electrolyte (H₂SO₄ vs. CrO₃)** | H₂SO₄ gives thinner, porous films for dyeing; CrO₃ gives thinner, corrosion-protective films on aircraft parts |
| **Voltage (12–22 V)** | Higher voltage → thicker, harder film; controls cell size |
| **Temperature (18–22 °C)** | Lower T → harder, denser film; higher T → softer, porous film (used for bright/dyeing) |
| **Current density (1.5 A/dm²)** | Controls growth rate |

**MIL-A-8625 Types:**
- **Type I** — Chromic acid anodizing (1–7 µm, aerospace).
- **Type II** — Sulfuric acid anodizing (5–18 µm, general purpose).
- **Type III** — Hard anodizing (25–150 µm, wear applications).

> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **anodizing with electroplating** in terms of (i) process polarity, (ii) coating material, (iii) thickness uniformity, (iv) hardness, and (v) need for sealing.

**(b) Model Solution:**

| Property | Electroplating | Anodizing |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Polarity | Substrate = **Cathode** | Substrate = **Anode** |
| (ii) Coating material | Different metal (foreign) | Same metal oxide (own) |
| (iii) Thickness uniformity | Non-uniform at edges | More uniform; possible to control via current density |
| (iv) Hardness | 200–1000 HV | 400–1500 HV (hard anodized) |
| (v) Sealing | Not required (densely packed metal) | **Required** (closes pores, locks in dye, enhances corrosion resistance) |

> **Final Inference:** Anodizing is preferred for **lightweight aerospace and architectural** parts; electroplating is preferred for **decorative, sacrificial, and electrical contact** applications.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Plating & Anodizing Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Forgetting to convert **µm → cm** (divide by 10,000) leads to a 10,000× error in mass calculations. KTU examiners specifically deduct **2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Current Efficiency Misapplication:** Students often *multiply* time by efficiency instead of *dividing*. Correct: $t_{\text{actual}} = t_{\text{theoretical}} / \eta$.
> 3. **Valency Confusion:** Gold can be **Au⁺ or Au³⁺** — always verify the *electrochemical reaction* in the question. For Au³⁺, $n = 3$.
> 4. **Anode vs. Cathode Polarity:** Many students write "Al is cathode in anodizing" — this is the **most common error**. Always remember: **Al is the anode (positive)** in anodizing.
> 5. **Missing Sealing Step:** Any anodizing answer that omits *sealing* is considered **incomplete** by KTU examiners and loses **1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Faraday's Constant Value:** Use **96,485 C/mol** (not 96,000) for higher accuracy; KTU key uses 96,500 by convention — use whichever the question specifies.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Plating & Anodizing**

- **Electroplating = Cathodic deposition** of foreign metal driven by DC current.
- **Anodizing = Anodic oxidation** of the base metal into its own oxide.
- **Faraday's First Law:** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$; **Second Law:** $m_1/m_2 = E_1/E_2$.
- **Faraday's Constant:** **F = 96,485 C/mol** (or 96,500 C/mol, as per question).
- **Current efficiency formula:** $\eta = (m_{\text{actual}}/m_{\text{theoretical}}) \times 100\,\%$.
- **Plating time formula:** $t = \dfrac{t_{\text{coat}} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M \cdot \eta}$.
- **Hydrogen evolution** is the main parasitic reaction at the cathode in acidic baths.
- **Common platings:** Cr, Ni, Zn, Cu, Au, Ag, Cd — for corrosion, wear, decoration, conductivity.
- **Sacrificial (cathodic protection) plating:** Zn and Cd on steel.
- **Anodizing types per MIL-A-8625:** Type I (Chromic), Type II (Sulfuric), Type III (Hard).
- **Anodizing of Al** occurs via simultaneous **oxide growth + oxide dissolution** in H₂SO₄.
- **Sealing (boiling water or nickel acetate)** closes pores, traps dye, enhances corrosion resistance.
- **Hard anodizing (Type III):** thickest (up to 150 µm), hardest (~1500 HV), used in aerospace and hydraulics.
- **Electroless plating:** no external current; uses chemical reducer; uniform on complex shapes; deposits on insulators.
- **Typical current densities:** Ni ~ 2–5 A/dm², hard Cr ~ 30–50 A/dm², anodizing ~ 1.5 A/dm².
- **Anodizing voltage:** 12–22 V; higher V → harder film.
- **Anodizing bath temperature:** ~18–22 °C (lower T → harder film).
- **Engineering applications:** Automotive (Cr), Aerospace (hard anodize, Cd), Electronics (Au/Ag), Architecture (anodized Al), Marine (Zn).
- **Always** convert µm → cm (×10⁻⁴) and minutes → seconds (×60) before substituting in Faraday's Law.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fusion Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fusion Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> **Fusion Processes** in Surface Engineering refer to a class of surface modification and coating techniques in which the **substrate surface (base material)** and/or the **deposited filler/coat material** is melted and subsequently solidified to form a **metallurgically bonded, integral coating layer**. Bonding is achieved by atomic-level interdiffusion across the molten pool interface, producing a *fusion zone* rather than a mechanical or adhesive bond.

These processes are predominantly derived from **welding technology** and are used for:
- **Hardfacing** (wear-resistant overlays)
- **Cladding** (corrosion/oxidation-resistant overlays)
- **Build-up welding** (restoration of worn/damaged components)
- **Surface alloying** (modifying surface chemistry)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 outcome-based framework, students are expected to distinguish *fusion* processes from *non-fusion* processes (like mechanical plating, PVD, CVD). The defining criterion is **melting of the base material** at the interface.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a wooden table with a damaged top. Two ways to fix it:
1. **Glue a new sheet on top** → This is like *mechanical/adhesive bonding* (PVD, painting).
2. **Heat the surface until it softens, press a new veneer, and let them fuse into one piece** → This is a **fusion process**.

In a fusion process, you use a concentrated heat source (flame, arc, plasma, laser, or electron beam) to create a **molten pool** on the substrate. Filler material may be added (like a stick of butter melting into a hot pan). When the heat source moves away, the molten pool **solidifies**, and the atoms of the substrate and filler intermix at the interface, creating a bond that is often **stronger than the parent material itself**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The bond strength in fusion processes is **metallurgical** (atomic-level), not mechanical. This is why fusion-coated components can withstand extreme service conditions (high temperature, abrasion, impact).

### 1.3 Classification of Fusion Processes

| Family | Heat Source | Typical Process |
|---|---|---|
| **Combustion-based** | Oxy-fuel flame | Flame spraying, Flame hardfacing |
| **Arc-based** | Electric arc | SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, Plasma Transferred Arc (PTA) |
| **High-energy beam** | Laser / Electron beam | Laser cladding, Electron beam cladding |
| **Resistance-based** | Electrical resistance | Electroslag surfacing |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Heat Input (H):** Measured in **kJ/mm** (or **J/mm**)
- **Dilution (D):** Expressed as **%** (percentage)
- **Deposition Rate:** Measured in **kg/h** or **g/min**
- **Substrate Melting Point (Tm):** Critical for material selection (e.g., Steel ≈ 1500 °C, Aluminum ≈ 660 °C)
- **Boltzmann Constant (k):** **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** (used in thermal radiation calculations)
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant (σ):** **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** (radiative heat loss)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mechanism of Fusion Bonding (Step-by-Step)

The fusion process follows a strict sequence of metallurgical and thermal events:

1. **Heat Source Application:** A concentrated energy source (arc, flame, beam) is directed at the substrate surface.
2. **Substrate Melting:** The intense heat raises the local temperature above the substrate's melting point, forming a **molten weld pool**.
3. **Filler Introduction:** Filler material (in the form of wire, powder, or rod) is introduced into the pool.
4. **Mixing & Marangoni Convection:** Within the molten pool, fluid flow (driven by surface tension gradients, electromagnetic forces, and buoyancy) creates **marangoni convection**, ensuring thorough mixing of substrate and filler atoms.
5. **Solidification:** As the heat source moves away, the molten pool cools and **solidifies epitaxially** from the substrate grains upward, creating a metallurgical bond.
6. **Cooling & Solid-State Transformations:** The solidified layer may undergo phase transformations (e.g., austenite → martensite in steels) depending on cooling rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Fusion" Matters:** The epitaxial solidification (where new grains grow continuously from the substrate's existing grain structure) is what gives fusion coatings their exceptional bond strength — typically **> 200 MPa** for most weld overlays.

### 2.2 Process Parameters (The "Big Five")

Every fusion process is governed by five critical, interrelated parameters:

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Effect on Coating |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat Input** | H | kJ/mm | Controls penetration, dilution, distortion |
| **Travel Speed** | S | mm/min | Higher S → Lower H → Less penetration |
| **Current** | I | A | Higher I → Deeper penetration |
| **Voltage** | V | V | Higher V → Wider bead, more dilution |
| **Filler Feed Rate** | F | kg/h or mm/min | Higher F → Thicker deposit, less dilution |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Legend:** All formulas below are **Kerala University Exam essentials**. Master these before the ESE.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$H = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}$$ | **Heat Input** (arc efficiency not included) | V = Voltage (V), I = Current (A), S = Travel speed (mm/min) |
| 2 | $$H_{\text{eff}} = \eta \times H \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}$$ | **Effective Heat Input** with process efficiency | η = Arc efficiency factor (e.g., 0.6–0.85 for GMAW) |
| 3 | $$D = \frac{A_{\text{base}}}{A_{\text{base}} + A_{\text{filler}}} \times 100\%$$ | **Geometric Dilution** | A_base = Area of melted substrate, A_filler = Area of deposited filler |
| 4 | $$D = \frac{B}{A + B} \times 100\%$$ | **Dilution (cross-section method)** | B = Penetration depth, A = Bead height above substrate |
| 5 | $$CR = \frac{k \times (T_m - T_0)^n}{H^m}$$ | **Cooling Rate** (empirical) | k, n, m = empirical constants; T_m = melting point, T_0 = preheat |
| 6 | $$\text{HAZ width} \propto \sqrt{H}$$ | **Heat-Affected Zone** width scaling | H = Heat input |
| 7 | $$\text{Deposition Rate} = \frac{\rho \times A_{\text{bead}} \times S}{1000} \quad \text{[kg/h]}$$ | **Mass deposition rate** | ρ = density (g/cm³), A_bead = cross-section area (mm²) |
| 8 | $$\frac{T}{T_m} = f\!\left(\frac{H}{S \cdot t^{3/2}}\right)$$ | **Thermal Cycle Parameter** (Rosenthal) | t = plate thickness, dimensionless solution to heat equation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in:
> - **Welding procedure specification (WPS)** development in fabrication industries
> - **Cladding design** for nuclear and petrochemical reactors
> - **Wear-resistant overlay** selection for mining and earth-moving equipment
> - **Repair welding** of high-value aerospace turbine components

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Heat Input (H)

**Starting Point:** Heat input is defined as the **total energy delivered per unit length** of weld travel.

**Step 1 — Define Power:**
The electrical power input to an arc is the product of voltage and current.

$$P = V \times I \quad \text{[Watts = J/s]}$$

**Step 2 — Define Time per Unit Length:**
If the travel speed is S (in mm/min), then the time taken to traverse 1 mm of weld is:

$$t = \frac{1 \text{ mm}}{S \text{ mm/min}} = \frac{1}{S} \text{ min} = \frac{60}{S} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Energy per Unit Length:**
Multiplying power by time per unit length gives the energy deposited per millimeter:

$$H = P \times t = (V \times I) \times \frac{60}{S} \quad \text{[J/mm]}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kiloJoules:**
Dividing by 1000 to convert J to kJ:

$$\boxed{H = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}}$$

> **Interpretation:** If V = 25 V, I = 200 A, S = 300 mm/min, then H = (25 × 200 × 60)/(300 × 1000) = **1.0 kJ/mm** — a typical value for GMAW cladding of carbon steel.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Geometric Dilution (D)

**Starting Concept:** Dilution quantifies *how much of the final weld bead is composed of melted substrate* versus added filler.

**Step 1 — Define Areas:**
From a polished cross-section of the weld bead, measure:
- $A_{\text{base}}$ = area of substrate melted (below original surface line)
- $A_{\text{filler}}$ = area of filler added (above original surface line)

**Step 2 — Form the Ratio:**
The total bead area is:

$$A_{\text{total}} = A_{\text{base}} + A_{\text{filler}}$$

The fraction that came from the substrate is:

$$D = \frac{A_{\text{base}}}{A_{\text{total}}}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Percentage:**

$$\boxed{D = \frac{A_{\text{base}}}{A_{\text{base}} + A_{\text{filler}}} \times 100\%}$$

**Step 4 — Alternative Cross-Section Method (Bead-on-Plate):**
For a single bead, the cross-section method uses:

$$D = \frac{B}{A + B} \times 100\%$$

where:
- $B$ = penetration depth (depth of substrate melted, measured from original surface down)
- $A$ = reinforcement height (height of filler above original surface)

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Problem)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]:**
> In a GMAW hardfacing operation, the parameters are: V = 28 V, I = 250 A, Travel speed S = 350 mm/min. The arc efficiency is 0.75. Calculate:
> (a) Heat input H
> (b) Effective heat input H_eff
> (c) If the cross-section shows penetration B = 1.5 mm and reinforcement A = 4.5 mm, find the dilution D.
> (d) Comment on whether this dilution is suitable for a hardfacing application.

**Solution:**

**(a) Heat Input:**

$$H = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$$

$$H = \frac{28 \times 250 \times 60}{350 \times 1000}$$

$$H = \frac{420{,}000}{350{,}000} = 1.2 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**(b) Effective Heat Input:**

$$H_{\text{eff}} = \eta \times H = 0.75 \times 1.2 = 0.9 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**(c) Dilution (Cross-Section Method):**

$$D = \frac{B}{A + B} \times 100\% = \frac{1.5}{4.5 + 1.5} \times 100\%$$

$$D = \frac{1.5}{6.0} \times 100\% = 25\%$$

**(d) Comment:**
A dilution of **25%** is **moderately high** for a hardfacing application. For hardfacing, KTU standard practice expects dilution in the range of **10–20%** to preserve the hardfacing alloy's wear-resistant carbides. Higher dilution dilutes the alloy content of the deposit, reducing hardness. **Recommendation:** Reduce heat input by increasing travel speed or reducing current.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Fusion Processes

| Process | Heat Source | Dilution Range | Deposition Rate | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flame Spraying** | Oxy-fuel flame | 5–15% | 2–6 kg/h | Large area, low-precision coatings |
| **SMAW (Shielded Metal Arc)** | Electric arc | 15–40% | 1–4 kg/h | Field hardfacing, thick overlays |
| **GMAW (Gas Metal Arc)** | Electric arc + shielding gas | 15–30% | 3–8 kg/h | Automated production hardfacing |
| **GTAW (Gas Tungsten Arc)** | Non-consumable arc | 10–25% | 0.5–2 kg/h | Precision cladding, thin layers |
| **PTA (Plasma Transferred Arc)** | Constricted plasma arc | 5–15% | 4–10 kg/h | **Low-dilution, high-quality** cladding |
| **Laser Cladding** | Focused laser beam | 2–10% | 0.5–3 kg/h | Precision, low heat input |
| **Electron Beam Cladding** | Focused electron beam | 2–8% | High (in vacuum) | Aerospace, vacuum-chamber parts |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Classification of Fusion Processes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FUSION PROCESSES IN SURFACE ENGINEERING] --> B[Combustion Based]
    A --> C[Arc Based]
    A --> D[High Energy Beam Based]
    A --> E[Resistance Based]

    B --> B1[Oxy Fuel Flame Spraying]
    B --> B2[Flame Hardfacing]

    C --> C1[SMAW Shielded Metal Arc]
    C --> C2[GMAW Gas Metal Arc]
    C --> C3[GTAW Gas Tungsten Arc]
    C --> C4[PTA Plasma Transferred Arc]

    D --> D1[Laser Cladding]
    D --> D2[Electron Beam Cladding]

    E --> E1[Electroslag Surfacing]

    B1 --> F1[Low Cost Low Precision]
    C1 --> F2[Rugged Field Use]
    C2 --> F3[High Productivity]
    C4 --> F4[Low Dilution Premium Cladding]
    D1 --> F5[Precision Low HAZ]
    D2 --> F6[Vacuum Aerospace Grade]
    E1 --> F7[Thick Section Overlay]
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fusion Cladding Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Power Source V and I] --> H[Heat Generation Arc or Beam]
    H --> MP[Molten Weld Pool Formation]
    F[Filler Wire or Powder Feeder] --> MP
    MP --> S1[Substrate Base Material]
    MP --> CZ[Coating Zone Fused Layer]
    S1 --> CZ
    CZ --> SG[Solidified Clad Layer]
    SG --> HAZ[Heat Affected Zone in Substrate]
    SG --> QC[Quality Control Testing Hardness and Bond Strength]
```
