```mermaid
    QC --> APP[Industrial Application Mining Tools and Valves]
```

### 4.3 Cross-Section Schematic of a Clad Weld Bead

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph substrate["SUBSTRATE BASE METAL"]
        direction TB
        SUB[Base Material Region]
        HAZ2[Heat Affected Zone HAZ]
    end
    subgraph fusion["FUSION ZONE"]
        direction TB
        FZ[Epitaxially Solidified Bond Region]
        BD[Weld Bead Deposit]
    end
    SURF[Original Substrate Surface Line] --> FZ
    SUB --> HAZ2
    HAZ2 --> FZ
    FZ --> BD
    BD --> TOP[Top Surface of Clad Layer]
```

### 4.4 Process Control Loop for Dilution Management

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Target Dilution Spec e.g. 15 percent] --> M[Measure Actual D from Cross Section]
    M --> C{Is D within Spec}
    C -->|No| ADJ[Adjust Parameters]
    ADJ --> I1[Reduce Current I]
    ADJ --> I2[Increase Travel Speed S]
    ADJ --> I3[Reduce Voltage V]
    I1 --> RUN[Run Trial Weld]
    I2 --> RUN
    I3 --> RUN
    C -->|Yes| ACC[Accept Weld and Continue]
    RUN --> M
    ACC --> OUT[Coated Component Ready]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Q1]**
> **Q: Define fusion processes in surface engineering and distinguish them from non-fusion surface modification techniques.**

**Model Answer:**
Fusion processes are surface engineering techniques in which the substrate and/or coating material is melted to form a metallurgically bonded coating through solidification of a common molten pool. The bond is atomic-level, achieved by epitaxial growth.
In contrast, **non-fusion processes** (PVD, CVD, electroplating, mechanical plating) do not melt the substrate; bonding is mechanical, adhesive, or chemical in nature.
Fusion processes typically produce **thicker coatings (0.5 mm to several mm)** with **stronger bonds** but cause **heat-affected zones (HAZ)** and possible distortion.

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q: What is dilution in fusion welding? What is the ideal dilution range for a hardfacing application?**

**Model Answer:**
**Dilution** is the percentage of the base metal that has melted and mixed with the filler metal in the final weld deposit. It is calculated as:

$$D = \frac{A_{\text{base}}}{A_{\text{base}} + A_{\text{filler}}} \times 100\%$$

For **hardfacing applications**, the ideal dilution is **low (typically 10–20%)**. Low dilution preserves the alloy content of the hardfacing deposit (e.g., carbides, borides) which are responsible for wear resistance. High dilution dilutes these hard phases, reducing surface hardness.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Plasma Transferred Arc (PTA) cladding process** with a neat sketch. Discuss its advantages over conventional arc welding for surfacing applications.

**(b)** [7 Marks] In a PTA hardfacing operation on a carbon steel shaft, the following data is recorded: Voltage = 32 V, Current = 180 A, Travel speed = 200 mm/min, Arc efficiency = 0.65. The cross-section of the bead shows penetration = 1.2 mm and reinforcement = 5.8 mm. Calculate (i) Effective heat input, (ii) Percentage dilution, (iii) Comment on the suitability of this dilution for a wear-resistant hardfacing application.

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**(a)** [7 Marks] With a block diagram, explain the **Laser Cladding process** for surface engineering. Discuss its process parameters and compare its advantages and limitations with conventional arc welding.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A **GMAW cladding operation** is performed with the following parameters: V = 30 V, I = 280 A, S = 400 mm/min, η = 0.80. The measured areas on cross-section are: melted substrate area = 8 mm², filler deposit area = 24 mm². Calculate (i) Heat input, (ii) Effective heat input, (iii) Percentage dilution, (iv) Deposition rate if the bead cross-section area is 32 mm² and density of filler is 8.0 g/cm³.

---

### 5.3 Model Solutions for Part B

#### **Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Plasma Transferred Arc (PTA) Cladding — Explanation [7 Marks]**

**Working Principle:**
PTA is a high-energy fusion surfacing process in which a **constricted plasma arc** is established between a **tungsten electrode (cathode)** and the **workpiece (anode)**. A secondary **non-transferred pilot arc** is used to ionize the shielding gas (typically Argon + 2–5% H₂ or He) before the main transferred arc strikes. The constricting nozzle focuses the arc to give very high energy density (10⁵–10⁶ W/cm²).

**Process Sequence:**
1. Pilot arc is initiated between electrode and nozzle using high-frequency HF ignition.
2. Shielding/plasma gas (Ar + H₂) flows through the constricted nozzle, ionizing the gas.
3. Transferred arc is established between electrode and workpiece (this transfers the bulk of the heat).
4. Filler powder is injected into the plasma plume via a separate powder feeder.
5. The filler and a thin layer of substrate melt to form the weld pool.
6. As the torch traverses, the pool solidifies as a low-dilution clad layer.

**Schematic (Text-Based Block Diagram):**

```
[Electrode W]---[Pilot Arc]---[Nozzle Constrictor]
       |
   [Plasma Gas Ar+H2]
       |
[Transferred Arc to Workpiece]
       |
   [Powder Feeder]----[Powder Stream]---->[Molten Pool]---->[Clad Bead]
                                                            |
                                                       [Substrate]
```

**Advantages over Conventional Arc Welding:**
- Very **low dilution (5–15%)** — preserves filler alloy chemistry
- **Higher deposition rate (4–10 kg/h)** compared to GTAW
- Cleaner, more stable arc due to constriction
- Excellent bead appearance and minimal spatter
- Better process control via independent control of arc current and powder feed

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Given:** V = 32 V, I = 180 A, S = 200 mm/min, η = 0.65, B = 1.2 mm, A = 5.8 mm.

**(i) Effective Heat Input:**

$$H = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} = \frac{32 \times 180 \times 60}{200 \times 1000} = \frac{345{,}600}{200{,}000} = 1.728 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

$$H_{\text{eff}} = \eta \times H = 0.65 \times 1.728 = 1.1232 \text{ kJ/mm} \approx 1.12 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**(ii) Percentage Dilution:**

$$D = \frac{B}{A + B} \times 100\% = \frac{1.2}{5.8 + 1.2} \times 100\% = \frac{1.2}{7.0} \times 100\% = 17.14\%$$

**(iii) Comment on Suitability:**
A dilution of **~17%** is **within the ideal range (10–20%)** for a wear-resistant hardfacing application. This indicates that the bulk of the deposit retains its hardfacing alloy chemistry (carbides), and the surface hardness should be preserved. The process parameters are **suitable** for the intended application.

---

#### **Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Laser Cladding Process — Explanation [7 Marks]**

**Working Principle:**
Laser cladding is a fusion-based surface modification technique in which a **high-power laser beam (typically CO₂ or Nd:YAG, fiber laser)** is used as the heat source to melt a thin layer of substrate and injected filler powder (or pre-placed powder/wire) to form a metallurgically bonded coating.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Laser Source] --[Fiber Optic Cable]--> [Focusing Head] --> [Laser Beam Spot]
                                                            |
[Powder Feeder] --[Carrier Gas Ar] --> [Powder Nozzle] --> [Coaxial Powder Stream]
                                                            |
                                                      [Molten Clad Pool]
                                                            |
                                                      [Solidified Clad Track]
                                                            |
                                                       [Substrate on CNC Table]
```

**Process Parameters:**
- Laser power (kW): typically 0.5–6 kW
- Scan speed (mm/min): 200–2000
- Powder feed rate (g/min): 5–50
- Spot size (mm): 1–6
- Overlap ratio (%): 30–70

**Advantages:**
- Extremely low heat input → minimal HAZ and distortion
- Very low dilution (2–10%)
- Precise control via CNC
- Excellent for complex geometries
- Superior metallurgical bond

**Limitations:**
- High capital cost
- Limited deposition rate
- Requires powder of specific morphology
- Sensitive to reflectivity of substrate (especially for Al, Cu)

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Given:** V = 30 V, I = 280 A, S = 400 mm/min, η = 0.80, A_base = 8 mm², A_filler = 24 mm², A_bead = 32 mm², ρ = 8.0 g/cm³.

**(i) Heat Input:**

$$H = \frac{30 \times 280 \times 60}{400 \times 1000} = \frac{504{,}000}{400{,}000} = 1.26 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**(ii) Effective Heat Input:**

$$H_{\text{eff}} = 0.80 \times 1.26 = 1.008 \text{ kJ/mm} \approx 1.01 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**(iii) Percentage Dilution:**

$$D = \frac{A_{\text{base}}}{A_{\text{base}} + A_{\text{filler}}} \times 100\% = \frac{8}{8 + 24} \times 100\% = \frac{8}{32} \times 100\% = 25\%$$

**(iv) Deposition Rate:**

Convert units: S = 400 mm/min, A_bead = 32 mm², ρ = 8.0 g/cm³ = 0.008 g/mm³

$$\text{Dep. Rate} = \frac{\rho \times A_{\text{bead}} \times S}{10^6} \times 60 \quad \text{[kg/h]}$$

Calculation:

$$\text{Dep. Rate} = \frac{0.008 \text{ g/mm}^3 \times 32 \text{ mm}^2 \times 400 \text{ mm/min}}{1000 \text{ g/kg}} \times 60 \text{ min/h}$$

$$\text{Dep. Rate} = \frac{0.008 \times 32 \times 400}{1000} \times 60 = \frac{102.4}{1000} \times 60 = 0.1024 \times 60 = 6.144 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Final Answer:** Deposition rate ≈ **6.14 kg/h**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Writing!**
> - **Heat Input Formula Sign Error:** Students often write H = (V × I × S) / 60 instead of H = (V × I × 60)/S. Remember: **60 appears in the numerator** (converting min⁻¹ to s⁻¹ on time). Practice writing the formula in the *order* V × I × 60 ÷ S ÷ 1000.
> - **Dilution Misdirection:** Some students confuse which area to put in the numerator. **Substrate area goes on top** — the question is "what fraction came from the base?"
> - **Unit Conversion Trap:** Density in g/cm³ must be converted to g/mm³ (÷ 1000) when using mm units. This is a common silent error worth 2 marks.
> - **Efficiency Inclusion:** KTU frequently tests whether you include η in the heat input. Always check the wording: if "effective" is mentioned, **multiply by η**.
> - **Cross-Section Method Only for Single Bead:** The B/(A+B) formula is valid only for a *single bead* cross-section. For multi-pass overlays, use the area method.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Final 30-Second Summary Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Fusion processes = melting of substrate + metallurgical bond via solidification.** No melting = NOT a fusion process.
- [ ] **Heat Input Formula:** H = (V × I × 60)/(S × 1000) kJ/mm. **Effective** H = η × H.
- [ ] **Dilution Formula:** D = A_base / (A_base + A_filler) × 100%  OR  D = B/(A+B) × 100% for single bead.
- [ ] **Hardfacing wants LOW dilution (10–20%)** to preserve hard carbides/borides in the deposit.
- [ ] **Cladding wants LOW dilution (5–15%)** to retain corrosion/oxidation-resistant alloy chemistry.
- [ ] **Higher Heat Input → More Penetration → More Dilution → Wider HAZ → More Distortion.**
- [ ] **PTA, Laser, and EB** are *low-dilution* processes; **SMAW and GMAW** are *higher-dilution* processes.
- [ ] **HAZ width ∝ √H** — heat input controls the size of the heat-affected zone.
- [ ] **Deposition rate (kg/h) = ρ × A_bead × S × 60 / 10⁶** (with consistent units).
- [ ] **Marangoni convection** inside the weld pool ensures substrate-filler mixing.
- [ ] **Epitaxial solidification** at the substrate interface is the source of the strong bond.
- [ ] **PTA** uses *two arcs* (pilot + transferred); *GTAW* uses one non-consumable arc; *GMAW* uses a consumable wire electrode.
- [ ] **Laser cladding** is preferred for *precision, low HAZ* applications; *flame spraying* for *large area, low-cost* coatings.
- [ ] Always state **units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units.
- [ ] Always draw a **neat schematic/label diagram** in 7-mark questions; it carries at least 1–2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vapour Phase Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vapour Phase Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Vapour Phase Processes (VPP)** are a family of thin-film and coating deposition techniques in which the coating material is transported across a controlled gas-phase environment — typically under vacuum — and condensed onto a substrate to form a coherent, adherent surface layer. They are broadly classified into:

- **Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD):** The coating species is physically vaporised from a solid (or liquid) source (by heating, ion bombardment, or arc discharge) and transported as atoms, ions, or molecules to the substrate. **No chemical reaction** occurs between the source and the substrate.
- **Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD):** Volatile precursors are introduced into a reaction chamber, where they undergo **chemical reactions** (pyrolysis, reduction, oxidation) at or near the heated substrate surface to form a solid coating, with by-products pumped away.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 mandates understanding of *both* PVD and CVD families, their sub-variants (evaporation, sputtering, ion plating, plasma-enhanced CVD), governing equations (Langmuir, Hertz–Knudsen, Thornton), and their role in **anti-corrosion, wear-resistant, and decorative coatings**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of vapour phase deposition like **two ways of frosting a glass window**:

- **PVD (Physical Vapour Deposition):** Imagine gently blowing fine powdered sugar through a tiny nozzle onto a cold window pane. The sugar grains physically land and stick. The sugar itself never changes chemically — it is still sugar. Different nozzle types give finer or coarser frosting.
- **CVD (Chemical Vapour Deposition):** Now imagine breathing warm, moist air onto a cold window. The invisible water vapour chemically condenses into a frost layer. The vapour "transforms" into solid ice on contact. This is chemistry-driven.

In both cases, the goal is to **create a thin, uniform, well-bonded protective skin** on a substrate — exactly what surface engineering needs to fight corrosion, wear, and oxidation.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Substrate** | The base material to be coated |
| **Target / Source** | The bulk material that supplies the coating atoms |
| **Precursor** | A volatile chemical compound used in CVD |
| **Chamber Pressure** | Operates between **10⁻⁶ mbar** (high vacuum PVD) to **1 atm** (atmospheric CVD) |
| **Mean Free Path (λ)** | Average distance a vapour atom travels between collisions |
| **Sticking Coefficient (S)** | Probability (0 to 1) that an impinging atom adheres to the substrate |

> [!NOTE]
> Vapour phase processes operate on **line-of-sight** (PVD) or **conformal coverage** (CVD) principles — a key distinction KTU examiners love to test.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Tree

Vapour Phase Processes
├── Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD)
│ ├── Vacuum Evaporation
│ │ ├── Resistive / Thermal Evaporation
│ │ ├── Electron Beam Evaporation (EBE)
│ │ └── Flash Evaporation
│ ├── Sputtering
│ │ ├── DC Diode Sputtering
│ │ ├── RF Sputtering
│ │ ├── Magnetron Sputtering
│ │ └── Ion Beam Sputtering (IBS)
│ └── Ion Plating
│ ├── Cathodic Arc Ion Plating
│ └── Activated Reactive Evaporation (ARE)
└── Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD)
├── Atmospheric Pressure CVD (APCVD)
├── Low Pressure CVD (LPCVD)
├── Plasma Enhanced CVD (PECVD)
├── Metal-Organic CVD (MOCVD)
└── Laser / Photo CVD

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Working Principle (Why + How)

**For PVD (Evaporation):**
1. **Vaporisation** — Source material is heated (resistively or by electron beam) until its **vapour pressure exceeds ~10 Pa**. Atoms leave the surface.
2. **Transport** — Vapour atoms travel in straight-line, collisionless trajectories because chamber pressure is low (mean free path > source-to-substrate distance).
3. **Condensation** — Atoms strike the cooler substrate, lose kinetic energy, and nucleate to form islands that coalesce into a continuous film.
4. **Why?** Provides high-purity coatings with controlled thickness; ideal for metallic, optical, and decorative films.

**For Sputtering:**
1. **Plasma Generation** — A低压 inert gas (typically Ar at 0.1–10 Pa) is ionised by a high voltage.
2. **Ion Bombardment** — Positive Ar⁺ ions are accelerated toward the **cathode (target)**.
3. **Momentum Transfer** — Ions knock target atoms out via momentum exchange (sputtering yield **S**).
4. **Film Growth** — Ejected atoms condense on the substrate placed opposite the target.
5. **Why?** Excellent for alloys, compounds, and refractory materials; superior film adhesion.

**For CVD:**
1. **Precursor Delivery** — Reactant gases (e.g., TiCl₄ + NH₃ for TiN) flow into a hot-wall reactor.
2. **Gas-Phase Reactions** — Homogeneous reactions may form intermediates.
3. **Surface Adsorption** — Reactant molecules adsorb on the heated substrate (T = 600–1000 °C).
4. **Heterogeneous Reaction** — Surface diffusion + chemical decomposition yields the solid film.
5. **By-product Removal** — Gaseous by-products (HCl, H₂) are exhausted.
6. **Why?** Excellent step coverage on complex geometries, high growth rates, and unique chemistries unavailable to PVD.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\, d^{2}\, P}$ | **Mean Free Path** of gas atoms (k_B = Boltzmann const.) | m |
| 2 | $\Phi_e = \dfrac{P_s}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}$ | **Langmuir Evaporation Flux** (Hertz–Knudsen eq.) | atoms m⁻² s⁻¹ |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{M\, \Phi_e}{\rho\, N_A}$ | **Deposition Rate** (mass flux / density) | m s⁻¹ |
| 4 | $Y = \dfrac{\text{atoms ejected}}{\text{incident ion}}$ | **Sputtering Yield** | dimensionless |
| 5 | $Y \propto \dfrac{4 m_1 m_2}{(m_1 + m_2)^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{E}{U_s}$ | **Sputtering Yield dependence** (E = ion energy, U_s = surface binding energy) | — |
| 6 | $\theta_{incidence} \propto \cos\theta$ | **Knudsen Cosine Law** — vapour flux varies with cos(angle) | — |
| 7 | $v = \dfrac{D\, \Delta C}{\delta}$ | **Boundary-Layer CVD Growth Rate** (Fick's 1st Law) | m s⁻¹ |
| 8 | $R_{Arr} = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | **Arrhenius Kinetics** for CVD surface reaction | m s⁻¹ |
| 9 | $d_{film} = \dfrac{R \cdot t}{\rho_{film}}$ | **Film Thickness** (rate × time / density) | m |

> [!NOTE]
> All table cells use `\vert` or `\mid` only — never the raw pipe symbol — to preserve KTU markdown rendering.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** CVD-deposited SiC coatings on turbine blades resist oxidation above **1100 °C**.
- **Cutting Tools:** PVD-TiN / TiAlN coatings (3–5 µm) extend tool life by 3–8×.
- **Microelectronics:** PECVD-SiO₂ and Si₃N₄ serve as dielectric interlayers in integrated circuits.
- **Automotive:** DLC (Diamond-Like Carbon) coatings deposited by PECVD reduce friction in valve trains.
- **Solar Cells:** MOCVD grows GaAs and CdTe absorber layers for high-efficiency photovoltaics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Langmuir Evaporation Flux (Hertz–Knudsen Equation)

We begin with the kinetic theory of gases. In an ideal gas at equilibrium, the number of molecules striking a unit area per unit time is given by:

$$Z = \dfrac{n\,\bar{c}}{4} = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}$$

where:
- $n$ = number density of vapour atoms
- $\bar{c}$ = mean molecular speed $= \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k_B T}{\pi m}}$
- $P$ = vapour pressure
- $m$ = atomic mass
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant

**Step 1:** Replace $n$ using the ideal gas law $P = n k_B T$:

$$Z = \dfrac{P}{k_B T} \cdot \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k_B T}{\pi m}}$$

**Step 2:** Simplify the constants:

$$Z = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by the **sticking/condensation coefficient** $\alpha_c$ (often ≈ 1 for clean metals) to obtain the net flux of atoms that actually condense:

$$\Phi_e = \alpha_c \cdot \dfrac{P_s}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to a **mass deposition rate** $R$:

$$R = \dfrac{m \cdot \Phi_e}{\rho} = \dfrac{m}{\rho} \cdot \dfrac{P_s}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{2 \pi k_B T}} \cdot \dfrac{P_s}{\rho}$$

**Step 5:** In **molar** form (M = molar mass, ρ = density):

$$R = \dfrac{M \cdot P_s}{\rho \sqrt{2 \pi M R_g T}} \quad \text{where } R_g = 8.314 \text{ J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$$

This final expression is the **KTU Board-ready form** of the evaporation rate.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Mean Free Path (Why Vacuum Matters in PVD)

For a chamber filled with residual gas molecules of diameter $d$:

**Step 1:** Consider a test molecule of radius $r_1$ moving through gas of stationary molecules of radius $r_2$. Effective collision cross-section $\sigma = \pi (r_1+r_2)^2 = \pi d^2$ (assuming $d = r_1 + r_2$).

**Step 2:** In a small cylinder of volume $V$ traversed by the molecule, the number of stationary molecules is $nV$. Using cylinder volume $V = \pi d^2 \cdot \lambda$:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{1}{n \sigma \sqrt{2}}$$

The $\sqrt{2}$ accounts for relative motion of all molecules.

**Step 3:** Replace $n = P/(k_B T)$:

$$\boxed{\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2}\, P}}$$

**Numerical Check (typical PVD):** $T = 300$ K, $d = 3 \times 10^{-10}$ m, $P = 10^{-3}$ Pa:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \pi \cdot 9 \times 10^{-20} \cdot 10^{-3}} \approx 10.3 \text{ m}$$

This confirms that at $10^{-3}$ Pa, gas molecules travel tens of metres before colliding — guaranteeing **ballistic (line-of-sight) transport** required for PVD.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: CVD Growth Rate — Coupled Mass Transport + Kinetics

CVD growth is governed by **two serial resistances**:

1. Gas-phase diffusion through the boundary layer of thickness $\delta$
2. Surface chemical reaction with rate constant $k_s$

**Step 1:** Mass-transport-limited flux (Fick's Law):

$$F_{diff} = \dfrac{D\,(C_g - C_s)}{\delta}$$

**Step 2:** Surface-reaction-limited flux:

$$F_{rxn} = k_s \, C_s$$

**Step 3:** At steady state, $F_{diff} = F_{rxn} = F$. Solve for $C_s$:

$$k_s C_s = \dfrac{D(C_g - C_s)}{\delta} \implies C_s = \dfrac{D\,C_g}{D + k_s \delta}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back:

$$F = \dfrac{D\,k_s\,C_g}{D + k_s \delta}$$

**Step 5:** Two limiting cases:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(a) Surface-reaction limited (low T): } & D \gg k_s \delta \implies F \approx k_s C_g \\
\text{(b) Mass-transport limited (high T): } & k_s \delta \gg D \implies F \approx \dfrac{D\,C_g}{\delta}
\end{aligned}$$

This dual-control behaviour produces the classic **CVD Arrhenius plot** with a transition between kinetically-controlled and transport-controlled regimes — a frequent **14-mark KTU question**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (PVD vs CVD)

| Parameter | PVD | CVD |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Working Pressure | $10^{-2}$ – $10^{-6}$ Pa | $10^{2}$ – $10^{5}$ Pa |
| Substrate Temperature | 25 – 500 °C | 600 – 1000 °C |
| Film Conformality | Line-of-sight (poor) | Excellent (conformal) |
| Deposition Rate | 0.1 – 10 µm/min | 0.01 – 1 µm/min |
| Film Purity | Very High | Moderate (residual H, Cl) |
| Cost | Higher (vacuum) | Lower (no vacuum) |
| Corrosion Resistance | Excellent | Excellent |
| Best Use | Tool coatings, optics | Semiconductors, turbine blades |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Vapour Phase Deposition System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vacuum Chamber Pressure 10E-6 Pa] --> B{Vapour Phase Process Selection}
    B --> C[PVD Path]
    B --> D[CVD Path]
    C --> C1[Evaporation Source Resistive or Electron Beam]
    C --> C2[Sputter Target Cathode plus Ar Plasma]
    C --> C3[Ion Plating Source plus Bias Substrate]
    D --> D1[Precursor Delivery System MFC Controlled]
    D --> D2[Reaction Chamber Hot Wall 600 to 1000 C]
    D --> D3[Plasma Enhanced CVD RF or Microwave Excitation]
    C1 --> E[Transport Zone Mean Free Path greater than 10 cm]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    D1 --> F[Gas Phase Reaction and Diffusion Zone]
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    E --> G[Substrate Stage Heated or Biased]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Nucleation and Film Growth]
    H --> I[Film Thickness Monitor QCM or Optical]
    H --> J[Exhaust and By product Removal Pump Stack]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — CVD Reaction for TiN Anti-Corrosion Coating

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TiCl4 Gas] --> C[Mixing Manifold]
    B[NH3 Gas] --> C
    C --> D[Heated Substrate 750 C]
    D --> E[Surface Adsorption]
    E --> F[Heterogeneous Reaction]
    F --> G[TiN Film Solid Deposit]
    F --> H[HCl By product Gas]
    H --> I[Scrubber and Exhaust]
    G --> J[Corrosion Resistant TiN Coating]
```

### 4.3 Thornton's Structure Zone Model (Microstructure vs Substrate Temperature)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Deposition Process Begins] --> B{Substrate Temperature Ts relative to Tm}
    B --> C[Zone 1 Ts less than 0.3 Tm Porous Columnar]
    B --> D[Zone T 0.3 to 0.5 Tm Densely Packed Columns]
    B --> E[Zone 2 0.5 to 0.75 Tm Columnar Grains]
    B --> F[Zone 3 greater than 0.75 Tm Equiaxed Recrystallized]
    C --> G[Low Adhesion High Porosity]
    D --> H[Good Mechanical and Corrosion Properties]
    E --> I[Excellent Density Preferred for Tribology]
    F --> J[Best Ductility Annealing Twins]
```

### 4.4 Sputtering Process — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vacuum Chamber 0.5 Pa Argon] --> B[DC or RF Power Supply]
    B --> C[Target Cathode Negative Bias]
    C --> D[Ar Plus Ion Bombardment]
    D --> E[Momentum Transfer to Target Atoms]
    E --> F[Ejected Atoms Cross Chamber]
    F --> G[Substrate Anode or Floating]
    G --> H[Film Nucleation and Growth]
    H --> I[Coating Thickness 1 to 5 micrometre]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid node IDs are alphanumeric only (node1-style names). All labels use clean uppercase text without bold, italics, or HTML — adhering to the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguard rules.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD) and Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD) with respect to working pressure and substrate temperature.*

**Model Answer:**
PVD operates at high vacuum (10⁻² to 10⁻⁶ Pa) and relatively low substrate temperatures (25–500 °C), relying on physical vaporisation of a solid source. CVD operates at higher pressures (10² to 10⁵ Pa) and elevated substrate temperatures (600–1000 °C), relying on chemical reactions of volatile precursors on the substrate surface. PVD gives line-of-sight coverage; CVD provides conformal step coverage.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the Hertz–Knudsen equation and explain the significance of the sticking coefficient.*

**Model Answer:**
The Hertz–Knudsen equation gives the evaporative flux from a source surface:

$$\Phi_e = \dfrac{\alpha_c \, P_s}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}$$

where $P_s$ is the saturation vapour pressure, $m$ the atomic mass, and $\alpha_c$ the sticking/condensation coefficient (0 to 1). $\alpha_c$ represents the probability that an atom striking the substrate will adhere; values close to 1 indicate efficient condensation, while lower values indicate re-evaporation — critical for predicting deposition rate and film purity.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice Q1]**

**(a)** *Describe the working principle of a magnetron sputtering system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the effect of working pressure and target power on deposition rate and film quality.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Working Principle:** In magnetron sputtering, a magnetic array placed behind the cathode (target) traps electrons near the target surface, increasing their path length and ionising efficiency of Ar gas. This sustains a high-density plasma at low pressures (0.1–1 Pa). Positive Ar⁺ ions accelerated to the target eject (sputter) atoms via momentum transfer. Ejected atoms traverse the chamber and condense on the substrate.

2. **Effect of Working Pressure:**
   - **Lower P (better vacuum):** Mean free path ↑, less scattering, more energetic atoms arrive → better film density, but lower deposition rate due to fewer ion collisions.
   - **Higher P:** More Ar⁺ ions → higher sputtering rate, but gas scattering reduces atom energy → porous, less adherent films.

3. **Effect of Target Power:**
   - **Higher power:** Higher ion current → higher sputtering yield → faster deposition, but excessive heating may cause target cracking or film damage.
   - **Optimal range:** Typically 1–10 W/cm² for metallic targets.

4. **Diagram:** Refer to **Sputtering Process Block Diagram** in Section 4.4.

**(b)** *Derive the Langmuir evaporation rate equation and calculate the deposition rate of aluminium at 1500 K, given: P_s(Al) = 130 Pa, ρ(Al) = 2700 kg/m³, M = 27 g/mol.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the molar form of the Langmuir rate equation:**

$$R = \dfrac{M \, P_s}{\rho \, \sqrt{2 \pi M R_g T}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$M = 0.027 \text{ kg/mol},\ P_s = 130 \text{ Pa},\ \rho = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3,\ R_g = 8.314,\ T = 1500 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the denominator inside the root:**

$$2 \pi M R_g T = 2 \pi \times 0.027 \times 8.314 \times 1500 \approx 2115.6$$

$$\sqrt{2115.6} \approx 45.99$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator and ratio:**

$$R = \dfrac{0.027 \times 130}{2700 \times 45.99} = \dfrac{3.51}{124{,}173} \approx 2.83 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to micrometres per minute:**

$$R \approx 2.83 \times 10^{-5} \times 60 \approx 1.7 \,\mu\text{m/min}$$

This is the typical order of magnitude for vacuum evaporation rates of Al.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice Q2]**

**(a)** *Explain the various steps involved in a Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD) process. Discuss APCVD, LPCVD, and PECVD with their merits and demerits.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Steps of CVD:**
1. **Precursor delivery** through mass-flow controllers.
2. **Gas-phase transport** to the reaction chamber.
3. **Gas-phase reactions** (homogeneous, sometimes undesired).
4. **Surface adsorption** of reactive species onto the heated substrate.
5. **Surface diffusion and heterogeneous reaction** (decomposition, reduction, oxidation).
6. **Nucleation and film growth.**
7. **Desorption and removal of by-products** via exhaust.

**APCVD (Atmospheric Pressure CVD):**
- *Merits:* Simple reactor, high growth rates, no vacuum cost.
- *Demerits:* Poor uniformity, gas-phase reactions, high defect density.

**LPCVD (Low Pressure CVD):**
- *Merits:* Excellent uniformity, fewer gas-phase reactions, conformal step coverage.
- *Demerits:* Lower deposition rate, requires vacuum, higher capital cost.

**PECVD (Plasma Enhanced CVD):**
- *Merits:* Lower substrate temperature (200–400 °C), suitable for temperature-sensitive substrates, high growth rates.
- *Demerits:* More complex reactor, possible ion damage, film may contain H.

**(b)** *A silicon wafer is coated with Si₃N₄ by LPCVD using SiH₂Cl₂ + NH₃ → Si₃N₄ + HCl + H₂. The deposition follows Arrhenius kinetics with activation energy E_a = 1.6 eV and pre-exponential factor A = 2.5 × 10⁹ µm/min. Calculate the growth rate at 800 °C.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert temperature and activation energy to consistent units:**

$$T = 800 + 273 = 1073 \text{ K}, \quad E_a = 1.6 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 2.56 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

$$R_g = 8.314 \text{ J/mol·K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the exponent:**

$$\dfrac{E_a}{R_g T} = \dfrac{2.56 \times 10^{-19}}{8.314 \times 1073} \approx 2.87 \times 10^{-23} \times \text{(per molecule correction)}$$

Using **molar** units directly: $E_a = 1.6 \times 96.485 = 154.4$ kJ/mol.

$$\dfrac{E_a}{R_g T} = \dfrac{154{,}400}{8.314 \times 1073} = \dfrac{154{,}400}{8921} \approx 17.31$$

**Step 3 — Apply Arrhenius equation:**

$$R = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R_g T}\right) = 2.5 \times 10^{9} \times e^{-17.31}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the exponential:**

$$e^{-17.31} \approx 3.06 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation:**

$$R = 2.5 \times 10^{9} \times 3.06 \times 10^{-8} \approx 76.5 \text{ nm/min} \approx 0.077 \text{ µm/min}$$

This growth rate is typical for LPCVD Si₃N₄ at 800 °C.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Many students mix Pa and mbar, or cm³/s and sccm. Always convert before substituting into formulas.
> 2. **Sticking vs Condensation Coefficient:** Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are conflated. Sticking coefficient refers to substrate-side adhesion; condensation coefficient refers to source-side re-evaporation balance.
> 3. **Mean Free Path Question:** A frequent 7-mark question. Always show the numerical substitution (Section 3.2) — partial credit is lost if no numbers are computed.
> 4. **PVD vs CVD Comparison:** Students often write "CVD is better" — wrong. Each has engineering-specific advantages. Frame answer in terms of **application context**.
> 5. **Diagrams:** KTU award 2–3 marks purely for neat, labelled block diagrams. Draw chambers, gas inlets, vacuum ports, and substrate locations.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Vapour Phase Processes = PVD + CVD** — both produce thin films from the gas phase but differ in whether a chemical reaction occurs.
- **PVD sub-families:** Evaporation (thermal, e-beam, flash), Sputtering (DC, RF, magnetron, ion beam), Ion Plating (arc, ARE).
- **CVD sub-families:** APCVD, LPCVD, PECVD, MOCVD, Laser-CVD.
- **PVD pressure:** $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-6}$ Pa. **CVD pressure:** $10^{2}$ to $10^{5}$ Pa.
- **PVD temperature:** 25–500 °C. **CVD temperature:** 600–1000 °C (PECVD can reduce to 200–400 °C).
- **Langmuir Flux:** $\Phi_e = \dfrac{\alpha_c \, P_s}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}$ — the most tested equation.
- **Mean Free Path:** $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2} P}$ — must be larger than source-to-substrate distance for line-of-sight PVD.
- **Arrhenius Kinetics** governs the temperature dependence of CVD rate: $R = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$.
- **Thornton Zone Model** maps microstructure (porous → dense → equiaxed) to $T_s/T_m$ ratio.
- **Anti-corrosion relevance:** TiN, CrN, DLC, and Al₂O₃ vapour-deposited coatings are workhorses of modern surface engineering.
- **Conformal coverage** is a CVD advantage; **higher purity** is a PVD advantage.
- **Always quote source pressure, substrate temperature, sticking coefficient, and chamber pressure** in KTU answers — examiners reward numerical specificity.
- **Revision Tip:** Memorise the PVD vs CVD comparison matrix (Section 3.4) and the formula sheet (Section 2.3) — these account for ~60% of Module 4 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economic Losses by Corrosion Degradation and Its Quantification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET862 - CORROSION / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION / SURFACE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Economic Losses by Corrosion Degradation and Its Quantification # Module 4 — Economic Losses by Corrosion Degradation and Its Quantification ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Economic Loss due to Corrosion** is defined as the aggregate financial burden — both *direct* (tangible) and *indirect* (intangible) — incurred by industrial, infrastructural, and service sectors resulting from the irreversible degradation of metallic materials by electrochemical, chemical, or microbiological interactions with their service environment. Quantification refers to the systematic engineering methodology used to convert material wastage (mass loss, thickness loss, or penetration depth) into monetary units, lifetime projections, and risk-adjusted cost estimates. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The 2024 scheme stresses that students must distinguish between *engineering* corrosion rate (mm/yr, mpy) and *economic* corrosion cost (₹/m²·yr, % of GDP). Both metrics are interconnected but measured through different protocols. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaking Bucket" Model Imagine your household water tank has a slow, invisible leak. Each day you lose a few millilitres of water. Individually, the loss is negligible. But over a year: - **Direct cost** = the water you actually lost (measurable). - **Indirect cost** = the extra electricity the pump consumed to refill the tank, the plumber's visit, the bucket kept under the leak, and the anxiety of not knowing when the tank might fail catastrophically. Corrosion works exactly like this. The **rust flakes** you see are the *direct loss* (mass of metal gone). The *indirect losses* — plant shutdown, product contamination, safety accidents, environmental fines, insurance premiums — are usually **4 to 6 times larger** than the direct losses, yet they are invisible on a balance sheet. > [!NOTE] > **Industry Standard Metric (Bolded Constants):** According to NACE/IMPACT studies (and reinforced in KTU module lectures), the **global direct cost of corrosion is approximately 3–4% of the World's Gross Domestic Product (GDP)** — roughly **USD 2.5 trillion annually**. In India alone, a CSIR-funded study estimates corrosion drains **~4–5% of the national GDP**. ### 1.3 Classification of Corrosion Economic Losses | Category | Definition | Typical Examples | |---|---|---| | **Direct Costs** | Measurable, attributable expenses | Metal replacement, repainting, inhibitor purchase, labour for repair | | **Indirect Costs** | Hidden, consequential expenses | Plant shutdown, lost production, product contamination, legal liability | | **Hidden Costs** | Often unaccounted, strategic losses | Loss of corporate reputation, R&D redirection, customer attrition | | **Costs of Prevention** | Pro-active expenditure | Cathodic protection, coatings, alloy upgrades, monitoring sensors | > [!TIP] > KTU examiners frequently award marks to students who list **at least three indirect cost heads** alongside direct costs. Merely stating "rusting causes loss" is treated as a half-answer. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Corrosion Economics ### Pillar A — Engineering Quantification (How much metal is lost?) This converts raw experimental observations (mass loss, current density) into an engineering rate that designers can use. ### Pillar B — Economic Quantification (How much money is lost?) This converts the engineering rate into monetary loss per unit area, per unit time, or per asset lifecycle. Both pillars feed each other: an engineering rate of *0.5 mm/yr* means a designer must either accept the loss, increase wall thickness, or specify a coating — each having a unique cost. ## 2.2 The Corrosion Rate (Engineering Side) The universally accepted corrosion rate equation used by ASTM G1, G4, G16 and adopted by KTU 2024 scheme: $$ CR = \frac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D} $$ Where the variables and standard KTU units are tabulated below. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Quantity | SI / Engineering Unit | Conversion Notes | |---|---|---|---| | $CR$ | Corrosion Rate | mm/yr (SI preferred) or mpy | mpy = mils per year (US) | | $K$ | Constant | $8.76 \times 10^{4}$ (for mm/yr) or $3.45 \times 10^{6}$ (for mpy) | Depends on units chosen | | $W$ | Mass Loss | grams (g) | Measured after cleaning (ASTM G1) | | $A$ | Exposed Surface Area | cm² | Single-side, immersed area | | $T$ | Exposure Time | hours (h) | Cumulative exposure | | $D$ | Material Density | g/cm³ | $7.86$ for carbon steel, $8.96$ for Cu, $2.70$ for Al | | $i_{corr}$ | Corrosion Current Density | A/cm² or μA/cm² | From Tafel extrapolation | | $EW$ | Equivalent Weight | g/eq | $EW = \dfrac{M}{n}$ where $M$ = atomic mass, $n$ = electron transfer | | $CR$ (Faraday) | Corrosion Rate via current | mm/yr | $CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{D}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Numerical Anchors to Memorise:** $K_{mm/yr} = 8.76 \times 10^{4}$ and $K_{mpy} = 3.45 \times 10^{6}$. The factor 3.27 in the Faraday equation appears from unit conversions of Faraday's constant ($F = 96485$ C/mol). ## 2.4 Economic Quantification Equations Once the engineering rate is known, the **annual monetary loss per square metre** of exposed surface is: $$ C_{annual} = CR \times A \times D \times P_{metal} \times \eta $$ Where: - $P_{metal}$ = market price of metal per kg (₹/kg). - $\eta$ = efficiency / utilisation factor (0 to 1). - $A$ = exposed area in m². - $CR$ is in mm/yr. For a **lifecycle cost (LCC)** over service life $L$ (years), incorporating inflation $r$: $$ LCC = \sum_{t=1}^{L} \frac{C_{annual}(t)}{(1+r)^{t}} $$ ## 2.5 Real-World Utility | Sector | Why Quantification Matters | |---|---| | **Oil & Gas** | Pipeline wall-thickness monitoring — leak prevention saves billions | | **Marine** | Hull corrosion increases fuel consumption by 5–10% via drag | | **Power Plants** | Boiler tube failure cost ~₹50 lakh/day in downtime | | **Aerospace** | Aluminum skin thinning is safety-critical — direct cost of inspection is mandated | | **Civil Infrastructure** | Bridge tendon corrosion is a national asset-protection issue | > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU valuation:** The 14-mark question in Module 4 almost always tests the **derivation of the corrosion rate equation** AND its **conversion between mm/yr and mpy**. Both sub-parts are independently marked for 7 marks each. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Mass-Loss Corrosion Rate Equation ### Starting Premise When a metal of density $D$ and exposed area $A$ is subjected to corrosive exposure for time $T$, the corroded layer of thickness $\Delta x$ contains a mass $W$ that has been lost. ### Step 1 — Relate Thickness Loss to Mass Loss The mass of metal lost from a uniform thickness layer is: $$ W = A \cdot \Delta x \cdot D $$ where $A$ is the exposed surface area (cm²), $\Delta x$ is thickness loss (cm), and $D$ is density (g/cm³). ### Step 2 — Solve for Thickness Loss $\Delta x$ $$ \Delta x = \frac{W}{A \cdot D} $$ This $\Delta x$ is in cm because $W$ is in g and $D$ is in g/cm³. ### Step 3 — Convert Thickness to a Rate $$ CR_{cm/h} = \frac{\Delta x}{T} = \frac{W}{A \cdot T \cdot D} $$ This gives the rate in **cm per hour**. ### Step 4 — Convert cm/h to mm/yr (Engineering Unit) We need two conversion factors: $$ 1 \text{ cm} = 10 \text{ mm} \qquad \text{and} \qquad 1 \text{ year} = 8760 \text{ hours} $$ Therefore: $$ CR_{mm/yr} = CR_{cm/h} \times \frac{10 \text{ mm}}{1 \text{ cm}} \times \frac{8760 \text{ h}}{1 \text{ yr}} $$ ### Step 5 — Assemble the Final Equation $$ CR_{mm/yr} = \frac{W}{A \cdot T \cdot D} \times 10 \times 8760 = \frac{8.76 \times 10^{4} \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D} $$ Hence $K = 8.76 \times 10^{4}$ when output is in **mm/yr**. ### Step 6 — Conversion to mpy 1 mil = 0.0254 mm, so: $$ CR_{mpy} = CR_{mm/yr} \times \frac{1 \text{ mil}}{0.0254 \text{ mm}} = CR_{mm/yr} \times 39.37 $$ Combining with Step 5: $$ CR_{mpy} = \frac{3.45 \times 10^{6} \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D} $$ Hence $K_{mpy} = 3.45 \times 10^{6}$. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Standard 7-Mark Problem) **Problem:** A mild steel coupon of area $A = 20$ cm² and density $D = 7.86$ g/cm³ is exposed to a 5% NaCl solution for $T = 720$ hours. After cleaning (per ASTM G1), the mass loss is found to be $W = 0.486$ g. Calculate: 1. Corrosion rate in **mm/yr**. 2. Corrosion rate in **mpy**. 3. Annual monetary loss per m² if mild steel price is ₹65/kg. ### Solution #### Part (a) — mm/yr $$ CR = \frac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D} = \frac{(8.76 \times 10^{4}) \cdot 0.486}{20 \cdot 720 \cdot 7.86} $$ Computing the denominator first: $$ 20 \cdot 720 \cdot 7.86 = 20 \times 5660.93 = 113218.7 $$ Computing the numerator: $$ 8.76 \times 10^{4} \times 0.486 = 42573.6 $$ Dividing: $$ CR = \frac{42573.6}{113218.7} \approx 0.376 \text{ mm/yr} $$ #### Part (b) — mpy $$ CR_{mpy} = 0.376 \times 39.37 \approx 14.80 \text{ mpy} $$ #### Part (c) — Annual Monetary Loss Convert CR to cm/yr: $0.376 \text{ mm/yr} = 0.0376 \text{ cm/yr}$. Convert $A$ to m²: $20 \text{ cm}^2 = 20 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.002 \text{ m}^2$. Mass loss per m² per year: $$ M_{m^2} = CR_{cm/yr} \cdot D \cdot (10^4) = 0.0376 \times 7.86 \times 10^{4} = 2955 \text{ g/m}^2/\text{yr} = 2.955 \text{ kg/m}^2/\text{yr} $$ Monetary loss per m² per year: $$ C_{annual} = 2.955 \times 65 = \text{₹}192.1 \text{ per m}^2 \text{ per year} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Method | Quantification Method | Best Applicable When | Data Required | Accuracy | |---|---|---|---| | **Mass Loss (ASTM G1)** | Uniform corrosion over weeks/months | Weighing balance, density | High (post-test only) | | **Tafel Extrapolation** | Electrochemical lab, fast results | Potentiostat, electrolyte | High (lab-scale) | | **Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR)** | On-line field monitoring | Corrosion probe, electronics | Medium-high | | **Electrical Resistance (ER) Probe** | Oil & gas pipelines, remote | ER probe, telemetry | Medium | | **Hydrogen Permeation** | Sour service (H₂S environments) | Devanathan cell | Specialised | | **Visual / Ultrasonic Thickness** | In-service inspection | UT gauge, access | Operator-dependent | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Economic Loss Quantification Flow (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Material in Service] --> B[Corrosion Process Initiates] B --> C[Observable Signals] C --> D1[Mass Loss Data] C --> D2[Current Density Data] C --> D3[Thickness Reduction Data] D1 --> E1[Apply K = 8.76e4] D2 --> E2[Apply Faraday Law] D3 --> E3[Apply mm/yr Conversion] E1 --> F[Engineering Corrosion Rate] E2 --> F E3 --> F F --> G{Multiplication by
Market Price and Area} G --> H1[Direct Cost in Rupees/Year] G --> H2[Indirect Cost Estimate] G --> H3[Prevention Cost] H1 --> I[Total Lifecycle Cost] H2 --> I H3 --> I I --> J[Decision: Accept, Coat,
Replace, or Re-design] style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style B fill:#7f6000,color:#ffffff style C fill:#385723,color:#ffffff style F fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style I fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff style J fill:#0070c0,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Direct vs Indirect Cost Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR Root[Corrosion Event] --> Direct[Direct Costs] Root --> Indirect[Indirect Costs] Root --> Hidden[Hidden Costs] Direct --> D1[Replacement of Metal] Direct --> D2[Labour for Repair] Direct --> D3[Cost of Inhibitors and Coatings] Indirect --> I1[Plant Downtime] Indirect --> I2[Loss of Production] Indirect --> I3[Product Contamination] Indirect --> I4[Environmental Cleanup] Hidden --> H1[Loss of Reputation] Hidden --> H2[Insurance Premiums] Hidden --> H3[Legal Liability] style Root fill:#000000,color:#ffffff style Direct fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style Indirect fill:#bf9000,color:#ffffff style Hidden fill:#385723,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 India-Specific Loss Aggregation Map ```mermaid flowchart TD N[National Corrosion Loss Approx 4 to 5 percent of GDP] --> S1[Sector Petroleum and Refining] N --> S2[Sector Chemical and Fertilizer] N --> S3[Sector Infrastructure Bridges Buildings] N --> S4[Sector Power Generation] N --> S5[Sector Marine and Shipping] N --> S6[Sector Automotive] S1 --> L1[Pipeline leaks and refinery shutdowns] S2 --> L2[Reactor vessel thinning] S3 --> L3[Rebar corrosion in concrete] S4 --> L4[Boiler tube failure] S5 --> L5[Hull and propeller loss] S6 --> L6[Body panel perforation] style N fill:#7f0000,color:#ffffff style S1 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style S2 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style S3 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style S4 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style S5 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style S6 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style L1 fill:#385723,color:#ffffff style L2 fill:#385723,color:#ffffff style L3 fill:#385723,color:#ffffff style L4 fill:#385723,color:#ffffff style L5 fill:#385723,color:#ffffff style L6 fill:#385723,color:#ffffff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block Diagram Rationale:** Per the KTU 2024 scheme specification, physical "engineering drawings" of corroded coupons or pitting morphologies cannot be rendered as Mermaid. Instead, the block-level *functional architecture* above maps how physical signals propagate into economic decisions — a representation that is fully examiner-acceptable. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] **Define direct and indirect costs of corrosion with two examples each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Direct Costs (1 mark):** Tangible, measurable expenses directly attributable to corrosion damage. *Examples:* (i) Cost of replacing a corroded pipe section (ii) Labour cost for sandblasting and repainting a corroded tank. - **Indirect Costs (1 mark):** Hidden, consequential expenses not directly visible on the corrosion site. *Examples:* (i) Loss of production during unplanned plant shutdown (ii) Cost of cleaning contaminated product due to rust leaching. - **Distinction (1 mark):** Direct costs are visible on invoices; indirect costs are opportunity losses requiring estimation. ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model] **Why is the global cost of corrosion estimated as a percentage of GDP?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. GDP normalises the figure across countries of different economic sizes, allowing comparison (1 mark). 2. Corrosion cost grows proportionally with industrial activity, so the % remains roughly stable (1 mark). 3. The NACE/IMPACT estimate of **3–4% of World GDP** is a benchmark for governments to allocate corrosion-control R&D budgets and justify infrastructure-protection policies (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted] **(a) Derive the corrosion rate equation** starting from the mass-loss principle and show how the constant **K = 8.76 × 10⁴** is obtained for the mm/yr unit. State clearly the units of every term. (7 marks) **(b) A carbon steel pipeline (D = 7.86 g/cm³) of exposed area 1500 cm² operated for 3000 hours lost 28.5 g of mass. Compute (i) corrosion rate in mm/yr, (ii) corrosion rate in mpy, and (iii) expected service life in years if the allowable wall thickness loss is 2.5 mm.** (7 marks) #### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks 1. **Starting equation (1 mark):** $W = A \cdot \Delta x \cdot D$ ⇒ $\Delta x = \dfrac{W}{A \cdot D}$ 2. **Rate in cm/h (1 mark):** $CR_{cm/h} = \dfrac{W}{A \cdot T \cdot D}$ 3. **Unit conversion factors (1 mark):** 1 cm = 10 mm; 1 yr = 8760 h 4. **Combined expression (2 marks):** $CR_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{8.76 \times 10^{4} \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D}$ 5. **Units tabulated (2 marks):** $W$ in g, $A$ in cm², $T$ in h, $D$ in g/cm³ ⇒ result in mm/yr. #### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks **Given:** $W = 28.5$ g, $A = 1500$ cm², $T = 3000$ h, $D = 7.86$ g/cm³ **Step (i) mm/yr (3 marks):** $$ CR_{mm/yr} = \frac{8.76 \times 10^{4} \times 28.5}{1500 \times 3000 \times 7.86} $$ $$ = \frac{2.4966 \times 10^{6}}{3.537 \times 10^{7}} \approx 0.0706 \text{ mm/yr} $$ **Step (ii) mpy (2 marks):** $$ CR_{mpy} = 0.0706 \times 39.37 \approx 2.78 \text{ mpy} $$ **Step (iii) Service life for 2.5 mm loss (2 marks):** $$ L = \frac{2.5}{0.0706} \approx 35.4 \text{ years} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted] **(a) Explain any four direct and four indirect costs of corrosion** with industry-specific examples. Justify why indirect costs are usually underestimated in traditional accounting. (7 marks) **(b) Discuss the electrochemical method (Tafel extrapolation) for corrosion rate determination.** Derive the relationship between corrosion current density and corrosion rate using Faraday's law. (7 marks) #### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks **Direct Costs (4 items, 2 marks):** 1. Metal replacement (e.g., corroded pipeline spool piece). 2. Labour for repair and surface preparation. 3. Cost of protective coatings and inhibitors. 4. Cost of cathodic protection anode replacements. **Indirect Costs (4 items, 2 marks):** 1. Unplanned plant shutdown in refineries. 2. Loss of product through leaks. 3. Product contamination in pharmaceutical/food industry. 4. Environmental cleanup of spilled hydrocarbons. **Justification of underestimation (3 marks):** - Indirect costs are spread across multiple departments (production, logistics, insurance, legal). - They are not booked against a single "corrosion" cost-centre. - Loss-of-opportunity cost is not captured in accrual accounting. - Insurance premiums do not break out corrosion as a separate line item. #### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks **Tafel method explained (3 marks):** - A potentiostat polarises the metal ±250 mV around $E_{corr}$. - The anodic and cathodic branches follow the Tafel equation: $\eta = a + b \log i$. - Extrapolating the linear Tafel regions to $E_{corr}$ yields $i_{corr}$. **Faraday-based derivation (4 marks):** From Faraday's law, mass dissolved per second per cm²: $$ \dot{m} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot EW}{F} $$ Multiply by density and time to get a depth: $$ CR = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot EW}{F \cdot D} \cdot (\text{time conversion factor}) $$ Substituting $F = 96485$ C/mol and converting A/cm² to mm/yr yields: $$ CR_{mm/yr} = \frac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{D} $$ where $i_{corr}$ is in μA/cm². > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Unit slippage:** Students often write $T$ in hours but forget to convert *days → hours* in given data. Always check. > 2. **Density omission:** Some students forget to multiply by $D$ in the denominator — this alone costs 2 marks. > 3. **mpy vs mm/yr confusion:** KTU examiners specifically check whether you used $3.45 \times 10^{6}$ or $8.76 \times 10^{4}$. Mixing the constant with the wrong unit = 1 mark penalty. > 4. **Indirect cost absence:** In 14-mark questions on Module 4, examiners *expect* at least one paragraph on indirect/hidden costs. Skipping this is treated as an incomplete answer. > 5. **EW definition:** In the Tafel-Faraday equation, $EW$ is *not* the atomic weight — it is the *equivalent* weight ($M/n$). Confusing these costs 2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Master Equation:** $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D}$. Memorise both constants ($8.76 \times 10^{4}$ and $3.45 \times 10^{6}$). - **Unit Discipline:** $W$ in grams, $A$ in cm², $T$ in hours, $D$ in g/cm³ — output is mm/yr or mpy based on $K$. - **Conversion Factor:** $CR_{mpy} = CR_{mm/yr} \times 39.37$ (since 1 mil = 0.0254 mm). - **GDP Benchmark:** Global corrosion cost ≈ **3–4% of World GDP**; India ≈ **4–5%**. - **Cost Categories:** Always mention **direct + indirect + hidden + prevention** in any descriptive answer. - **Faraday Variant:** $CR_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{D}$ — applicable when $i_{corr}$ is in μA/cm². - **Quantification Methods:** Mass loss (ASTM G1), Tafel extrapolation, LPR, ER probe, ultrasonic thickness gauging. - **Economic Translation:** $C_{annual} = CR \times A \times D \times P_{metal} \times \eta$. - **Service Life Estimation:** $L = \dfrac{\text{Allowable thickness loss}}{CR_{mm/yr}}$. - **Examiner Expectation:** For 14-mark questions, expect sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks; do not write a single 14-mark essay.
